I've deferred buying the game for a while just to let it settle a bit. Have now come around, and here we are. The short of it is that, for the core game it self... The campaign is a bit alarmingly short. In typical modern Anno fashion, it is also the guided tutorial to get you through the basics of the Anno production chains and core game loop. I see why people say it is glaringly short and seems to just abruptly ends... Personally, I haven't even actually finished the 1800 campaign, that one actually felt long or complex than it needed to be. But, 117's campaign is not the correct course adjustment.  That said, you may be wondering why I still give this a thumbs up. Granted the short campaign story, the main core content is there.... Which, Anno veterans would be very familiar with at this point. The new religion/belief system, and the marshland mechanics are a good addition that still plays well with the core Anno gameplay.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            Given I'm only 10-ish hours in, I wouldn't say I've dived deep enough to late game production chains... but for what it is, the amount of "game" there is is pretty approximate to what you get from 1800 at launch. Just with a much shorter campaign, which a bit offset with the new mechanics they introduced.

I have thoroughly enjoyed Anno 117 as a relaxing game with gorgeous city building. As a lover of the time period, I am blown away by the detail that both Roman and Celtic cultures are brought to life.. There is easily hundreds of hours here already, if you're not trying to min-max and rush production and treat every island you settle as something unique to bring alive.    My advice would be to keep the difficulty settings low, as the AI is not even remotely playing the same game as the player and will field impressive fleets and armies untethered from an actual economy. Also, limit your FPS anywhere to 30 to 60. This isn't a game in which you're using movement and twitch reflexes, and the level of detail it renders your cities means this game can easily make mid-tier machines chug along.   As for complaints, people are correct: there's seems to be an entire third act missing from the campaign --which is something I may not have cared about, but this game surprised me by getting me attached to my governor (play the sister, the story is so much better!) and it all seems like it's leading up to an a-historical climax that never comes.  You've also probably seen cosmetics packs that are not included in the Season Pass, and yeah, it's a pretty crappy move. I can tolerate nickel and diming to an extent, in the context that city builders are a niche of an already niche genre (strategy), and I can only imagine the nightmare of trying to justify a game like this to executives.   If you love city builders and are a of mid Roman and late Celtic periods, get this yesterday. If performance and it's DLC pricing turn you off, I'd say wait for a sale and still pick this up and try it out.

I'm all for hating Ubisoft, but honestly the game is alright. It's not great, there are some downgrades from 1800, some bugs / missing qol, but overall you will get your money's worth of entertainment granted you like this genre. Some points:  The Albion citizen tiers and production chains were fun to solve.  War sucks even more. Combat is ass, going to war with AI is even more annoying than in 1800. Takes way longer to blitz the island you are trying to take, so you need overwhelming force all over the world to make sure your trade routes don't get disrupted by random AI fleets. There are also severe penalties for going to war in the first place. And there is no option to peacefully take over islands like with shares in 1800.  Volcanic island is annoying to deal with. It's supposed to be the Crown Falls of this game, and it kind of is, but it forces you to deal with this annoying chore of surviving / recovering from volcanic eruptions. That is what made me close the game after 60 hours, don't wanna deal with that. Just want to build.  Overall like 6.5/10 game. Dlc might make it better than that.

I highly recommend and enjoy this game. It performs very well and it only crashed once (Internet). The biggest concern I see with other reviews is the campaign and that it isn't complete or designed well. I almost entirely play the endless mode only so I can't give a 100% exact answer to if it's true in my opinion. This review focuses on endless mode and the game itself, and the main storyline is left out. That being said, I absolutely rave this game. It's miles ahead of the previous Anno: 1800, which was already a great game in my opinion. I highly appreciate the addition of land units that majorly diversify combat, as well as the ability to customize ships and modules. The combat is pretty fair, I could say the land combat would be nice with a bit more dynamics, but otherwise it is still pretty good. City-Building has never been so advanced, dynamic, and down-right entertaining in the Anno series before. It utilizes various new systems and features that I can't stop using. This game is highly re-playable in my opinion and I found myself creating multiple save files with different playstyles, techniques, and ethics just to see what would happen. I paid full price for this game and I'm more-than-satisfied with the product. Thumbs up from me!

I really enjoy the game.     I have Played 1800 (over 200hr playtime) before and I really liked the "switch" to 117. The option to Build diagonal and the available cosmetics as well as the general looks of the game allow for a playstyle focussed or interested in good looking islands.    The option for land combat is something I have (at the time of writing) not jet engaged in. It is a nice to have addition and allows for very cosmetic builds, especially with the city walls and towers which allow for more city defense (other then just turrets at the harbour areas like in 1800).    I am also not sure about the reserch tree. it was very annoying in the beginning, that forestry huts can't just produce anywhere and that this option is hidden behind a reserch tree. The option for this to be in base game is possible after unlocking it in the "Hall of Fame" which is a place, where you can unlock things permanently by achiving in game achivements. Over all I think that the reserche tree is a good addition to the game and just one you have to get used to if you are coming form 1800 into 117.    Despite early skeptecism I really like the local effects of buildings on the various attributes (Health, Fire safety, Happynes etc.). They Insentevice for production buildings to be build close to the consumtion and balancing of positive and negative attributes. Speaking of the attributes, I really like the addition of a display of the attributes (Health, Happines etc) they make it transparent, how often fires start, disease spreads etc.     I really dislike that the game got a new DLC within month after the release for quite a lot of money. An this DLC is not even included into the one year pass. This gave the Impression of a money grab on the DLC (which I have not yet bought for this reason and the price). Speaking of Money, I find the price of 50€ to be quite expensive (especially looking at recant indie Releases such as Hollow Knight Silk Song) so I deffinitly recommend to by the game when it is on a discount.      Over all I still think it is a good game and would recommend it. Especially those who have played and liked 1800 this is a recommendation. There are some mechanics you will have to get used to, but this is nothing out of reach so to say and gives some now wind into the world of ANNO.     Over all I would rate the Game a 8/10

Pros: It's a fantastic Roman city builder sim. The building of cities feels satisfying, the structures themselves look good, and the overall vibe is terrific. The combat system is solid, the trading system has a nice balance of depth and simplicity, and the resource system encourages trade and expansion without hard punishing you for having bad luck.   Cons: The campaign feels very half-baked. Some city management systems need some adjusting. The exponential decrease in fire safety and public health as you level up is very annoying. Sure, it makes sense, but it reaches a point where you genuinely cannot address it any further. You'll have nearly 100% of the possible coverage for your fire department and your fire safety rating will still be -1000 or greater, which then makes certain allies not like you and potentially pull out of trade deals or pacts.   Overall, solid 7/10. I expect that rating to creep towards 8/10 or 9/10 as future updates flesh out the campaign and some of the systems are adjusted.

Anno 117: Pax Romana has a really strong foundation and captures the classic Anno feeling surprisingly well. The city-building systems are satisfying, the Roman setting is visually beautiful, and watching your settlements grow across different regions feels rewarding. The soundtrack and environmental details also help create a relaxing atmosphere.  That said, the game still feels a bit uneven in some areas. Certain mechanics feel overly simplified compared to earlier Anno titles, and the pacing can become repetitive after a while. I also wish there was more depth in diplomacy and late-game progression. Some UI and quality-of-life features could use improvement too.  Overall, I still enjoyed my time with it and would recommend it to fans of city builders and the Anno series, especially if you enjoy slower, management-focused gameplay. It’s a solid experience, but it doesn’t fully reach the depth or replayability of the best entries in the franchise yet.

I was a big fan of Anno 1800, which is an excellent city-builder. Because of that, I decided to support Anno 117 by buying the Early Access + 1-Year DLC package. Big mistake. The biggest issue is the 1-Year DLC. I expected all the content promised for the first year to be included, but some items cannot be claimed and require extra payment. This feels like a scam and left a really bad taste. On top of that, the game experience itself is extremely frustrating. The story suddenly ends with almost no warning, and after that there is no clear way to progress. I got completely stuck with nowhere to go. After struggling for a while, I ended up uninstalling the game. Early Access is supposed to be about supporting development and getting a playable version, but this feels unfinished and poorly planned. I’ve now learned my lesson: no more Early Access purchases for me (goodbye Battlefield and similar titles too). They always drop big discounts after the full release anyway, so there’s zero point in paying full price early. If you loved Anno 1800 like I did, I recommend waiting for the full release and proper reviews before buying this one.

[h1]🏛️ Anno 117: Pax Romana — the empire builder I’ve been waiting for[/h1]  [b]🎮 I run the curator group [url=https://store.steampowered.com/curator/45719096/]Build, Rule, Repeat[/url] — simulators and system-driven games where mechanics matter.[/b]  [hr]  [h2]🦅 Rome finally enters the Anno formula[/h2]  Anno 117: Pax Romana feels like a natural evolution of the series.   Instead of futuristic logistics or industrial expansion, this time the focus shifts toward the Roman Empire — roads, provinces, trade, administration, and controlled growth.  And honestly? The setting fits perfectly.  [hr]  [h3]🧠 What already looks promising:[/h3] [list] [*]Strong Roman atmosphere and architecture   [*]Classic Anno production chain gameplay   [*]Large-scale city building with political flavor   [*]Potential for deep logistics and province management   [*]Visual quality looks excellent so far   [/list]  [hr]  [h3]⚠️ What still raises questions:[/h3] [list] [*]Long-term gameplay depth remains unclear   [*]Possible streamlining of mechanics for wider audiences   [*]Military and politics may end up too simplified   [*]Performance during late-game expansion is still unknown   [/list]  [hr]  [h2]⚙️ Why the setting matters[/h2]  Rome works especially well for Anno because the entire fantasy of the empire is built on systems: roads, taxation, expansion, logistics, supply, and administration.  This could become one of the strongest thematic matches the franchise has ever had — if the mechanics fully support that scale. Current Rating: 2.5 / 5 Stars (Pending Updates) ​The Learning Curve & Economic Friction The Roman setting is beautifully realized, but the beauty is often overshadowed by mechanical frustration. While I enjoyed the initial discovery, the game frequently fails to explain its deeper economic layers. There were many moments where I felt I was guessing rather than strategizing. I suspect veterans of the Anno series might have an easier time, but as it stands, the onboarding for the economy feels opaque. ​The "Start Over" Syndrome Just as I finally felt I had a handle on my settlement's logistics, the game pushed me to a new map. Losing the progress of what I had spent hours building—only to be told to start from scratch—left me feeling unsatisfied with the state of my original colony. Instead of feeling like an expansion of my empire, it felt like my hard work was being discarded. ​The Season Pass Disappointment The most frustrating point, however, isn't the gameplay—it's the monetization. I purchased the Season Pass under the impression it would cover the first year of content. To discover that it does not grant access to all Year 1 material is incredibly disappointing. I understand the need to fund ongoing development, but I don't agree with an "open wallet" policy for a game I’ve already invested in. This has definitely broken my trust in pre-buying digital content. ​Final Verdict I’m currently left asking, "What do I need to do next just to start enjoying this?" I plan to play through the material I’ve already paid for and see if the mechanics click once I relearn them, but for now, I’m walking away extremely frustrated.

I don't really get the mixed ratings so far.  A lot of people seem to compare it to Anno 1800.  Yes, it is not as rich as Anno 1800 with 5,5 years of DLCs and updates. But what kind of comparison is that? Comparing both Anno 117 and Anno 1800 at release (or with the first DLC), 117 offers way more content.  Now, enough of comparisons.  Pro (+) - Gameplay continues the Anno-formula without re-inventing itself. Gameplay loop feels nice and engaging, numbers go up. This is really the core of any game. - There are some nice additions adding more depth to the game, like the research tree and local religion bonuses. In Albion you can decide in which way you want to upgrade the houses, with a celtic path & a roman path available, each with their own needs and wants. - Runs stable for me - Specialists have been nerfed compared to Anno 1800, which is good, because some of the specialists in late game Anno 1800 were almost like cheating  Cons (-) - I could critizice the balancing & difficulty of some characters. While no big deal as a veteran, Syracus will absolutely wipe the floor with inexperienced / newbie players (but we had something similar with Beryl in Anno 1800) - Very late game, the content somewhat runs out, not much to do except building bigger and bigger cities (but i would say that's expected, once you fulfill most needs there are).  - Sometimes you run into problems with goods that are bottlenecked by the number of mountain or river slots available (like gold, sturgeon, glass and some others), especially with big cities, where you need those in massive quantities. - Research, espcially late-game research takes forever, i would recommend dialing back the research time - I don't like the emperor mechanic, maybe it's just me, but taking over a large competitor would almost always lead either to bankruptcy, due to high cost of island takeover and/or a war against the emperor, due to reputational damage while waging war - which is quite difficult, being forced to pay millions to take over the enemy islands, then being forced to defend yourself directly after a costly war against the emperor fleet - The city status' negative effects are scaling too fast with large cities, to the point you cannot effectively balance them out with needs / city services  I can't rate the campaign, as i never play it in Anno titles. Some minor bugs and glitches have occured, but nothing game breaking, as many people suggest in the ratings - even though i run it on Linux, which is not even officially supported.  Overall i would recommend the game, gameplay feels satisfying, the graphics are nice, you can endlessly optimize your islands and there is tons to do (more things to come with the DLCs). Complaints and annoyances are there, but i would say those are minor in the context of the entire game.

Anno 117 is probably the most beautiful entry in the series, both in terms of aesthetics and how its different systems interact.  The diagonal building grid combined with incentives to mix production buildings with residences for area of effect bonuses, encourages more organic city layouts. Using natural defenses further adds to this, which leads to your city looking varied and natural without much effort. Earlier titles didnt really manage to do that and you had to plan this in, otherwise you will have an island which has the same street layout as Manhattan.  Ground combat has returned and is quite enjoyable, though it isn’t really hard if you siege your targets before landing.  The game does have some issues, like some bugs that block some quests or the save getting corrupted when trying to enable the DLC, and the story ends abruptly.  Still, the overall experience is very positive.  I don’t fully understand the backlash. Some players expected more content and were angry at the use of AI-generated art, which has since been patched. However, expecting the game to match the amount of content in Anno 1800, as of now, is unrealistic.

Ignore the current Mixed score, 117 is a good base game of Anno currently, with the first DLC adding more to it. A lot of the reviews compare it to 1800 after it had years and years of content and polish added to it. To compare, 1800's base game had 2 realms, like 117 does, but it didn't have the god system which makes islands more specialized for product routes. Nor did it have a split of what type of populations you can get like this does in the second region. 1800 had an irrigation building system added in its africa DLC but it's an inverse version for 117 where instead of supplying water to make farmland, you can drain swamps for more solid land, but miss out on using the land for other production lines.  It's newest DLC adds a massive island like 1800 did, with the addition of having a repeat middle game or late game event to manage through with the reward for new resources and chains after.  I've had no major issues with programing and the only valid complaint i've seen is a desynching constantly multiplayer, but I mostly play solo.  117 Has more content than 1800's base game if we're going to compare, but will need time and more DLC's to match the level of content 1800 has now. It is still very much worth it.

This game is so beautiful with lots of attention to detail. This is my first Anno game and I am instantly hooked. As a longtime modern city builder fan (like Cities Skylines), this game has the automatic beauty and detail that is lacking in those titles. There are actually little people doing their work with amazing animations. The environment is filled with animals and life. The lighting is gorgeous and weather effects are detailed all throughout. I also really enjoy the land exploration mechanic (sailing to find new islands) and the fact that the base game has two very different regions with their own unique products, people, and environment. The combat is pretty simplistic, but it adds a fun additional element to the game instead of just city building.    So far, my only criticisms are...  - A seeming lack of overlays for the map to show things like coverages of all the service buildings, citizen needs, etc.  - Free placement of decorations and foliage.  - I know Anno has always been grid based even though I never played the other games. It would be nice if a completely grid-free design was featured in the future.    I really knew this game was good when my advisor NPC chimes in, "Governor, it's been 4 hours. Don't you think you should get some food or rest a while?"

[b] English version (for german version see below - Die deutsche Version siehe weiter unten): [/b]      Anno 117 Pax Romana welcomes you with its very beautiful start menu and great camera movements. While loading the session or campaign, wonderful concept art and paintings appear, setting the mood for the world and enchanting me.    Anno 117 Pax Romana is characterized by beautiful graphics. Water looks realistic, buildings are very detailed and lovingly designed and the landscapes and islands look breathtaking. I keep losing myself while exploring the beautiful islands and cities of the other factions. The bustling factor of the inhabitants adds to it. Much of it is simply beautifully animated, with inhabitants, buildings and nature. There is life on the streets and around and in the buildings. Many details and wonderful little things always bring a smile to my lips. And the residents' comments are always fitting and delightful.    The two provinces of Latium and Albion, which we are allowed to settle in the game, are a beautiful contrast. On one side, a warm, sunny, welcoming region; on the other, rugged landscapes and swamps accompanied by harsh weather. The swamp also offers new resources and you can build a few things on it. A welcome change.    I personal love the roman setting of Pax Romana and the artistic way it is implemented, both provinces and the era in general. Right up my alley!    With the UI you feel right at home. It works fine, functional and is easy to use. In addition, it simply looks beautiful with loving details. And the gradually slowing mechanism sound when you pause the game... I love it! In general, the menu sounds are very pleasant.    The sounds in the game generally create a wonderful atmosphere. There are "clinks" and "clangs" amidst the bustling activity, rain and thunderstorms lash over Albion, the residents comment on their situation and the very beautiful music underscores both regions picturesquely. This makes building relaxing. The voice actors are also pleasant to listen to and provide the right atmosphere. Great work!    The campaign, I played Marcia's part (you can choose between two) and liked it, is appealing and teaches you everything you need to know in the Anno-Universe and how it works.    After the campaign, the real fun begins. The typical Anno tasks of settling islands, building, establishing supply chains, optimizing, opening trade routes, of course also between the two provinces of Albion and Latium, fulfilling quests, and satisfying the ever-increasing needs of the residents await. And the DLCs on the horizon (beginning with "Prophecies of Ash") give a hint that new content is on the way and the universe is expanding.    Each of your settled islands can be "equipped" with a tutelary deity that is worshiped. Depending on the tutelary deity, there are additional bonuses on the respective island.    Anno 117 also offers an immense research branch that provides a lot of optimization opportunities throughout the game. I really like that!    Some buildings come with positive or negative (or even both) area effects that influence the surroundings, such as higher income, increased religion, or heightened fire risk. That makes the placement all the more exciting.    Everything in the game is well explained with detailed tooltips. So even on the first visit with the Anno series, one should be able to get into it well.    All in all, Anno 117 Pax Romana thrives with a fresh setting, gorgeous landscapes, great graphics and polished Anno principles. Building and optimizing is relaxed, easy to do and fun. The whole thing is paired with cozy, relaxed and chill vibes.    A masterpiece!        [b] Deutsche Version (german version): [/b]      Anno 117 Pax Romana begrüßt einen mit seinem sehr schönen Startmenü und tolle Kamerafahrten. Beim Laden der Session oder Kampagne tauchen wundervolle Konzeptzeichnungen und Gemälde auf, die auf die Welt einstimmen und mich verzaubern.    Anno 117 Pax Romana zeichnet sich durch eine wunderschöne Grafik aus. Wasser wirkt realistisch, Gebäude sind sehr detailliert und liebevoll gestaltet und die Landschaften und Inseln sehen atemberaubend aus. Ich verliere mich immer wieder beim Erkunden der wunderschönen Inseln und auch Städte der anderen Fraktionen. Der Wuselfaktor der Einwohner tut sein Übriges. Vieles ist einfach nur wunderschön animiert, bei Einwohnern, Gebäude und Natur. Es ist Leben auf den Straßen und an und um die Gebäude. Viele Details und wundervolle Kleinigkeiten zaubern mir immer ein Lächeln auf den Lippen. Und auch die Kommentare der Bewohner sind immer wieder passend und herrlich.    Die beiden Provinzen Latium und Albion, die wir im Spiel besiedeln dürfen, sind ein schöner Kontrast. Auf der einen Seite eine warme, sonnige, willkommen heißende Region auf der anderen Seite raue Landschaften und Sumpf in Begleitung von rauem Wetter. Der Sumpf bietet gleichzeitig auch neue Ressourcen und das ein oder andere kann man auch auf ihm bauen. Eine gelungene Abwechslung.    Persönlich liebe ich das römische Setting von Pax Romana und die künstlerische Art, wie es umgesetzt wurde, beide Provinzen und die Era im Allgemeinen. Genau mein Ding!    Bei dem UI fühlt man sich gleich zu Hause. Es geht leicht von der Hand, ist funktional und gut zu bedienen. Zudem schaut es einfach nur schön aus mit liebevollen Details. Und der langsam werdendere Mechanismussound, wenn man das Spiel pausiert... ich liebe es! Generell sind die Menüsounds sehr angenehm.    Die Sounds im Spiel sorgen durchweg für wundervolle Atmosphäre. Überall "klingt" und "klongt" es beim regen Treiben, Regen und Gewitter peitscht über Albion, die Bewohner kommentieren ihre Lage und die sehr schöne Musik unterstreicht beide Regionen malerisch. Das macht das Bauen entspannt. Auch die Synchronsprecher sind angenehm zu hören und sorgen für den richtigen Flair. Großartige Arbeit!    Die Kampagne, ich habe Marcia's part (man kann zwischen zwei wählen) gespielt und mag es, ist sehr ansprechend und bringt einem alles über das Anno-Universum bei und wie es funktioniert.     Nach der Kampagne geht es erst richtig los. Das typische Anno Inseln Besiedeln, Bauen, Warenketten Anlegen, Optimieren, Handeslrouten Eröffnen, natürlich auch zwischen den beiden Provinzen Albion und Latium, Quests Erfüllen und die immer höher werdenden Bedürfnisse der Bewohner gilt es zu befriedigen. Und auch die DLC's am Horizont (allen voran "Verheißung des Vulkans") geben einen Geschmack darauf, dass noch neues auf einen zukommt und das Universum erweitert wird.    Jede eurer besiedelten Inseln kann mit einer Schutzgottheit "ausgestattet" werden, die verehrt wird. Der Schutzgottheit entsprechend gibt es auf der jeweiligen Insel zusätzliche Boni.    Auch bietet Anno 117 einen immensen Forschungszweig, der im Laufe des Spiels für eine Menge Optimierungsmöglichkeiten sorgt. Das gefällt mir sehr gut!    Einige Gebäude kommen mit positiven oder auch negativen (oder beides) Bereichseffekten daher, die Einflüsse auf die Umgebung haben, wie zum Beispiel höhere Einnahmen, mehr Glauben oder erhöhtes Brandrisiko. Das macht das Platzieren umso spannender.    Alles im Spiel ist sehr gut erklärt mit detaillierten Tooltipps. Also auch beim ersten Besuch mit der Anno-Reihe sollte man sich hier gut reinfinden.    Alles in allem blüht Anno 117 Pax Romana mit einem frischen Setting, wunderhübsche Landschaften, toller Grafik und aufpoliertem Annoprinzip auf. Das Bauen und Optimieren ist entspannt, geht leicht von der Hand und macht Spass. Das ganze ist gepaart mit cozy, relax und chill vibes.    Ein Meisterwerk!

Not Recommended  I went into Anno 117 expecting the deep, sprawling campaign the series is known for, and walked out feeling like I'd played a prologue someone forgot to finish.  The campaign is shockingly short. I'm a long-time Anno player, and I blew through the story in what felt like a fraction of the time 1800 demanded. Worse, it doesn't end so much as stop. The narrative is clearly building toward something - tensions rising, factions positioning - and then... credits. It reads less like a conclusion and more like a setup for DLC that hasn't been announced. If you're buying this for the story, wait until the season pass is out and on sale.  The writing has zero nuance. Anno never pretended to be Disco Elysium, but previous entries at least let you feel the weight of competing interests - colonial guilt, industrial pollution, labor unrest. Here? Pure Saturday morning cartoon. The Romans good, the antagonists bad, your advisors unanimously cheerful, and not a single decision in the campaign asked me to weigh anything against anything else. No moral choices, no shades of grey, no consequences for how you treat conquered peoples. For a setting literally built on conquest and slavery, that's a bizarre design choice.  The research tree is bloated for no reason. I'm still not sure what most of it was for. Half the unlocks felt like padding to extend playtime artificially, and the genuinely useful nodes are buried behind filler. By the time you've researched something interesting, the campaign is over and you don't really need it anymore. In a sandbox game it might justify itself; in a 15-hour campaign it just feels like busywork.  The city-building loop is still satisfying, the Roman aesthetic is gorgeous, and the production chains are classic Anno. But none of that compensates for a hollow campaign and a story that ends mid-sentence.  Wait for a sale. Or wait for the rest of the game.

I haven't reviewed a lot of games on Steam, but I have been playing real time strategy PC games since the late 90s (would go over tech trees at recess for Age of Empires lol). I see a lot of negative reviews for the game, so that's why I felt like I'd offer a different perspective. Now a few important caveats; this is my only experience playing an Anno game, I LOVE ancient Rome and read 4 to 6 books on Rome each year, and I judge a game based on hours played and replay ability. Its seems odd to spend over 100 hours on something this recently released and say nah...it sucks. Like...you spent over 100 hours...something kept you there.     With all that, I enjoy the game for what it is. A chance to build ancient Roman cities and deal with varying levels of crisis (all adjustable in the settings). The graphics are beautiful. The buildings look great. I love that you can walk through your city in 1st person view. It's fun to just watch the streets of your version of Rome.     I haven't found any of the stability issues noted by other users. After the first patch most of the gameplay glitches were fixed. I have a decent PC (see specs...) so that may help. But, usually I'll stop playing or end my game because of something I end up doing and don't like (forgot to settle an island before an opponent did, don't like the Albion island layout, forget some part of the supply chain and watch my city fall to the lack of wool hats). These are parts of any game like this.     The maps are varied...in a sense. There are a limited number of island designs that are then set up in different configurations around the map. You can see different island seeds and play on ones you like.     For complaints, of course I have them. I don't like how much of the island is uninhabitable (mountains, valleys, etc...). I wish I could like...mine them or flatten them or something so I could use the space and make more interesting layouts. In most levels I'd love to build a tower or fort on the edges of the island...but can't. The campaign....was not complete. That critique is quite true. Though....I am not sure this is a game based on the campaign mode. I typically just play endless mode. And finally, the multiplayer speed being stuck at 1x....is brutal. It makes playing with a friend glacially slow....especially early game when not a lot is going on. Late game 1x is fine as I have so many islands and goals....but early game its brutal.     I have a friend that played and....played too much. Like many other games its a game of..."just one more thing before I quit". You'll add a resource, then have to supply it, then you unlock something else, and just haev to do that. Then a trade route opens and now you can sell stuff. It all builds and keeps you coming back for that little bit more.     Anno 1800 and other titles are well polished by now. They have been out for years. Its expected. Could this have been more polished on release? Obviously. Is this the only game that has that issue? Not remotely so. Most studios are struggling to get quality out (likely as they battle internally about how much AI they use...management wants more, devs want less). Meanwhile, we just want games that are worth the price we pay. Inflation sucks...discretionary spend on games is limited....so expectations have gone up.     So yeah, long review, but there ya have it. If you want to build a Roman city, respond to crises, walk around your city, build complex supply chains, and have control over your difficulty...its worth it. If you want a fully polished game like Anno 1800.....maybe just wait until its out for more than a year? As a fan of Rome, I like it. So many other Roman games don't have the beautiful architecture in such detail.

Clearly there was supposed to be a third act. The game was not finished and rushed. The UI, the scenery, the landscape, and the buildings are beautiful but clearly the actual gameplay is very limited. There are two realms, Latium which has the climate of Italy and Albion which has the climate of England. I won't spoil the story but essentially after the second act you come back to Latium. Guess what? That's it! You meet two new people (0 context who they are and 0 dialogue choices), and you now have free reign to build everywhere. The empress? gone. The daughter? gone. I don't get the opportunity to use siege machines, I don't get to unlock the Quinquireme for a full naval assault against the evil guy with the eyepatch, nothing! Overall the game is pretty much what you would expect from this franchise. One of the most noticeable differences is this version does include more land based military units and buildings then other versions I've played in the past. I have not played all the anno games, but I have played several over the years. Graphics are pretty decent and the game play overall is fine. I do like the Rome theme and generally the game play and progression is fun. Realistically, for the cost its hard to recommend when the same game is essentially available in previous versions for cheaper. But if that was all, I would still mark the game as recommended with the cavate that its basically a Hollywood sequel - the same just moar and shiny, but not a bad game per say. Just not a deal or innovative.  The real reason I would not recommend this game right now is its buggy as hell. Can't think of a recent game I've played that almost 6 months after release is STILL this buggy. The game is generally unstable and will crash out every few hours. So from a stability standpoint I would not even call this a full release version, feels like an early RC release. The auto saving does a decent job of making this crashing not too impactful but its really frustrating to constantly need to re-do stuff. It does sometimes get into bad states with cloud save sync state from crashing though, so get ready to gamble on which version is most recent (local vs cloud). Funny enough, the game also sometimes gets into a weird state when exiting a game safely where you cannot exit the game from the title screen. From inside a game, you quit to title, then from title you quit the game. Clicking exit from the title screen prompts 'Do you want to close the game' type confirm box. Clicking yes does nothing, game remains open. Alt+F4 - opens that confirm prompt and still does nothing. Open task manager and select end task - somehow ALSO opens the same confirm prompt and still does nothing. What.The.Actual.F*ck. I've had to reboot my machine multiple times just to force the game to close. So when your actually done playing, you can't exit, but when you want to play, it randomly just closes itself. Great job. For even more fun, theres also some issues with the save game management, so get ready to lose hrs of gameplay on a consistent basis. I just lost a day or 2 of game play, yet again. All my manual saves - gone. all my cloud saves - gone. The local autosaves that remain are distroed to about 1 per day starting from today and going back in time. The freshest is timestamped today, but i havent played today yet. After reviewing them, they seem to be old auto saves from days ago. Somehow autosaves that were generated sequentially from the same day of gameplay (that is already days stale), are now timestamped to one file a day starting on a day I havent even played on. They are all marked as local save files in the game as well. Again, great job.  Those are the big stability bugs I've run into that make the game almost punishing to play. Outside of the big issues, the game is riddled with tons of much more minor bugs that while annoying, you can really play around. For example when selecting a unit, about 1/2 the time the info pane for the unit shown is not the unit you selected. That can lead to sending the wrong unit into battle pretty easily. Or its the right unit, but the wrong view (army group view vs specific unit view). Selecting and unselecting a unit multiple times back to back will have inconsistent UI panels show. Ship renders at a distance are often wrong, so this ends up looking like ships teleporting around the map, usually not huge distances but still annoying / weird.

TLDR this is a very well done Anno with a perfect scenario, well designed new mechanics and a bad campaign about which nobody really cared anyway  I have played every single Anno (even the Wii one, except Anno Online) and found good things in all of them. This one executes a long waited for ancient scenario with suprising skill, seeing the general state of Ubisoft. Rome sets a great canvas for a trade based, stratified society with colonies. It looks beautifull in every way and is a joy to look at in detail, especially the production buildings. Albion works great, for me better than the new world in 1800. The trip over there is still very long, ut the amount of goods needed is a lot smaller this time, so it's less annoying and you have to adjust it far less often. The new goods and citizens are just ... good. Now I get some of the complaints. The AI art was embarassing, but does it affect the enjoyment of the game? The humor is bad, but you got to see the German roots somewhere! The campaign is truly a horrible joke and obvious victim of cut content and that actually stings a bit. But honestly, the campaign is a better tutorial anyway. You finish it and start your actual run after. The 1404 campaign was decent, but every other Anno could have done without (except the one in 2205, that one I liked, but I like space). What suprised me is how well thought out all the new stuff is. All the new mechanics are actually well implemented and complement each other! The workforce can't be transferred, so you need more towns and villages, but that gives you more chances to build up the new pantheon and try different gods. The citizens don't need all the goods, which make simpler workforce villages even easier. A diverse set of available ressources for your sprawling colonies? No problem, but a feature! The palace delimits experts? Build specialized towns! Even war is a bit more fun with the new diplomatic war options and unit upkeeps. The research lets you steer your empire just that extra bit. The positive and negative influences of production buildings add flavour, style and challenge, but only if you want to. All in all, this is simply a well crafted Anno through and through, well prepared for a slew of DLCs that can expand nicely upon old and new mechanics. So far, it feels more rounded than 1800 at the start to me, less overly complex. I will come back to it for the next couple expansions.

Not Recommended: A slight thumbs down that straddles the line of mediocrity with neither shining moments nor glaring issues.  Pros: The game is great visually and audibly. On par with modern high end graphics. The cities are detailed, the landmarks are grand and opulent. The islands are much more comfortable to build on compared to the frustratingly thin islands we got in anno 1880. The game runs smooth and have relatively few bugs. They added land combat on top of the existing naval combat we had in 1880. You get the option to conquer as the romans or respect local indigenous culture, or an option to have both cultures coexist together which is refreshing.   Cons: Anno 118 has a strange paradox. It plays slow yet is not relaxing. Earlier hours are enjoyable until you get to late game management, where it feels you are working a second job. Ship routes are in constant error modes, leaving you to spend most of your time observing the ships, wondering why things are not picked up, why the ships behave certain ways, etc. You often load up a game and just be overwhelmed with the 100 things you need to check before getting into the game again since you last logged off. It leaves little time to enjoy the beautiful cities and build and feels more like an overworked supply chain manager. It can feel emotionally discouraging to even start the game up, instead of thinking "ooh what can I build today!"  Overall it is very similar to its predecessor. For the few who are into this niche, it's more of the same. For lack of better words, anno 117 is just alright and a little boring. Once you get past the beautiful trailer and exciting music, you quickly fall into a sense of irritation rather than enjoyment.

I am just getting started with this game, but have decided to go ahead and post my review which will be updated as I continue to play. It is a disgrace that this game has Mixed review scores and they do not in any way reflect the actual quality of this gameplay experience. Most of us know that the Mixed score is due to the pure Ubisoft haters that will casually slap a negative score on any game with the Ubisoft name. These are ignorant people who don't understand or value a quality city building experience especially when it is such a rare thing these days.   The graphics, music, sound, and especially the gameplay are all top notch experiences especially when played on modern hardware. This game has a ton of quality of life features for a city builder that many other top games in the genre can only aspire to. The planning feature is one of the biggest. It is so nice to be able to easily plan out my city without committing my building resources upfront. Once my planned build looks good and I have everything set up in an optimal manner, I can then go ahead and have the buildings and other objects/ roads/decorations built. You can also easily and without added cost move any building or object that you have already placed in the game world to your preferred location. The game also does a very good job at providing visuals within the UI and in game reports/summaries to help me easily manage my resources and production lines so I am always meeting the demands of my villagers with just the right supply of goods and resources. When I want to trade with other islands, I can also easily make sure I'm producing just enough to cover demand from my islands and also produce just enough extra to supply the trade demands of the other islands that I engage with in trade agreements.

I have been an avid Anno player for years, always dreamt of a 'Roman' Anno and looked forward to Anno 117 for ages. What should have been a bold evolution instead feels like a stripped-down, overly safe rehash version of Anno—but with less content, weaker systems, and a design philosophy that feels driven more by monetization than creativity.  It looks polished. The Roman setting is attractive, cities are detailed, and the presentation is clean. Some new options are fantastic, like the option of customizing ships and the addition of Religion, which I see can be incorperated in other Anno's. But underneath that surface, the game feels hollow—and increasingly like it was built to maximize profit rather than deliver a meaningful new experience.  1. No Real Innovation Production chains, expansion, and progression feel recycled with almost no meaningful evolution for your whole population. After 2 main island per region, you're pretty much done.  The map variation should be endless but is pretty much the same; AI opponents, Pirates and other AI are always in the same spots.   2. Shockingly Weak AI AI opponents are barely functional. They don’t compete, don’t adapt, and pose no real threat. Their trading behavior is especially immersion-breaking—they sell the same items over and over, even across different regions, making the economy feel static and artificial. Even with Dorian and Athyr (that supposedly only stay in their region but I've had expanding to different regions, still sell their own, region bound items!)  The same goes for ships. You can't build pirate ships and Albion doesn't a new type of ship you can build either.  3. Content Regression & Monetization Feel Instead of expanding on previous entries, Anno 117 feels like it’s holding things back. Features that were standard in earlier games now feel reduced, missing, or positioned as extras. The only exception is the addition of religion which really adds another dimension to the game but overall, it comes across less like a complete experience and more like a foundation designed to be built up (and paid for) later.  4. Fewer Opponents, Less Variety Only 7 AI opponents (Only 1 Hard) are available, compared to the 10–11 offered in previous titles. This cuts down replayability and makes the world feel smaller and less dynamic. The addition of the Emperors are great but still feels 'safe'. You almost have to get out your way to mess up the relationship you have. Missions from AI opponents frequently don't work or are way too easy (provide them with items they sell themselves)  5. Shallow Side Systems Diplomacy and combat are minimal to the point of irrelevance. They add little strategic depth and feel like box-ticking features rather than meaningful systems. Why are ships customisable  but not your army?  6. Dead-End Endgame Once your economy stabilizes, the game effectively stops evolving. There are no meaningful late-game challenges or systems to keep players invested. Even 'big' achievement (defeating opponents/emperor) don't feel like a big win.  Hall of Fame achievements are buggy, stay locked when completed or unlock at random stages.   7. Wasted Roman Setting Ancient Rome should bring political intrigue, military expansion, innovation, drama, myths, legends and regional diversity which were all the reasons I looked forward to it so much. Instead, it’s mostly cosmetic, with little impact on gameplay. I think this is what's the biggest disappointment and frustration; you can see what the game COULD (nee, SHOULD) be.   8. Poor Communication & Uncertain Post-Launch Support Communication has been frustratingly vague. The release date of the game itself was vague to start with. The worldmap with all launch dates and times wasn't accurate. As of writing on April 6 2026, the first add-on, Ash of the Volcano, is supposedly releasing in April—but still has no confirmed release date. Yet, the've thrown in a 'cosmetics pack' (that could only be bought seperate, again highlighting this game is only for Cosmetics and profit) That lack of clarity doesn’t inspire confidence, especially when the base game already feels light on content.  9. Feels Built for Shareholders, Not Players More than anything, Anno 117 gives off the impression of a rushed product designed to hit safe targets and maximize long-term revenue rather than push the series forward. Between cut-back features, reduced variety, and a structure that feels primed for add-ons, it comes across as calculated rather than passionate.  I'd only recommend buying after all the add ons have been released, on discount.

Took about 35 hours to play this game to death, which is why the first thing I'll say is - maybe wait until there's more content. I've also had a fair share of crashes, which is unusual to me with the games I play, but the consistent and frequent autosaving has made that largely a non-issue. They're not frequent enough to be more than an annoyance. The game is also immensely taxing in terms of hardware so do note that; on my setup my final tier city is still occasionally dragging frames to 29fps on high settings. It's rough.   But all in all, there isn't a lot to do at this time. I also find that the Albion islands tend to be too small to fully expand the gameplay beyond challenge; city-building is more of a frustration there than a joy, with the lowest tier of population always too low unless production is scattered widely across islands.  But in terms of being a foundation for what I assume in proven and true Anno fashion is intended to be an abundance of expansions and packs to drown in? It is very good, and I'm excited to see more of it. The world is built fantastically, the buildings are appealing, the tiers of advancement are pleasing, and production chains are fun to manage. The system with the Emperor could use more incentive imho; by the time I got to the top tier of reputation, I didn't need a single one of the benefits I got from it. However, the concept of it is fun and I'm happy about that. Building the amphitheatre and hosting games is great fun also, and the Latium islands are massive, which at least provides you plenty of space to build up your main hub. Game does not feel repetitive and it actually came as a surprise to me when I realised I'd ran out of things to do and I tried to figure out if that was because I actually had or because I'd just grown too complacent just sitting by micromanaging my trade routes, but... no, there really wasn't much more to head for.  Overall, I really liked this game. I don't think it has much replay value as it is right now, and I wish the Year 1 content would have come a bit faster, because I spent forever being too busy to actually start the game after preordering it and somehow half a year later the base game was still the only thing that was there for it. But at least I've got my basics down now so it won't feel overwhelming when more is added.  The game is very decent, enjoyable, not quite worth its price tag as it is right now. But we all know the Anno franchise goes on frequent sales so once it has 1000 DLC and whatnot to include? Certainly pick it up. Right now? It's not NOT worth it, either, just bare bones, is all.

Honestly, Anno 117: Pax Romana is an amazing experience.  I’ve played a lot of city-building games, and most of them come with a lot of stress and pressure that can feel overwhelming after a while. This one feels different — it’s smooth, relaxing, and actually fun to play instead of exhausting.  One of the best things about it is the freedom. You’re not forced into one specific path to grow your city. There are multiple ways to reach a big, successful city, and all of them feel valid. That makes the gameplay a lot more enjoyable and keeps things fresh.  The graphics are also really beautiful and add a lot to the overall experience.  If I had to mention one small downside, it’s that I personally prefer when city builders show the construction process step by step, which isn’t really a big focus here. But overall, the concept and gameplay are really well done.  If you like city builders, this is definitely worth trying.

It lacks the distinctive charm and atmosphere that made the earlier games so engaging. The visual style, in particular, feels conflicted—leaning toward realism without fully committing, while also abandoning the stylized, cartoonish aesthetic that once gave the series its personality. As a result, it ends up in an awkward middle ground that feels uninspired rather than innovative.    On top of that, the game demands a steep learning curve that can feel more frustrating than rewarding. Instead of gradually drawing players in, it overwhelms them with complexity early on. By the time you’ve invested the effort to understand its systems, the payoff simply isn’t satisfying enough to justify the time and energy required. Overall, it struggles to recapture what made its predecessors memorable, leaving the experience feeling more like a chore than an enjoyable challenge.

Playtime: 32.0 hrs  Why I don't like it anymore almost hate it!  I’m usually pretty patient with games, but this one genuinely pissed me off. I gave it loads of chances (obviously, I have 32 hours in it), but I’m finally throwing in the towel.    You never seem to catch a break in this game. Even when the game tells you to take a break, you’re always dragging your feet just trying to stop everything from falling apart. The balance is so insanely tight that it just sucks the fun out of it. I have restarted this game well over 8 times, and every single time I get to a certain point, the same thing happens: everyone is either furious at me, a fire breaks out and burns down something critical, or the whole colony is about to die from sickness. There’s no wiggle room.    And don’t even get me started on the Tutorial. It sucks. It barely shows you what to click on. Sorry, but for a game this complicated where one wrong move ruins your run, I need all the answers upfront. If the tutorial isn’t going to hold my hand properly, LOL, I am never going to get anywhere on my own.    The research unlocks are highly unintuitive. I kept getting told to pick the "Celtic" way or the "Roman" way to unlock useful upgrades, but where the F*** is that menu? I am well aware that I need to read everything, but even when you read all the tooltips, you still can’t figure out where to go or what the game actually wants from you.    This game honestly reminds me too much of Steam Punk, I eventually defeated that game out of spite, but I never wanted to bother with that style again. Look, I know strategy games. I bought and played Age of Empires (all of them), Company of Heroes (all of them), and if you want me to go way back, I played Command & Conquer (all of them) back when Westwood Studios was still around. I’m not a newbie. But this? It’s just frustrating, not fun.    I wish I could get back all my time and money on this one. It’s sad it took me 32 hours to finally admit the game sucks.

Really tried to enjoy this Anno title but some of the core mechanics are beyond tedious and spoil the game. The health, happiness, and fire safety system is restrictive and forces a repetive and limiting construction format. The negative events associated with this system are catastrophic and can simply end the game for you if they occur on your main financial island and you are not prepared for them.    Ships and armies demand such a significant amount of workforce (until you hit tier 4) so when it comes to handling the higher tier cheating npc factions you will almost always lag behind in development and will be outnumbered. Be prepared to be constantly interrupted by "Ship under attack" - worse than Anno 1800.    The emporer system is not interesting and just slows down expansion - charging exorbiant funds for islands just hinders the player while NPCs have free reign to rapidly expand.    To summarize, in my opinion the game is half-baked with a sad excuse for a campaign it has promise to be great at some point but at the moment I'd skip this for now. Check back in a year and get it on sale.

Frustrating Experience – Not Worth It    My wife and I love Anno 1800, but this was one of the most frustrating games we’ve played. About 10 hours in I had 130k saved, two strong islands, and +6k income, yet I was constantly forced to spam emergency services. With no defensive turrets, enemy ships can camp outside your island and destroy everything you produce, leaving you stuck.    Then a riot hit and everything spiraled. My money vanished, income dropped to -6k, my city burned, production stopped, and pirates destroyed every ship I made. Even after stabilizing, the island never recovered—it felt like a death spiral with no way out.    A single random event shouldn’t ruin 10 hours of progress or kill a co-op run. Skip this and play Anno 1800 with DLC instead—this version removes key mechanics, adds little, and relies on frustrating systems.

Super fun game, great entry into the Anno series.  The story section as it is, is a bit short. It definitely should have been fleshed out more before launch. But the underlying systems are great, and just like Anno 1800 there will be tonnes of more content added over time.   For example, currently there are only 2 regions in the game, Latium and Albion. There's a button where you can switch regions, but for some reason there is an additional map with lots of other space for locations on it. They'll do the same thing they did in Anno 1800 and add more regions.  The swamp system is super cool. Moreover, you get to actually pick a civilization, both of which have completely different playstyles. You basically end up building the Netherlands in an Anno game, slowly terraforming your islands.  The graphics are beautiful. The effects are polished. The buildings look... genuinely breathtaking. The ground combat is lots of fun and feels very realistic, especially with the collision physics. The sound is wonderful.  All in all though, you can tell Ubisoft is viewing this as a live service game and didn't do a great job fleshing out the base game. If you want to give it a try now, you should, don't let the negative reviews get to you. You'll end up playing basically sandbox mode for a while at the end, but I'm not one of these people that needs my hand held the whole time. The story mode is basically like a long tutorial, with the actual game being what comes after.  Great, (but short) game, at least at time of reviewing. 8/10

I’ve been thoroughly enjoying the historical atmosphere of Anno 117: Pax Romana. The regional contrast in Albion is a highlight; being able to choose between Celtic or Roman paths—each with unique requirements and demands—adds a great layer of flavor to the city-building.    As a player who values 'build and balance' over 'battle and conquest,' I spend most of my time in Sandbox mode with conflict turned off. I build armies more for the aesthetic joy of seeing them in my city than for actual expansion. While I’ve seen some critiques regarding a lack of complexity—and I agree that you can 'max out' your islands and complete the Colosseum relatively quickly—the core gameplay loop remains incredibly satisfying. Given that more content is on the way, the game is a lot of fun as-is and will likely only improve from here.

[h1]A LOT of Bugs. [/h1]    [h2]Cons:[/h2]   [b]Seems very unfinished.[/b]     -Distance rendering is shot, meaning textures dont load in in time.    -Ship-Ghosts are a thing - Ships being seen, where there once were ships, which can be very annoying in particular with enemy ships.     -Sound is shot, meaning music and sfx stutter.    -Many small things also: Renaming the template in the shipyard? Must change another thing first, else it does not register.     -Gameplay issues: The new features mostly have great potential. Army and Fleet are probably best out of all Anno Games. However: The Happiness/Firesafety/Health Statistics are not helpful and seem random. Going from +1k to -16k in a matter of seconds is wild. Chosing a patronage god is neat (also seeing their painted statues as is historically accurate) but their buffs can break you if you swap gods (as the game recommends, without warning of the risk).     [h2]Pros[/h2]  It is definitley playable and if the textures are finally loaded in it looks very pretty.  Many things are true to what i like about Anno things, the new additions fit in nicely in general.     [h2]Summary[/h2]    Neat game, if it were finished. As is i can not recommend, since the bugs are annoying, particular the visual and audio ones.     It feels rushed. Unlike the industrial focus of [i]Anno 1800[/i] or the futuristic settings of [i]Anno 2205[/i] and [i]Anno 2070[/i], [b]Anno 117: Pax Romana[/b] returns the series to antiquity. You govern [i]Roman[/i] territories such as [i]Latium[/i] or frontier provinces like [i]Albion[/i], balancing [i]Roman[/i] culture with local traditions while expanding settlements, building infrastructure, and maintaining imperial authority.    This historical setting gives the game a fresh tone for the series while still retaining the signature [i]Anno[/i] gameplay loop.    Careful logistics and optimized layouts remain essential. Roads, warehouses, and trade routes all influence productivity.    A major addition is governorship mechanics. Instead of simply expanding islands like previous games, you manage entire provinces and make political decisions that affect loyalty, culture, and taxes.    Diplomacy plays a larger role than in some earlier entries. Rival governors, tribal factions, and imperial politics influence your expansion. Military forces exist mainly to secure trade routes and maintain order rather than constant warfare.    The [i]Mediterranean[/i] setting creates a warm aesthetic with coastal harbors, farmland, and [i]Roman[/i] architecture that looks fantastic when cities reach higher population tiers.    The soundtrack leans heavily into [i]Roman[/i]-inspired orchestral themes with [i]Mediterranean[/i] instruments. Ambient sound design—markets buzzing, blacksmiths hammering, harbor ships unloading—helps make cities feel alive.    [b]Anno 117: Pax Romana[/b] successfully blends the classic [i]Anno[/i] formula with a compelling historical setting. By focusing on provincial governance and [i]Roman[/i] cultural influence, it introduces new strategic layers without abandoning the intricate logistics and city-building depth the series is known for.    For fans of deep economic strategy games, it’s shaping up to be one of the most promising entries in the franchise. After roughly 100 hours in ANNO 117, here’s my take:    It’s a good game. Rock solid, and it’s still fun on the second or even third playthrough.    But there’s a problem.    At the end of the day, it’s still a Ubisoft game. And right now that shows quite brutally. Compared to ANNO 1800, 117 somehow feels a bit rough and unfinished. Here a graphics bug, there a missing piece of information (for example, what you actually need to build siege machines can only be found in forums, not anywhere in the game).    And with Ubisoft currently more or less falling apart, ANNO feels like it’s being left behind a bit. It honestly doesn’t deserve that, and it does make me a little sad.    I wish the whole brand could just move to another company overnight. But for now, all that’s left is hoping that ANNO brings in enough money for Ubisoft to keep enough competent people working on it, so that it improves over time.

I went into this expecting a relaxing city builder and somehow ended up losing entire evenings without noticing. Anno 117: Pax Romana has that dangerous “just one more thing” loop where you fix a supply chain, expand a district, adjust production, and suddenly two hours are gone.    What I’m enjoying the most is how smooth the progression feels. Expanding your settlement, balancing resources, and watching your Roman city grow is incredibly satisfying. There’s always something meaningful to improve, but it never feels overwhelming. The atmosphere and setting also do a great job of pulling you in—you really feel like you're building a living Roman province.    If you enjoy city builders or management games, this one is very easy to get hooked on. Time genuinely flies by while playing.    Definitely a recommendation from me.

Unbiased Review from a long time Anno player. If you've never played Anno 1800 this won't do you much good.  Rundown:  Graphics - 10/10 QoL - 10/10 UI - 10/10 Mega-city feasability and fun - 6/10 Mechanics - 8/10 (Fertilities call for a ridiculous amount of trade routes) Replay-ability - 6/10  My playstyle: I like sandbox games that span weeks. I like building out cities to the maximum. I like being able to work towards a massive goal to build something that seemed absolutely out of reach at the start of the game. I enjoy CLEAN trade routes that make the game more enjoyable.  The graphics are incredible. I play on a 5090 w/ 9800X3D and I am blown away by the fidelity every time I play. It's definitely a step up from 1800, although when you're zoomed out (like I usually am), this plays a very small role. Game seems well optimized, although I did have the occasional crash (like hours and sometimes days between though).  The gameplay. I really disliked the two tier system. It's a personal shortfall of mine. I like Anno because you can build your empire on one or two or three large islands. This new two tier system somewhat forces you to create tons of trade routes and it really makes it difficult the first few times I played. Over time, it got better but the thing that absolutely drove me crazy are the fertilities. In Anno 1800 I felt like you could find most of the resources you need in 2-3 islands, and the resources on those islands *usually* worked well together. In Pax, I have tried to find a good seed, but every seed I found in v1.3 always had something that nags me. Like I would find a seed with a perfect island to build a massive city (this is how I like to play) just to find that it has some great resources but it lacks the one that would make it perfect. I absolutely hate having to ship so many things back and forth.  There's something about the end-game that bothers me as well. If you know how to play Anno from previous versions, it doesn't take long to get to the Colosseum. I can't really pinpoint it to a single thing but the endgame just feels unrewarding. There should be things you can strive for that seem absolutely unobtainable when you start out, and this just wasn't that. The trade routes become so insanely complicated due to the way fertilities are on the seeds I've played (9670, 1647, all the popular seeds) that it becomes more of a chore than a fun feature.  Most of my playthroughs so far have been fun, don't get me wrong. But there's always a point in expansion where I dread having to go to Albion to set up all the different routes, just to find out you need more trade routes because the fertilities here are also oddly spread across the islands.   I kind of miss just having that "best seed" that gives you islands with fertilities that in Pax would correspond to the gods we choose. I know I'm complaining about one of the main features of how to play the game (trade-routes) but it seems like I have to unnecessarily rely on trade routes that could have been avoided if there was just 1 more of a certain fertility on an island. In Pax, I got to the highest city state, had the nicest buildings, yet still felt like I didn't really do a whole lot or have anything to be proud of.  Again, I do still really enjoy the game, and I think it was a fantastic continuation of 1800, I just wish a few things were different to give me the same feeling I had in 1800.

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ “50 Hours In: Rome Didn’t Fall—It Took My Weekend Instead.”  After 50 hours in Anno 117: Pax Romana, I’ve reached a simple conclusion: if Caesar himself asked me to step away from my provinces, I’d tell him to file the request in triplicate and wait 6–8 business days. This game has its marble hooks in me.  🏛️ A City Builder Wearing a Togas and Brass Knuckles  What I love most is how Anno 117 blends serene city‑building with the constant hum of imperial politics. One moment I’m placing bathhouses and debating where to put my next vineyard; the next I’m negotiating with a governor who thinks “tribute” means “empty your coffers or else.”  The pacing is immaculate. Every system feels like it’s part of a living empire—trade routes pulse like arteries, legions march with purpose, and your citizens complain with the passion of true Romans.  ⚔️ The Frontier Is a Slow Burn… Until It Isn’t  The frontier provinces are where the game really flexes. Barbarians don’t just show up as red dots on the map—they’re personalities, factions, cultures. Sometimes they want peace. Sometimes they want your wine. Sometimes they want your head.  And when things go wrong, they go beautifully wrong. Watching a supply chain collapse because I forgot to maintain a single road station was the most Roman experience I’ve ever had.  🏺 Economy So Deep It Should Have Its Own Senate Seat  The economic simulation is peak Anno:  - Every resource matters  - Every trade route has a story  - Every bottleneck is a self‑inflicted tragedy  I’ve spent hours optimizing a single province because I knew I could squeeze out 3% more olive oil production. This game turns you into a micromanaging emperor, and you love every second.  🌅 A World Worth Conquering Slowly  The visuals are absurdly good. Sunsets over the Tiber. Fog rolling across the frontier. Tiny citizens arguing in the forum. It’s the kind of detail that makes you zoom in just to watch life happen.  🐎 The Moment It Clicked  Around hour 32, I realized I wasn’t playing a city builder—I was running an empire held together by ambition, spreadsheets, and the faint hope that my grain supply wouldn’t implode. That’s when the game became magic.  🏆 Verdict  Anno 117: Pax Romana is the most engrossing, elegant, and quietly intense entry the series has produced. It’s a love letter to empire management, a masterclass in pacing, and a dangerously effective time thief.  50 hours in, and I’m still discovering new systems. Rome wasn’t built in a day, but this game will make you try.

I wanted to hold off on writing this review, but recent developments have encouraged me to no longer wait. Steam offers two options for the review - a thumbs up and thumbs down. I wanted to hold off because I had a lot of hope that this game would be a thumbs up, even though right now, its absolutely a thumbs down. Recent decisions by the studio have greatly reduced any hopes I had for this game, leaving no reason to hold out for an improvement.     First off, I consider Anno 1800 to be one of (if not the) best games in the genre. I discovered that title fairly later in its lifespan, so by the time I started playing, there were already multiple DLCs which had been released. Because of my extremely positive experience with that title, I chose to pre-order Anno 117 with the Year One Pass under the assumption that it would improve with future releases. Now, this will certainly be the last Ubisoft title I pre-order, and it has a strong chance to be the last one I buy at all.     For its positives, it was fun playing for the first several minutes. It feels a lot like Anno 1800 in the mechanics, with slight improvements. it does fall short a little in the theme with a lot less to do (without zoos and museums, without a lot of variety on ships, with ancient tech, with shorter and less complex trade routes, etc.). But for major flaws, one need only scroll through the reviews of this game to see the numerous issues players have experienced. I understand that no studio is perfect and games are often full of bugs and errors upon release, so I don't fault them too strongly for the bugs I experienced and see complained of throughout the other reviews. I can also look past the requirement that this game makes you use Ubisoft Connect, which requires an additional account and causes errors in trying to launch the game.    However, the unfinished story here is egregious. You start the story with the introduction of characters and setting. After a sudden development, new characters and setting are introduced, complicating the plot lines established earlier. Then, it ends. No acknowledgment that the story is over. No solution to the issues that were raised through the opening acts. No choices to make. Just done. It took me another half hour to figure out it was done since the conclusion was so sudden and underwhelming that I was sure that I was simply missing something which would trigger the rest. But no, there is no "the rest." This entire game could have been a DLC to another Anno game where you get to try out these few tools in a Roman setting.     Ubisoft did not release Anno 117 as DLC to a different title, they released it as the newest entry in a long-established series. They sold players a Gold Edition with a Year One Pass, promising future DLC to be included in that purchase. Upon release of this unfinished and underwhelming entry, I still had hope that this game, for which I had purchased that Year One Pass would improve. Then Ubisoft announced the first new addition to game - paid cosmetic DLC, not included in any version of the game they've already sold. Now, I understand additional paid cosmetics (in fact, I bought all of the ones they released for Anno 1800) and that the Year One Pass listed what was included, but when you release a game like this and sell players more expensive versions with the promise of additional content, then instead of responding to numerous complaints raised, you make the first release a paid, cosmetic pack, it feels like a slap in face. I feel foolish for having placed faith in Ubisoft.     This is a game I spoke about with a lot of excitement to my friends before its release. Now, its one I have to warn them to forget about when they remember to ask about it. I would recommend any prospective buyers to beware. Anno 117 is not worth your money nor is Ubisoft worth your trust.

PL: Gra jest ogromnym rozczarowaniem. Po wielu latach spędzonych przy serii Anno czuję po prostu zawód – ta odsłona wypada bardzo słabo na tle poprzednich części. W starszych grach spędziłem setki godzin: 1701 – ~200 h 1404 – ~100 h 2070 – ~50 h 1800 – 597 h 38 min  Żadna z nich nie była jednak tak odrzucająca jak 117. Po około 20 godzinach gry przeszedłem w zasadzie wszystkie etapy, zbudowałem koloseum i… ani przez chwilę nie bawiłem się dobrze. Zamiast tego towarzyszyła mi głównie frustracja związana z bardzo nieudaną odsłoną serii.  Minusy: - ukośne budowanie jest uciążliwe i frustrujące - zadania od NPC są monotonne i nużące - kolorystyka męczy oczy, a wiele przycisków (np. ulepszenie magazynów) w ogóle nie wyróżnia się w momencie odblokowania - menu statków jest nieintuicyjne, pełne małych przycisków i pustych przestrzeni - statki są trudne do rozróżnienia – nie wyróżniają się i brakuje im charakteru - fizyka okrętów praktycznie nie istnieje (często „driftują” przy wyspach), a wyładunek wojsk jest zbugowany - ciągła potrzeba przestawiania budynków, aby zmieścić dodatkowe warsztaty, psuje sens rozbudowy miasta; w efekcie miasta są nienaturalnie kompaktowe i trudno wykorzystać rozmach dużych wysp - menu budowy jest nieintuicyjne – w Anno 1800 poziomy populacji były oznaczone ikonami, co znacznie poprawiało czytelność - wydajność jest bardzo słaba - mapa regionów jest nieczytelna.  EN: The game is a huge disappointment. After many years spent with the Anno series, I simply feel let down — this installment performs very poorly compared to the previous titles. I spent hundreds of hours in the earlier games:  1701 – ~200 h 1404 – ~100 h 2070 – ~50 h 1800 – 597 h 38 min  None of them, however, felt as off-putting as 117. After about 20 hours of gameplay I had essentially completed all stages, built the Colosseum, and… I didn’t enjoy a single moment of it. Instead, most of the time I felt frustrated by what is a very unsuccessful entry in the series.  Cons: - Diagonal building is cumbersome and frustrating - NPC quests are monotonous and tedious - The color palette is tiring for the eyes, and many buttons (e.g., warehouse upgrades) don’t stand out at all when they become available - The ship menu is unintuitive, filled with tiny buttons and empty space - Ships are difficult to distinguish — they don’t stand out and lack character - Ship physics practically don’t exist (they constantly “drift” near islands), and troop unloading is buggy - The constant need to rearrange buildings just to fit an additional workshop ruins the whole idea of city expansion; as a result, cities become unnaturally compact and it’s hard to take advantage of the scale of such large islands - The construction menu is unintuitive — in Anno 1800 population tiers were marked with icons, which made the interface much clearer - Performance is very poor - The regional map is unclear.
