Contrary to what people are saying, I love this game because the new foundations and design choices are fantastic improvements on the Anno recipe. Definitely deserves a much higher overall score.  There is clearly a lot of love and thought into correcting abuses in previous anno titles that force the building of more beautiful and better planned cities. Having to spread out the happiness/fire/health bonuses and maluses means no longer dumping all of your heavy industry or farms in the crap corner. Instead you have to think of how to weave your farms inbetween your production areas and towns to minimize maluses and maximize benefits. Easily my favorite change to the series, although the diagonals are obviously also sick and also create really fun opportunities for your farms.   The design shift of making regions self sufficient contenders instead of a main zone and sub regions is much more engaging than 1800 and allows for some super interesting starts. Excited to see how the additional regions impact that balance. Hopefully the campaign also expands as we get new regions instead of each sub region having its own campaign. Can't wait for goofy Marcus to reunite with his dad in Aegyptus.  Additionally, I love that the new system of needs provides persistent bonuses for every tier instead of needs shifting with each population tier. The ability to partially complete needs creates lots of interesting opportunities to maximize smaller islands in particular to create focused amounts of higher tier workforce without having to develop a huge chain. While you could do this in 1800, it feels so much more elegant of an implementation than 1800 by allowing you to choose which bits fill up the food/fashion/etc bars. Also, no mass destroying my earlier farms as the island grows as they still provide benefit! Makes long term planning a necessity.  Aqueducts also scratch the itch that electricity does (the one where you get a ton of satisfaction out of really sneakily snaking a line in where it looked like something was completely locked out) without me having to rework everything, and, while not as impactful, looks cool as hell and provides a unique challenge with the water flow to height restrictions. Makes an interesting trade off of where you put your mining industry and town.  Game looks beautiful, that goes without saying. Read the other reviews and I think the major gripes that people are having is a lack of content compared to 1800 and the short campaign. Those will work themselves out in time--the game oozes the fact that the team has passion for their product. My personal strangest gripe is that they removed the delivery time tracker between shipments that was in anno 1800. I hope there's a way to add that back in the future so I can properly balance shipments and needs between islands instead of manually timing the travel time vs good consumption per minute or "load the boat and use an island goods minimum stock limit and hope for the best".  Very excited to see this game develop in the coming years.

I still remember trying to play Anno 1404 on my old Toshiba laptop. It barely ran. Low settings, stutters, fans screaming like the Roman Empire was collapsing inside the laptop. I couldn’t truly experience it the way it deserved.  Fast forward to today — I’m 26, running Anno 117 on an RTX 5080 paired with an OLED display — and it honestly feels surreal.  I’ve played every major title from 1404 onward, and seeing how the series evolved into this is something special. Anno 117 doesn’t just improve systems — it matures them.  The Roman setting isn’t just aesthetic. It changes the tone. There’s a weight to governance now. Province management, expansion, logistics — everything feels more strategic and deliberate. Production chains are deeper but cleaner to manage. Trade routes feel smarter. The UI respects your time.  And visually? On an OLED screen, this game is insane. The lighting, the gold reflections, the depth in the cities — it’s probably the best Anno has ever looked.  What I love most:  Deep empire management that actually challenges you  Beautiful late-game cities that feel alive  Strong soundtrack and atmosphere  Optimization that handles massive populations smoothly  That constant “just one more optimization” addiction  Anno grew up. And honestly, so did I.  From barely running 1404 to building Roman empires in 117 at max settings — this one hits different.

Anno 117: Pax Romana feels like a true evolution of Anno 1800. Every now and then I catch myself thinking, “Maybe I’ll go back to 1800,” but almost immediately I follow that up with, “Honestly… 117 just feels better to play.”  It’s true that 1800 currently offers more overall content, largely thanks to years of additional DLC. But 117 will get there in time. And for the average player — especially those of us with full-time, grown-up jobs — jumping into 1800 with all of its DLC at once can feel overwhelming. I only started 1800 recently, and beginning from scratch with that much layered content makes for a pretty steep learning curve. It can also feel like an intimidating time investment.  By contrast, starting 117 with just two provinces feels focused and manageable. It offers a satisfying “temporary endgame” that doesn’t demand hundreds of hours upfront while we wait for future expansions. (That said… I’m already craving the third province!)  The Roman setting is a refreshing change from 1800’s industrial era, and the thematic shift really works. Combined with the new gameplay improvements, it gives the whole experience a modern, refined feel without losing what makes Anno special.  I actively encourage my friends to give it a try. Honestly, if you don’t give this game a proper chance, you’re missing out on something genuinely great. It’s beautiful, packed with things to do, and easily delivers hours of addictive fun.  100% recommended.

The Anno series will always be my love. I respect the developers’ hard work, and I like it so much that I pre-ordered two copies of the Anno 117 Gold Edition without hesitation the moment it was available. But because of some things Ubisoft has done, and considering the current level of completion of 117, since there is no mixed rating option, I can only leave a negative review.  [h1]The following points are the reasons why Ubisoft is making me more and more disappointed and annoyed. (Ubisoft, can you stop before it’s too late and turn back?)[/h1]  [h2] Ubisoft’s black history is something I will never forget.[/h2]  [h3] After The Crew was shut down, I finally understood what digital games mean in your eyes [/h3] What makes me the angriest is that Ubisoft turned a so-called purchase into a one-time rental by revoking licenses and shutting the game down: I clearly paid real money on Steam, yet at the critical moment I realized I didn’t truly own the game at all—I only had a ticket that could be taken back at any time. For someone like me who has spent over a thousand hours in Anno 1800 and Anno 117, this is not just as simple as servers shutting down. It’s basically turning the act of buying a game into empty words: I already paid the money, but with a single “service terminated” from Ubisoft, I can’t play anything, and I have nothing left.  [h3] For players who pre-ordered Anno 1800 on Steam in 2019, the later DLCs and Season Pass would not continue to be updated on Steam. [/h3] What hurts me the most isn’t simply losing one purchase channel, but this forced platform split that completely shatters my sense of belonging on Steam. I’ve always loved Steam’s community atmosphere—one-click screenshot uploads, showcases, guides and reviews, and interacting with each other. For me and many players, the process of playing games is inherently tied to the Steam community. But with Anno 1800 where the base game is on Steam while later DLC and content are pushed to be bought on other platforms, it basically forces me and other players to keep cutting back and forth between different platforms. In other words, I paid money on Steam, yet I can’t get all the content in the same ecosystem the way a normal Steam game works.  [b] [u] Based on what I said above, these black history incidents are not just a small convenience issue for someone like me who treats Steam as a game library and a social space. Ubisoft, you! You directly push the cost of platform choices onto players, weakening the purchasing experience, community interaction, and the sense of ownership all at once.[/u][/b]  [h2] Back to 117 itself, here are the reasons I can’t give it a positive review right now: [/h2]  [h3]The campaign is far from finished[/h3] The current game feels more like an Early Access game, because it abruptly ends after I only played two chapters, and my relationship with the NPC rivals in the game doesn’t have a complete description. At this price, it doesn’t match a full release at all.  [h3] The three content DLCs in the Gold Edition went on sale before they even released! [/h3] This is the saddest part for me. As one of the first players to pre-order your game, I can understand discounting the base game, because I have played it. But why would you discount content that hasn’t even been released yet? It’s like Back 4 Blood back then. Many players bought the Ultimate Edition, but even when the Ultimate Edition was 50% off, the DLCs included in the Ultimate Edition still weren’t released. Then why didn’t I just buy the Standard Edition from the start? Don’t talk to me about how Standard Edition + a separate Year Pass costs more than the Gold Edition. Your marketing has problems, and you should seriously reflect on it. Don’t make pre-order players pay the price. If you keep doing this, it will only make the players who are truly willing to spend money never buy any of your versions again.  [h3] With my current specs, the game starts dropping frames in the mid-to-late game [/h3] If Ubisoft’s recommended enthusiast specs are an RTX 4080 and an i7-13700K with 32GB RAM, then performance shouldn’t drop below 30 FPS, right? But once I reached the mid-to-late game, the game started stuttering from time to time. Especially when I switch from Albion to Latium, sometimes it drops to under 10 FPS and I can’t even move. Don’t tell me my specs aren’t good enough.  [h3] Why does a game I bought on Steam still have to go through Ubisoft Connect? Cancel it already!!!! [/h3] I buy games on Steam for the Steam experience and ecosystem, not to go through Ubisoft Connect again every time I launch the game. Can you remove this as soon as possible, or at least weaken it so it doesn’t affect the experience?  [h2]What I hope you prioritize fixing (there are many! but I’ll pick the most important ones first):[/h2]  [h3] Too little decorative content, and more and more paid decorative content [/h3] I’m a building enthusiast. You can add more free decorations for all players, and then sell paid decorative DLC on top of that.  [h3] Decorative roads should have the function of normal roads[/h3] One of my biggest headaches in 1800 was that decorative roads could not function like normal roads. You responded to players saying it was due to technical reasons. But the problem is, mods have long been able to make decorative roads have normal road functionality, so I don’t believe it’s technically impossible—I believe you just don’t take it seriously enough.  [h3] Remove Ubisoft Connect for the Steam version [/h3] Remove or weaken Ubisoft Connect. And don’t let it happen that you update DLC for one year and then withdraw from the Steam ecosystem again.  [h3] Bring the PC UI and co-op markers back to the level of 1800 [/h3] 117’s UI and multiplayer marker experience are both regressing. I play co-op building, and the markers have no sound prompts and weak feedback, so communication efficiency drops straight down.  [h3] Hurry up and complete the built-in mod browser [/h3] How did such a mature mod browser in 1800 become so difficult in 117?   [h3]  In short, it’s not that I don’t love the Anno series. On the contrary, I’m this angry precisely because I love it so much. Right now, Anno 117 is burning away my and others trust through its lack of content completeness, performance issues, pre-order and discount strategy, and the Steam version being held hostage by Ubisoft Connect and fragmented across platforms. If you still want to keep the core players who are truly willing to spend money and invest their time, then please fix and complete the fundamentals first. [/h3]

Idk whether to recommend this or not.   So it plays like an Anno game, which is what I wanted, buuuuuuuuuut it's VERY empty in the state it is in. It's not fair to compare a base game to it's fully kitted-out predecessor, but also, if you're enjoying 1800 and want *more* this really isn't it. It's about as complex as the base game of 1800 was, but that was nearly a decade ago, and to go from a fully fledged game with all of its DLC to this just isn't enjoyable. The complete version of Anno 1800 has 5 regions to explore and colonize. This has 2. Crown Falls in 1800 gives you a massive continent to build on, that's not in this game yet.  There's also the problem of historical accuracy. If you want to feel like a Roman noble, this isn't the best experience on the market. Total War Rome 2 still is. There's a lot of fudging the history here to justify the mechanics.   I don't think it's unreasonable to expect a game to exceed it's predecessor in depth or in amount of content. Otherwise, like, why would you buy it? CS2 is broken, but at least it kind of makes a lot of things available in CS1's DLC a part of the base game. This isn't an evolution of Anno. It's basically the same as the last one, but with a lot less to do right now. And honestly, Anno 1800 actually looks better too. It is just a prettier game than 117 is.   Also, why the hell is this over 110 gigs? That makes no sense to me. MAKE YOUR DAMN GAMES MORE EFFICIENT. There is no logical reason for this to be almost the same size as a Call of Booty game.   I'm going with not recommended because ultimately, this feels like a lazy cash grab, and I know the team that made this game can do better. I'll be picking up the DLCs because I believe in the franchise, and because I hope they add the depth I've come to expect, but I highly recommend against buying this until it has more stuff in it, and for sure don't buy it at full price.

As a huge fan of Anno 1800, Anno 117 feels like a step backward in a few frustrating ways. My biggest issue is the lack of a multiplayer game speed option. 1800 had a great mod to speed things up, but 117 completely lacks the feature natively and doesn't have a mod for it, making multiplayer sessions painfully slow. It's a massive minus.  On top of that, the expansion mechanics feel forced. In 1800, you could easily grab a secondary island, keep it at Tier 1 or 2, and just use it as a raw material feeder for your main hub. In Anno 117, the game practically forces you to over-develop multiple islands just to keep your main settlement functioning. It feels less like strategic logistics and more like an artificial hurdle.

I played the base game of anno 1800 and played it for almost 300 hours, before buying this one so I think I can give my opinion on how they compare.    First I'll tell you what I liked:  - I LOVE the optional needs, even though I often want everyone to have every need fullfilled I love how you can choose wich needs are required for your goal with a certain island. I also really enjoy how every tier can benefit from all needs below.   - I really enjoy the complexity in albion. The double paths give it in my opinion even more complexity than  anno 1800. imo an even more fun complexity, in anno 1800 the complexity is mostly in the complex production chains while in anno 117 it gets complex by the enormous amount of options. Do I go the celtic road, or roman or both and how can I fit this all on my islands. I love it.  - I think it's great how both regions are seen as (mostly) equal. I like this a lot more than anno 1800 where new world kind of just supplies your old world.  - I love the diagonal building, I don't use it that much in cities even tho I can see how beautybuilders might, but I use it to more naturally follow the layout of the islands and imo it makes a big difference.  - I love the setting, roman empire seemed like a great setting for anno and it really is. the vibe is great and it looks amazing. I think all the different possible future regions are gonna be amazing.  - I like the fact that we now have land combat as well.  - I'm kinda bored of writing this review, so I'll move on even though I'm sure there is more I like about this game.    The only thing I can think of right now that I didn't really like is the campaign, I do think it's good that it forced you to focus on both regions seperately but I wasn't that bothered to hurry up with the quests. Granted I never really am and wasn't either in anno 1800 but still.    Overal I think right now this is a great game with potential to get even better!

⭐⭐⭐⭐☆    Anno 117: Pax Romana feels like coming back home. If you’ve ever played Anno, you’ll immediately know what you’re doing, and that’s a good thing! The core systems are familiar: population tiers, house upgrades, production chains… everything clicks fast, but there’s still enough depth to keep you learning as you go. Nothing feels overwhelming, even after just 10 hours in.    Visually, the game is a joy. The maps feel alive: weather effects, vegetation, buildings, and city life all blend beautifully. Even on a non–high-end laptop, it looks fantastic. At times it almost feels tangible, like you could reach out and touch the city. I especially love how organic everything feels, the diagonal streets and the mix of nature and urban life make cities look natural instead of rigid.    Overall, it’s a modernized, refreshed take on a classic formula. Familiar, cozy, but polished enough to feel new again. If you like city builders in the Anno vein, this one is absolutely worth your time.

Is it better than anno 1800  or 1400?   No.   Anno 119 is a well made game , but it restricts the player massively  Meaning the game-loop is after two runs kinda dead and repeats itself. Horrible for a sandbox game !  You can see it on fan-based tool sites , a lot of work in progress and there is no new updates.   Is it because of an unfinished campaign ?  I could care less about that , you don't play Anno for a stupid campaign.  I did two playthroughs : - One on easy mode, get the biggest city possible.  - One on hard mode , conquer all !  Both are fun , don't get me wrong , but they also did not satisfy that anno itch , that made previous games dangerous for your social life.  Why ?  Let me put up the things I did not like and maybe I will figure it out myself.  - The colour scheme is horrendous , especially on a bird-eye's view and let's be honest , that is how you play.    You can't see the difference of the upgraded buildings and the difference of the buildings and it tiers your eyes very quickly.      The satisfaction is cut immensely by that, you have no overview.  -  The Research tree :  liked the idea at first , but quickly you figure out that you are stuck reproducing the exact same game strategy as your first run , your second and your third.  The game becomes repetitive very quickly, because you will get stuck to research , if you do anything different.   -  The whole new  , fire,health and security gameplay , makes sure you are even more confined.      Grandiose strategy , with one island doing all the basic production , moving that product to other islands , with   a crazy fleet ?     Nope , your production Island will never be fireproof.     You can easily survive , but that is not what your goal was.   - No sharing of workforce through islands.  Makes it even more restricted how you play the game as well.   -  The islands are small , even on easiest mode , big crazy cities are out of the question.   - The Aqueducts instead of trains  - feel like they have put in last minute  - are boring.       They could have done an elaborate waterwork system to replace the trains.      None of that.   You don't feel like a whole new world opens up, like the trains in 1800.        The big city play. Fun , but there are no really big islands.  I am sure they will make a 30 dollar DLC for that , but then you already lost me.   The first time it is fun figuring out how ... but you won't do a second playthrough.   The conquer all fast playthrough. Not really a fan of the new real time strategy units.  They are clunky and you are actually stuck doing a normal run with fast research to make it happen.  Meh ...  Miss the real towers , hate the whole new unit approach.   I think the biggest game-breaker is the research.  Means your gameplay loop keeps restricted.  If they work on that , it might change things.  Thumbs up ? or Thumbs down?  I am thorn, but it is not a buggy mess, the game is solid , so I cannot give it a thumbs down.

Rome’s story begins as legend: in 753 BCE, Romulus supposedly founded a small settlement along the Tiber River. Behind the myth, Rome grew because of its location—good farmland, defensible hills, and access to trade routes. After an early period of kings, Romans overthrew monarchy around 509 BCE and created a Republic, built on elected offices, a Senate, and a belief (often imperfectly practiced) in civic duty. Over the next centuries, Rome expanded across Italy through alliances and warfare, then fought major rivals like Carthage in the Punic Wars (264–146 BCE), gaining control of the western Mediterranean.    Expansion brought wealth and power, but also deep inequality, civil unrest, and political violence. Reformers like the Gracchi brothers were killed; generals such as Marius, Sulla, Pompey, and Julius Caesar used armies for political leverage. After Caesar’s assassination in 44 BCE, another round of civil wars ended when Octavian—later Augustus—became Rome’s first emperor in 27 BCE, marking the shift from Republic to Empire.    The early Empire reached its height in the “Pax Romana,” a long period of relative stability and prosperity supported by roads, law, trade, and professional armies. At its greatest extent under Trajan (early 2nd century CE), Rome ruled from Britain to the Middle East and North Africa. Over time, pressures mounted: economic strain, plagues, internal power struggles, and repeated invasions. In the 4th century, Constantine legalized Christianity and founded Constantinople. The Empire split into western and eastern halves; the Western Roman Empire fell in 476 CE, while the Eastern (Byzantine) Empire endured until 1453. Rome’s legacy—language, law, architecture, governance, and religion—helped shape Europe and the wider Mediterranean world.

I, personally, really enjoy this game and I recommend it, but there is a lot to improve. I'll break it down into several points including the pros and cons.  1) Campaign mode is boring and I think I quit halfway through. It's not especially engaging and extremely limited. It seems like something that was just thrown together and not actually meant to be the main focus of the game. To be fair, because I never actually finished it, maybe there is potential. But...I don't see any. HOWEVER, sandbox mode is very fun. I enjoy a good sit down mindless city builder at the end of the day, and the raiders provide some challenge... but that leads into my next point:  2) The NPCs are either boring or way too challenging. Too challenging as in, the "advanced" NPC has such a large unfair advantage that he has entire war fleet and declaring war on me before I even have the population size to make a solid army. At that point it's not a challenge but almost impossible and extremely annoying. It's either no challenge at all (the raiders are fairly easy) or you have to dedicate your entire playthrough to beating this one guy- which isn't easy because it is a city builder and not a war game. In a semi-related note, I would like to have more interactions with the NPCs. Not sure what that might look like, but making decisions that impact relationships with them, sending nasty letters, etc etc that could make it a little more engaging. Interactions with the emperor too would be a fun way to keep things interesting, instead of just a "they approve, they disapprove" situation.   3) This is more of a quality of life critique, but the islands are extremely limited. The islands are small even at their largest setting, and I think it would be slightly more visually interesting if there were ways to have either larger islands or no islands at all- just land masses with large boarders. I find myself running out of space and getting bored.  I think there are some aspects to this game that feel either lazy or unfinished. I still recommend this game for people who enjoy city builders! and I will continue playing it myself. I hope for improvements in the future. I wish the game now seemed more complete, but it is limited in ways that make it boring and repetitive after a while. This game needs serious work but there is potential, I love Roman games and I love casual city builders. I am excited for the update coming in 2026.

Wish I could recommend it. It's a fairly fun game and all, but the first strike is certain content being locked behind bizarre and annoying gates that range from interacting with the Ubisoft store (ew) to watching specific twitch streams (yuck). This game is a big enough time suck already without having to watch someone else play it to access content.  The experience that specifically lead to me writing this review was launching this game that I legally purchased and seeing a giant obnoxious error icon on the side of my screen because I wasn't connected to Ubisoft servers. This is ABSOLUTELY INEXCUSABLE in any game that isn't explicitly shipped as a multiplayer only experience.   Additionally, the profanity filter is NOT optional and is so extremely sensitive that you basically have a choice between using the random names your islands are given, or naming them a string of asterisks.   Notably, the Latin word for island (insula) is enough to set off the filter.   Yes, you are reading that correctly. In this game about Romans on islands you can't use the Roman word literally meaning island.   Pardon my French, but what the ♥♥♥♥ is even remotely profane about that string of letters??? While I would normally recommend such a feat, I cannot in good conscience do so, due to a severe absence of customization settings that even small indie devs put the effort into including in their games. Also the use of generative AI in asset development is alarming.  Urgent fixes required: 1. Provide a setting for # of islands, or at least scale # of islands to governor count.  2. City unrest should be factored by military infrastructure, not generic city watch/custodia. This would allow the military component of the game to integrate much better into city design.   The developers did an amazing job putting this game together. I’ve always been attracted to Anno games for their out-of-the-box difficulty in terms of expansion, resource gathering, and growing cities. The same is true with Anno 117.  However, I would like to see more customization features to improve the single player experience. Competing against AI governors really isn’t very fun. They don’t bother you if you don’t bother them. I could do without the arcade combat. I love the integration of military infrastructure into city building, but for my purposes it’s purely aesthetic since I could care less about the arcade combat. It should have replaced the city watch mechanism, forcing all players to have some degree of militarism.   The game currently doesn't scale the number of islands to governor count. This breaks many of the game mechanics if you choose to play alone, without competing governors. For example, even on max difficult settings, there are still way too many islands with way too many resource options. You don’t have to care about # of mines or river slots on islands, because you can just find other islands with the same resources. This can easily be solved by lowering the total number of islands in a map. Alternatively, another fix for this is to create new difficulty levels for game creation, e.g. less island slots, less resource availability per island.

I'm writing this about 6 hours in, having just completed the campaign.  [h2]Pros:[/h2] [b]Gameplay-wise, It's a good Anno game.[/b] It takes a lot of what the excellent 1800 did and improves on it, adding a few new elements to explore, like land combat and a fairly expansive tech tree. The game looks stunning, and runs quite well.  [h2]Cons:[/h2] [b]The campaign.[/b] I'm not sure what they were thinking - they build up a story, you can see where they might be going with it and start getting settled in for the next act, and then *poof* - it's over: campaign complete. Once you 'beat' Albion, you come back to Latium a hero to the new emperor, but with Empress Juliana's comeback and the restoration of the Pax still unresolved. At this point you're expecting another act, and perhaps for the intrigue and challenge to step up a notch. Maybe you'll find your loyalties challenged? Some difficult choices to make between the new emperor that lauded and rewarded you for your success in Albion, and your loyalty to Juliana and the old emperor? Maybe they'll build up the side characters, Diana and the pirate Syracus? Maybe they'll introduce you to the other leaders and build up their characters? Nope, campaign over - enjoy endless mode.  [h2]Conclusion:[/h2] [b]t's a worthy Anno game based on the pure game mechanics and city building aspects - more than an 1800 re-skin, but not much more.[/b] But if you're looking to sink your teeth into a campaign, don't bother: the campaign as it stands basically just functions as a tutorial mode. It's short, not particularly challenging and just before it's about to get interesting it's over.  I'm going to keep enjoying this as a great Anno game, but I'm disappointed with the missed potential of the story mode. Pick it up if you loved 1800, and let's hope that the DLC and expansions help to build on the story.

I've really enjoyed my time playing 117. I think its a good step forward from 1800. I played a ton of 1800 and I was worried it couldn't compete but it does. There are some new hidden mechanics that once you know improve your play through. I assume they go through this in the campaign but I go straight to endless.  Workforce management was probably the biggest change for me. Ships now cost residents from your island. If you want a big army or navy, you need to make sure you have the population to support it. The part that was hidden was the ability to change the source of the workforce. On my first play through I couldn't figure out why my population kept going down even when all the goods were supplied. The good thing is you can choose any island in the providence to source the workforce and it'll free them up from the original island they were the ship was built on. Same goes for ground troop.  There are now area effects of buildings, positive and negative. Some production building you want to put in your city and others you want to keep away from buildings. The negative effects stack while the positive ones don't.  You can now remove and add AI opponents or AI pirates from the game. If you want to start up a playthrough to get the hang of things before interacting with the AI, you can now just add them later to whenever you are ready.  I look forward to the new areas the dlc has provided and expect I will play this for hundreds more hours.  The one annoying thing that is still around is the computer telling you that you've been playing for too long. I don't need that reminder I spent a whole weekend playing Anno.

It's the same old Anno in a good and bad way.  What's new? It has diagonal roads/building placements as new element, but it really doesn't change much. It also added religion, but it's nothing interesting and just some numerical buffs. Land combat is barebones and feels it requires too much population to build. It feels similar to the barebones system akin to the Tropico series, but army in that game blends more naturally to the gameplay. Some production buildings now have AOE like +happiness and +coins on housing so you want to put it near housings instead of making a separate manufacturing district away from houses. Interesting change, but feels half baked at this point. Graphics looks better, especially the landscape, but Anno 1800 might look better if you aren't able to play with the highest graphics settings in this game. Other than that, I don't really see improvements from Anno 1800. May be if you really, really like the Roman ascetics?  The foundation Meanwhile, UI is a step backwards, campaign is poor and unfinished, and content is lacking. It feels like a 50% to 75% finished game. The future DLCs would expand the game, but that's behind a pay wall probably... The worst offender is the lack of charm. The entire time I played, I never felt like having a new experience, which to be fair is not a strength of Anno, but even considering that, I felt like doing the same thing as Anno 1800, but little bit worse in all the aspects. Anno never was easy to make organic cities, but it feels harder than ever before with some of the production buildings giving island wide buffs/debuffs for fire, health, and happiness.   For new players This game (or Anno series) is mainly a production chain management game and leans toward optimization of layout more than city builders like SimCity/Cities Skylines. You'll mainly have to gather resources, manufacture goods, and build amenities for the citizens to "level up" and repeat that cycle. The production is not complex, but require setting up chains in different islands (similar to outpost) and manage trade fleets to move the goods around.  If you do not own more recent Anno titles, I advice buying base Anno 1800 which can be bought pretty cheap, especially during sale. As for DLCs, don't buy them unless you really enjoy the base game.  -For old fans If you have Anno 1800, just play that game. You are not missing out on anything.  If you don't have it, buy Anno 1800 on sale. For Anno 117 itself, wait until there is anniversary or complete editions few years later for "finished" version of the game. Although I doubt there will be anything worthwhile to buy instead of just playing Anno 1800. May be you will be forced to but this game when they shut down Anno 1800 down the line because you don't own your game according to UBI.

It's literally just Anno 1800 with a Roman reskin. But as with 1800, the history is more of a supporting flavor than a focus by the designers. Will you learn about important historical figures and their deeds? No. But will you learn about historical societies and their relationships with one another? Also no. Will random Latin and Latin-sounding words be shoehorned in, in a lazy attempt to create a vibe? Absolutely.  All the classic signs of design by committee that are present in other Anno games are present here (hooray for being reminded that I'm a loser who plays too many games, every two hours), along with more poor voice acting (from all the same voice actors as other Anno games), spelling errors in written copy, animations that don't match the sound clips, and memory leaks that hinder performance over time. So many of the production buildings are just straight-up copied over from 1800 that the game feels less like a new game and more like an overhaul mod.   At least the characters are interesting and don't feel shoehorned in to pander to a progressive audience-- oh, wait, nevermind. it's actually quite the opposite.  If you've never played Anno before and like games about micromanaging economies, pick it up for the Anno experience. If you own other Anno games, save your money and go play those instead.

We tried all the usual stuff: restarting the game and Ubisoft Connect, deleting desync folders, verifying files, disabling firewalls and VPNs, only joining through the in-game lobby, etc. No luck.  At this point, the main feature we bought the game for just doesn’t work. And yeah, I know launch bugs happen, but it’s pretty wild that basic co-op is broken on first launch for two brand new accounts on the same network.  This feels like a Ubisoft backend or entitlement issue, not something on our end.  The game itself looks great and I’m sure it could be fun, but if you’re buying this for multiplayer, be aware it might not even let you play. We’ve already put in a support ticket and a Steam refund request.  Might update this review if it gets fixed, but right now it’s a frustrating first impression.

I have to give some context before I do this. I have played every Anno to date, even before steam was a thing, accumulating well over 2000 hours in the series. I have loved all of them, they hit the spot for me in games like no other franchise.    Now with that said, Pax Romana is an abomination to the series. It instills a level of boredom like no other Anno entry to date. The only thing i can relate it to is like eating the most disgusting food when your starving. This entry has depressed me to a certain level knowing itll take years to get a new entry. I am posting this review in hopes to stop you from buying it as I sit and choke it down.    The first thing Id like to point out is the new troops on the ground system it has. This is by far the biggest "innovation" to the series in this entry. On paper its a good idea, and in some instances it actually is well done in terms of producing and supplying them HOWEVER, the biggest issues with this new war system is an underlaying problem that has existed in all ANNO's, its static AI. What does this mean? This means the AI doesnt build and generate resources as you play, it just has a fixed amount of things at all times. Now in the past ANNOs where war isnt really a thing we overlook this static AI, but when its such a large part of the DNA of PAX it is impossible to look past. It really sums up all the issues with this game.    The campaign is so bad its not even worth mentioning, a legit 1/10        The biggest elephant in the room is its comparison to 1800, perhaps an all time top 3 games for me. People use 1800/s content as an excuse and I am here to say that is not the case at all. Sure content helps but everything 1800 does, all of its systems, its items, etc, are all vastly superior. The one comparison Id shine the light on because its the easiest to understand are the items in the 2 games. The items in 1800 are impactful, interesting, and desirable to obtain as they provide real valuable boost to your production lines. In Pax all of that is wiped out, the boosts are almost irrelevant, the balance in price and difficulty to obtain, non existent.     Go play 1800, let that be your impression of what ANNO is, not whatever this game is trying to be.

WARNING!!! MAKE SURE THAT YOUR GAME IS NOT BLOCKED BY THE WINDOWS DEFENDER BEFORE YOU START THE GAME!!!    After 16 hours of an initial gaming session, wanted to save the game. The save page / module at first seemed wrong because the title of save page / module was exactly the same as the 'load' one. For some reason, the UIX designers thought it is a great idea to differentiate them as if they are totally different features... anyway, since the main in-game menu had load and save game modules as different options, you expect to see an appropriate title for each module / screen.     I have had already expected some bugs to happen since I'd read many of the bad reviews. At first the situation with the save function not working tipped me off but then I noticed the message on the menu telling me that my game was last saved a few minutes ago with autosave. This was not a temp system feedback message like an average 'toast notification message' you encounter on a commercial app. It was static, sitting there on the menu, telling me everything is fine and it is ok for me to quit the game. Well, guess what? It was a lie.    Believing the game's feedback system that my gaming session was auto saved, I quit game. Just a day after, when I went back, what do I see? Suprise suprise. There is no save to load, but only the usual option to start a new game. Turns out that, Windows Defender blocked only the saving function to access and write to the corresponding save file location.    - Am I suprised?   + No. During the gaming session, I found a lot of technical and non-technical bugs BUT the GUI & system feedback (UX) ones have been really, but really bad. Even worse than the champions in this field, such as EA, Bethesda & Ubisoft games. All of these findings during the initial 3-4 hours set the tone for me. I was kind of expecting such an outcome.    - Am I pissed off?  + I lost 16 hours of gaming session and I was playing the main campaign.    - Is Windows Defender the main offender here?  + Hell NO! At least, not the main one.    - Why do I think the designer, developer and QA teams as well as the product manager that worked on this project should never employed by any company?  + Yes, I am getting emotional over this but you should have seen me when I found out that I lost 16 hours of my precious time over the weekend which I supposed to relax after a stressful work week. If only I had the product team before me when this happened... I GET IT, there is always a management, board, shareholders side to the development story of every product that is being sold on the market. Been there, done that. If you are experienced in the field, you acknowledge and manage those risks even before the business case comes up. The managing part of the risk is sometimes, working overtime without getting paid. That is not a nice thing, but it is the reality. It is actually the line you often find yourself crossing to deliver a product of passion and work ethics if you respect yourself, team and the product you create.      Final verdict: This game and development team behind it follows same, not-so-old pattern of well known AAA games nowadays. Half baked piece of money grab without single ounce of talent behind it.            -----------------------------------------------------------------------------  SPOILER ALERT!!!! SKIP THE PART BELOW IF YOU DON'T WANT TO STORY OF THE CAMPAIGN TO BE SPOILED!!!  .  .  .  .  .  .  .  .  Bonus: After the anti-climatic death of the Emperor who appears to be portrayed as kind of a beloved leader(?), the way the next emperor is presented seemed forceful. The Decimus of whatever his name is, the next one supposedly is a tyrant, a bad guy. However, this so-called TYRANT:  1- Left the previous one's family alone, nothing bad happened to them  2- Did not touch their home island except for taking their monetary assets  3- Gave us a chance to prove ourselves even though were of one of main supporters of the previous deceased emperor, meaning did not kills us on sight. Exile in the form of redemption.    I mean, I am not that familiar with the history of Rome. Maybe this was a canon event that happened. But since our guy (I picked Marcus, the male governor) is an inexperienced, scaredy, spoiled and a bit traumatized piece of crap, I found no reason for him to act like "oh I'm so loyal to the previous Emperor, I have principles". On the contrary, once I found out what type of person the new Emperor was I was delighted to work for him, weird right?    Either the writers of the story are the bunch that worked on Netflix version of Witcher & Dragon Age the Veilguard, or my inexperience in Roman history and ways is telling.

After about 43 hours, I really like this game. There are certainly issues, such as: [list]     [*]The multiplayer is very temperamental, with constant closing of the game required to reconnect to saved multiplayer games. Apparently this has been fixed, but not that I can see.      [*]The campaign does seem to end quite abruptly, but it was very fun to play overall. It took me a solid 30 hours to play through it, with coop multiplayer being a large portion of that.     [*]The game is £50.00. While this is not bad itself, they game has only just been released at time of writing, and ALREADY you can buy a season one pass for the three DLC's they are going to be adding. [/list] That being said, there are some great pro's as well: [list]     [*]The graphics are beautiful. Even playing on medium, I am blown away by the level of detail.     [*]The maps feel very lifelike, and the AI plays well.      [*]The ground combat feels exciting, but don't expect total war. This is primarily a city builder.     [*]The campaign is fun, and I genuinely found myself interested in it.      [*]When it works, the multiplayer is very fluid. There is very little lag, even when playing with other players who are at 200+ ping.     [*]The game is a definite upgrade over Anno 1800, both the ships (with their customisation), differing population progression in the "new world", and a "research" area that unlocks some nice upgrades. [/list]  Overall, I feel that this game is worth the £50 price tag. If you see it on sale, even better. While the year one pass does leave a sour taste in my mouth, I enjoy this game so much that I will most likely buy it anyway!

This game has some upgrades from a QOL point of view that Anno 1800 clearly lacked. Moreover, the re-addition of land invasions is greatly appreciated, albeit a bit too simple. Unfortunately, this game just lacks a certain sense of wonder and depth that previous Annos have had.   For me, what I found the most disappointing is the 'new world' of choice for the game, Albion. It is boring, and I feel has zero value when compared to the New World, Ambessa, the Arctic or the Orient from previous titles. It sucks that i am in Albion for...cheese and silver brooches, while in Anno 1800 I was in the new world for oil, rubber, resources that makes sense to fight over. Ship combat sucks, of course its going to suck, this isnt the age of sail or exploration, ships during this time were very simple...this does not translate well for an entertaining strategy game.   My further concern is the future of the game. The biggest trap they fell with chosing Rome at its height, you have few options for DLC area expansions. North Africa? Nope, very well established and populated by 117. Spain? Also well established, and, it would look too much like Latium. Maybe the Caucasus or Arabia?   The genius of 1800 is that you played as if you were expanding across a century, while 117 feels like you are literally locked into 117, severely limiting options. I am open minded and am hoping to be proven seriously wrong. As it stands, this game is not worth its asking price. I suggest waiting until the first DLC comes out and see if you can get it at a bargain price during summer sale 2026 or winter sale 2026.

I was really looking forward to Anno Pax Romana 117, and I was genuinely surprised by how beautiful the game turned out to be. The graphics are stunning, with richly detailed islands and vibrant colors that truly bring the Roman era to life. From the very beginning, the amount of variation and possibilities feels impressive, and I found myself completely immersed in this new Anno experience.  That said, the game currently feels a bit limited in long-term content. Playing for more than 50 hours is possible, but beyond that point there simply isn’t enough variety to keep things feeling fresh. The island and map designs start to feel familiar, and you begin to recognize the same worlds repeating themselves. It might be that Anno 1800 set the bar incredibly high and is hard to surpass, but I’m not entirely sure. With upcoming DLCs, this could very well change and become a thing of the past.  The campaign also feels unfinished. It ends rather abruptly, and it feels like there is much more story to tell and many more hours of gameplay that could have been added. The sudden ending left me wanting more depth and continuation.  Despite these shortcomings, the biggest reason I remain such a fan of this release is its Roman setting. Experiencing this era with such breathtaking visuals and a warm, colorful atmosphere is truly something special. I absolutely cannot wait for the upcoming DLCs and to see how the game evolves over time.  Score: 7.3/10

Just my two cents on it.   tldr: The game is great and worth buying, even on a small sale.

The game is cool and has a lot of potential.    Is it as good as Anno 1800? In its current state, no—but it could be better in time. There are still some rough edges here and there. The core gameplay loop is still Anno, which is a good thing.    I enjoy the deity feature.  I enjoy the land combat.    Sea combat is alright, but it doesn’t have the punch it had in 1800. One small but persistent annoyance: every ship pauses for a second when you give a fleet new orders. Call it a pet peeve, but please fix that.    I like the modular ship system, though I would have liked this mechanic to be a bit deeper.    Diplomacy, while expanded, is difficult to actively engage with—for me, at least. I may be missing some of its mechanics, but it often feels like things just happen without clear cause or feedback. The idea is solid; the execution needs work.    I like the ability to decide which needs to fulfil, but let’s be honest: you end up trying to fulfil everything anyway. I wouldn’t call this a game-changing feature.    The research tree is a cool idea.    Diagonal building is a very welcome addition, although it sometimes forces you to waste space.    The story, however, is a travesty. I’m sorry, but I didn’t even realise it had ended. Just… wow. Ubisoft Mainz, please give us a free update with a third act or something. (Yes, free—this is not in a finished state.) What’s there now feels unfinished. It starts with intrigue and a bit of mystery—and then it’s just over. What? Come on.    Overall, if you enjoyed 1800, you’re going to enjoy Anno 117.

This is my first ever review written on steam. I really like Anno series, but this one I cannot recommend at it's current state.  Pros -  One of greatest pro is land combat which seems to be well made and is very interesting, but have it's own issues which doesn't really root from core mechanics of it, but rather of bugs around - units sometime pathfind to weird location to be able to shoot (catapults to be exact), brutal friendly fire as you cannot stop ships from attacking targets automatically without moving them out of range  Diagonal building - adds a little more natural look to cities as they doesn't need to be only in one directional grid so this offers option to make some really cool looking islands.  Religion and knowledge systems are good idea which could be nicely scaled with more content added  Prestige seems ok mechanic, but it's kinda lacking purpose other than being able to shove more specialist into villa and have more officirum building (on small islands this mechanic seems pointless)  Cons -  Game breaking bug in campaign when I transitioned to Albion got me immediately game over, but anyway the "story" was terrible, probably never going to play that campaign again.  Game breaking bug in some of map types, where pirate island is not even spawned and all quests with him are automatically failed because there are no pirate ships in Latinum.  Profanity filter - you are kidding me right? Trying to filter all naughty words, you could put your effort in something more meaningful because you will never have them all even when you try to do it in other languages which obviously you don't understand - ie. Latin city names. Also why when I rename ship from it's generated name to something else and back to original it get censored. Ubisoft doesn't seem to know how to implement generator with respect to it's own blacklist, which is kinda ridiculous. Also the game is marked to contain "Bad language" so why is this even in game?  Never before was I so annoyed by citizen portraits blabbing nonsense when I have clicked residence - Anno 1800 had this feature, when citizens states what they are missing or having but I almost never noticed it as annoying  Citizen portraits looks souless and weird  Constant storm in Albion is getting annoying, it was unplayable when it changed to snow for Christmas season.  Why am I being noticed every two hours that I'm playing game? What is the point? Having the game paused for half day spawned me 6 notifications that I'm playing it for 12 hours!  Religion tab with images of Gods looks so sloppy, I wanted to poke my eyes when I saw them. I'm guessing this is the AI slop they used  Diplomacy in it's current state is terrible downgrade compared to 1800 - I get locked out of war because all npcs are in one alliance, never fight each other and never attacked me. You can't even provoke them to get to point where they would attack you as there is no "reputation" visible with them.  Emperor mechanic looks fun for a while until it start to get annoying and boring to deal with. Only thing why you could want emperor praise is at start of the game where it can lower prices for islands but this become pointless in late game with millions of denari. Currently my greatest npc rival with army multiple times bigger than me is being marked as rebel and is practically destroyed by Emperor so all I have to do is just capture his islands for free of Emperor reputation costs. Also you get locked out of bigger gladiator games for having bad reputation, but only thing you get new from high reputation is "reroll" of specialists at traders so you can basically gamble with your denari. The reputation cannot be controlled proactively and only if Emperor decide to give you quests where he request some delivery or money, you can raise it a little bit by having the "empire approved" religions.  Core mechanic bugs in religion where you have to reset your global status to get the effect on newly captured islands.  Game is freezing often when building something, or just out of nowhere while fps is still 60+ so something is causing performance issues. I seen very strange things done by npc where they are rebuilding/destroying ground patterns in big amounts  Building ships without oars? You don't know what even trireme or quinquireme means right? This should not be optional  Quests are stupid and boring, basically mostly the same "finding waldo" as in 1800 but here, it's somehow even more difficult to find what is needed so I stopped doing them because the rewards are not really meaningful.

Makes me want to go play Anno 2205 or 1800 again rather than this.  Figured I'd paid far too much so I may as well play the campaign. It was 3 whole "Acts", then it just abruptly ends. Current game has 13 hours play time, a lot of that was spent just waiting for resources to build up because I didn't bother spamming out new islands, or waiting for research to end so I could make peace with a campaign character. Exceptionally disappointing, definitely not worth the full asking price. Wait for a heavy sale if you want to get it.

The Devs have done a great job with the Anno series, and this game is no different.  The innovations here feel natural and I'm enjoying them thoroughly. And I had my doubts about the Roman time period being fun because what I really wanted was a remastered Anno 1800 with Anno 117 features. And I still want that. But I am looking forward to see what DLC and modders bring forward to this game.     I will say though, that I am hoping that some day Anno games won't just magically teleport goods and population around on an island. It would help with realism and city development strategy, and I think it would be so much more fun, if this game could model traffic and transportation.

Here we go again, with people complaining about moot points on the Anno titles/series...  And before you give me the clown award (thanks for the points if you do), I'm not a Ubislop apologist. I'm a Blue Byte apologist.  For those unaware, the Campaign in Anno 1800 isn't that much longer, it took me slightly less to complete this one, maybe like 2 hours(?), as it is shorter, but the Campaign of this game is in pretty much the same vein as the previous title, and Anno 1800 is one of the best city builders out there, reviews plagued mostly by the "Ubislop launcher issues". It had Scenarios added at later dates through DLC that entered deeper in the in-universe lore. But honestly, barely anybody plays Anno titles for the story, most people want to see how high numbers can be pushed and how large or how beautiful can you make your city.  I won't tarnish Blue Byte's efforts on this franchise by leaving a review based on the publisher's dumb actions, because that's not relevant to the game itself and would spit in the face of the only studio that actually put decent titles from "The Ubliette", as I call it.  REGARDING THE GAME: Extremely solid mechanics and balancing, if we're comparing base game, 117 beats 1800 hands down. It's more feature rich, more balanced and honestly, setting things up around the isles is a lot more fun.  Production chains are not straightforward and actually require more thinking beyond the 2:1 or 1:2 or 4:1 ratios you would see in 1800. There's variety in how you choose to progress your islands and the way they dealt with public buildings IMO is really smart. 1800 had the issue of plastering services everywhere, here it's a bigger endeavor to build the buildings, but with a greater reward overall.  City building and setting things up is an absolute pleasure. Setting up the aqueducts is so satisfying, even more so that electricity in 1800, because of all the bonuses it provides. There's multiple uses for it, and it's not just a "double all production" button. There's another variation of streets from the usual dirt/brick/stone roads in this game, and they look absolutely gorgeous.  The return of the river slots and the universal mountain slots is also very welcome. Do you use one of the slots for another aqueduct? Do you squeeze another mine in the main island? How can you get the aqueduct to supply some of the mines as there's researches that give various bonuses. You need to account for the slope too because the heigh and the direction the water flows matter a lot. How are you gonna procure the gold for your Patricians? Where are you moving your Flour Mills or Tile production? Problems and solutions.  Which leads me to my next point - the research tree. God, I love it. I love progression trees in games ( I'm a Path of Exile player ). It actually has unique mechanics in it that will significantly alter how you build the city or organize things, and even if you unlock everything, there's nodes that you can keep researching repeatedly for various bonuses, so you're not gonna be "Well, I just unlocked everything. Nothing else to research, it's useless now."  AI. It's okay, it's pretty much the 1800 thing all over again, which is disappointing TBH, however, the pirates/raiders are actually pretty threatening now if unprepared. Good luck raiding their bases. Opponents are relatively easy to conquer if you know what you're dealing with, with the exception of the highest difficulty one and the retired soldier.  Visuals/performance are amazing. It actually runs better than 1800 while also looking better SOMEHOW. I've no idea what they've done, might be the lack of hugely inflated numbers not straining your CPU, but it runs ridiculously well even on older systems. Congratulations are in order in this regard. The game looks breathtaking IMO. I would often zoom in on my city and just take in how nice everything looks especially as you go into the higher tiers and start upgrading your roads. Kudos.  All in all, a great title in the franchise, might become my new favorite if the DLC is good. I am actually looking forward towards the upcoming DLC, btw. I've played 1800 for 2.5k hours, mostly because of mods and all the stuff the DLC adds. The game will have mod.io integrated at a later date, so longevity will increase further. And I'm not even done with 1800, I just swapped because this released but didn't get the chance to play properly until my Winter time off.  That said, buy on sale. BlueByte/Ubi Mainz deserves the money, The Ubliette does not.

Having played about 100 hours of this, I thought it was time to write my review.  Do I recommend this game? Currently No. Would I recommend this game in the future? Hopefully Yes.  My reasons: This game, gets boring very quickly. The Campaign is so dull and so badly written it felt like the intern or the writers 10 year old son wrote it. You do a few 'fetch' quests which provide nothing towards the story, you meet the Pirate yet do nothing to stop them, the Emperor shows up randomly and blames you(?) and sends you off to Albion in what is probably the worst story telling I have seen. You get to Albion, it then throws you head first into combat, conquering and military, without giving you ANY hints or tips or help. By 'conquering Albion' it also means just fight the local Pirate, who at first presented a huge challenge, but with a few ships and 4 units you can destroy her undefended, weak outposts and ruin the entire challenge. You then return to Rome as a hero, the Emperor praises you, doing a complete 180 on how he felt about you before and that's where the 'Story' ends. It puts you into normal 'Endless' mode with a few Ai opponents and you can just 'Play'.  The Campaign was and is awful and really not worth doing, I suggest people just jump straight into Endless and play that way.  Ai Opponents may as well not exist.  Dorian - doesn't expand quickly, doesn't go to Albion so doesn't go above tier 3 and is laughably the easiest and worst AI in any Anno game. Zara Nitu - Says she expands quickly, but only expanded to 2 islands and had to ask for my permission, very easy to beat, no threat. Tarragon - Can be a threat IF you anger him, but most of the time he settles 2-3 islands. If you do declare war on him, he will put up a light defense but equally be defeated. Concordia - She focuses on 'Roman Virtues' - doesn't do military or combat, doesn't expand much, very easy to defeat. No threat. Athr Iorgwyn - Only a stays in Albion, is suppose to be militaristc but doesn't bother you. Will be easily defeated if you have a Neferneru on. No threat. Neferneru - Broken AI if played on Hard or higher. Will declare war on you if you can't keep up, which with this game is VERY likely. Will knock out other opponents who don't stand a chance, has cheats involved allowing him to unlock things and build things quicker than you, making it hard to do anything but a war game with him as he will reach any pop or construction goal before you. The Emperor - Don't even attempt to fight the Emperor. it SHOULD be a fun challenge but it's not. In my game he arrived in both Albion and Latium with 30+ ships and about 70+ units. there was no way I could fight that.  Mechanics: You will spend most of the game fighting the city status and city mechanics. The city status is a BIG negative to your health, fire risk and happiness. This mean you cannot build massive cities as they will just struggle and suffer. What does this mean?  You will have to build small but wide. Expanding to different islands to build certain resources and then ship them back to your main island, all the while keeping the population class you need also happy on the new island. This game forces you with the City Status to build multiple big towns with maybe a slightly bigger city. Workforce is the bane for me in this game, everything requires workforce, higher tiers offer less workforce but offer more knowledge and income with the lower tier giving 50% towards your workforce but less income/knowledge. You can also place down buildings and add bonuses (like every Anno game) to add more, but in reality you need way more of the first tier than you do any others. This is difficult because the first tier workforce is the most needed for farms, military, and ships. This mean you either need to move your farming, ship building and military to an island which is solely first tier. The balancing just doesn't feel right, I don't like how I have to have an entire island dedicated to ship building and military, provide them with the resources they need and lose income because the tier 1 houses provide less income than the upkeep of military and ships.  The last major problem I have with this game is.....I got bored. I got bored very quickly. Once you build the colosseum that's kind of it. There are only 4 tiers of residence and none of the things needed by them are all that difficult to make and get. I ended up playing with mods very quickly because they just made the game more enjoyable and playable to a point, which isn't what I wanted or expected when I paid £75 for this game. It's such a shame as the Anno series is by far my FAVOURITE series, I have played hours and hours, days and weeks for these games, I love the Romans too so this was going to be epic. Yet, it's not.   I really, really hope they fix some of these issues, that they add more content, DLC which is free, because paying for DLC for this game in it's current state is a crime.

1. Overall Impression  Despite being quite stuffy and requiring a huge amount of manual micromanagement, the game is still very beautiful and genuinely interesting. You can clearly see strong ideas and ambitious systems under the surface, which makes the experience engaging—even when it becomes frustrating.  2. Negative Aspects  Co-op experience is painful Playing with a friend can take up to an hour just to join a cooperative game. Even if you’re lucky enough to connect, new problems begin immediately.  Critical co-op bugs  After a friend connects, the host loses the ability to pause the game for about 30 minutes.  The bug eventually fixes itself “miraculously,” but this shouldn’t happen at all.  Broken labor system  You can have huge cities with almost no available workforce.  Creating just 2–3 military units can push labor into negative values.  The system may show that only 2 out of 6 citizens are able to work.  With each new citizen tier, the number of able-bodied workers becomes even smaller, which feels completely illogical.  Overly manual resource management  Industrial and residential buildings must be combined, which is a cool idea in theory.  However, the reinforcements they provide only work while buildings are actively producing.  You must constantly monitor warehouses to avoid clogging.  There is no automation, such as auto-discarding excess resources when storage reaches (for example) 90%.  As a result, you are forced to manually manage everything, which becomes exhausting by mid-game.  Blueprint system is poorly implemented  When you copy a fully built city and try to place it on a new island as a blueprint, the game often does not allow it.  The reason is that roads are not included in the blueprint, and since the original roads are made of concrete, the game blocks placement due to a lack of resources — even though this is only a blueprint.  As a result, copying and reusing city layouts becomes unnecessarily restrictive and frustrating.  3. Positive Aspects  Visuals and atmosphere The game looks great and has a strong sense of atmosphere.  Innovative diplomacy system A very interesting diplomacy system that takes into account many different factors. This implementation feels fresh and well thought out.  Ship crafting system A new and enjoyable ship crafting system that allows you to choose which modules to install and which to leave out, giving real customization and strategic depth.  4. Final Thoughts, Hopes & Ideas  The core ideas of the game are strong, and the potential is clearly there. The main hope is that the developers will:  Fix critical co-op and gameplay bugs  Reduce excessive micromanagement  Add automation for repetitive and unnecessary manual actions  If these improvements are made, the game could become truly excellent instead of frustrating by the middle of a playthrough.

I will recommend this game even tho it has flaws.  To me this game is what Anno is about, islands, building population, production chains, solving happiness, fire safety and production chains, trade routes. Difference from Anno 1404 for example is addition of workforce, you actually need certain amount of population on each island to work in production chains so you need to build a small settlement everywhere. I also like Albion islands, architecture and mechanics. So far I have 24h played and I simply enjoy it, the feeling, the music, the aesthetics of the game.  Stuff that I do not like is the campaign. Campaign is 2 acts, that are very shallow, not even much of a tutorial. Act 1 is Latium, Act 2 is Albion and then you are transported back to Latium and get game over screen and then can continue playing in endless mode. I would have liked knowing that before because you continue using your old Latium islands and ones you made in Albion and I would have prepared for that. I liked Anno 1404 campaign and those 8 levels with multiple missions and secret missions. But I am more of an endless player so its not end of the world for me.  Another thing that I do not like is that Albion is a separate map from Latium. Its not like in 1404 where in the north were Occidental islands and in the south were Oriental islands. No here its one map only Latium islands, and 2nd map is Albion. You unlock Albion when you progress to Equites (3rd stage of Latium) and ships have literally to go off-map and then have some transfer time until they are in Albion (i guess geographically this makes sense since you are kinda going from Mare Nostrim to North Sea but cmon who cares about history realism in an island building semi fantasy game. Its tedious because sometimes I forget I have Albion map (and vice versa) and that I have to care about stuff there and you literally have loading screen when switching maps.  But maybe that is just a skill issue until I get used to it working like that. Would have liked one huge map with both biomes together but maybe we get it later who knows.  I do not want to talk about Ubisoft DLC practices that can ruin any game they make.

The most important thing I can say is Ignore almost all of the bad reviews. This game is great and plays like a mix of the originals with new features that Anno 1800 brought. The small bugs at launch have been fixed. You have the ability to build complex cities or turn it into a great RTS for combat. Combat is a hybrid of 1503 and 1404 and is very enjoyable, immersive, and can be scaled to huge land and sea battles that take strategy to win. You can make the game as easy or as difficult as you want for your play style, building habits, or desire for combat and conquest.

I really wanted to recommend this game, but I cannot. I love the Anno series and Roman history, so this looked like a perfect game for me. Unfortunately, this game needs a lot of work before it really is playable. The campaign isn't a campaign, it's a tutorial that ends too soon. 8 hours in and the campaign is over, just dumping you into a sandbox game. There's so many storylines in the campaign that are left unresovled that it feels rushed and unfinished. The diagonal building leads to some really interesting looking cities but it doesn't actually help in gameplay, especially since the size of the grid changes when you're in diagonal mode. I also think the game has too many complicated systems. Requiring specific work forces from specific tiers of population changes the emphasis of the series. Instead of having the focus on production chains and building complicated trade routes between islands, you have to juggle independent colonies on each island. I always found the core of fun in the Anno series to be designing the complicated trade routes to feed my massive main city. Yes, you'd have to ship construction materials around to build up production islands, and that was always a bit annoying. But now, the problem is much bigger because you need to ship around construction materials for all the housing and supporting buildings in addition to the production buildings. I think this game has forgotten what made the series great and has just added things to complicate it that don't actually add fun to the gameplay. Final gripe, there's no scenarios in the game. There's just the very short tutorial that's called a campaign and then just free open play. There was so much room here to do more. Hopefully they'll fix things and make this game interesting, but honestly after playing for 15-20 hours, I feel no need to come back to it.

Time played: ~19 hours  I gave this game a fair chance. After 12 hours I was bored, so I played another 7 hoping the systems would click. They did not.  The core problem is immersion. Pacing is slow, and the game is not intuitive. City growth feels mechanical, and key systems are poorly explained. A clear example: I am stuck because I cannot figure out how to produce rope. There is no clear in-game encyclopedia, tech tree, or production reference showing required buildings or inputs. Rather than planning strategically, I am guessing and hunting through menus, which stalls progress.  By comparison, Age of Empires, Rome: Total War, and Workers & Resources: Soviet Republic make production chains clear and decisions meaningful.     Absolutely no immersion.  At $50+, I do not recommend this game.
