Edited for more detailed review:  🧑‍🤝‍🧑 Multiplayer (Unacceptable) - this has somehow gotten even worse this past month!!! •	Fails to join session ~4 out of 5 times. •	Still broken even after hotfix. •	This should have been fixed before release. •	Absolutely shameful for a full-price game.  ❌ Deeply Disappointed After 50 Hours – A Huge Step Back from Anno 1800 Veteran Anno player here – hundreds of hours in 1700 and 1800. I wanted to love this. After ~50 hours, I’m honestly just frustrated, disappointed, and bored. This game does not hook you, feels unfinished, buggy, poorly balanced, and lacks the artistic beauty, clarity, and polish that made previous Anno games special.  🧭 Interface & Information Design (Major Regression) The biggest problem: you constantly don’t know what’s going on. •	UI is a massive downgrade from Anno 1800 (even 1800 at release was far superior). •	Budget, prestige, and balance screens are confusing, shallow, and unhelpful. •	No quick navigation:    o	No button from production buildings straight to stats (already solved in 1800).    o	Every tab dumps you back into the production overview for no reason. •	Trade routes:    o	You can’t see routes or ships on hover anymore.    o	You must click into each route (why remove an existing QoL feature?). •	Attack strength, tower damage, troop power? Not visible or incomplete. Basic info missing. •	Warehouse / fire / police units:    o	You can’t track where units are.    o	Firefighting/police often disappears from the screen entirely. This game is far too complex to have such a weak interface.  🎨 Visuals, Colors & Artistic Direction (Shockingly Bland) Where did the beauty go? •	Extremely bland color palette – everything is yellow/beige. There is a design tool, but it's very limited as of now. •	Residences, warehouses, spinners, pileus, production buildings all look the same. •	Warehouses look identical across tiers. •	Decorations and artwork feel limited, uninspired, and lifeless. •	Festivals:    o	Just sad icons and dull effects.    o	In 1800, festivals felt special. Here they feel like a 2000s mobile game. •	Snow effect:    o	Released completely untested.    o	Made the game nearly unplayable and hurt your eyes.    o	Hotfixed later, but this should never have reached release.  🏗️ Mechanics & Systems (Simplified, Broken, or Unrewarding) Aqueducts vs Railroads •	Aqueducts are way too simplistic. •	Zero challenge, zero depth. •	Railroads in 1800 were vastly superior. Area Effects vs Road Effects (Huge Clarity Issue) •	Sometimes buildings use circles. •	Sometimes circles apply to roads. •	Sometimes only roads apply. •	Sometimes both. •	The game never explains this properly, and visual feedback is inconsistent. •	AREA EFFECT DO NOT SCALE WITH HIGHER POP TIERS. This makes the whole balance very weird. Highlighting & Effects •	No clear overview of which buildings are affected by what. •	Trade unions in 1800 gave clear green outlines. •	Here you must click every building individually. Tedious and opaque.  📦 "Trade Union" Items System (Underwhelming & Confusing) •	Items are hard to navigate. •	Too many items that feel nearly identical. •	Nothing feels game-changing, epic, or legendary. •	No “wow” moments at all. •	Inventory management is frustrating instead of exciting (what is the "minimum stock" option for? Tooltips needed).  ⚔️ Combat (Good Idea, Poor Execution) I was excited about walls and troops… until I used them. •	Troops behave strangely. •	You can’t clearly see if they’re attacking.    o	You literally have to watch an archer’s hands to see if he’s firing. •	Reinforcement mechanics are not explained. •	Walls:    o	No clear indication if there’s a gap or not.    o	In 1700, this was obvious. •	Ships:    o	No red attack icon when hovering over targets.    o	Hard to tell what you’re engaging. •	Ship customization is nice, but:    o	Very limited ship variety.  🐛 Bugs, Bugs, Bugs •	Building ranges randomly stop showing (green overlay disappears). •	Aqueducts randomly claim “water can’t flow uphill”:    o	Build two paths and it works.    o	Replicate the same incline with one road and it doesn’t.    o	Clearly bugged and inconsistent. •	Marble roads:    o	Same as tier 2 roads?    o	Radius visuals often missing or broken. •	Firefighting visuals disappear. •	Quest ship spam after loading a game (12 ships spawning suddenly). This feels early access, not a finished product.  🎯 Progression, Balance & Endgame (Fundamentally Broken) •	I reached full needs for all populations within ~13 hours. •	Endgame arrives way too fast. •	It feels like the only goal in this game is to build high population. This is not Factorio.  •	The game feels complex for the wrong reasons (confusing UI), not meaningful systems. •	You can buy EVERYTHING and neutrals will instantly supply it. I didn't even need to settle a third island and I still got all the raw materials I needed. That's too OP.  Gold & City Design (Game-Breaking) •	It is objectively better to NOT build production buildings. •	Flat +2 gold effects:    o	Do not scale with population.    o	Tier 1 houses get the same benefit as Tier 4. •	Best strategy:    o	Cram houses everywhere.    o	Ignore mixed-use city design entirely. •	This completely destroys the incentive to build interesting cities. •	Area/road effects + gold balance are fundamentally flawed. Fire Risk, Happiness & Prestige •	Fire risk:    o	Always massively negative despite fire stations everywhere.    o	Few actual fires → constant red warning anyway.    o	No way to meaningfully get positive fire stats. •	Happiness:    o	Weirdly hard to keep positive. •	Prestige:    o	Almost meaningless beyond villa slots.    o	Removing influence was good, but this system feels hollow.  📜 Quests & Events (Terrible) •	Quests are inconsequential and poorly explained. •	Rewards unclear. •	Hard to track. •	Random event spam feels chaotic and untested. •	“Choose” events:    o	Sad UI.    o	Temporary buffs/debuffs that feel boring and joyless.    o	Choosing feels worse than attending a funeral.  🎼 Music & Atmosphere (Another Downgrade) •	Anno 1800 had a stunning soundtrack. •	This music:    o	Not memorable.    o	Not even distinctly Roman.    o	Feels generic and lifeless.  ❓ Missing Content & Odd Choices •	No Tier 5 population on release – seriously? •	Diagonal building:    o	Nice idea.    o	Creates awkward half-tile gaps.    o	More frustration than benefit.  🧾 Final Verdict This game: •	❌ Feels unfinished •	❌ Is riddled with bugs •	❌ Has poor balance •	❌ Lacks visual clarity and beauty •	❌ Removes or downgrades existing QoL features •	❌ Fails to capture the magic of previous Anno titles  Anno 1800 was beautiful, deep, challenging, and satisfying. This feels like a rushed, early-access prototype with a Roman skin.  Strongly disappointed. I genuinely wish they had delayed this by several months and released a polished, complete experience instead.

Really promising game with some refreshing elements compared to anno 1800. Has a good 20 hour of gameplay to reach endgame. For veteran players the game feels like it has a lack of content. Also the AI players are very passive and will never declare war on you.    PRO's  - Refreshing revamp of the game compared to ANNO 1800 with new city tiers and challenges to face.  - Good 20 hour of fresh gameplay and maybe more depending how much you care about beauty building or efficiency.  - Campaign is good  - New combat system with ships and land combat is nice.  - In-game music is fantastic and really makes me happy and blast my speakers.    CONS  - AI is very passive and doesn't prove much of a challenge other than just taking all the islands. This makes harder difficulties just a longer playthrough due to less money, rather than more difficult.  - Multiple mechanics demotivate you from trying to build big and play a long run:  1. City status effects provide massive fire or happiness debuffs which cannot really be countered in any way. No matter how much fire stations or hospitals you have, you will be deep negative.  2. 90% of the specialists feel useless as + 1 happiness or +1 fire status radius doesn't change much for your town. Anno1800 had some broken ones, but there must be a fine balance between the two.  3.  Island layout is very repeatable, not much variation, so essentially you keep building the same town.    I'm expecting most of these issues will be fixed in the future through DLC's to create a new epic game of the likes of Anno 1800. Definitely recommend this game to other people who want to have a chill city builder with some epic music.

I found the UI to be rather enjoyable, though some might take issue to the use of Gen AI for art which I noticed in the menu for the pirates.   TL:DR give this game sometime to bake, if you enjoy Anno 1800 you'll like this and you may be deterred by the Gen AI which is fair tbh.

Anno Pax is the first game in years that has truly pulled me in. I’m completely hooked.  As a former AoE and classic The Settlers player (the older titles), this hits exactly the right spot for me and honestly, even more than I expected.  I enjoy RTS elements, especially when ships are involved, but what really sells this game is the slow, methodical pacing combined with deep and demanding production chains. It feels like old-school Settlers design pushed much further: slower, more complex, more layered – and incredibly satisfying.  At the time of writing this review, I have just under 30 hours played, and I still feel like I have a lot left to explore and learn. There is so much going on beneath the surface. Whether that’s because the game genuinely has a huge amount of content, or because the progression is intentionally slow and thoughtful, I’m not even sure – but the result is the same: the game keeps unfolding instead of burning out.  There’s always something to optimize, expand, or rethink. It’s not a game you rush. It’s a game you sink into.  This is actually my first Anno game, so I can’t compare it directly to Anno 1800 or earlier titles – but judged purely on my experience here, this is an outstanding city-builder focused on patience, planning, and long-term satisfaction.  If you enjoy complex production chains, slow progression, and a strategy game that rewards thinking ahead rather than speed, this is an easy recommendation.

Recommended 👍 Anno: Pax Romana is exactly the kind of strategy game I didn’t realize I was missing. It captures the feeling of building something meaningful inside a living, breathing Roman world rather than just pushing numbers or painting the map. What really stands out is the atmosphere. The cities feel grounded and authentic, the pacing is calm but purposeful, and there’s a strong sense of order and civilization behind every mechanic. It’s not about constant war or frantic micromanagement — it’s about stability, planning, and long-term vision. That alone makes it refreshing compared to many modern strategy games. The economic and city-building systems are deep without being overwhelming. You’re encouraged to think like a Roman administrator: balancing growth, logistics, infrastructure, and public order. Small decisions matter, and watching a settlement slowly evolve into a thriving Roman city is genuinely satisfying. Visually, the game is clean and atmospheric. It doesn’t rely on flashy effects, but the art direction fits the era perfectly and reinforces the calm, methodical tone of the gameplay. Sound design and music quietly support the experience without becoming distracting. Overall, Anno: Pax Romana feels like a thoughtful, mature strategy game that respects the player’s intelligence. If you enjoy city builders, historical settings, and slower, more deliberate gameplay focused on construction rather than conquest, this is absolutely worth your time. 🟢 A solid, immersive experience — and a welcome change of pace. As much as I loved Anno 1800 and loved the premise of Anno 117, my sentiments echo many of the other reviewers who have already posted. The game at launch is unfinished with an abrupt end to the campaign that barely feels like it was patched together, but rather abandoned entirely. The advisor who worked alongside me my entire campaign reintroduced himself as my "new" advisor right after it ended. I didn't even want to keep playing because it felt like I'd just wasted my time!   Small bugs are littered throughout: river slots that will take my resources but won't register the building itself, quests that I can't turn in, etc... Some immersion-breaking assets are recycled from Anno 1800, namely the pirate skull and matchlocks.   Worst of all, Ubisoft used generative AI in the development of art and assets. What are human beings doing here on earth if not creating art? Even folks who don't consider themselves 'artists' are always leaving their mark in little ways on whatever tasks they pursue. I'd happily pay the price of the game for beautiful human-made art like that in Anno 1800, but to know that even some of the works for this game were generated by AI is so disheartening. Trading items lack a clear and accessible overview, and planning trade routes feels more cumbersome and less intuitive than in Anno 1800. As a result, the overall user experience suffers, especially during more complex economic phases. The story also feels rather flat and tends to drag at times.    I hope that upcoming patches will improve the story and refine the overall user interface.

I need to preface this by saying I have never, EVER, cared for/played a campaign mode in Anno in the past 15 years+ playing this franchise. So if that's why you're thinking of buying, you can skip past my review. I would absolutely recommend this game if you're a lover of the classic building/production line aspect of the Anno series. Don't get me wrong, there are a couple of cons to the game but the added pros far outweigh them.   I want to get the cons out of the way: The UI is clunky and the graphical requirements are insanely heavy. The graphics I can understand because it is by far the most detailed game of the series from a graphics standpoint, the up-close detail is just STUNNING. However, I have a relatively very nice PC and Anno 117 is by far one of the most stressful games on my PC. And then... UI... gosh the UI definitely seems like they tried to unify it with console and even after the dozens of hours I have put in, I still struggle with it. The way things are organized is very unintuitive, and I encourage the devs to put in the work to shake it up some more and improve it.  HOWEVER, the PROS!!! Despite my two big cons, the game itself really does expand upon the refined (even perfected) experience they introduced with Anno: 1800, the added features and gameplay capabilities really steal the show here. Of course, the major thing is the diagonal roads. Once you get a hang of them, they are a joy to play around with and really let your mind thats conditioned with previous installments really work and give you the opportunity to build cities that stun even more than previously possible. And they provide opportunities for production layouts that people will likely be coming up with for a long time.        The new needs system, where individual goods or services provide concurring needs and different bonuses based on what you give your people is a VERY nice addition. I found myself seeking out specific goods to fill the needs for specific islands, and give a really nice layer of depth to the game that I didn't previously realize I wanted. Maybe I just wanted to get more money, maybe my people are super unhappy, or maybe I desperately need more workforce for my productions islands. Letting each service and good provided determine which one of those areas are fulfilled is something I enjoy playing around with.        And then comes beliefs and knowledge! I am very happy they decided to bring back technology trees first introduced in the orbital expansion for Anno 2205. Technology research is an essential part of the game, providing bonuses/upgrades, and providing new buildings gives the game another layer of depth you didn't know it needed. But it has it's own counterparts- patron beliefs. Patron gods are a new addition to the series. Each island can now have a patron god assigned to it. They provide bonuses based on which one you choose (based on classical Roman gods) and many are unlocked through the research trees.        Land combat makes a return in this game, and this is one of those areas I don't much more so I haven't really toyed around with it. All I will say is that the workforce requirements for maintaining an army and ships are very high, and I think that it does need to be tweaked.       Overall, I would definitely recommend a game if you're a fan of the series in general. I think the team really took the leap from Anno 1800, which aimed to solidify the core game experience, to trying to build upon that foundation. I understand why people say that the depth feels lacking, because the game attempts to create an even experience between both basegame provinces (Rather than 1800 that hyperfixates on the main one and leaves the new world as a colony expansion) But they have genuinely created a great base here and I look forward to what comes in the future.

Anno 117 has that sort of feel that says hello with a smile while secretly plotting to devour your whole evening.  So, with a background in playing strategy games but a complete newbie in Anno, I thought that I might be in for a brutal learning curve. What I didn't see Anno 117 delivering is a refreshingly chill learning curve, which begins with make a town, keep your people fed, but don't go broke, thank you very much... Then, BAM!, You're on your tenth production line and one resource shortage from Armageddon.  The themes from Rome are really effective. It is a great thing to see the roads come alive with activity, ships transporting trade, and your town gradually change from tiny coastal outpost to respectable province of the empire. The game is clearly encouraging the player to feel proud of themselves when things are going well, but outrage when things go poorly. That said the character development could’ve been a little better.  There is a lot going on, but the UI is very good at keeping you informed of why things are going wrong, rather than letting them go wrong silently. The errors that are made are very much your own doing, which is not fun, but in a just one more tweak, and I’ll be golden sort of way.  Despite that, Anno 117 is not a game that is entirely flawless. Pacing can temporarily drop when the expansion curve really begins to snowball, with micromanagement making an appearance sooner than you might have anticipated. This might sound like a nightmare for someone who hates juggling multiple islands, for everyone else, it’s the good kind of chaos.  Anno 117 is, on the whole, a game that feels deep but not mean, complicated but not heartless. It’s a city-builder game that encourages careful planning, patience, and a sense of humour when your empire implodes because, say, you'd forgotten about sandals or wine, which is apparently what the Romans lived on.

Anno 117 is a very fun game, i love the core gameplay. Im building my main island full of patricians at the moment, i love beauty building so thats what ive been chipping away at for the last couple of days/weeks.   I’ll start with what i enjoy compared to what i am used to from anno 1800.  I enjoy the production chains and the new mechanics of managing fire safety, happiness etc.   I also enjoy the radius-based effects at the end of a supply chain, it really makes you incorporate the production buildings into the city itself.   Furthermore, building has been revolutionised as well with diagonal roads and you can also click a building to surround it with road tiles.  The removal of infulence replaced by land tax is a very welcome QOL feature that really makes it easier to have many islands settled for your production.  Though i do enjoy the game, i do have some pet peeves with it.   -I miss specialists that change production chains or supplies goods when a different good is also supplied, like the costume designer and actor from anno 1800  -There is a scarce amount of ornaments making beautifying my city difficult or very alike to different regions in my city.  -Albion, though its interesting is rather stale, i prefer Latium. It has a fun concept, but having to reach the end of one population to be able to start with another feel counterproductive since the eques tier requires both cheese and brooches which are in different tiers in albion, so either you reach tier 3 in albion and then branch to the different population, or you settle a second island. The weather, though immersive is a bit loud and obnoxious to look at too.  -I miss statistic menus like the specialist menu that allow you to see all the specialists in the game and the way to obtain them. Along with the trade menu that allows you to see profit margins and amount of time between two posts.  Even though i miss these features, the game is still very enjoyable with vivid characters and amazing graphics. Its really not fair yet to compare this game to anno 1800 which has had 6 years of updates and DLC to add to it. That being said, i cant wait for the upcoming DLC’s and the interesting things it will add onto the game.

IF YOURE FAMILIAR WITH ANNO: its basically the typical ANNO mechanics, Roman style. Almost everything is going to feel familiar, from the HUD to economy mechanics. Even the multiple region mechanic makes a return, where some resources can only be found in respective regions. Ship combat and mechanics are still basically the same, and the addition of land military units is fairly well implemented. The individual soldiers move independently when engaged to form and fill the frontline. OVERALL: I think its well done, and a very good starting point for further polish, refinement, and DLC.   IF YOURE UNFAMILIAR WITH ANNO: Anno is a very chill city building economy game. There are combat elements, but most of the gameplay loop is economy and city building. Starting off with basic supplies, you gradually build up a small settlement, meeting the basic needs of the plebs; mainly food and clothing. As you upgrade and move your citizens up the socio-economic ladder, they demand more foods, clothing, and goods. If you enjoy a deeper -but not wildly burdensome- economy game, consider checking the ANNO series out.    Overall: a solid game and starting point for a new Anno entry. Graphics are solid, animations are well done, and I haven't run into any bugs or issues yet.

I’ll start with the negatives, because they do matter: the quest achievements are currently bugged and don’t spawn even when requirements are met, which is frustrating. Also players shouldn’t have to datamine files just to figure out what triggers an achievement, the game should communicate this clearly. I really hope this gets fixed soon, because right now it leaves a rough impression.    That said, I honestly think the mixed reviews don’t reflect the actual quality of the game. Anno 117 is not Anno 1800, and that’s completely fine. It has its own vibe, its own pacing, and its own charm. Once you start building, the game really shines. The city-building feels smooth, the production chains are fun to set up, I personally am okay with them removing the OP Trade Union from 1800, while I enjoyed it a lot, I still have 1800 to go back to, and this is different. The overall atmosphere pulls you in, and the new mechanics keep things refreshing without losing the classic Anno feel.    If the devs fix the achievement issues and polish a few rough edges, I think a lot more people will see what’s already here: a very good Anno game with tons of potential. For me, it’s absolutely worth playing and I’m excited to see how it develops.

Pax Romana surprised me in the best way. The art direction is gorgeous: cities, landscapes and units all have a lot of detail, and the atmosphere (music, lighting, weather) makes it very easy to just sit and watch your provinces.  The campaign. It’s much deeper than I expected. You constantly have meaningful choices to make:  diplomacy ? all-out war ? compromise ? crush ? Having two different characters/perspectives to play with adds another layer, because the same situation can feel very different depending on who you are and what you’re trying to achieve. Because of that, the campaign is actually replayable.   If you like Roman history (just like I do!), strong atmosphere and campaign modes where your choices really matter, Pax Romana is absolutely worth your time. This is a game I strongly recommend !

I’ve had two runs in Pax Romana: First, the main campaign, and then a run on endless. The core of what’s here is wonderful.  –  I love all the tiny details they stuffed into the game. Citizens wander around the cities you make for them, going for a swim in the baths, attending to their religion in temples and shrines, and so on. You can encamp your military units, and zoom in and find that the camps actually differ based on what type of unit is encamped. Ships’ appearance reflects their capabilities, featuring arrow towers or shields or rows of oars.  As a city building game, it’s exceptionally complete. There are all sorts of things you can build: lots of different fields (hemp, grain, lavender), manufacturies (soap, garum, amphorae), and all sorts of urban buildings (temples, aqueducts, shops). You can make a visually splendorous Roman city, and live that fantasy brewing in your mind since you walked the streets of Pompeii. (Or perhaps that’s just me?)  To cap it all off, you can build the Colosseum, that humongous ancient amphitheater that you absolutely should not write on if you visit Rome. It takes tons of resources and several stages to complete, and when done it’s essentially a whole new tech tree that opens up - representing the gladiatorial games that can be hosted there.  Like all Anno games, the idea is to start with farmers and work your way up to increasingly sophisticated urbanities. If you fail to meet their needs, they’ll leave the island, potentially throwing your production chains into chaos.   There are tons and tons of choices for how and what to build. There’s a lot of gameplay here, far – far – more than a traditional city builder like Cities Skylines, or even more gameplay focused ones like Frostpunk. Buildings are shaped in all sorts of weird ways, and some have bonuses in a circular aura that can’t grow, and some give bonuses via road connections. Then there’s the Governor’s Villa, which can be slotted with Officials that give their own crazy bonuses, some of which give aura bonuses to connected buildings.  This can get complex. For example, I had an official that gave a big aura bonus to markets, meaning I wanted the edge of the official’s circular aura to touch as many useful markets as I could, extending a bonus even further still.  The game is like a huge Tetris puzzle, trying to fit all these bonuses in some sort of optimal way. It’s fun. It’s also a little time consuming, and maybe a bit too much. I found myself endlessly tweaking my main city, then realizing I’d been doing it for a whole hour and had done little else.  –  We have to talk about the campaign.   Pax Romana has the best campaign of a game like this since Frostpunk, until it reaches the final act. Until then, the story was challenging, serious, interesting – a far cry from Anno 1800’s whimsical fire-people conspiracy plot. But for some reason, it just suddenly ends.  Marcia’s story drew me in. A young girl sold into a marriage she doesn’t want, to a man she has never met, and thrust into a dangerous political game. Several villains are set up, including a new and dangerous Emperor. But sadly, instead of that Emperor, say, invading your main island and having a climatic battle, the campaign just unceremoniously ends and endless mode begins. There’s not even a dialog box saying the campaign is over - I eventually just realized I wasn’t getting any more quests and had to google it.  It’s a real shame. They did a great job with the story, but then mysteriously just didn't follow through. It also ends a good deal before you reach the higher tier of citizen needs, unlock the Colosseum, or have any serious battles. I would have loved to see the game prompt you to explore those things while supplying you an interesting story.  –  Anno Pax Romana adds ground combat for the first time. It’s not bad. They’ve drawn inspiration from the Total War games, and I enjoyed trying to build armies with a well-rounded unit composition. I also enjoyed defending my islands at war. You now need a combination of naval defense and land defense, and it’s definitely fun building towers and walls in your city - and balancing the needs of a well-connected city with the practical considerations of how expensive gates are to build and maintain.  What doesn’t work for me about combat, though, is the diplomacy and war system. I’m very glad to see a new (I think) Limited War option, allowing you to say, “Hey, I just want to fight your military; I don’t want either of us to fight our trade ships.” That’s a huge improvement over Anno 1800’s endless babysitting of your trade ships.  But it’s not good enough given the extra attention you need to pay to both naval and land fights. I try to play all games on “hard” or equivalent, Pax Romana being no exception, and so played my Endless game on the default “Veteran” difficulty. But even a “Limited War” means being outnumbered 10 to 1 and having to defend everywhere, all at once, all the time, across all my islands, in two game worlds separated by a loading screen. This is too much, even for me. It’s a not-fun amount of multitasking.  Also, AI makes alliances with each-other, meaning it’s essentially a war of you against everyone. (Having an alliance of your own doesn’t seem to make this better: AI chooses their alliances over you.)  What would be better is to allow players to declare the intention to fight over one specific island, allowing that one island to be attacked and invaded - but no others. The casus belli system from Paradox games could be imported here: First manufacture a claim on an island, then press the claim. In this way, wars wouldn’t be quite so out of control, so sprawling and difficult to wrap your head around. You could just focus on one theater - one island - at a time. Alas, this is not how it works.  –  There’s a new religion and technology system that I don’t recall from an Anno game before. It’s deep, and I like it.  Each island can be set to follow a specific deity, which provides specific types of bonuses. You could, in theory, use this to hyper specialize islands into particular production chains, or even sources of manpower for your fleet or military, or even make one island a source of trade. Though, I wasn’t able to make that happen in my games. I think religion could use a bit more cooking, personally. I do enjoy the flavor of it.  The technology tree is fantastic, though. It’s huge, and even now I find myself cooking about how I could pull off a faster opening in my next game. There are tons of nodes, and some are locked behind little achievements in the game, such as settling a Celt island or setting up a trade route. Some are very powerful, unlocking new tiers of ships for example.  –  I think Pax Romana is fantastic. It’s a little too addictive, frankly, as I’ve been up til 1 AM playing it several nights in a row. That said, the missing final act of Marcia’s campaign feels bad.  Then again, it’s Rome, and the Rome setting is very fun. I visited Pompeii with my wonderful boyfriend a couple years ago, and we had a fantastic time. The site is wonderful. It felt like walking through history, and I wished I could play a game that let me build a city like Pompeii. Well, now I can. And it’s awesome.  Recommended.

Anno 117: Pax Romana is a very solid game and a worthy new entry to the Anno francise. There are many very valid points to critique, but all in all I can still recommend buying this game.    As for details:  The biggest negative point to be aware of is the somewhat borked multiplayer. Playing the game with one person in coop is fine for the most part aside from a couple desyncs here and there (about one every 3-4ish hours). However should you get more people for a coop session the desyncs increase to a very annoying degree. They are working on fixing the issues and we have already seen some improvements in terms of stability.    There is also the use of AI which everybody needs to decide for themselves if its a dealbreaker or not. Personally I dont like it, but I am not writing the game off entirely for that.    The new features however are very nice. Production buildings giving buffs to surrounding residences makes planning cities and supplying them very interesting. The aqueduct system is very nice and feels much nicer to work with than the trains in Anno 1800. The techtree feels nice and gives another way to progress aside from just leveling up your residents. The belief system is neat, but I havent gotten around to toy with it alot, sofar mostly picked Ceres by default. Building diagonally is neat, but not that big of a deal for me, some more artistically gifted people may disagree.     As it stands atleast the basegame is definitly worth getting in my opinion.
Campaing is too short.  But love the atmosphere and the music of the game!  Overall fun.

Akhirnya ada game Ubilembut yang bagus rilis di tahun ini. Setelah 43 jam main game ini, aku berani jamin beli game ini worth it banget buat kalian yang suka sama game dengan genre yang satu ini. Aku merupakan newbie dari franchise ini sehingga mungkin reviu ini tidak relevan buat para veteran.     Buat aku yang newbie tentu meragukan untuk beli game ini pada awalnya. Beberapa alasannya adalah karena game anno itu sendiri yang selalu identik dengan game yang rumit dan memusingkan, belum lagi genre game seperti ini cukup demanding dari segi perangkat karena visualnya yang indah banget. Aku yang udah pake laptop dengan RTX5070Ti pun mengalami lag di mid to end game saat kota kita udah sangat padat penduduknya.     Dari segi gameplaynya cukup newbie friendly. Awal2 main kita bisa memilih untuk dipandu sama sistemnya dengan berbagai tutorial yang dapat kita baca pada saat awal2 game. Memasuki pertengah game, kita akan mulai dipusingkan dengan micromanajemen resource, populasi, bahkan sampe ngatur posisi damkar, polisi, dan RS untuk menanggulangi bencana hahaha. Semuanya simple dan gak rumit kok, bahkan untuk aku yg newbie ini. Selain manajerial kota, kita juga diberikan sedikit intermezo di dalam game seperti adanya perang-perang kecil baik dari pasukan darat ataupun perang kapal di laut. Walaupun tidak semasif game RTS seperti Total War dan Age of Empires, perang di game ini sudah cukup untuk memberikan penyegaran disaat kita lelah mengatur dan menata kota hahaha.    Akhir kata game Anno 117: Pax Romana ini merupakan game terbaik yang pernah kubeli di tahun 2025 ini dan tentunya fullprice gaess. Sumpah dah buat kalian yang suka game RTS pasti suka sama game 1 ini.

Anno 117 is an excellent game with beautiful visuals, deep city-building mechanics, and impressive historical detail. The gameplay is engaging and full of strategic depth, making every minute enjoyable.  However, the game is missing one very important feature: ((((((  Arabic language support  ))))))  The Arabic-speaking gaming community is huge, and many players who love strategy games – especially the Anno series – struggle to fully enjoy the experience without proper localization. Anno relies heavily on reading, management, and understanding detailed menus, so having Arabic supported would make a massive difference.  I truly hope Ubisoft adds Arabic localization (UI, menus, and dialogues) in a future update. It would open the game to a much larger audience and make the experience more accessible and enjoyable for millions of players.  Great game overall, but Arabic support is strongly needed.

Wow -- The negativity is so unwarranted! People are nitpicking what's otherwise a fantastic game. Some thoughts:  Yes, you have to use Ubisoft's launcher. It failed at first and I had to reinstall it, then it worked and I never thought about it again. No big deal, get over it guys.   Yes the campaign ends abruptly because its *not finished*. The devs have acknowledged this and there's more coming, not to mention more campaigns in DLC's. Also Marcia's campaign is better than Marcus. These days everything comes out early release, so the fact it's practically complete is refreshing if anything!  Yes some loading screens use AI images... I have zero idea why anyone cares about this, you spend 0.05% of your gaming time looking at them. What a ridiculous reason to downvote this game.  The graphics are *stunning* and the attention to detail is lovely. It also runs beautifully, even when I zoom out all the way (and there's a mod to zoom out further). My rig is pretty new though, so YMMV. I expected not to like the religion aspect but its well implemented and also you can turn off or adjust practically anything in the difficulty menu.   I'm a fan of Roman history so this time period was a pleasant surprise. I've also always wanted some RTS elements in the game and the naval and ground battles really do scratch that itch. Naval warfare is great, and ground warfare is just good enough to add some spice to the game when you're tired of just trading and expanding.   There are a couple areas for improvement that I suspect are coming. First, the UI could use some QOL improvements... there's numerous opportunities to embed more information in certain views which would save time switching screens to find what you need. Personally, I would appreciate a customizable inventory bar rather than a tiny window on the bottom right you have to scroll through constantly. Of course I would have liked to see more rivals and maps in various biomes (the Roman empire spanned from Spain to Persia, afterall), but that looks like it'll be in DLC which is fine. But these are nitpicks, really.   Do yourself a favor and don't get spooked by the negative reviews. The game is a 9/10 IGN score and lives up to every bit of that. Enjoy.

I can't even call it a reskin because the skins look the same unless you zoom in very close. 2. The UI is mediocre and calculating how much of each good you need per trade route is basically impossible. 3. The AI just sucks. The computer controlled govenors don't do anything approaching what a human player would do. Wouldn't it be great if the AI played by the same rules and couldn't advance without certain resources, just like the player? Wouldn't it be great if the AI TRADED the player for resources it didn't have? Such a bad system. 4. Independent traders have the same goods at the same price every game, for the entire game, making things feel stagnant. The quests are also pretty bad. AI players all have the same resources in the same quantity at the same price regardless of game conditions. Really inadequate system. 5. I heard that the first expansion will have a big island like Cape T. Tbh I hated that map because it's one more map to manage, but adds nothing new because it's the same pop/resources as the original map.

It's fun, though I feel there's an issue with mid to late game balancing. No matter what I do, even in the easiest setting, I wind up screwing the pooch economically for one reason or another with no apparent hope of redemption. Still need to play the campaign mode all the way through, so I can't speak to the many controversies mentioned in its regard as of yet.  If you're a previous Pax player and into citybuilders / economic simulation and warfare AND you have the money to blow, I'd say you might as well. If you're none of the above, are living on a tight gaming budget, or have a better idea of what to play as an alternative, don't waste your money unless it comes on a major sale or developers fix the balancing issues at some point in the future. This is a VERY tentative 'recommend.'

Classic Anno experience in the endless game with all the improvements of the predecessors, a new exciting setting, and a much bigger military part. Can very much recommend.  As for the many negative reviews: the game is as amazing as the Annos that came before. I don’t have any game-breaking bugs regarding Uplay or anything of that matter, so I can’t speak on that. In fact, I encountered very few bugs, if any at all.  Anyhow, as amazing as the game is — and it will most likely become even better with the DLCs — I do have one major point of contention.  They need to change the campaign. And by change I mean continue. I’m not usually interested in Anno campaigns, and for once I was impressed that it seemed to go in a very interesting direction… but then it just ends right at the point where it gets very interesting? I have to spoil a bit to explain:  – story spoiler –  Why is the foreshadowing with the Latium pirate never picked up again? Why is the emperor portrayed as evil in the beginning and then suddenly changes his mind? WHY DID THE STORY END THERE? It really feels like it shouldn’t have! I genuinely expected to either build up a very big city and somehow gain allies through trading or missions and resolve it diplomatically, or to invade Rome and replace the emperor with the widow who was teased in the beginning as someone with a lot of allies left.  Please, you need to finish this. You built the city (Rome) already — you showed it multiple times in cutscenes. I really thought that for once the campaign would be interesting, but I was left very disappointed.  Edit: I must also add that I, too, disagree with the use of AI for ethical reasons. Artists need to be paid — yes, even for loading screens. Nevertheless, I feel this is just a minor point.

This might well be one of the best Anno games to date. Lots of new and returning mechanics to explore. Love the diagonal roads, the way you are encouraged to place production buildings in your city, the diplomacy options including non hostile takeovers. The assets are beautiful, the settings are well executed. So why the negative review?    To be honest, I hate giving negative feedback to a game I genuinely love and have sunk over 100h in. And this has nothing to do with some of the "woke" comments about the campaign (who plays Anno for campaign???) or the use of AI to replace real artists. None of those are a concern for me. What is a concern is that this game was released undercooked and I'm genuinely tired of the practice in the industry.     There are several massive oversights. The disappearance of the initial deities (who are extremely important in your playthrough) from saves. Or the fact that you can experience crashes from taking over an island. Or just clicking on a keyboard while you're using a controller. Or, speaking of controllers, that you cannot group ships with a controller- how difficult is it to code a grouping in the radial menu? The function is already IN the game for keyboard and mouse.     So while I love the game, and I think several major patches and DLCs later it might well be the best one yet, I cannot recommend it in its current state. My advice is wait at least 3-4months before this beta approaches something that a product launch should be like. Until that happens I am leaving this negative review to let people know I don't appreciate being an unwilling beta tester.

The base game lays a foundation to become the best city builder game ever created, however Rome wasn’t built in a day and neither was Anno 117.  The current campaign is anticlimactic to the point where it feels unfinished. While offering a fun introduction it suddenly drop you off in sandbox mode when you feel like the story is just getting interesting.  The overall city builder experience is exceptional and true to the Anno formula. Land combat is a great addition and evolves the series in the right direction.   Relax and build your Roman city or conquer all as a Roman general.  Excited to see the DLC roadmap as this has me hooked and ready to play more.

This game is good. It runs flawlessly on my setup with a 3070, and visually it’s a slightly noticeable upgrade from Anno 1800. The graphics are sharper, the animations are smoother, and the Roman setting is honestly fantastic.  That said, coming from 1800 with all its DLCs, 1800 is the better and more fleshed out game right now. If you’re new to the series, I’d still recommend starting with 1800, especially if it’s on sale, because it simply has way more content.  Anno 117 introduces some interesting design choices. Charter routes are gone, so you have to rely entirely on your own trade ships. I actually think this was smart given how quickly you could progress through the tiers with charter routes. The UI is also a step up from 1800, cleaner, more intuitive, and easier to navigate.  One thing worth mentioning is that the game currently lets you cheese the economy pretty hard. You can bypass production chains entirely by just buying base resources, and even full end game goods, directly from the warehouse. After the second or third tier, my income skyrocketed simply by purchasing instead of producing, which removes much of the challenge Anno usually offers. Granted, the resources do have to be available for purchase, but that never seemed to be an issue in an AI-filled game with multiple traders constantly supplying the map.  The new tech tree is good in theory, but it also feels like it exists mainly to slow down progression. Without it, you’d advance through everything far too quickly because of the limited content at launch. In Anno 1800, there was enough depth and variety for progression to feel natural without needing an artificial gate like this, so the contrast is noticeable.  The land combat is simple but satisfying enough, and it does add an extra layer to the gameplay that helps make up for the fact that there are only four housing tiers at launch. It’s not the deepest system, but it gives you something engaging to do beyond just building and optimizing your economy.  My first sandbox, where I was discovering everything blind, took about 20 hours to reach the point where I could build whatever I wanted and field any military force in Latium. More experienced players will probably hit that milestone in half the time or less.  Overall, the game shows a ton of promise. I love the Roman theme, the quality of life improvements, and the familiar Anno flow that makes 10 hours disappear without noticing. But I also hit a dead end too quickly due to the current lack of complexity.  I do recommend it, especially if you have friends to play with, but public matchmaking or servers would be a huge plus, and the game definitely needs more content. Hopefully the upcoming DLCs will round this into a full, satisfying Anno experience.

Anno 117 is the second game of the series I have played, after Anno 1800--which I admittedly have spent far too much time playing. So, while not familiar with all the differences within the Anno world, I am tempted to draw some comparisons with 1800. I got into Anno 1800 after all of the DLCs/expansions had been released, so I was playing the most polished version of that game, although I did very much enjoy playing 1800 without any expansions because there were so many it got overwhelming.  Anno 117 feels quite bare bones and less engaging than vanilla 1800, which makes this new title feel like a step back in terms of features. Now, I will give the developers some slack for getting the game out--although it frustrates me to know end that developers these days will release buggy/incomplete games, cash in, and exploit the good faith of their dedicated fans to forgive them for a game being riddled with issues that have no excuse for not being fixed during beta testing. For this reason alone, and tilting the scales a little for the seeming use of AI art, I find it hard to recommend this game.   If you want the beta tester experience, go ahead an jump in.   The gameplay is otherwise really fun, but quite identical to the core 1800 experience of supply chains and transport routes. I do not play with aggressive AI or bandits (i.e. pirates) so I am not commenting on any of the combat aspects. The game encourages more of a dynamic building placement due to radius of effect consequences of many buildings, which is quite neat. It does become a challenge to maintain fire/health/happiness ratings in your Roman cities. The aesthetics of the game are quite nice, albeit the day-to-night transitions feel frequent and the color pallet changes often which I find a bit distracting--you can advance the day-night cycle whenever you want, independent of the game clock, for instance if you dont want to lay out your city in the dark.   I'm going to list out some issues not resolved as of this review:  1. Short main campaign. Feels incomplete. 2. Graphical glitches seen during campaign. Example: Scene with funeral pyre had the tiny little 3D character's polygons shattering and flinging artifacts all over the screen.  3. Some quests unable to be completed in regular game. Quest marker doesn't respond, so possible to get soft locked out of certain quest chains. 4. Incorrect graphics used for certain production chains. (How this got passed beta testing blows my mind) The production chain for loungers says it uses flax, however the cushions actually use wool (as evidenced when you click on the production building after placement).  5. NPC character dialogs are out of sync. This also blows my mind that it made it past beta testing! When an NPC engages with the player for diplomacy, a pop up will appear in the top left. You can select to accept/reject the diplomatic policy (e.g. extend non-agression pact). AFTER accepting the policy, a pop up will appear with the NPC's avatar reading a script about diplomacy and asking the player if they'd like to accept. Clearly this is supposed to be the original audio-visual trigger to get the player to click on the original pop up to the diplomacy accept/reject screen.  6. There are graphical user interface issues with the trade screen. Once you have dozens of trades going, there are instances where the trades at the bottom of the screen get cut off the bottom of the screen when selected and you cant see anything. To get around this, you need to put all your trades in groups and minimize the groups. Note that if you have a dozen groups, then things will get cut off the screen again! Nice! 7. There is not much motivation to work between the two regions, Albion and Latium. You can get to the max tier in either by only using the unique goods within each respective region. The goods demands from the other region are optional. The only exception is that your island which have colloseums will need to have all goods available to them because, like the world's fair in 1800, a random basket of goods will be needed for the games. This lack of connection between the regions isn't "bad", it's just that when you hit the top tier citizens in Latium it feels a bit like a natural stopping point in the game--I dont feel highly inspired to go to Albion to make brooches, cloaks, and bird tongue aspic. (I did for the sake of my playthrough) 8. I havent done a side-by-side, but I feel like the tradescreen in 1800 was better--you could hover over ships/trade routes on the mini-map and see where they were going and what they were carrying. In 117, you dont get any feedback from the mini map, really. To find out more about your routes, you need to click on each one individually. Also, the trade route screen cuts off the icons for all the goods in the trade! You can only see 3 goods on the route in the trade list. Considering the base trireme moves 4 goods, this is quite annoying. 9. Specialists feel useless in this game, especially in comparison to 1800. I've completely ignored them my entire playthroughs. Sometimes they pop up and provide a nice passive bonus, but generally are inconsequential. In 1800, specialists were game breaking! They were super important, and youd spend hours and hours farming them!  10. Not a complaint, but the religion and tech tree aspect of the game are pretty cool add-ons. Considering the game is usually played as a single player sandbox, the research feels like it drags the game on--I recommend playing on fast research speeds. In PvP, I could see research being a critical element since your tempo and the tech focus (civic vs military) could make or break a strategy.   TL;DR: I feel like I am paying to be a beta tester. There are a lot of issues with this game. It needs a large number of fixes before it feels polished.

I haven't played an Anno game in a long time. I have some gripes about this game, both as a sim and in campaign.  Key takeaways:  -The campaign story is poor, short, and ends abruptly.  -Progress grinds to a halt for unoptimized gameplay after the 50 hour mark.   -Tokenistic 'artistic license' for the purpose of checking off diversity checkboxes instead of meaningful inclusion leaves a bad taste in my mouth.   -Ship design is limited, and combat is unbalanced.  -Armies/combat are primitive, unbalanced, and not fun.  -I love the challenge presented by resource diversification by region, and how they connect with different population groups and tiers. You can also overcome being outpaced by being willing to goldsink to make up for missing resources.  -Still activates my neurons as a Roman city builder, I guess. You can really botch your gameplay momentum if you don't keep up with the AI, especially in story mode.    It's fun. I don't think the season passes will be worth it, but I love the idea of a future state of the game with Latium, Albion, Egypt, the Levant, Germany, France, Spain, and Greece as playable regions. So far only Egypt is confirmed for this year.

I have over 300 hours in Anno 2070 Somewhere around that amount with Anno 1800  As you can see I have little interest in playing this one and here's why  Economy: Absolute trainwreck from ridiculously high maintenance fees for everything to it's extreme take on the needs system it's not fun for people who prefer a relaxed playstyle over one that requires a calculator  Combat: I love that they brought back on-island combat but I hate that there are no emplacements of any kind. Combat doesn't feel very exciting and that's assuming you ever get to that point since they made it grueling to even get your populace high enough to build even a shipyard.  There's so little variation in the units and customization.  Research: Really fun in Anno 1800 you discover museum pieces or zoo pieces and build really pretty ornamentals in your city. Well... let alone the fact they don't have those anymore the research system is used for progression and can leave you pretty stuck without it. Unfortunately they have really annoying challenges to unlock parts of the research tree. So it's not just tedious, it doesn't feel rewarding.   Ornamentals: HUGELY disappointed in this. There really aren't any, they basically gave us the barebones like braziers and statues but there's nothing really cool to add to your city.  AI: they're not groundbreaking at all pretty much the same thing from the last game. Very predictable and very boring.  Story: Not really sure since they sent me to the new map and it didn't send my boat with. So they keep telling me to settle an island with NO BOAT.   Needs: The city people are so high maintenance you might as well kiss any idea of having fun with this game goodbye.   Overall unless they change these core systems of the game I can't see this becoming a fun game even after the DLC gets added.

Anno 117: Pax Romana is basically “I think about the Roman Empire every day: The Game” — and honestly, it delivers.    You’re a Roman governor who somehow got put in charge of everything from grand temples to… sheep logistics. One minute you’re gazing lovingly at your marble forum, the next you’re frantically trying to figure out why half of Albion is rioting because you forgot to supply them with enough fancy bread and slightly nicer shirts.    The city-building itself is oddly cozy: you plop down roads at satisfying angles, watch your little citizens hustle around like stressed ants in togas, and slowly turn a sad swamp or rocky coast into something that looks like a tourism ad for “Visit Rome (Before It Collapses).” The game leans more into chill management than hardcore war, so it’s more “Spreadsheet Caesar” than “Total War Dad.”    It’s also super welcoming if you bounced off Anno 1800’s “twelve-step supply chain to make a single hat” energy. Things are simpler, cleaner, and easier to grasp, but there’s still enough depth that you’ll wake up at 3 a.m. thinking, “If I just move that wine production to Albion…”     Is it perfect? Nah. Some systems are a bit too streamlined for veterans, and a few bugs and AI-art drama mean it’s not exactly as pristine as a freshly polished aqueduct. But when you zoom in on your bustling Roman streets, with markets chattering and ships rolling into harbor, it’s hard not to feel a tiny spark of imperial smugness.

Anno Pax Romana is an ambitious blend of grand-strategy depth and city-builder satisfaction, placing players at the center of Rome’s most stable and prosperous era. From the moment your fledgling settlement takes shape on Mediterranean soil, the game makes one thing clear: empire-building is as much about diplomacy and logistics as it is about expansion.  The standout achievement of Anno Pax Romana is its atmosphere. The world feels alive, from bustling ports filled with merchant vessels to distant provinces sending tributary goods along sprawling trade networks. Every system—taxation, culture, religious influence, and military oversight—interlocks with a clarity that makes even complex decisions feel intuitive. Balancing civic development with imperial politics requires foresight, but never feels overwhelming.  Where the game truly shines is in its economic simulation. Managing resources is engaging and rewarding, with a constant push-and-pull between efficiency, growth, and the stability of your population. The developers clearly studied both history and player psychology: small decisions ripple out over time, and watching a thriving city-state evolve into a key pillar of the Roman Empire is deeply satisfying.  There are, however, a few rough edges. The AI can feel passive at times, and late-game management occasionally turns into micromanagement fatigue as your territories sprawl across continents. Some players may also wish for a more dynamic military system, as combat often takes a secondary role to administration and trade.  Still, these issues don’t overshadow what Anno Pax Romana accomplishes. It’s a thoughtful, methodical strategy game that rewards planning, patience, and creativity. Fans of Anno, Caesar, or Paradox-style grand strategy will find plenty to love.  Verdict: A richly crafted, immersive journey through Rome’s golden age—deep, elegant, and endlessly engaging. A must-play for strategy enthusiasts who enjoy shaping history with both vision and detail.

Anno 117: Pax Romana Review  “I installed it to kill time… now I'm planning an empire.”    ===[ ❤ Audience: ]===  🏛️ History nerds  🌱 City-building enthusiasts  💸 People who secretly dream of ruling an ancient civilization  🚫 Not recommended for people with an aversion to managing resources and constantly battling for territory    Anno 117: Pax Romana is for anyone who loves building there Iland and the sweet sound of taxes being collected and trade routes getting disrupted by pirates.    ===[ ☼ Graphics: ]===  🏙️ Gorgeous, detailed cityscapes  🎨 A beautiful, historical art style that makes you feel like you’re living in ancient Rome  💥 The occasional pop of majestic Roman architecture that feels like it just critted your senses    Every building looks like it’s been carefully handcrafted by some ancient architect and I love the feeling of watching my city slowly come to life.    ===[ $ Price/quality: ]===  🪙 Worth every sesterce  🥂 I paid full price and would do it again for the sheer satisfaction of watching my aqueducts work perfectly    If you measure fun by time spent, the devs are living the good life while I’m constantly running my empire with a grin on my face.    ===[ ☣ Requirements: ]===  ⚙️ Runs on a gaming computer  ⚡ It can melt your CPU if you get too ambitious with your empire  🖥️ Doesn’t need a NASA PC, but you’ll need some patience when your city grows too big    ===[ ☼ Difficulty: ]===  🥄 Easy to pick up  💪 Hard to master  💥 Mentally exhausting when you realize you accidentally left your entire trade fleet exposed to pirates because you got distracted by the stunning architecture    You will think you’ve mastered it… until the gods of RNG smite your city and you lose it all to a sudden plague or an invading barbarian horde. It’s part of the fun.    ===[ ۞ Game time/length ]===  ⏳ “Just one more turn”  ⏰ 10-minute sessions that inevitably stretch into 5 hours  🔁 Infinite replayability if you enjoy watching your city grow (and then get sacked, because you forgot to build a wall)    You could stop playing. But that city isn’t going to manage itself.    ===[ ♬ Story ]===  📜 Very nice campaign, I recommend to play  as Marcia Tetria!  💥 Political intrigue and epic battles as you try to claim dominion over the ancient world, one resource at a time    The game is telling a very nice story and you should play it yourself    ===[ § Bugs ]===  🐞 Rare hiccups  🛠️ Doesn’t break the game, but every now and then you’ll get a pleasant "Wait, what?" moment    If you encounter a bug, it’s likely because the game’s systems are so intricate they occasionally glitch like a Roman chariot in a modern-day parking lot.    ===[ ✦ Others: ]===  🧍 Singleplayer: Yes, and it’s brilliant  👯 Multiplayer: Yes, you can play in Co-Op or manage your island  alone  🎲 RNG: Absolutely, and it’s a spicy mix of chance and strategy    Anno 117: Pax Romana is like being a Roman governor, constantly managing resources, political alliances, and your citizens’ moods while trying to expand your empire. Will you be a benevolent ruler or a ruthless dictator? Your call.    Final Thoughts:    This game has:  ✔ Addictive empire-building gameplay  ✔ Stunning visuals that make every city feel like a living, breathing Roman hub  ✔ The thrill of watching your civilization rise and fall  ✔ The constant desire for “just one more hour”  ✔ Enough stress to make you question why you wanted power in the first place    If city-building had a baby with history and fed it ambition and strategy, Anno 117 would be that glorious child.    11/10 – would again get sidetracked by building another aqueduct while my economy crashes. 🏛️🔥

I really expected more from the German Ubisoft studio.     I've played Anno games since Anno 1503 (released in 2002) with several hundred hours in each game since. This new installment is very disappointing. I'm not even going to get into the abhorrent use of AI for the artwork and campaign, you can read all the other comments about that).     Yes, the game play looks beautiful and the land combat is a new facet, but overall it is a very surface level Anno game with an extremely slow pace and poorly executed technological advancements.     This game also severely lacks historical accuracy compared to previous games, which says a lot since the Anno franchise hasn't always been great about that, but this is just atrocious.      Some of the most pressing issues so far:  - Diagonal building is nice but the buildings don't adapt to fit 45 degree angles, so you have a lot of dead space when trying to build anything but a square city.   - New island colonization costs gold instead of resources, which is just lazy.   - Land and sea combat is basic at best and the true Roman tactics play no role at all.   - The entire game only has two locations that feel very limited and (again) surface level at best. (Also, if we are being historically accurate, don't start with Celtic regions, but maybe Spain or Africa).   - Having to build absolutely massive cities that are not cohesive to field a simple army is ridiculous. At least provide options to increase the people per house with more amenities provided.   - Some production buildings make zero sense, such as the wood cutters and charcoal kilns. How is there no option to replant trees?  - Lazy resource design in terms of river and mountain building slots. Any slot can accommodate any type of mine, which makes zero sense.       The list is getting too long. So overall, don't buy it yet.     The game has been out for 2 weeks, and no updates have been released, which is again very disappointing. If you are a die hard Anno fan, keep an eye on it and hopefully they will drastically improve the mechanics and game play, otherwise I would say never buy it.

I really like the game's graphics and the new building menu, which focuses on population levels and their needs. The game also feels very similar to other Anno games, while adding new mechanics such as gods, the knowledge tree, and area effects for production buildings. I love the Anno series games and know its never perfect it will change in a few years. people seem to have forgotten that Anno 1800 was a mess when it was released.. but Pax Romana, I've got some mixed thoughts. On one hand, I really appreciate the smooth city-building mechanics. They strike a great balance between being deep enough to engage players while still being accessible for newcomers. The visuals are absolutely stunning; the game does a fantastic job of bringing ancient Rome to life. Plus, I love the flexibility it offers with campaign and endless modes, not to mention the fun co-op options. On the other hand, I've noticed some downsides. Some of the AI-generated content feels  insulting,  and I’ve run into a few bugs, especially during co-op play. Performance lags have been an issue on older systems, too. I also found the campaign length to be a bit short and  it felt "Fake" , which limits how deep the story can go. and I get why some people are frustrated with the technical issues. All in all, Anno 117 is fun but start with endless mode First. as it definitely has room for improvement, particularly in player experience and stability. It’s worth a try for fans of the genre, though I’d recommend newcomers to keep the mixed feedback in mind.

I'm new to the Anno series but I must say I'm positively surprised so far. What I like about it is that building a roman city and economy on a small island is a very satisfying thing to do. The game manages to make you feel strongly connected to the whole city you build and which you slowly see expanding. It sometimes even reminded me a bit of the feeling I got while playing Black and White: you feel like you are a god managing a community and you feel the need to make it prosperous.  It is just a very satisfying game to play in my opinion and they also managed to make the whole city building work intuitive and don't annoy the player with to many economic details.    What I don't particularly like about the game though are the graphics and then specifically that the buildings are not really neat looking (a bit sloppy) so you never seems to be able to build  a real aesthetic roman city. How further you zoom in how less good it looks (and walking around in third person with a citizen is looking absolutely horrible: they should be a bit ashamed that they don't had the time to make this look like it is 2025 and not 2002). If they managed to make it as good looking as for example Planet Coaster 2 and blend that with the artist impressions you see when the game starts up it would be a much better city building experience. Now the game feels more like a roman settlement economy manager with city build elements. You never really can get the aesthetically satisfying looking cities which you see in the opening screens (and they seem to market this game with). In this regard it feels a bit like the difference between a Mac Donald burger in the commercial and in reality.  But the total package the game offers feels polished and the ' Black and White' feeling you get from playing it makes this aspect more then good. A very recommended game in my opinion which deserves better graphics (of the buildings).

Synopsis: 8/10, lacks story depth, but makes up for that with excellent gameplay and setting.    Boy oh boy, what a game. I have always been a big fan of history with the roman and greek antiquities as big outliers. This game scratches that itch in my brain I've had since the Caesar games came out.  Is it a flawless game? Nope, the story lacks depth and feels left open too much, to a point where you didn't even know the story bits are over. However, this is not the reason I play these types of games. I love building something myself, from scratch. This game allows that. It feels rich enough to keep you playing, yet is not so dauniting that it feels overwhelming. If you are a big fan of history games, this could be for you. If you love citybuilding, this will definitely be for you.     There are some people who mention that it is not as good as anno 1800, since I have not played that game, I cannot chime in here. What I do know is, if 1800 is even better than this, it was one hell of a game.     To round it all off, play the game and see for yourself. Reviews are good to see if you find peers who love it, but to experience it yourself, that is what it is all about.

Posting this to try to counter-act the unfair review-bombing of the game.   A great game in the series. I've been playing since 1602, and this one manages to improve even more after 1800. I love the Classical period, so this was always going to be a good choice of setting for me. The campaign isn't great, and limits you too much, but I've never cared about ANNO campaigns... The game is made for endless mode.   I'm so glad land combat has come back, cus it's made playing with other NPCs much more enjoyable. In previous games, the NPC factions were always building and expanding way too fast, and pirates were attacking trade routes all the time, even on easy. This time, I feel they're more on pace with me, and the pirates basically send a warning before attacking, and I prefer that. I like to play at a more comfortable speed, making sure everything looks good and works well.   It also looks better and runs better than 1800. I can tell that the loading system is much smarter, loading in low-poly assets at a distance, allowing for a greater view distance. Every island looks like a cute little model, and I love that. I also enjoy the diagonal placement options, and the ability to put walls even along cliffs outside of the building-zones.   I also enjoy the different vibes of both regions. The constant storms, rain, and fog of the Albion realm is very immersive. I'm looking forward to the Mideast region when that innevitably comes out.

It really feels like the game was developed by many separate departments, each with its own focus, resulting in an overall experience that feels disjointed. Some parts are polished and look great, while others feel unfinished, rushed, or incomplete.    The story ends abruptly in Act 3, leaving it unclear what comes next, maybe just a bug. After that point, the game devolves into managing passive islands that simply trade goods. Opponents feel passive as well, functioning more like interface elements you click on rather than meaningful contributors to gameplay.    The ship trade route menu is also confusing. You can’t easily see which ships are carrying what unless you manually open each route. This makes managing numerous routes and balancing island supplies unnecessarily difficult and feels like a missed opportunity for better mechanics.    As many players have mentioned, the use of AI-generated art is not appreciated. Please, please consider hiring real artists. AI art breaks the visual identity and aesthetic consistency of the game, especially for future development.    Overall, the gameplay feels dull.    On a positive note, the island visuals and weather effects look genuinely impressive. The world map also looks nice, though it serves little purpose since it doesn’t show routes, ships, or other meaningful locations.

The main gameplay loop, particularly with its innovations from previous titles in the series, is very engrossing -- big time fun, lots of style, and a nifty new setting. There's lots to learn, experiment with, and fall genuinely in love with for new and veteran players, I feel. That's why it's easy for me to recommend Anno 117.  However... it's buggy and has some glaring pain points. A big thing for me is that there isn't as much information offered through the UI as there needs to be (i.e. inventory/island info). If things turn catastrophically wrong all of a sudden, it can be very difficult and frustrating to try to figure out what on earth needs to be fixed. While trial and error is a fundamental part of city builders, there needs to be more & clearer information available here.  The most significant issue I have with the title in its launch state, though, is that the campaign storyline stops short. By the time you reach the current "ending" there are so many hanging plot threads left dangling about you, and it's jarring. Will the devs add Act 3 in a coming patch? Or will it be... as I'm really worried it likely will... in a DLC? If it's introduced behind a paywall, I will be angry. In theory, I'm generally okay with games having story added in DLC, but only if the base game's storyline is complete and can stand on its own without any additions. Anno 117's base game story is not complete and falls flat on its face without the conclusion we clearly weren't given here.  All told, this title offers a lot, both in terms of what there is to have fun with right now and how the devs will expand upon it in the future. Even so, there are some definite shortcomings that drag the game down. Hopefully, the bugs will be squished, the wonkiness will be patched (maybe they'll even clean up the many grammatical errors in the dialogue/text???), and we'll see Act 3 make its way to the game sometime soon... ideally, as a free update. Fingers crossed!

**Not Recommended — at least not right now.**  I’m deleting my previous positive review. Please ignore my hours played; a lot of that time was spent AFK farming achievements.  I almost never leave negative reviews, but I’ve owned this game since launch and have been genuinely excited to play it. Unfortunately, the experience has been consistently frustrating. At launch, the game was a buggy mess, with several core features simply not working. Patches improved things somewhat, but major bugs continued to linger.  Now the latest DLC has released after a week-long delay, and once again it launched in a rough, buggy state. After another patch, the main features finally seem to be working, but I’m still running into constant issues. Are they making the game completely unplayable? No. But am I repeatedly reloading saves or restarting the game because overlays, grids, and other basic elements keep vanishing for no clear reason? Yes.  What makes this more disappointing is that, from what I’ve seen, the broader community seems to feel much the same. This edition of Anno simply does not seem to have the same level of community enthusiasm or support that its predecessor had. Sadly, even some of Anno’s most prominent content creators appear to have stepped away from it for now.  To their credit, the developers have acknowledged mistakes on both the development and marketing side, and I appreciate that. I do think the game may improve over time. I love the Anno series, and I sincerely hope they can course correct before this becomes a permanent black eye on the franchise.  But right now, I’m probably hanging up my hat again and waiting a few more months to see whether they can finally get the game into a stable state.

The year is 117 AD, and I've been given a slice of the world to shape—either the sun-drenched predictability of Latium or the mist-shrouded, rugged mystery of Albion. I chose the latter, and a strange, deep magic began to hum. It’s a magic not of wizards or dragons, but of logistics and supply chains, a meticulous, hypnotic dance that has stolen dozens of hours of my life, leaving me utterly delighted and slightly dazed.  My world is a canvas where the brushstrokes are the clip-clop of ox-drawn carts and the murmur of citizens haggling over fish sauce (garum, a true staple). The genius here is in the flow, the elegant ripple effect of a single, well-placed field. Building placement actually matters in a deeply satisfying way; a carefully laid paved road extends the influence of my bathhouses and temples, creating desirable patrician enclaves separate from the charcoal burners and clay pits. For the first time, my city layouts aren't just efficient grids—the new ability to build diagonal roads allows me to create organic, winding boulevards that snake up hillsides and curve around natural harbors, transforming mere function into an art form.  The loop is an addictive one: start with basic needs (porridge and sandals), then slowly, methodically, scale up to the complex demands of wine, chariots, and the exotic "bird tongues in aspic". I found I didn't need to fulfill every single desire to progress, which eased the pressure when a specific production line inevitably wobbled into a "death spiral" of lost income and population. The game lets you breathe, offering an "active pause" feature so you can reorganize an entire district without your empire crumbling in real-time panic.  Naval combat feels weighty and strategic, a real part of the experience rather than a mini-game, essential for protecting my crucial trade routes of cheese and grain from pesky pirates. Land combat is also back, a solid if simple rock-paper-scissors affair that rewards preparation and a strong economy over tactical wizardry. Anno 117: Pax Romana is a triumph of balance: it's approachable for newcomers to the series, but deep enough to consume veterans for weeks. It is gorgeous to look at, with lighting effects that make me want to stay in "postcard mode" (first-person view) just to watch my tiny toga-clad citizens go about their day, listening to the ambient sounds of a living, breathing city.  While the campaign narrative felt more like a well-paced tutorial that ended abruptly, the infinitely customizable sandbox mode is the real heart of the experience. The game has its minor launch bugs and performance quirks, and some veteran players might miss the sheer volume of endgame content that its predecessor, Anno 1800, has after years of DLC. But as a foundation for a new era of Anno, it is sturdy, elegant, and utterly compelling.  Ave gubernator, imperium tuum exspectat!
