Pax Romana exceeded every expectation I had going in. It’s not just another historical city-builder — it’s the most ambitious, atmospheric, and richly textured entry the series has delivered in years.  From the moment you establish your first Roman outpost, the game pulls you into a world that feels alive. The art direction is stunning: bustling forums, meticulously rendered villas, dynamic trade routes that stretch across the empire, and landscapes that actually feel like regions of the ancient world rather than generic maps. The attention to detail is classic Anno — but sharper, cleaner, and more immersive than ever.  Gameplay is where Anno 117 really shines. The balancing between military presence, local cultures, diplomacy, and economic expansion is the best the series has ever produced. Managing Roman law, integrating local populations, and negotiating with regional powers adds real weight to your decisions. It’s the classic Anno production-chain satisfaction layered with new strategic depth, and it hits that “just one more hour” loop perfectly.  The campaign is brilliantly paced, with characters and storylines that bring humanity and tension into the empire-building experience. Meanwhile, free play mode gives you endless room to experiment, optimize, or simply build the ancient city of your dreams.  And the soundtrack? Absolutely exceptional. It feels cinematic and period-authentic without ever becoming distracting.  Overall: Anno 117 is a triumphant addition to the franchise. It respects the series’ core identity—complex economies, deep systems, and gorgeous cities—while introducing fresh mechanics that keep everything feeling new. Whether you’re a longtime fan or completely new to the Anno series, this game is an absolute must-play.

I'm writing this review coming off a 15 hour multiplayer session and after having played through the campaign. As games go, it's more of what we've come to expect from the Anno series.  Good sound design, the graphics and visuals are top notch, gameplay is decent and I encountered very few bugs throughout, so a big thanks to the test players.  Towards the "end" of the game things start to get sluggish as there are many assets on screen at the same time but nothing game breaking.  Technically speaking, you are in good hands. However, this game has the great misfortune of coming after Anno 1800, which is a high point in the series, mainly because of the amount of content and the variety of things to do.  As a result Anno 117 feels empty by comparison.  Gone are the expeditions, grand projects, zoos, museums (or their equivalent) and you are left with the two territories with only one "grand project" which is a coliseum and that's it. I'd be willing to look past all of this and chalk it up to the fact they will, no doubt, make additional content and addons much like they did for Anno 1800.  But I want to remind everyone they will charge you for those addons.  Meanwhile this shell of a game is selling for $60.  If you're playing the campaign there is maybe 10 to 12 hours of content, multiplayer with your friends will push that out maybe an additional 30ish hours if you play a few matches.  Regardless of how you play, you will see everything this game has to offer in under 10 hours, and after that it will be variations on a theme.  I hope the expansions can redeem the missing content and not charge me another $60 for the missing content.  TL;DR  Impressive, but very little of it.

Anno 117: Pax Romana attempts to push the long-running strategy series into bold new territory, swapping the familiar Age of Discovery aesthetics for the grandeur and grit of the Roman Empire. The result is ambitious and often impressive—yet not without some uneven edges.  What the Game Gets Right  • Stunning presentation and atmosphere The shift to ancient Rome pays off visually. Cities feel alive with bustling forums, marching legions, and detailed villas. Ubisoft’s artists clearly poured love into the setting, making every province look vibrant and lived-in.  • Dual-province setup adds strategic variety Managing both Latium and Hadrian’s Wall introduces a refreshing dynamic. Each region has distinct challenges—political intrigue in the heart of the empire versus harsh survival and frontier defense in Britannia. It forces you to think beyond one-size-fits-all city building.  • A grounded economic model The supply chains feel tight and purposeful. Roman society’s dependency on trade, slaves, and military logistics creates interesting pressure points, and achieving stability is genuinely satisfying.  • Strong narrative flavor While not a full story-driven campaign, the contextual events and Roman cultural touches make decisions feel meaningful. The game is at its best when it leans into historical flavor.  Where the Game Stumbles  • Slower, sometimes sluggish pacing Early game buildup can feel grindy. With more complex production chains and restrictive starting resources, progressing from “struggling settlement” to “functional city” takes patience—maybe too much for some players.  • AI and diplomacy are undercooked Barbarian factions and rival governors often feel passive or predictable. The diplomacy system is serviceable but lacks the depth needed for a setting driven by politics and treachery.  • Frontier combat isn’t fully satisfying Despite the Roman theme, battles feel more like mild interruptions than strategic challenges. Military units lack personality, and engagements can feel automated rather than tactical.  • Performance hiccups Large settlements strain even strong PCs, and some players may encounter stuttering during big population surges.  Verdict: Respectable, Atmospheric, but Imperfect  Anno 117: Pax Romana is a worthy addition to the franchise, especially for fans who want a deeper strategic layer and a fresh historical setting. Its worldbuilding and dual-province structure shine, but technical issues, pacing problems, and limited AI complexity keep it from reaching its full potential.  If you love painstaking city building and the Roman aesthetic, you’ll likely enjoy it. If you’re looking for razor-sharp strategy and dynamic diplomacy, it may leave you wanting more.

Anno 117 is a mixed bag. Even though I like the Roman Empire as a setting, I don't think it fits the Anno setting as well. I have reached the highest Roman civilization now, but I feel like this game is still a bit too empty to launch it this way. When compared to the release of Anno 1800, I feel sad when I see that EVERY ship looks and feels the same. Yes, you can put in a module to let them have rows or archers, but it feels bad. The ships look identical; why can't I just put in archers, scorpions, or catapults AFTER I build or buy them?  Anyway, enough ship talk. The new two starting regions don't feel well implemented. There is a clear focus on the Roman region, while Albion feels like the forgotten sibling. I played on most settings on normal, but the game doesn't feel particularly well balanced because you run into constant workforce problems while you get tons of money without any issues. I don't find this approach good, and I will adjust the workforce settings down to "easy" next time.  The neutral NPC offers junk. Really. When I compare this to Anno 1800, where you could get some really nice items that would impact your gameplay in a meaningful way, you get offers for items that usually don't affect your gameplay at all, like:      +10% to production     +0.6 to happiness for every affected house (you still run into problems)     +20% range for the scorpions (mostly ship ballista)  You absolutely won’t feel or see these affecting your gameplay.  They also overdid the fire, riot, and health needs system in a really bad way. You can have your whole area covered in firefighters, police, and hospitals, plus the newly added small 2x2 and 2x3 buildings like a guard tower or a well, but you will still run into a negative balance, leading to too many complaints from your people without a good way to address this.  Additionally, some requirements feel way too large, like the basic food needed to have extra people live in your houses. I feel like I have to set up another field of crops when I build 20 new houses. At some point, I felt like being a liberty nation because you just don’t get enough workforce to handle your isle.  The new research and belief system also feels like a very flat system. Yes, at the beginning, you get some good and cool skill points to research, but that quickly diminishes once you’ve researched them. About 3/4 of the skills feel useless or require too many research points.  Religion: Yes, it is there, but I haven't felt it affecting my game in ANY way. It’s as if it’s not even there; you get not enough bonuses to even care about this system.  There is also meta progression in this game. Why? I mean, yes, you get some good specialists, but there is: A) mostly useless stuff, B) stuff that is so good that you HAVE to pick it, and C) not enough things to unlock. After my playtime now, I feel like I have EVERYTHING unlocked that I want.  My honest review: Get Anno 1800 over this game; even the base game is way better and offers more. Currently, I can't really recommend Anno 117.  Oh, and the performance also feels worse for no reason, even though my PC could handle it. Still, it feels unnecessarily performance-hungry.

👍 Recommended Hours Played: 32 hours An Amazing Anno Experience - With One Major Flaw After 60 hours in Anno 117: Pax Romana, I can confidently say this is one of the best entries in the Anno series. As someone who's played previous Anno games, the improvements to gameplay are incredible and the Roman setting brings fresh life to the formula. The Good:  Outstanding city building - The level of detail and flexibility is unmatched. Watching your Roman provinces come to life with realistic citizens going about their daily routines is mesmerizing Engaging story and campaign - The narrative between Roman and Celtic cultures actually kept me invested, which is rare for a city builder Best Anno gameplay yet - The new optional needs system, attribute mechanics, and production building synergies make this the most refined and strategic Anno to date Beautiful setting - Both Latium and Albion are gorgeous and distinct, with tons of replayability  The Bad:  Multiplayer is broken - Constant desync issues make co-op nearly unplayable. Playing with friends is frustrating and not smooth at all. This is a major problem that desperately needs fixing  Bottom Line: If you play solo, this is easily a must-buy for Anno fans and newcomers alike. The gameplay refinements make it the best Anno yet. However, if you're planning to play with friends, I'd recommend waiting until Ubisoft fixes the multiplayer desync issues. The campaign and sandbox modes are absolutely worth your time - just don't count on co-op working properly right now. Rating: 8.5/10 (would be 9.5/10 with stable multiplayer)

I really wanted to like this game as a fan of both the franchise and Roman history but there are a few glaring issues that can't be overlooked to where I can't give this game a thumbs-up (for now).  TL;DR: Feels incomplete for $60, campaign was only 24 hours (without rushing), not enough notable improvements over previous entries to justify price tag.  One thing to keep in mind - I don't generally do multiplayer in Anno so if you are interested in that aspect, your mileage may vary.  If you like Anno 1800, it does feel very similar mechanically - so much so that it feels almost like a DLC rather than a new game. The graphics are better than 1800 and the default control scheme finally using WASD for camera movement is a welcome addition. It's still Anno so the supply chains and population mechanics are still solid and the game is fairly polished - I didn't encounter any major bugs throughout the time I played.  The biggest downside is the length of the campaign - I am not, by any means, a speedrunner and was not rushing through the game but was easily able to complete the campaign in about 24 hours. I still had the maximum level of citizens in most cities including both branches in the second region and still had almost all supply chains running smoothly but the ending of the campaign felt very abrupt and, frankly, felt like there was an entire 3rd act missing (or more).  Candidly, I expected more when it comes to content delivered with the base game at $60 USD. I know there is additional content (free and paid) yet to come but the base game just feels like it's not delivering enough. I don't want to have to pay $90+ USD to get the full experience that will eventually be available to feel like a complete game.  I enjoyed the time I spent on this game but as it stands now, I don't think it's worth the $60 price tag (or $90 if you buy the pass that will eventually get you the DLCs that will make the game feel complete by late 2026) when you can pick up Anno 1800 for a lot less and get a similar experience.  This is a thumbs-down only because there's nothing between thumbs-up and thumbs-down - it's not all bad but there's not enough value here to merit a thumbs-up at it's current price tag.

Anno 117 is a flawed potential masterpiece, one with some amazing bones as a starting point. Even if you do not buy the DLCs and play right now, you can expect a solid citybuilding game with a Roman theme, that has some solid mechanical depth.  First off, I'd like to explain the huge amount of negative reviews, this boils down to four main reasons:  1. Anno 1800 heads who are used to 12 content DLCs that greatly expand the game and its content, giving hundreds of hours of collection, tweaking, and optimization. Anno 117 being new, does not have this, and so many are comparing it against a veteran game, whereas instead if should be compared to anno 1800 at launch, and in which case Anno 117 blows it out of the water.  2. Ubisoft Launcher. It's a pain in the ass, I admit, and something I've grown begrudgingly used to. I do find the reaction a bit too much, but I cannot comment and their complaints are justified.  3. The campaign. In that gamemode, it cuts off pretty much midway through the game, with datamining showing that a third act was planned, and potentially might be continued in the DLC. I personally don't mind it, as despite the great writing and me wanting to continue the campaign itself, anno has always been front and foremost a sandbox citybuilding game, and the campaign has always been a very good tutorial for the sandbox mode, especially since this anno rewards multiple playthrough with its hall of fame. Their complaints, however, are justified.  4. AI usage. In the game, there is one *confirmed* usage of AI to make a piece of background art. This is the only one I've seen be confirmed, and the only one that I myself can see as AI, and all the other examples I've seen on reddit where quickly debunked as people tried to spy out small details only for some roman nerd to come in and tell them, "oh that's a Roman Signum!" or something else like that. I'd also point out that one of the largest leads in the art design has an artstation under karakter, who has a lovely style but its a messy, oil painting style. Since in oil painting a lot of minor detail is left up for the mind to interpret, a lot of people have been calling out the rest of the non-AI paintings. Point is, there has been one confirmed piece, and genuine artists did work on it.  Moving onto my own opinions, I believe that anno 117 was rushed, and while all the solid mechanical work, the building, and the tiers are done, they were rushed in other degrees, like the story, and art. Does this make it a bad game? No. But I do wish it had a bit more time in the oven.   You should also keep in mind that while some things are historically accurate, this game is not. This is ultimately a game inspired by the roman empire, and it does take a lot of its cues from old works involving that, so much so that the romans have british accents! I blame the old movies personally. For more accurate takes, look at something like... Pax Augusta, though that also has its share of problems.  I would still gladly review Anno 117 as a positive however, as for the game itself, it has some solid QOL changes and some minor downgrades compared to anno 1800 (with all of its updates and DLCs) and while there are some strange design choices (like not letting me join aqueducts together) overall its a solid upgrade, and with a beautiful theme, music, and decoration building to match. If you're at all interested in citybuilding or like making supply chains and managing islands or things like that, I would wholeheartedly reccomend this game, especially for people who want something more in line with anno 1404 instead of anno 1800 which can be too much sometimes.  And if you liked this game? Check out Anno 1800 and 1404!

Disclaimer this is my first Anno and I am not that deep into it yet.    The bad: Ubisoft account for login, use of AI for art allegedly, circular building radiuses. In a game where you can only build squares isn't it weird that when you build a fire station it covers a circle? Surely it should be a square so you can fit in everything instead of having a handful of houses or buildings just outside its radius. The lack of trees/replanting trees, a bad forest start can really hamstring wood production.   The good: Its quite fun and not what I expected. Its not the most deep/challenging building game ever and doesn't have the same challenge as say Manor Lords or even Banished where everything can collapse and go up in flames if you mismanage your resources, but the way progress is divided up into different societal classes and production chains is fun. If anything I would have liked more detailed production chains with more steps though as well as more production chains overall so you had a bit more flexibility on what you built to advance your citizens. Its just the right level of depth though that you can focus on making something pretty, while also being distracted by making sure its balanced and providing what is needed to your citizens.   I haven't done combat yet, but the presence of armies was a big draw for me, I think as a pure city builder it wouldn't have been as attractive as other options. So far I've mostly played it coop and it worked fine no major problems.

Anno continues to be one of the best city builders available and I would recommend anyone who has a remote interest in the genre to try this game!

The worst issue happens when discovering Albion. Every time we reach that point, the game desyncs and crashes, making it impossible to continue our co-op session. And not only is it a lively roman city, it's one I helped grow! Just a great city builder. As always for Anno, I love that you don't have to micromanage, everything is macromanagement which is actually more fun in my opinion. I love a paradox game from time to time, but this is relaxing city building.    I like the new stuff too, the marshes and sluces are cool, albion is cool in general. The new quest system is better. Can't wait for the DLC.    Good stuff.

Anno 1800 started great and became a masterpiece. Thus there are big expectations towars its successor. Unfortunately Anno 177 doesn't start as great as 1800 did on release. You, the governor, now speaks but is a rather odd type, especially for the brutal roman era. Anno refuses to engage with its setting and is opting for a very weird jovial governor that remindes me of the movie adams apples. The governor simply refuses to acknowledge reality around him. Anyhow, that would not be that bad if the rest of the atmosphere would hold up, but it does not.  Anno 1800 greeted the player with inspiring concept art or rather atmosphere pieces, not shying away from the conflicts of the 1800s. Additionally it greeted the player with a memorable enthusiastic soundtrack. In contrast Anno 117 starts without music and with poorly done AI art or rather something close to it.  Still it is the second best modern city builder, although it falls way behind Anno 1800 it is still a very good game. Disclaimer: I've played every Anno that's come out since 1404, I'd cop to being a pretty big fan. I find something to love in every installment and I average about ~60ish hours for each one.  [h3]The Gist[/h3] Is it worth getting? I'd [b]wait until a discount[/b] unless you are a diehard fan of this breed of city building and trade. It's a good Anno game but story content is short; if endless is where you have the most fun, that might move the needle for you into "buy".  [h3] Impressions[/h3] I can't see myself playing this one as much as those (especially my personal favorite: Anno 2205), and that's partially due to how short the main campaign is.   At 10ish hours, it moved at my pace but I felt I had exhausted most of what there was to see and I was surprised to see the main quest end so quickly before throwing you into the endless mode. I foresee them adding more regions as paid content which I wish had padded out this release, it felt like a step backwards in terms of story-driven content.  All of that said, this is a really polished and solid outing in terms of economy, systems, general core game play. It is GORGEOUS, I wanted to spend the time decorating every little corner. I wish there were more doodads to do that with.  There's plenty here to love but I wouldn't rank this among my top 3 Anno games and you may reach the end of it wondering "was that it?"

I have played hundreds if not thousands of hours of Anno. And Anno 117 is a good evolution of the franchises core mechanics. After almost 90 hours in the base game I have seen most of the content the game has to offer so far.  Right now I would say that you can buy the game if you liked Anno 1800 and you do not mind small (but annoying) bugs. Also some of the background art of the game is AI generated which is very disappointing. @BlueByte or Ubisoft: Pay for your artworks, especially if you want to sell a game as full price title.  The bad or critique worthy first: -The UI is very confusing. Especially for new players. Anno 1800s was way more accessable and easier to read. -AI artworks. Again a big no-no in my eyes. But thats for you (the buyer) to decide. -Sound Bugs: Often encountered sound bugs in longer sessions. -Command in Battle: Commanding troops in battle needs improvement. In larger battles the troops do not react to commands. I encountered various bugs with unit selection as well as move and attack commands. -Specialists: This is a very subjective point. In Anno 1800 the Specialists were beyond broken, even without mods/cheats. but that made them exciting. Right now I care way less about specialists in my city then I did in Anno 1800. Sure it makes the game more balanced in Multiplayer but it took away a source of excitement. -Inhabitant Tier caps at Lvl 4: The cap for inhabitants in Latium at level 4 / Patrician is a bit of a letdown. The ridicolous production chains for investors from Anno 1800 made the chase for optimized production chains in the late game way more exciting.  The good and praiseworthy: -Gameplay: The gameplay loop is as satisfying as ever. -Combat: Land Combat is a well done, "light" version of Total War. There is a lot of potential for expanding this aspect of the game. -Population tiers and variations in Albion: Having two population variations in Albion  is a nice evolution of the "second" region of the base game. -Choose your starting region: Being able to choose your starting region, even in multiplayer, is a great addition to the franchise. -Rebellion: Being able to rebel against the entity that allowed you to settle your first island was one of my biggest wishes for Anno 1800 (Royal Taxes are theft). In Anno 117 it is finally possible. The idea to make it a horde mode, base defense evnet is great.  I also like the presented options after the first 10 waves to either becomne Pro-Consul, change the Emperor or go for the Gauntlet of never ending war. The obvious: Almost everything that made Anno 1800 great can also be found in this game. -Tech tree, religion and AoE effects of production buildings: I like the new mechanics and building efficient town centres has never been more important.  Hopes and other thoughts: The future: I hope that we get a long running support for this game. The foundation is solid and will get even better once the launch bugs have been fixed. Anno 117s success compared to it's predecessor wil be measured once it has received it's first few Add-Ons Campaign: I have not played it and I do not intend to play it in the future because I'm only interested in the endless mode. It would take a very noteworthy story to get me to play the campaign of Anno.  I'm excited for the future of this game. If you have read this review and feel like this could interest you then I can  just say that it probably is worth your money. Good luck and have fun, building your empire.

Last Anno game I played a lot was 1404 and I loved it. I tried the futuristic ones but they were very meh and I didn't play 1800 because I wasn't interrested in the time period.  I don't know what exactly happened to the campaign. You choose between a male and female protagonist so at first I thought there would be two campaigns, but no. The events play out exactly the same, just the reasoning is sometimes different. The campaign is also incredibly short. There are two acts and then it just ends. There is no finale, no climax just a "ok, you can now play on this map forever, congratulations." The campaign in 1404 had an interresting story and gave you a taste of everything the game had to offer, both factions, all technology tiers and monument construction. In 117, you don't even have to go past tier 2. The intrigues, murder mystery, nothing is explained. You just get two AI players on the map and one of the NPC docks turns into a pirate faction. When you look at reddit, you will find many people asking if their campaign bugged out and didn't continue. There are also no scenarios like in 1404 Venice. Some of them were nice challenges. Once you finish the campaign in 117 (which is a few hours long) you have no choice but to play endless mode. It just feels like there is so much missing from this game. The finale of the campaign, different AI opponents, scanarios, ornaments, different islands (there are only a few islands that the game chooses from when creating a world)...  Anno 117 doesn't feel like a full game. It feels like an early acces, with some locked out option titled "work in progress" but its not... It's a full priced game from a large studio so I would be expecting a full game.  I don't know what the roadmap is supposed to look like. I also don't know why a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ major release from a large studio needs a roadmap. As of writing this review, Anno 117 is a buggy, unfinished, empty game that was released too early. Wait until they put more work in and buy on sale.

I’ve been playing Anno 117 for a bit now, and honestly, I think this game has huge potential. The first thing that hit me was the atmosphere. The world is beautiful, calm, and super easy to get lost in. The visuals alone make just exploring the coastline feel relaxing.    Gameplay wise, it’s definitely more beginner-friendly than older Anno titles. The systems are easier to understand, and it doesn’t overwhelm you with complicated menus or production chains right away. I actually like that — it makes the game way more approachable for new players.    That said, you can tell the game is still finding its full depth. Some of the classic Anno layers like logistics, trade routes, and more strategic military stuff feel lighter than usual. It’s not bad, but it’s the type of thing you hope gets expanded as updates and DLC roll out.    Even with that, the foundation is solid. The setting is unique, the vibe is strong, and it’s one of those games you can chill with for hours without realizing it. If the developers keep building on this, it can easily grow into one of the best entries in the series.    If you want a beautiful, easy-to-get-into city builder with a Roman twist, this is definitely worth jumping into.     Imagine this city builder with Total war fighting would be crazy!

This is a refreshingly polished game release. Anno 117 is similar to Anno 1800 with a new setting, a few new features, and improved graphics. It suffers from feeling a little hollow since it follows a game with several DLC packs, but it's a fun game worth a purchase.    Graphics wise, I thought Anno 1800 was pretty enough, but Anno 117 really did add more to beauty of the game. This is most evident when you zoom in.    Performance wise, on my GTX 4080 I have no issues with performance on default settings at 1440p. My fan does blow a little louder than when running Anno 1800, but nothing worrying.    Gameplay wise, I like a lot of the new additions. I'm not yet a fan of diagonal zoning and it feels like it needs a little polish, but luckily you can just toggle off the diagonal zoning. The research progression is fun and adds a new level of strategy without being overwhelming. The choice between Roman/Celtic could use a little balancing but it's interesting. This game suffers from having a prequel with many DLC packs; compared to Anno 1800 the content feels sparse.    Campaign wise, I thought it was a little short and simple compared to Anno 1800. It would have been nice to have an Act III or at least some indication that the campaign was done. Having a quest pop up right after the campaign is done did confuse me a little. That said, I don't play Anno for the Campaign so it's not that important to me.    Value wise, I think the base game would be fairly priced if this wasn't a DLC heavy franchise. It does feel like the campaign would be longer, more areas would be available, and the game would be more polished if Ubisoft wasn't reliant on adding things in a DLC pack. But since I can play this game for hundreds of hours I feel like it's worth the price in the end.    Elephant in the room time - the launcher is annoying and sometimes prevents you from playing the game. It puts overlay stuff on your game sometimes and Ubisoft tries to entice you to go to their launcher to spend more money on cosmetics. I want nothing to do with this launcher and it will only do me harm. But so far, no issues.

Long time Anno Fan. I'll cut to the short of it - I recommend this to fans of the Anno series and for those who are just beginning. It's more forgiving to pick up and play, which is a plus for new players over the previous installments. While Anno 1800 is absolutely fantastic, it can feel daunting to play it now with all of the additional content.  PAX PROS: - Fantastic Graphics. Easily the best in the series to date. - Incredible World Building. - Rome Theme is fantastic and they really nailed it. - Ground Combat which actually plays nicely and fits the setting well. A good assortment of troops you can pick from to create your armies. - Easier to pick up and learn than Anno 1800. - Some UI Changes which I've learned to appreciate. - Modular Ship design which is cool.  PAX CONS: - Some QOL things have been removed, or just forgotten.       * Ability to see trade routes between islands at a glance, and where the ships to those routes are on the map. Previously you could hover over the trade route and it would highlight this information, now I have to click in and actually open the trade route to see it. Not well thought out when you have dozens of trade routes and are looking for something specific. Anno 1800 did it better.       * The inability to see Passive trade. If you aren't looking at NPC Ships when they come to your port to buy/sale, then you wont know what they actually sold you, or bought, and how much. - Specialist seem to have been nerfed somewhat, still useful, just not as strong as they were previously. - Campaign abruptly ends without closure.  - Needs more ship types that can be built. - Released with Multiplayer being completely broken, which is a huge shame.   Is it better than Anno 1800 right now? No. Is it better than Anno 1800 at release? Maybe. I feel Anno 1800 still had more content when compared to Anno 117. However, with additional DLC, which the first year looks promising, I can see this becoming one of, if not the, best Anno to date.

There is something off with this anno compared to 1800.  It’s not the lack of content (and boy does it lack content), but the game design itself.  The supply chains feel simplified too much. There is no meaningful interplay between the regions. For example, you can get to max tier without going to the other region.  There is very little reuse of intermediate products.  A lot of crops can be grown on any island limiting the amount of trade routes required, and then there are some consumer items that don’t provide either money or population so you can almost just skip them if the rest of your island stats are good(and aqueducts are so op once you have them your stats will be good.)  I’ve played a few different runs now and my last run I got to max tier in about 4-5 hours, and max tier just doesn’t feel satisfying enough to grind out large cities like in 1800.  It’s just kinda meh. 6/10.  Also there are enough bugs and crashes for me to say this game needed at least another 3 months in the oven before release. Having to wait a whole month for the first patch is just insane. Clown devs.

Clicking on a (group of) building (-s) to put down a road around them, not being limited to iron/gold/.../... mines in set locations, but rather a fertility of mountains and letting the player pick the one they want/need, the tech tree that makes building easier in various ways. All in all, it's a pretty decent Anno game, although I'm not sure I would've paid full price knowing what I know now.

There are a lot of bad reviews for this game and I have to disagree with most of them. There are comments that say the production chains are less complex than anno 1800, That's blatantly not true, the chains start exactly the same as 1800 does and achieves more complexity by tier 3 population, a lot faster than anno does.     There are a bunch of reviews complaining that the campaign is not finished and people are mad that act 3 is not in the game at launch.  Personally I couldn't care less about this, I don't play anno for some sort of complex and intricate story, if that's why you want to play Anno I think you are playing the wrong game.     Albion is completely different from The New World.  Unlike 1800 the first province expansion is much more complex with more than just 2 population tiers and interesting differences in the production chains and interesting choices to make about what type of society you have. It took until the New World DLC in 1800 for the New World to feel like it was an actual place you could build.  That took literally years, during which time the New World was disappointingly lacking in mechanics and content. Albion does not feel like that at all imho.     At first glance diplomacy feels simpler, but it functions the exact same way that anno 1800's diplomacy works, The only difference is that 117 does not hide your reputation interactions with the ai.  It is still just a number that goes up and down depending on your interactions with the AI. The difference in 117 is that you get a break down of all your previous decisions and how they are impacting your reputation score.  Its still just a number that goes up and down, but there is more transparency on how your number is being calculated.     Overall I would give the game an 8 out of 10 at launch. I got exactly what I expected from a DLC focused game that is going to add more content over the life of the game. Is the game as deep as anno 1800, of course not.  Will it be just as deep as 1800 by the time there are 4 seasons of DLC like there was for 1800?  Yes I do think it will, the amount of depth in this launch version with no DLC is already more than the base anno 1800 provided.     Pros:  Graphics  Combat Mechanics  Research/faith mechanics  Unique population tiers in the regions that feel unique from each other and not just a rehash in a new place.    Cons:  Price, I paid this much for BG3. There will be more content in the future for 117 but for a full price triple A game it does feel lacking just a little bit for the price point. I do agree that at least completing the campaign before launching would have helped with this, but I'm not going to down vote the game for that.   Currently no map/seed selection options that I saw.   Not a lot of banners and portraits available through the in game progression system.   Lots of Female portraits that all look great but very few Male portraits that I enjoy the look of.     Clearly a lot of love and effort has gone into the game, from the beautiful building models to the new mechanics there is a lot of care that has gone into making the base game. Would recommend if you are a fan of colony/city building simulation games.  Would not recommend if you care about a deep engaging campaign story (As if 1800s story was complex, I don't understand the hate on this campaign, 1800s campaign was not something to write home about on launch. Its barely noteworthy with all the DLCs included.)

Edit: Ok after getting to the endgame, I have to say it's sadly really half baked.  The controller support led to very questionable design decisions and buggy/unintuitive UIs. Performance is also very bad, when you build the Colloseum and have games on will tank your FPS 70% lower. Ship path finding is at a new low, really buggy mess and annoying. Notification voicelines are firing over and over or not at all. Multiplayer desyncs a lot.  Great new Anno with some really cool improvements to the mechanics. Especially that the final production buildings give boosts to your surrounding buildings. Makes cities so much better looking and planing more interesting.

The game's campaign is cut off randomly, as everyone else has said, which sucks. They clearly ran out of time. But everything else is exactly what you could want from a Roman Anno game. Years ago after 1800 was wrapping up its last DLCs I remember saying "They should make a Roman one and call it 117" and here it is, exactly as I imagined.  The diagonal building mechanics are a massive innovation if you spend time beautifying cities, the ground combat is actually satisfying, and the two regions it starts with are beautiful. The potential for new regions around the classical world is obvious.  They've taken Anno 1800, improved a ton of mechanics and added actual QoL improvements. Things like being able to set all commodities to trade at once from the port, instead of each one individually. The potential for DLC is huge with this game. And if they are on the scale of 1800s DLC then I'm happy to buy it.  Performance is great, at high settings on a 10 year old PC I'm getting 50fps. No crashes, no bugs at all.

Game is buggy at launch, just like most games these days.  To be fair, this game shows a lot of promise to eventually be better than Anno 1800.   There's one particular mechanic with a "happiness tax" exponentially proportional to island population. I haven't decided yet whether or not I like this. It will definitely influence playstyles to inhabit more islands and ship more cargo between them, but the days of maxing out a Crown Falls like paradise island appears to be impossible due to this mechanic alone. So, densely populated areas absolutely have unhappy residents. At least that's realistic IRL.  Discovery and use of a series of distinct deities (polytheistic gods) is one of the largest core progressions in the entire game. Ubisoft somehow in their wisdom decided to link the deities to the online-ness of your Ubisoft Connect agent/spyware account, such that if your single player game gets autosaved even one time with your Ubisoft agent offline, half of the deities disappear from the UI and can no longer be found or chosen within that game save. The recommended workaround is to either revert to a previous save (losing days or weeks of progress) or start a new game (always losing all progress). This is where I park all playtime until patched.  4 of the first 30 quests I encountered/accepted were impossible to finish. Worse yet, I cannot even fail out of or dismiss the unfinishable quests, so they just clog up the active quest list.  https://www.ubisoft.com/en-us/game/anno/117-pax-romana/bug-reporter/issues I recommend referring to the bug report list here and waiting to purchase or play this game until the vast majority of them are resolved by patches, or until the game goes on sale for $9.99, whichever comes first.

A solid entry into the series that still needs further work to truly be great. The basics of this game are the same as Anno 1800 for the most part and work just as well as they did there – if you enjoyed that game, you will enjoy this one. The best new change is the addition of area of effect bonuses/penalties for industrial building – this encourages mixing residential and production buildings rather than building separate districts. This makes for a great puzzle in figuring out where to put things and which bonuses to pursue. I hope they stick with this and improve upon this good foundation over the next year, and I look forward to playing more once updates start rolling in.  Some pros and cons: + Setting is very well done – Latium is beautiful, Albion is atmospheric + Music is brilliant, and as epic as the setting deserves + The range of goods to produce is plentiful and varied, and allowing you to choose which to produce to progress is a welcome change +Reintroduction of land military is simple but functional enough, a welcome return + The building design is very well done, particularly the larger buildings/monuments + The research tree works well + Diagonal roads and buildings are a great addition, but are still a bit glitchy  - The game lacks overall polish – the UI is often clunky and difficult to interact with and get information from, and there are many bugs and glitched assets. Much of the UI in Anno 1800 was superior and it is unclear why changes were made for the PC version of this game – specifically the diplomacy screen, financial statistics, trade, and how NPC dialogue and interaction appears. - An overlay system for the new attributes (health, happiness, fire risk) is sorely needed. It is often a case of guesswork figuring out how to address these scores when they are low. - The use of AI for much of if not all the loading screens and specialist art is disappointing and seems to be the product of rushing development to meet deadlines. The quality of these assets is noticeably poor and does detract from the experience. Hopefully all of these are replaced with actual assets rather than left with the current placeholders. - The lack of enough 3-star opponents leads the only available one to dominate every match without competition beyond the player. The opponents rarely seems to go to war with or interact with each other. - While the ability to choose which goods to produce to level up is nice, it is odd that you can reach the final level of each region without ever needing to trade between them. The game overall demands far less interaction with trade routes than previous entries, which is odd. - The workforce requirement for military and ships is an interesting idea but is far too punishing on the player – the required workforce for ships is excessive and requires tweaking. - The range of quests in sandbox mode is limited and leads to interactions with the NPCs and opponents feeling stale

This is my first foray into the Anno franchise, and I'd say it's a good place to start. It's not particularly hand-holdy in the campaign mode (the only one I've tried), which allows you to substantially over-develop cities that you're probably going to hate having to reorganise later. There's a couple of things I dislike about it at a macro level, namely that you can't just push a button to see how given production buildings interact, or precisely where your city needs aren't being met. But overall it's a very playable game. I've sunk nearly a day into it so far and literally every play session has ended with "oh **** it's 3am again?". I can count on one hand the number of games this year that have had that level of engagement for me.   I'm also looking forward to finally developing my settlements enough to start engaging properly with the combat mechanics. What little I've seen of them so far suggests that there's a decent level of tactical flexibility, and if you know what you're doing, you can probably absolutely break the game balance with the right level of economic prosperity/excess labour.   I like the tone of the game too. It's generally quite relaxed, and in my 20 hours with it I haven't found myself getting sick of the music, where I'd often have muted it and started streaming it in the background otherwise. You get nice little voice-lines whenever you interact with a property, often charming and kind of funny. Good starter tip also: click on your player character icon at the top of the screen and look at the "hall of fame" and "accolades" stuff. You can unlock free battle-pass like stuff by getting "totally not achievements" that can really help in early-game, like free concrete, research unlocks, specialists to boost particular city attributes.

Coming from 1000+ hours with Anno 1800, it feels lacking in character and charm compared to its predecessor (DLC aside). Gameplay feels clumsy rather than satisfying, like the organization of quests, trying to find financial information i.e passive trade information and looking at building buffs requires a double click-through. It just makes the pacing a bit tedious.   The music (one of the best things about 1800) is nice but repetitive and other tracks are locked away behind an in-game point system which feels a bit strange. The research tree is a great idea but some things seem arbitrary and a time-waste (tree replanting, warehouse/dock upgrades). I also read that expeditions are not planned to be in the game which is really disappointing; Rome, if any time period, seems to be a perfect setting for building collections from across the empire. It was also very odd to just jump to Albion with no story/build-up or effort needed to get there. A bit lacklustre.  The performance of this game is also absolutely terrible. I feel like I spent more time trying to get the game to work rather than playing it. I would expect more from a $60 game.  Lastly, the whole debacle of poor quality AI backgrounds has soured the artistic element of the game for me, which was so appreciated in Anno 1800.  Edit: I have been playing multiplayer since this review. It can take +30 minutes of restarting the game and tweaking save files to load the game, this is also following a recent patch meant to fix multiplayer issues. The game loop once you get in is enjoyable, but I personally still wouldn't recommend due to lacking QoL & absurd multiplayer experience.

I’ve never been much of an Anno player and these kinds of games aren’t usually my thing, but my wife wanted to give it a try—and honestly, I’m really enjoying it! It feels like a refreshed take on Anno 1800, to say the least.    Graphics: To me, the graphics feel very similar to Anno 1800. That’s not a bad thing—the visuals in 1800 were already impressive, and this game maintains that same quality. It’s a good-looking title overall, and with the settings maxed out, it runs smoothly even when managing larger cities.    Audio: The audio is solid overall—good music, clear sound design, and a nice atmosphere. The AI voices sound very similar to those in Anno 1800, and even the NPCs oddly have accents that feel more Bristol (UK) than Rome. Authentic Roman accents aren’t really there, but honestly, it’s not a big deal—the audio works well and fits the game.    Controls: The controls can be a bit confusing at first since not everything is clearly explained, but you gradually figure out which keys to use as you play.    Game-play and everything else after: This game is a city builder at its core, and the ability to tailor each match makes for a highly customizable experience. If you’ve played previous Anno titles, you’ll have a good idea of what to expect here.    It feels like a modernized version of Anno 1800, with certain features added and others removed. For example, the new religion mechanic is a welcome addition. On the other hand, the fertility system from 1800 is gone—in that game, you could purchase fertility items from the trader and place them in a town hall to expand crop options. In Anno 117, this has been replaced entirely by specialists, and so far I haven’t come across one that restores fertility. It’s a bit odd and takes some getting used to.    Co-op is one of the highlights of the game—when it works. Unfortunately, desync issues are still present at the time of this review, which makes the experience frustrating, though hopefully it gets patched in the future. I think the co-op supports like 64 people or something, it's a LOT and can get busy.    You can play on the same team, but that means sharing the same island, which feels odd. Alternatively, you can play separately, but then you need to set up trade deals. Compared to Anno 1800, where you could simply join the same team and get started, these changes feel unnecessarily complicated.    On the positive side, there’s plenty of micromanagement to dive into, with skills to earn and features to unlock, which adds depth compared to earlier Anno titles. I keep referencing 1800 because it was the previous entry in the series and is widely regarded as one of the best.    This is a slow-paced yet relaxing city-building game where plenty can happen—citizens might riot, fall ill, start fires, or indulge in alcohol. There’s always something to manage, but it’s also a chance to sit back and shape your city however you like. From farming and trade ships to warfare and even conquering new worlds, the game offers a lot of depth. Expect long sessions, which is a good thing if you enjoy building and expanding over time.    Would I recommend the game? Yes, without a doubt it's a fun city builder and co-op which offers a different experience. Is it worth the full price? Not really, cheaper would be better but still it's a fun game!

I understand where some of the negative reviews are coming from, but I simply don’t agree with them. A lot of the criticism seems rooted in the fact that Anno 1800 is overflowing with years of DLC and expansions, which makes Anno 117 feel a bit “empty” by comparison. But that’s not a fair comparison: Anno 117 is at the very beginning of its lifecycle, and judged on its own merits, it’s a remarkably strong Anno title.  The atmosphere of the Roman Empire is beautifully crafted, with great attention to detail in both architecture and landscapes. The new mechanics feel fresh, such as the regional differences and the way trade and infrastructure are implemented. It’s true that the core gameplay mechanics haven’t been given a dramatic overhaul — but honestly, they didn’t need one. Anno 1800 had already perfected the formula, and Anno 117 wisely builds on that strong foundation rather than changing things for the sake of change.  The game plays smoothly and intuitively, inviting you to build “just a little more” every time you think you’re done. To me, Anno 117 delivers exactly what a new Anno entry should: a solid base game, a fantastic setting, and enough depth to sink countless hours into. And if the series’ history is anything to go by, this is only the beginning. I’m already excited to see how the game will grow over the coming years — but even without future expansions, what’s here is already excellent.  In short: don’t let comparisons to a game with a five-year content head start overshadow what Anno 117 actually offers. Judged on its own, Anno 117 is absolutely a standout city builder.

Anno 117 has been another fantastic title in the Anno Series. It had a few issues at bugs but at launch but most are resolved now. This game being set during the Roman setting.   Anno games if you're unaware are economy games based on setting up production chains. They also have many city builder and RTS elements such as the ability to create military troops and invade other islands. The core of the game is you start on a single island and need to expand. This is because as you grow your population you upgrade houses which provide the next tier of worker, but a building can only provide a single time. The higher tier the worker the more needs wants they have.   Fulfilling those needs not only allows you to upgrade the house but higher tier workers provide more income in taxes and more population. But it becomes a balancing act since higher tier workers won't work low tier jobs. If you have Nobles they will not work farms, so you are required to keep a population of every tier. (though there are ways around this)  You must expand to more island because each island will have a limited amount of resources it can provide. Islands will only have fertility for certain crops or there mountains only certain types or minable material. As you get high tier workforce you will need to setup trade routes between islands. Another aspect of Anno games are regions. At launch this game has two. Latium (Italy) and Albion (Britain). These separate regions not only have certain unique resources to them but also different needs and types of workers. Which means middle and end game you are setting up trade between regions to full fill needs. It can be very satisfying to get those complex trade routes finally working sending goods across the regions.   The game also has military. In the form of naval and land combat. You ♥♥♥♥♥ are generally going to be civilian used only for trade or a warship which still could do trade but has limited cargo space. You are able to customize your military ships weapons and utility. Land forces you unlock through research and it is more about managing the workforce/population to have an army. Armies are expensive. You can also build walls and towers to defend you settlements from attack. War in Anno is often expensive and a long process. Taking an island by force isn't a simple task.   Anno 117 has a short campaign which was fun but your mileage will vary. At the heart of it Anno games are open sandbox with no win condition. In 117 there is a loose an end goal of building a colosseum which you can host games at. The "soft" victory in Anno games is usually who builds the first monument and triggers an event at it. My first game of 177 it took me about 20 hours to achieve this. That said you can continue until you eliminate all the opponents off the map. That said usually you get to a point where you know you won or lost. You are powerful enough no one can stop you or your enemies out grew you.   City Builder Econ RTS games are not for everyone but I still suggest checking out Anno 117. It is one of my top 5 games of 2025.

They fixed the unplayable multiplayer bugs.  As a master Anno 1800 player with over 2k hours and a history of playing Anno 1701 and all others (including the black sheep, 2205) since childhood, Anno 117 is an interesting mix on new and old mechanics.  THE GOOD:  Albion is the most interesting way to start the game, while Latium offers the classic Anno experience.   COMBAT - 4/5 I was in the slight majority that voted to KEEP ground combat before Anno 1800 and I am ecstatic to A) have it back and B) have it better than ever. Ground combat is engaging with enough depth to be fun but not too much to be annoying. It is optional if that isn't your thing.  Modular ships are neat. I feel like I prefer the old items way in 1800 more, I don't really feel like modules make much change vs the pirates or the emperor since both are wickedly OP compared to your ships (in health and damage).   I wish the difficulty added more enemies, rather than increasing their health because the super health ships are just unsatisfying to shoot at. Enemy AI appears to have 4x the health of your ships and I just find it to be the least satisfying naval combat, which is ironic considering how good the ground combat is.   CITY BUILDING - 5/5 There are a few good improvements to designing your city. Aqueducts and the new huge city buildings (that are semi-optional) change the way you make a city. While past Annos are about supplying services, I found myself fighting the fire, plague and happiness stats even more than I was providing the needed services. It's different but I don't think its better or worse.  Customizing your city is better than ever. It is the best in any Anno as some of the fields and other buildings add bonuses to houses, such as Lavender fields. I think this leads to a 117 unique city layout where all previous Annos are distinctly clumped cities, this one is truly different.   Walls are fun to place, and fun to strategize with.  ECONOMY - 4/5 I think the new optional goods are neat, but as an expansive 100% player, I find myself just making every good anyways. I like supply chains so this doesn't bother me at all but I have not played this the way I suspect is intended by the devs.   I find myself missing the happiness and lifestyle goods and their effects from 1800.  GAME TIME - N/A Anno 1800 is my favorite Anno and likely my favorite game, so I do miss the 10+ levels of houses that came with High Life in 1800. I feel like I beat Anno 117 in under 40 hours but Anno 1800 games go on for well over 500. 1800 has 5 years of content backing it up and I'm sure 117 will age well with new regions and DLC.  GRAPHICS AND PERFORMANCE - 1/5 Guys, what the ♥♥♥♥? Why does Anno need to be raytraced? Anno 1800 ran pretty bad, but Anno 117 is just slow and frankly, it just doesn't look any better than 1800. I wish they had chosen to just optimize rather than jump on the BS bandwagon. I get under 60 at 4k on a 3090 and under 40 in the end game. It doesn't even really look THAT good to justify it. It looks as good as 1800 to me but runs 4x worse. It's just unacceptable to me. I will spend time toying with the graphics and see if I can make it better.  TRADE ROUTES - 3/5 - UPDATED 5/16/26 They finally restored the shortcuts from 1800. They are much easier to use now. They're slower overall and I think that is intentional. Still looking for more UI improvements and more parity with 1800 (hovering, clicking on ships etc.) Needs to be easier like it was in past titiles.  OVERALL - 3/5.  Combat is great. Cities are great, building is good. Goods are cool. I need more content. I dont like the ship-health of the enemies. Trade routes need fixed desperately.   If you have to choose between Anno 117 at $60 or Anno 1800 Complete on Sale for $60 and you don't need combat to have fun, go Anno 1800 10/10 times. Anno 117 has some maturing to do.  This review will be updated regularly.

I LOVE THIS GAME.  I've played now for 52 hours and about to put in another few after I submit this short review. I'm sure they will come and the game will only get better.  As for it being buggy, I can honestly say that aside from 1 bug (where a citizen seems to step on a trampoline every few steps on one particular island) I have not encountered any, and I'm playing this on an older system with a Ryzen 5 3600 processor and an AMD Ryzen 6800 video card as well as on a 2 year old Legion Pro 5 laptop sporting an NVidia RTX 4060 with 32GB of RAM.

The new gameplay loop is much more satisfying than before. They found good balance for first 2 level builds for multiplayer skirmish and level 4 and end of the tree builds that take 6 to 10 islands to optimise and complete. Not to mention game is beautiful and walking around your own city really made me change how I design and solve problems. No more perfect grids and template builds. I even decorate like I am playing sims 3 just to have that perfect sunset. Fully built islands are wallpaper and screenshot heaven. Good game. Goes back to the series earlier roots with iterations like Anno 1404 by reintroducing land armies. This game introduces a tech tree, which has been an interesting change. And it improves replayability by giving you more options on which production chains to build instead of forcing you to build all of them. Like Anno 1800, island development is significantly slowed by requiring workforces on each island.   It also improves on Anno 1800 by streamlining some of the production chains, which became too complicated and unwieldly after a while for many players. Most of all, they got rid of the annoying Royal Taxes mechanism in Anno 1800. They did a better job balancing income to production costs in Anno 117, so you don't need the flimsy Royal Tax mechanism they stapled onto Anno 1800 that financially penalizes metropolitan development in a metropolitan development game.   People seem to be complaining about the campaign on here, which is a completely fair criticism but not a dealbreaker for me. I didn't realize that many people played Anno games for the campaigns. To my knowledge, they've usually been alright but not great. Though I can understand it definitely felt anti-climatic, since it was. The campaign was better in Anno 1404 since it introduced all major features and culminated in the monument building in a way this campaign did not. But I have always played them for the sandbox and multiplayer so it didn't bother me that much.

Since I see a lot of negative comments here, I would like to offer some insight from someone who has never played Anno before.  I think it's a shame that there are so many negative reviews because of minor aspects. I'm not a big fan of Ubisoft or AAA titles, but I think it's important to recognize good games. Even though people here call themselves Anno veterans, it doesn't feel like they're real gamers.  It's a shame that more and more negative reviews are being posted without evaluating the game as a whole, just because one thing is negative.   Gameplay: - The gameplay is a lot of fun and feels very fluid throughout. - It feels intuitive and it's easy for a newcomer to immerse themselves in this game world. - The building feels complex, but also logical and coherent.  Music: - The music is very beautiful, fitting, and gives a Roman feel, and can be listened to for long periods of time.  Campaign: - For a solo game that I expect to be endless, I also get a campaign, which is no longer the norm today. The campaign is okay, there are better ones, but it's fine as an introduction.  General: I like how the advisors are portrayed, giving me useful tips. It also looks nice and feels like a living world.   Negatives: There are still some bugs and unsatisfactory elements, such as the fact that when I select multiple buildings, I can only rotate them at a 90° angle instead of 45°. The multiplayer desync. 1-year pass – this is an outdated model.  But all in all, I think it's a very good game with a lot of fun and replay value. Don't be intimidated by the negative reviews. ----------------------------------------------------- German: Da ich hier viele negative Kommentare sehe, möchte ich gern dazu ein Einblick geben von jemanden, der zuvor noch kein Anno gespielt hat.  Ich finde es schade, dass es wegen Kleineren Aspekten so viel negative Bewertungen gibt. Ich bin kein großer Freund von Ubisoft oder AAA Titeln, jedoch finde ich es wichtig, gute Spiele anzuerkennen. Auch wenn sich hier Leute Anno Veteranen nennen, fühlt es sich nicht an, als wären es wirkliche Gamer.  Es ist sehr schade, dass immer mehr schnell negative Reviews raus gehauen wird ohne das gesamte Spiel zu beurteilen, nur weil eine Sache negativ ist.   Gameplay: - Das Gameplay macht sehr viel Spaß und fühlt sich sehr flüßig im gesamten Ablauf an. - Es fühlt sich intuitiv an und es fällt einem Neuling leicht in diese Spielwelt einzutauchen - Das Aufbauen fühlt sich komplex, jedoch auch logisch und zusammenhängend an.  Musik: - Die Musik ist sehr schön, passend und gibt ein Römisches feeling und kann auch auf dauer gehört werden  Kampagne: - Dafür das ich ein Solospiel im Endlos erwarte, bekomme ich noch eine Kampagne dazu, was heute nicht mehr die Norm ist. Die Kampagne ist okay, gibt besseres, jedoch als Einstieg ganz in Ordnung.  Allgemeines: Ich finde es ist schön mit den Beratern dargestellt, die mir Sinnvolle Hinweise geben. Auch optisch sieht es schön an und fühlt sich wie eine lebendige Welt.  Negatives: Es gibt noch einige Bugs und unzufriedenstellende Elemente, wie das ich wenn ich mehrere Gebäude wähle diese nur im 90° Winkel drehen kann, statt 45°. Der Multiplayer Desync. 1 Jahres Pass - das ist ein veraltetes Model.  Aber alles in allem, ist es für mich ein sehr gutes Spiel, mit einer Menge Spielspaß und wieder Spielwert. Lasst euch nicht von den negativen Rezensionen einschüchtern.

After 40+ hours in Anno 117: Pax Romana, I can honestly say this is a well-built game with a strong core foundation. Yes, there are bugs and rough edges, but let’s be realistic, even Anno 1800, one of the best entries in the entire franchise, had similar launch issues. What matters is the underlying quality, and Anno 117 definitely has it. The atmosphere, the pacing, and the economic layers all feel true to the Anno spirit. The setting is refreshing, the mechanics are already engaging, and there’s a clear direction for depth and expansion.  What also gives me confidence is the Anno community. This series has one of the most dedicated player bases and, most importantly, a fantastic modding community. Anno 1800 became the masterpiece it is today thanks to years of updates, balancing, DLCs, and an active mod scene. I can easily see the same thing happening here.  Give this game 1–2 years of updates, patches, and community support, and I genuinely believe Anno 117 will stand shoulder-to-shoulder with (or even surpass) Anno 1800. If you enjoy city-building, logistics, and the unique Anno gameplay loop, this is absolutely worth jumping into early.

I've been anticipating this game for a good long while now.  Highly recommend it. Yes, launch has been rough and the campaign seems to have been cut short by an act. The AI stuff was overblown and admittedly it's deserved criticism.  Despite that there is a very deep, very engaging set of systems here that Anno veterans will find hundreds of hours of enjoyment in. If you've played 1404, accolades are back.   I will say, we all seem to forget the state that Anno 1800 launched in and are immediately comparing this entry to one that has over 6yrs of work put into it refining and improving systems.  The setting is done beautifully and there is a lot of great care taken with the lebel of detail. The animations, the building designs and the liveliness of the world. If you've played 1800 you can expect a greater attention to detail here.  I particularly like how you can invite rivals into ongoing games, vassalising or conquering them and then using them as a specialist. They all have alliance effects too boosting certain parts of your economy.  The religion system is interesting too as it provides local island buffs and a global bonus to that patron's focus (Ceres boosts farms, Neptune boosts naval vessels, Mars boosts military unit stats, etc)  I look forward to seeing 117 fleshed out.   My only real criticism is the Ubiconnect launcher, it's just entirely unnecessary. I just hope the Mainz team can keep 117 when Ubislop goes under finally.

It still isn't quite on par with Anno 1800 Complete, but the gameplay, performance, and stability fixes released since launch are heading in the right direction.     Compared to 1800, Anno 117 offers an interesting branching system for population development (Celts or Romano-Celts) that presents a very tough challenge, as well as a well-researched historical accuracy regarding the industries and agriculture of the Roman era. Even if the game isn’t yet worth buying for owners of 1800, it lays an interesting foundation that promises a very rich experience once more substantial DLCs are released.    There are still some rather poor design decisions that need to be revisited: in particular, the management of happiness, research, and health risks, which disrupt the flow too much and remain unclear even after dozens of hours of play.    But when it comes to managing population thresholds and production chains, the game already shows great finesse, and that’s undoubtedly the most important aspect for an Anno game.    Let’s hope the devs keep up their efforts, and in a year or two we might finally be able to put Anno 1800 to rest for good. Fingers crossed.

it's anno. if you like their formula, you'll like this game. people are overreacting to AI placeholders making it into the game. they obviously have skilled artists that made a pretty big number of legitimate, man-made splash art. they all look awesome btw, kudos to the artist. i really don't think they just reeeaallly needed one more and were like, eh, we can't spend another couple hundred bucks for this one last splash screen.  anyway, yeah, it's anno through and through. it's a logistics-based city builder, so there are lots of little products and supply chains to manage, like a baby version of factorio. the city builder part is like a baby version of... well, any city builder. i've been playing since 1404, and this feels like basically the same game, but each new iteration has new toys tacked on. this one has land warfare (kind of lame imo) and a tech tree (it's a little boring; lots of passive upgrades instead of neat new things to unlock). you can customize ships, but it's pretty shallow. aqueducts are cool. the big roman buildings look and feel awesome. The game's AI isn't awesome, and there's a lot of tedious nitpicking to handle quests and diplomacy and war.  the big addition here is diagonal roads, which are both awesome and obnoxious, but the game sticks to a strict grid system, so you're not going to end up with things too weirdly misaligned like Cities Skylines or something.  i know i didn't say much nice about this game, but i really do love it. anno has always been a bunch of okay but kinda janky systems strung together, and when they get it right (they did here) it really is much greater than the sum of its parts.  this one won't blow you away, i think, but anno isn't a blow you away kind of series. anno is like the epitome of gaming comfort food. sometimes you just want a plate of cheese fries.  anno is if a game were cheese fries. i love cheese fries.

I have to say that this game feels a bit disappointing at the moment. I saw many people comparing it to ANNO 1800 with all the DLCs.. Of course it doesn't compare, and I would argue that it doesn't compare to the base game of ANNO 1800 too. Somehow it feels less responsive, rich and enticing as the previous installation.  The main roman islands with only 4 civilian tiers feel somehow simplistic. And the game progression feels slow in general. The research tree slows things down as well, making a slight feel of a hindrense rather then an additional exciting thing that allows for a much greater gameplay. I do like the research tree as a whole, but why block some of the pantheon behind it? Or the ability to upgrade the port and warehouse? Especially when I can easily just spam more warehouses or if are honest, ignore the pantheon all together?  Also the AI feels very.. Docile? I don't feel like I'm in any competition playing against it, not in the campaign nor the sandbox. There is not much interaction with the AI, not hostile nor friendly during a match, while in 1404 and 1800 I would feel their existance throughout the match.  Trading route establishment is also much worse. Now you can't tell the ships to wait until full, drop excess cargo and the like. I do miss the stamps option, after you have stamps (in the base version of 1800) you can't go back.  I love the graphics, music, and placement mechanics (horizontal and circular designs, and how it all fit together so nicely). I love the new ship design and land battles. The setting of anciant rome is great, and with the mystic of anciant celtic traditions, I can see many great adventures and conflicts that can be disgned around it. The ability to pick and choose different goods to use to upgrade the civilian tiers is great, although to be honest except for the very end, I would not give up on any of them as they are easy to obtain and also very benefitial, so why not?  In general the game feel to simplistic and easy, espacially compared to the base game of ANNO 1800. While there is a huge potential here to make it a much greater game (the setting, engine and most of the mechanics are great additions for single and multiplayer games) I'm disappointed that like most say, it seems like the "great" game will be hidden behind DLCs rather then have a great standalone with some DLCs making it even better.

It is a half decent entry into the Anno Universe with a few nice improvements (land combat, area effect buildings etc.).  That being said: The old Anno / Ubisoft formula is still in effect. Basically more than half the game has been stripped out to be sold as DLCs over the coming years.  Right now, you should compare Anno 1800 to Anno 117. 117 is the new shiny thing, but 1800 has so much more content, so many more features.... you can play it for hundreds of hours and not be done yet. 117 however, can be "done" in a matter of a casual week.  Look. I am not saying the game is terrible... because it is not. It just is not worth the money yet. Too little content and its own prequel is just so much better for much less money.  If you are looking at this and don't own Anno 1800 yet. Get that one. If you have played 1800 too much already,... still strongly consider if your urge for more Anno is worth full price for 10-15% of a game. Because that is what Anno 117 is right now. It is a nice base to build on, but I can hardly see it being more than 15% of the full deal.  For now: Avoid this, unless you really just need another Anno in your life and you don't mind the cash being sucked out of your pocket for little more than a generous demo of what the game will be in a year or two.

It's Anno. I may be biased, because I play Anno since Anno 1602.   After  70 hours on the slowest pace in the campaign game now, I think it is really really really good! It is NOT as complex as Anno 1800 + All DLCs at this time, BUT they really made a great structure for the future and added some awesome mechanics!   The campaign end apruptly... But I don't really care, because this is a sandbox game. And it will be continued in the future, I guess. I use that campaign savegame to unlock everything and to learn the mechanics.   The new stuff:  1. You cant have "unlimited" storage anymore on islands, so that it is more important to actually plan functioning routes and productions,  2. You can build very beautiful stuff with diogonal harbour and road stuff, with stone, marble and wooden planks.  3. There a lots of decisions you have to make inbetween 4. More diplomacy options 5. More and better war options, troops, castle building. :) 6. A more dynamic ship building 7. Research / knowledge and religion features.  8. The area effects of buildings are a great way to "try to get it all" or to specialize a city".  9. 2 different starting reagions, with more depth than the new world in anno 1800 at release. 10. more options for multiplayer, even in existing single player savegames.  11. Adjustable AI opponents. Want to play for yourself and then add AI characters one after another into your savegame? Just do it. Defeated the pirate-factions and got bored? Just add them in again. :D  Things I don't like: 1. There are not many flag and portrait options to choose from for the player profile, even when you unlocked "everything" - there will be probably more symbols and portraits to unlock in the future from DLCs and with mods?-, but there should be way more, imo - at least symbols!  2. I don't know... It is not as complex as a full DLC Anno 1800, but that needed time to get there and the foundation of Anno 117: Pax Romana is WAY STRONGER with the potential to get way more complex! 3. It's quite easy to upgrade your citizens as you dont need to supply them with everything they ask for, BUT in the end it was "the same" in Anno 1800 with the lifestyle needs that gave some bonuses and lots of income, but were not vital for upgrading your citizens. With this system in Pax Romana, you have more different options to specialize. That may come in handy when you join a savegame, where there are lots of island aleady taken. You have to consider that that is a thing now! :D   All in all: Just buy it. I can't stop playing for days now. It is a very beautiful chill game on low pace while it can provide the typical annostruggles when playing more efficient and aggressive. I can't wait for all the DLC Content!
