Anno Review  I want to start off by saying that I have been having some fun playing the game. Still giving me that Anno feel that sucks me in, logistics, min/maxing the location of my buildings, the beauty building aspect. But I have to say overall I am left disappointed…that said, I think in time this game will get better but I have to judge the game now and will update at a later point when things change.  First the GOOD: -Visually an improvement: Absolute love the Roman and Celtic aesthetic, and going into first person you can easily tell the difference between 1800 and 177 as an improvement. Btw I have around 200+hrs in 1800  -Combat evolves: I love how they added land combat. While it’s not the most advanced/grand strategy army game it’s honestly refreshing and nice they are trying to improve the product by adding just a bit more.  -Citizen needs & production areas of influence: Again, I am glad they are taking risks and changing how things were done while still being Anno. Citizens still need things from prior levels (now someone please correct me if I am wrong but it looks like the needs are less as they evolve: for example when you have Equites they don’t need that many hats as compared to when they were Liberti) and production buildings have areas of influence for good and bad affects making me think of where I place things and giving a reason to have more variety within my min/maxed stacked citizen housing blocks. Wasn’t liking the production buildings among housing but it grew on me.  -Knowledge is key!: I enjoyed the research tree and makes it awarding and guides me to the next step in my progression. Good feature here.  -Diagonal Building!: I can finally really layout things naturally and squeeze things in! The roads are a little wonky with this new feature but overall it’s a thumbs up for me.  -Hall of Fame: I like the challenges and the rewards it gives and it has me look forward to actually wanting to take on some of the more challenging AI and offers re-playability.  But I do wish they gave us more cosmetic items for beautifying my island. I don’t think they gave us enough between what’s already available and locked behind the hall of fame. DLC better provide here.  Now the BAD: -AI slop: I do not condone the use of AI to replace art (honestly based on the dialogue from the campaign I think they used it there too) pay artists and writers!!! Ya’ll tried saving money but it backfired tremendously, AI has not gotten to that point and AI cannot beat out passionate developers, artists, and writers. Why do think the indie scene is defeating AAA and AAAA companies (Ubisoft smh quad A my ass)  -Unappealing Voices: Valeria is probably the only npc that has a voice for the time period while speaking English, and the Celtic npcs get a pass, but everyone else sounds like they are directly from 1800. This takes me out of the game so many times. I cannot stand hearing the npcs talk. And some of the voice lines are very close to 1800, like they just sort of changed some words so they can move on to other things. For example the Liberti say something like “here comes the governor” like the Workers do in 1800…smh  -Underwhelming Campaign: short, unfinished, and just disappointing. I felt rewarded by the end of Anno 1800’s campaign. Not here. This is what tipped me off the most and made me really re-examine things (along with the AI garbage)  -Underwhelming Specialists: While I like the refined re-rolling for specialists at NPCs I stopped doing it after the sheer amount of crap I got offered. Even the legendaries are mid compared to 1800. They give minute increasing (0.6 income to 1.2 income, 10% productivity to 20%) I only saw one or two that replace workforce. I have not gotten to the end of the research tree but maybe I will be surprised (someone tell me before I get disappointed)  -No faith in Religion: I will say the god aspect is a cool feature but I feel like they didn’t give us enough gods here and the gods don’t cover enough things, I feel like I am sticking to just the same three: Minerva, Ceres, and Cernunnos. I will probably use Neptune for maxing Garum to send to Albion when I get that need unlocked in the research tree but honestly underwhelming over time.  -Passive Trade who?: This is a small feature which blows my mind that it is not in the release for he game. I WANT TO KNOW WHO BOUGHT WHAT, HOW MUCH, AND WHAT I GOT! This is in 1800, such a simple thing that will likely be in later but why is this not in now??? Wtf Ubisoft  -Fleet Settings: another small feature/quality of life. Why can’t I set the stance of the overall grouped fleet like in 1800? Why are they leaving out small things?  -Aqueducts headaches: I also like this feature, man does it suck me into the Roman aesthetic: however, I have gotten so annoying with the finicky setup of getting my aqueducts to connect even thou its clearly leading to a lower place. The game is just not smart in altering the heights with multiple locations. I have redone a few given two locations multiple times to get it right. Just frustrating….!!!!  -No day one patch?: I saw there was some update evolving removing the AI and stuff but they said they don’t expect a big patch till mid December? Ubisoft get some bodies on this!  Look at what you are doing to my boy :,(  This is all I have, still engaging with the game. Still more to feel out. Is the enemy AI annoying like 1800. How big can I really take it. And trying to complete the other hall of fame accolades. Again disappointed but I have hope that one day it’ll be better than 1800. As of now you’re better off buying 1800 discounted and playing that instead of spending the high price for this new one which won’t be discounted for a while.

The game is Art, evolved gameplay to enable one to choose how they want to segment there island and grow far larger than i feel we ever were able to grow in the past, brought land combat back finally, navel combat is not as good as the last iteration in the series (to be expected considering the era). The skill tree is quite expansive. Once can do massive selection copy and paste building. Unfortunately it seems a lot harder to leave the game whilst afk as if workers dip below needed the economy crumbles into a downward spiral without micro...   I understand health and safety mechanics that scale to counter increased island population but firespread is oppressive, unrealistic, and  unbalanced imo... I have 4 different cities on my island but the aggregate population makes it way more likely to burn down... there needs to be a density in area mechanic independent of population as it is immersion breaking that so many houses can catch on fire despite them being 3 near-identical towns on 1 island including fire coverage... had to use research to get it down. There's not even value from overlapping firehouses to reduce likelihood of fire... and wells only go so far.  The islands are far bigger, mountain (historically ore) deposits align with island specific resources, so choose what you want/need the most from each one (based on island resources (as it's more than just fertility now).   A.N.N.O. is one iteration away from it's former glory and has surpassed it in many cases. The navel combat leaves one wanting, if A.N.N.O. 1800 had everything this A.N.N.O. has (yes land combat too) then it'd be a masterpiece. Navel combat in the ancient times of this game is lacking. Land combat in the future just wouldn't be believable pacing wise, so the final option is in the era of the musketeer, rifleman, and cannoneer. When navel combat is fun.  That being said, just like ANNO 1800 there are region specific factions skins... yet to be seen if you can mix and match regardless of region. I wonder if different world areas will be even more different just like A.N.N.O. 1800 was.  As with the more recent iterations you can cheese the story mode timing to have a far stronger and oppressive start. What is weird is I did not see a way to customize the game difficulty, or it wasn't as clear as it was in A.N.N.O. 1800's campaign as I simply missed it in this one.

Anno 117: Pax Romana     In regards to the Campaign:  My Friend and I started a playthrough of the campaign together and I have to say this is by far my favorite iteration of an Anno campaign so far. We get proper, more detailed cutscenes than we have ever seen really. I've played 1404, 2070, and 1800 a fair amount and I really love the progression of the story here. Markus is really funny, him being the golden retriever BF to Ben Ballion as his Alan Rickman's esque depressive state is a fun interaction I wasn't expecting. I'm honestly excited for another playthrough of the campaign once they fix the de-sync issues on launch as we opted for a pacifist-ish playthrough and I'd love to see what full on war with the Scathach brings. We wanted to go back to Latium after developing Albion for a while but that's when we hit the biggest wall of desyncs, so I can't speak for anything after that yet.    The Game itself feels like Ubisoft Mainz wanted to combine the elements from their previous games and bring it all into one. They brought in the Eco/Tycoon alt population stuff from 2070 and combined it with a lot of the pleasant updates in 1800. I really like the diagonal building as now I can make my cities that much more organic and it just looks lovely. I do find the snap on feature with roads and buildings a bit irritating. I would love to be able to move a 2x5 of houses and rotate them from diagonal to straight but I understand mathematically there are some constraints there with triangles. One of my favorite elements in Anno games funnily enough is the voice acting. Clicking on the various buildings and hearing them blabber some gibberish in an exaggerated tone makes me giggle. Markus even has a couple of funny quips and it is delightful (no one can replace Willie Wibblesock). One element that takes me out of my immersion is the transition from Latium to Albion. With 1800 you have the expeditions and it feels a lot more impactful to go between regions that way rather than just having the narrative from the campaign imported into sandbox mode. I feel the inclusion of the research tree and religion adds another level city specialization close to how I felt playing CIV 6. I've been knowledge maxing with Minverva and that's cool. I don't think most of the religions will come in handy for me since I don't really enjoy the RTS aspects of Anno, but when I get around to doing an army thing I'll give them a shot. I like having the trade off with putting production buildings in your city, it gives me a reason to make every city different. I personally love throwing in the lavender fields everywhere, they just look beautiful.     I've run into bugs during my playthroughs, such as buildings not being placed when they definitely should have been or upgrading blueprints just doesn't work some times. The multiplayer desync is probably my biggest gripe at the moment, and I hope they fix that soon or at least make resyncing work. Out of all the Ubisoft studios, Mainz is the one I trust most to fix their issues. Can't leave an Anno player hanging after all.    If you already liked 1800, and you've even touched one of the previous Anno's, you will most likely enjoy this one, especially if you want a slightly different flavor of time period in between Anno sessions. It has some release issues, so potentially wait for it to go on sale and snag it when some of the DLC is out, as you'll have some pudding to go with your meal. The whole Ubisoft account thing can be tedious initially but once you've logged in you won't bat an eye.     Final thing, I've seen comments claiming that there is AI artwork in this game and I've personally not seen much evidence of such. I personally love the Anno concept art from 1800, I use that for inspiration in my Minecraft builds a lot of the time so for that to go away in favor of AI art would be dreadful and upset me dearly. The closest thing I've been able to spot regarding AI art is the portrait for Voada and Ceaso (his name escapes me) when selecting their options in the sandbox mode.

The game’s core concept is superb — a genuinely refreshing and long-awaited addition to the Anno franchise, which I, like many long-standing fans, hold in high esteem. However, despite its excellent design, the technical execution is, regrettably, far below acceptable standards.  I am consistently encountering severe D3D12 errors and frequent crashes. In order to even launch the game, I am forced to disable one of my display adapters, which should never be necessary on a modern and well-equipped PC. Even when the game does start, the performance is plagued by visual stuttering and lag, even on the lowest graphical settings.  This issue was widely reported during the demo and still persists after the official release, which suggests that the technical problems were neither adequately addressed nor resolved prior to launch. My system is more than capable of running modern AAA titles effortlessly, so these problems are clearly rooted in poor optimisation and inadequate development work, not hardware limitations.  It is imperative that the development team provide a timely and comprehensive fix. Until then, my enthusiasm is overshadowed by genuine disappointment, as such technical instability undermines an otherwise brilliant vision.

I really wanted to like this game. I loved Anno 1800, but this game didn't do much to innovate off of it. All that's been changed is the items and citizen classes. The split path for Albion and the marshes are a nice touch, but there are glaring quality of life issues that are difficult to look past.  First off for me is the annoying trade system (which was also a problem in Anno 1800), if your stocks are full your trade ship doesn't know what to do and constantly spams alerts. Adding in a more robust logic system for trade like the trains in Factorio would greatly increase their usefulness. Now this might just come down to the way I play (maybe I should be selling off more excess stock), but the games tutorial is quite limited and I'm not even sure I played it because I had to exit the game the first time I opened it and never found an option for a tutorial again (other than the in-game tips). I think the game needs a more robust, accelerated tutorial that can teach the advanced mechanics involved in trade and new world expansion and give recommendations on approaches to try. I ended up just buying everything through trade for my main island and setting the game to max speed to build the final, big construction project. What annoys me about this strategy though is you have no idea who will trade with you or what resources are even out there, having a way to account for this and ensure long lasting trade is viable would be incredibly beneficial. I do think production timing is too slow and it should be shorter compared to the real world clock. The production analytics charts are in desperate need of long term charts so that the player can see average production over the past in-game hour, day, and week (with other increments in between).  This leads me to my next points. It's already been brought up in other reviews but the story line abruptly ends halfway through the game and to be completely honest it's not very engaging from the beginning. It feels something added as an afterthought to move the game along. I tried playing the Sandbox version of the game and  realized the map is exactly the same! There's no dynamic map generation and if you choose to start in Albion, you basically get the starting island that you would get in Story mode.  Finally, the game is poorly optimized and I ran into frequent issues trying to switch out of the game. All it needs to do is pause the game, but it would take a couple seconds to minimize and then load during any Alt-Tabbing from the game, even on my NVIDIA 2070 Super.  Overall $60 is an absurd ask for a game that is basically a reskin of Anno 1800. If the game provided major innovations, $30 or $40 would be appropriate, but this game is worth $20 at best. If you're interested in the Anno series, get 1800 on sale.

Anno 117 is a fantastic step forward for the series and proves that Ubisoft can still surpass even the high standards set by Anno 1800. The game manages to retain the deep city-building, economic complexity, and immersive atmosphere the series is known for, while introducing fresh mechanics and quality-of-life improvements that make managing an empire smoother and more satisfying than ever.  The visual and artistic presentation is stunning, and the historical setting is brought to life with impressive attention to detail. Watching your cities grow from scattered settlements into thriving Roman centers feels incredibly rewarding, and the new systems add depth without overwhelming newcomers.  At the moment, the only notable drawback is the online co-op experience. Connection issues can occur, which can interrupt matches and cause frustration. However, the developers are already actively working on a fix, and once this issue is resolved, Anno 117 can confidently be called the best city-building and management game on the market.  A must-play for fans of the genre and a worthy successor that somehow manages to outdo its already outstanding predecessor.

I first started playing Anno waaaayyyyy back when 1602 was first released in 1998, so let that date me if you wish lol. I have also played every Anno title - even 2070 and 2205 (yesh), and I can say without a doubt that this is one of the best in the series. While there is not as much right now as Anno 1800, this game feels like it will be able to expand a lot better than 1800 did, and I am very excited to see how the DLC for this game plays out.    TL;DR: Don't buy into a lot of the negative reviews. A lot of them are over very, very minor things. And things that are generally not at the core of an Anno game. This game is very solid, very few bugs, super fun and will suck you in to building a new Rome.    Pros:  - Beautiful graphics - they captured the Roman feel very well, and the Celts and Romans feel and play like very different cultures  -Music is great - just like every Anno title  -Gameplay is super smooth. I haven't noticed any framerate drop at all, even when all of the islands are settled and dozens of ships are sailing around  - The variety of buildings. The types of buildings that you get to build is pretty diverse, and each has a purpose  -The area of effect for production buildings. This was an awesome feature. I love how I can't just shove my production buildings into one corner of the island, or make one super city supported by a bunch of production production islands (cough cough Crown Falls cough cough). The need to put industry throughout the city is a great challenge - and also realistic of Roman times as well  - DIAGONAL FREAKING ROADS!!!!    Neutral  - The head animations are a little weird. They tend to get fuzzy, and the head bobbing is also weird. But it is an Anno game. You don't play Anno for character animations.  - There are a couple of minor audio glitches when multiple characters talk over each other  - AI art? I don't honestly see it, or what people are talking about with it    Negatives  -The campaign does end rather abruptly. Like I feel like there is more story to tell with Diana, Julianna and Calidus. But again - a campaign story isn't generally the focus of any Anno game I've played.  -I want to feel the wider world of the Roman Empire, not just these little corners of it. I do have hope that there will be several new provinces judging by the size of the World Map. Egypt, Carthage, Middle East, Spain would all be great places to visit and build cities in!

Story + Setting: The game feels like a 2025 modern scenario in an ancient setting, in the sense that some characters act very modern and lighthearted, while others act serious like you would expect it from a roman procurator. Unfortunately, modern plus ancient does not work very well together. It becomes very grotesque when you are the governor with a legion, archers, and battle ships loaded with Ballistas, ready to take over Albion by force and then Diana appears with her anime voice like "hiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiii, could you plz deliver these 50 love letters for me?". ♥♥♥♥♥, I'm trying to take over Britain, go ♥♥♥♥ off.  The writing is simply horrible, and it's not even funny like in Anno 1800, where the artisans were dramatic comedians and engineers were sarcastic feminists. All NPCs in this game are flat as hell, don't act Roman at all, and have no edges. They are what you expect a 7th grader to produce when they use an LLM for their homework. Idk if Ubisoft used AI for their writing, but if they didn't, they hired the worst writers they could find.   The game has an identity crisis on whether it wants to be serious or playful.   Even if we, as the player, decide for one thing, e.g. have a strict administration, the game will challenge that decision with stupid dialogues every few minutes. It's also true vice versa: if I want to have a true funny/playful game, the bandits will be dead serious which doesn't fit to the rest at all. I know that Anno always had a light humorous tone, and was never historical accurate, but this game is simply inconsistent in that regard, and I find this identity crisis terrible.  --- Gameplay: That's my opinion on the "story stuff around", the core gameplay is great! They improved on many things, especially the pacing which is my biggest plus. You can now play multiplayer without ever feeling the need to skip time (not that you can do that anyway, but the devs says they will deliver time skip at a later time). Unlike in Anno 1800 you never have idle time, there is always something to do. Also, it doesn't take ships 40 minutes to go from A to B, which was the case in 1800 even with steam ships. Great improvement imo!  45° buildings + streets make beauty building enjoyable easier, is fun to use with farms and is overall a great addition.  Production lines are reaching the complexity of 1800's base game, and this sets a great foundation for future DLC. Building aqueducts is great, they look great, they make sense (water physics! you have to build from mountains to valleys, so the water can flow downstream), they are simply fun a fitting version of trains in 1800.  The building system is great as always, with good improvements like automatic clamping of buildings, automatic street circling around buildings (although sometimes buildings and streets clamp to the wrong spots and you can only fix that by using "shift" which temporarily disables clamping -- the key bind is unfortunately not mentioned anywhere except the keybind menu. Took me a while to find it.)  The building menu is improved as well, although I would like to have a bigger hot bar which decreased from 10 to 5 for no reason. Now I need more clicks to manage my ships, compared to 1800.  Religion has no big impact, but maybe future DLC builds on top of this mechanic.  For whatever reason they decided to add another cooldown to quests. So, you can only do one quest of an NPC and then have to wait 15 minutes. Meh.  Overall I am recommending this game, because the core gameplay is a solid 9/10, and albeit the poor writing and some annoying characters, I am having a lot of fun and I'm looking towards the DLC. Maybe they can improve their writing for DLC and make it respect whether we want to rule with an iron fist, or like in a slice of life anime. ^_^   Edit: also, for the love of god, please show me the stats of fire safety, happiness etc when I have to make decisions based on these stats for quests.

If there was a neutral rating, I'd give it to Anno 117, but since there isn't, I'd have to give it a negative review instead.  It's a difficult decision to make, because the game itself is quite good, but interspersed with too many annoyances (which are subjective, to be fair).  Gameplay itself is typical Anno (I've got many hours in Anno 1800), and you have to remind yourself with Anno 117 that this is a new release - so it doesn't have anywhere near the depth of Anno 1800, nor the world scale, with multiple different regions, expeditions and the like.  You'd have to assume that future DLC will significantly expand on the base game.  So that itself is not necessarily a negative, unless you feel that should be in the base game at launch.  What's new is research, which is kind of meh, hiding improvements away behind gates with criteria you have to meet before research is available, whether that's having certain buildings, or even visiting the only other region that's in-game at this point.  Also new (and this is most annoying for me, personally), is that the sandbox/endless mode comes with what feels like forced progression, in the form of a ruling emperor that you must keep happy, lest he start a war with you.   This completely ruins the relaxing play-at-your-own-speed approach that having a sandbox mode implies.  Keep that for the campaign (which I didn't try).  Example of this, is that as soon as I built my first tier 3 house, I had a demand to supply to the emperor with 60+ tons of 2 different items within 90 minutes.  Neither of which I could produce on my home island, but needed me to expand to a 3rd island (I already had a second island, to support tier 2 houses, which enabled me to make 1 of the 2 items).  But expanding to the 3rd island takes a massive (for that game stage) amount of coin, thus creating a frantic and unnecessary situation for no apparent progression in the game.  Refusing to do the task simply drops your reputation, which ultimately triggers a war if it's too low. It seems like it's a way to force you to push through the game tiers faster than you might want or might like to do.  You don't have the ability to turn it off.  The other annoyance, is censorship.  You're not allowed to use any names (for cities, ships) that might contain words that have other meanings.  My first city was randomly named by the game as "Modicum".  At some point during the playthrough, the game renamed my city to ******* - replacing the entire name with asterisks.  Further investigation revealed that it had decided that the letters c-u-m in the name (that it created) were prohibited!  This extends to using names such as "Essex" (an English county, containing s-e-x), and other users have reported similar issues with other languages.  Gameplay itself is typical Anno, and enjoyable, and the use of diagonal roads enables you to create more interesting city layouts.  Still, it's early days, and things might improve.  Or might not, depending on the future of Ubisoft, which also seems in doubt.

After 5 hours into Anno 117 I know I haven't seen everything this game has to offer, but I feel like I've seen enough to give a decent first impressions. TL;DR at the bottom    My first REAL dive into the Anno series was 1800 but I had meddled in 1404 before. So this review is coming mainly from the perspective of an 1800 player.    In a lot of ways, 117 is a step up from 1800 but in a LOT of ways it feels like a step back, like they tried to take parts of 1800 people really liked, and put it into 117 without making it cohesive.    The best example of this is how you get to the new world in 1800 vs. Albion in 117.  In 1800 you build up your populations and islands until you reach the 3rd tier (iirc) at which point you're given a quest to take a ship, filled with supplies and head off in search of adventure! You need to pack accordingly for whatever events you might find along the way and it feels like a time of exploration.    In 117 you reach tier 3, told to go to the map and select Albion and there you go, a new ship with some supplies and a new map ready to be explored. It just feels less... epic when its just handed to you with no real build up or challenge.    Other issues I have are with the Hall of Fame. Now I like the idea of this system, I think Age of Wonders 4 does an incredible job with this system rewarding you for playing, not in a specific way just playing and finishing games, win or loose. Your rewards are new cosmetics and society traits. Things that will make your next game different.  Anno 117's approach however is to complete specific challenges, and reward you with over powered items to help you blast through the early game as fas as you can. I... hate this. I hate that you seemingly can't turn it off without loosing the actual cosmetics and GODS (actually important in gameplay). You can keep the OP speacalists in your warehouses and stuff but they are there tuanting you, making you feel like you're wasting time and resources for not using them.  It's not a good system and has been done SO much better in other games.    TL,DR BUT... overall as a game experience Anno 117 is a really good game. There isn't anything wrong with it parsay, just some things that have been done better in other games. If you liked 1800 and want a new experience, I do recommend 117 but be warned that, at least in my opinion, it is a step back in some ways.

I've played enough of this game to realize the following (Pardon My Rant):  The bugs aren't game breaking until pops hit a certain number, and that number may vary based on your rig/ components and settings. Not a big deal. Can be managed.  The AI generated content in the game isn't atrocious, but it definitely doesn't come anywhere near the polish of a real artist with a story to tell. It feels cheap because it is.  The performance requirements for maxed out settings at 4k are such that your rig will need to be worth the GDP of a small island nation. So there's some serious optimization headroom (CPU binding at scale) that could still be worked on - which was also a problem with the last game. So, in that way, the engine doesn't feel much more refined. But it is not what I would consider in any way worse.  But these things are forgiveable at launch - and could definitely be changed within a patch or two. They aren't why I'm throwing my thumb down here (which, I think this is the first time I've written a negative review). My point-of-no-return on this game was the plot.   The basic, spoilerless version of the plot line as a guy is that you are a sheepish, ignorant, moron who is tasked with saving the empire by fighting the misogynistic, racist, and slaver bad guys. It's such a common combo of tropes in video games and media nowadays that I'm honestly just cashed out.   I know there's more intrigue - I know there's more f's (in chat) to give about the other plot steps along the way and about the overall functionality improvements, and they deserve their spots in the sun. The game mechanics in particular, the maps/ environment/ build-systems/ menus/ etc - I find all to be out-performing 1800, especially at release.    But at the end of the day, I just can't keep excusing this lazy writing theme appearing everywhere in media. I don't care what your political views are, you don't need to care about mine - that's not the point at all. All I am saying is that using the same content/ theme/ story arc over and over and over and over and over and over and over again is getting exhausting as someone who genuinely just wants to play an engaging game again.   Maybe this is my 'I'm too old for this' moment and I'm just crashing out. But this game and many other recent major titles just feel soulless and it took me 42 hours to be able to put my finger on just why it is so taxing to stay involved-with.  Overall, thumbs-down. Not trying to be based - just trying desperately to not be bored.   Thanks for sticking it through if you read this all. Cheers!

TLDR; This is a great game and a welcome addition to the Anno family of games  The negative reviews you'll read cite the following:  * Weak Campaign  The "campaign" for any Anno game is intended above all to be a *tutorial* not a full story driven campaign that you would see in a strategy / RPG. However, I first played Anno 1800 after it was a fully mature game with many DLCs and packed with content. I think that campaign is far too long. After a while you catch on that it's just an extended tutorial meant to introduce you to all of the mechanics of the game. Anno 117 is brand new. After a few DLCs I'm sure that the "campaign" will get longer and longer.  * AI Art  So what? Many games are now using AI generated stock "art". Much of it is just utilitarian and you only glance at it while you're focusing on strategy and mechanics. That said, this is a beautiful game! The attention to detail is extraordinary. The research put into this game had to have been meticulous. You can learn a lot about ancient Roman culture by playing this game. The landscapes are extraordinary. Probably the best of the series.  * Notification hell  At first I thought this was a valid complaint. Why am I being notified about the missing patron deity of an island in another province? Enough already. But then I remembered: almost everything in an Anno game is configurable. For the people complaining about notifications: did you look at the settings on the notifications panel? You can configure what notifications you see, or if you see any at all, when you see them, etc. This complaint is more of a RTFM problem than a fault of the game.  When I reread some of the complaints I grew a little frustrated. I wanted to ask the reviewers: have you played other Anno games much? Because the pattern here is the same. What did you expect? Could they have made the campaign even longer? Sure but at some point you've gotta let go of mommy's hand and play on your own. Take a chill pill people.  Original Review:  From the moment you begin playing Anno 117, you can tell it's an Anno game. Your first reaction might be "this is Anno 1800 with an ancient Rome skin on it".  But you would be making a mistake to judge so hastily. This wouldn't be an Anno game if it didn't have similar mechanics. But most of the game is new. There's a new approach regarding religion, which plays a significant role. The pace of the game is slower, which reflects the era. The Roman political system is modeled fairly well. And I've never seen a combat mechanic like this in an Anno game before. You get a good sense of what it would have been like to raise and maintain an effective army, and you can see how governance plays a huge role regarding military might. And, the navy and the land cohorts compliment one another very well. They are equally important. The research tree is enormous, and is tied to cultural paths - will you "go native" or establish Roman culture in your colony? Each culture has advantages that can only be leveraged via the "native" route.  I did not immediately find the user interface to be intuitive. I figured it out - but I've played Anno games previously so I made educated guesses as to where I would need to click in order to find information or take advantage of a hidden nuance that would give me an advantage. I've never before bought an Anno game when it was first released. The other iterations were far more mature by the time I started playing them. But Anno 117 seems to be starting out a little light on content. There's plenty to do and see! Don't get me wrong. It just doesn't seem as mature as the other Anno iterations. This game is obviously placed to see some excellent DLC released for it. There are so many ways to expand on the unique features. I hope that the first DLCs are not just "flavor packs" with some new skins and some additional fixtures you can place around your cities. I hope that they add cultures and provinces, expand the religious system to include many more deities and effects, and beef up the military mechanic. This game is ripe for a more in depth military aspect than previous Anno games. They've made a good start but I'd like to see a lot more.  This is a gorgeous game. The animations, the detail, the landscapes and the skies are beautiful and capture that feel of the ancient Roman period. The devs did a great job with atmosphere, and I love it. So I would recommend this game highly to those players who enjoy:  * Ancient Rome! * Anno games! * Interwoven mechanics that compliment one another: building, research, religion, governance / politics and of course ancient military battles. * Another Anno game that has huge potential for years to come  If you enjoy those things - give it a try! I highly recommend it.

I bought the Gold Edition on June 15th. Anno 1800 is one of the best games I have ever played. The best Anno of all time. I figured this game would be a reskin of that, only ancient Rome. I've always wanted a newer Caesar III, and who better to do that than Anno?   Boy was I wrong.   The game is stuffed with terrible AI slop for art. They don't even try to hide it by touching up the melted faces and limbs. The loading screens are all AI, the cinematics are all AI, too. The music feels empty and soulless, unlike every other game I've ever played. Every note I hear, I wonder if it's also AI. Then I wonder whether the voice acting is AI because it feels so clanky. I checked IMDB, and it doesn't list the Maria character or a few others from the campaign—are they AI voices?   The problem is, if Anno is going to lie to me once, how can I trust any of this is legit? Even the boring UI is without soul.   As for the gameplay, it's more or less the same idea as previous Annos, but now you need to research things that used to be just part of developing your city. In order to upgrade your warehouses, you need to research it—and it takes 35 minutes. Want a paved road? You have to wait an additional 35 minutes to research it. Research must be done sequentially rather than simultaneously, meaning it takes hours to get things that used to become available as your city evolved.  Then there's the campaign, which isn't even completed. It just ends without you even knowing. So you pay CAD $136 for a game that isn't even done? What?   I've spent hundreds of dollars on Anno 1800. I bought every DLC except the one with that revolutionary guy and pretty much every cosmetic pack. I was happy to pay for all of that because I knew it went toward a dev team that respected its players. You could feel their love for their product because they were one of the last devs who made their game a work of art.   My refund is currently pending, I'm very sad to say.   If they don't care enough about their game to use real art, music, and actors, then why should I should I care enough to spend my time and money on it? This game is a tragedy.

Graphics are better too, but turn off ray tracing -- it does very little for image quality while causing a ton of stutter.

So, I've played this game for a while now, and let me tell you this: if you wanna buy [b]Anno 117[/b] right now, you are buying an unfinished mess. Don't get me wrong, this game has a ton of potential, but its current state isn't what you would call a finished product.  [h3] Pros [/h3] [list] [*] [b]Graphics are sharp:[/b] Major improvements compared to previous entries. [*] [b]Classic Feel:[/b] It's the same old satisfying [i]Anno[/i] gameplay loop we love. [*] [b]Regional Variety:[/b] Good variety in terms of different regions to explore and settle. [*] [b]War is fun:[/b] The combat mechanics feel engaging. [*] [b]Non-restrictive Goods:[/b] (Though this is also a con, it does offer flexibility). [/list]  [h3] Cons [/h3] [list] [*] [b]Bugs, bugs, and more bugs:[/b] It's just frustrating.... [*] [b]Shallow Production:[/b] The "Non-restrictive Goods" system causes a noticeable lack of depth in production chains. [*] [b]Massive Space Hog:[/b] There are simply too many goods. Fulfilling all the needs of your populations requires an absurd amount of space. [*] [b]No Commuter Pier Alternative:[/b] While unrealistic, the lack of an alternative forces you to build fully-serviced cities on every single production island just to save space, which feels tedious. [*] [b]Clunky UI:[/b] The user interface is pretty bad and lacks essential information. For example, why can’t I see the time it takes to complete a trade route, or the total coverage area of my service buildings? [*] [b]Broken Service Mechanics:[/b] Beyond basic pop needs, you have to build service buildings for health and happiness. The problem is that at higher population numbers, they stop mattering—you'll never be able to counteract the mounting unhealthiness and unhappiness anyway. [*] [b]Island Blandness:[/b] It seriously lacks island variety. There are only four different XL island presets available for both Latium and Albion regions. [/list]  I really wanna like this game a lot, but the state of it right now just makes it an [b]annoying, frustrating mess.[/b]

🏠 Housing & Population Management One of the biggest issues is the sheer number of houses required to sustain the workforce. Every time you levy a unit, the game deducts population — which makes sense in principle. However, the execution feels excessive: losing 40 people means you need to build around 40 more houses. Each of those houses must then be carefully placed within the range of markets, taverns, medics, city watch, and other essential services. This creates a domino effect where a single military decision spirals into massive urban expansion, often without enough land to realistically accommodate it.  The economy is mostly based on population, which is fine in theory. But when you end up needing 400 houses on a single island, the fun quickly disappears. There simply isn’t enough space, and the constant housing grind overshadows the joy of city-building.  🌍 Land & Space Limitations The maps feel cramped. There’s rarely enough buildable land to balance housing, production chains, and military needs. Instead of encouraging creative layouts, the game often forces players into awkward, inefficient sprawl. This undermines the satisfaction of designing a thriving Roman city and makes expansion feel more like a chore than a strategic choice.  📦 Service & Storage Range Markets, storage houses, and other service buildings have ranges that feel far too small. This means you’re constantly duplicating infrastructure just to keep basic functions running. Instead of focusing on grand city planning, you spend much of your time micromanaging overlapping service zones. It’s tedious rather than engaging.   Military Integration The idea that levying units reduces population is logical — armies should come from the citizenry. But the scale is disproportionate. Recruiting a modest force shouldn’t cripple your city’s housing system. The mechanic ends up punishing players for engaging with the military side of the game, rather than integrating it smoothly into the broader city-building loop.   Diplomacy & Trade Trading with other factions is unnecessarily difficult. Much like Total War: Rome II, rival factions seem to dislike you from the very start, making diplomacy feel one-sided. Instead of fostering interesting trade networks, the system often leaves you isolated, with limited options to expand your economy through external partnerships. This review brought to you by the Guild of Millers. The Guild of Millers uses only the finest grain. True Roman bread, for true Romans. - Coming off the sandals of Anno 1800, Anno 117 is a rather mixed bag overall. I loved Anno 1800, and getting to play a new Anno set with the Romans was genuinely a dream come true, and while there is a lot I do enjoy about this game, there is a lot I’m not fussed over, and some clear downgrades from 1800.  The Campaign for 117 was interesting, though it ends rather abruptly which I found rather strange and frankly jarring, very much a “Wait, that’s it?”. I spent 8 hours in Latium building up Juliana before being exiled off to Albion with 300k in my pocket and managing to set myself up there with little fuss. I know some people won’t enjoy being stuck in one province for most of Act 2, but I kind of enjoyed the novelty of it, getting a taste of Roman beauty, screwed over and exiled to a backwater and getting rewarded with coming home, but then it all just abruptly ends and you’re left scratching your head. Act 1 sets up an inevitable clash for the throne, Calidus versus Julia for leader of the Roman Empire, but that never happens, dozens of plot points that were established are never resolved. Annoyingly it seems there was meant to be an Act 3 that datamines have found, dozens of strings still exist in the games code, but presumably for time reasons Ubi were forced to cut them, and turn an actually enjoyable campaign into a waste of time. Act 2 in Albion is bar far the best act, I enjoyed setting up in Albion and going up against Voada, slowly whittling her operations down and storming her island, and I enjoyed the RP-options to become a more Celtic-friendly governor, or pro-Roman. I of course am a loyal son of the Empire, so that grove got cut down swiftly for precious, precious mines. I know the game wants you to consider Calidus an antagonist against you, but I liked him a lot more than Lucius and Julia, Calidus is an ass but he’s an honest and upfront ass compared to the lying Lucius and Julia and he’s a far better choice for the Empire than she would ever be, never mind more historically accurate.  The land combat is interesting, and I really enjoyed building up an army to storm Voada’s island and bring that Celtic curr to heel, it’s not exactly Total War Rome, but for what it is, it’s not bad. Sea combat isn’t that different from 1800, though this time there’s a lot more modularity with ships. Just going full arrow ships and kiting the enemy so their scorpions/catapults can’t hit you works a treat, though I think taking down the Latium pirates will be a real challenge for naval-combat, that place is stupidly well defended.  Population management is a swing and a miss in 117, t1 workers are as valuable as gold, so many just random buildings need them, and ships and armies need to take them too. I found myself in a constant struggle to balance my t1 waders in albion, every time I plopped down a well or a latrine or built a new depot, it was suddenly more workers I have to build houses for. It’s more realistic than 1800 I will give it that, and I think in time I’ll adjust to it, but at the moment it’s a slog. Also I know a lot of people hated Influence in 1800, I’m one of like 6 people who liked the system, it’s a shame it’s gone, taking islands now is just a money-chore and one that’s going to make going up against higher difficulty-rivals in the early game of a sandbox a nightmare.  The game’s a real beauty if you can play it on higher settings, the changes to the skybox, the weather in Albion, the detail on people waddling your streets (literally waddling for some bizzare reason like half my cities are morbidly obese) and the buildings, it all helps make for a much more immersive experience. Albion conveys that rough and rugged, craggie and wet weather that I know and love from real life, and Latium is warm and joyfilled, the beautiful Mediterranean glow to rest atop my lounger, sipping a fine glass of wine.  Fortunately I’ve been lucky and only encountered one bug a relog fixed, no save issues or glaring problems there as some other people have, and overall game stability at least for me was very good.  While I have mostly enjoyed my first play-through on 117, and I am looking forward to seeing Egypt added, I can only recommend this if you understand you’re not getting the whole package. Anno 117 is the new house you bought and while it stands upright and tall, the builders only did about 75% of the work inside and there’s whole rooms which are just wooden-framed panels waiting for someone to do something, there’s a lot here to enjoy and play with, but Ubisoft’s corner cutting really shows here. I hope Ubi add in the once-planned Act 3 for the Campaign in upcoming patches, or at least that the Ocassus DLC is an act 3 of its own, because Egypt really should have its own story focus like Land of Lions did.  Acta non verba.

Anno 117 is among the best entries in the series. The Roman setting feels fresh without losing the classic Anno identity. Building up a scattered settlement into a thriving province is as engaging as ever, and the atmosphere, visuals, and production chains all work together to create that familiar flow that gets you hooked.  While some players mention performance issues, the game runs perfectly for me on a 3080 and a 5950X. With ray tracing turned off I get a steady 60 fps even in very large cities. No dips, no stutters, just smooth gameplay from start to finish.  The UI takes some adjustment. It is a bit less intuitive than Anno 1800 at first glance, but once you learn where things are, it becomes fine to use. It is still far more straightforward and beautiful than the Civilization 7 UI, so although there is room for improvement, it does not get in the way of enjoying the game.  Content wise it cannot be compared to Anno 1800 with all its DLCs, but that is expected. Vanilla 1800 did not launch with significantly more content than Anno 117 has now, so the current state feels normal for a new release and leaves plenty of room for future additions.  The campaign is the weakest part. It is not particularly memorable and does not add much to the experience. That said, most people do not play Anno for the campaign and the core city building is strong enough that this should not discourage anyone. Also, I don't care for combat in Anno games but ground troops are back in this one. They are alright, I guess.  If you enjoy city builders or the Anno series, Anno 117 is easy to recommend. It delivers a strong foundation, a great setting, and an addictive gameplay loop that already kept me glued to my PC longer than I should have been.

Anno 117 is a [b]wonderful game[/b], currently held back by technical issues, the use of AI-generated art, and a truncated campaign. Gameplay-wise, Anno 117 compares to Anno 1800 much like 1800 compared to earlier installments.  It offers a fresh variety of gameplay options, both large and small. My favorite is how production buildings now affect nearby population in different ways; the greater variety of Albion pops is a close second. The game looks gorgeous even on my fairly old PC — although, for some reason, I had to roll back my NVIDIA driver to get stable FPS.  If I compare release-day Anno 117 to release-day Anno 1800, 117 comes out on top. It’s better in almost every way. I still recommend playing 1800 (especially with some essential DLC) — that game is fantastic. But please give 117 a chance too. It’s worth every minute.

Anno 117 starts strong, looks beautiful, and has that classic Anno charm, but after a while it becomes painfully clear that this game is basically Anno 1800 wearing a Roman skin, with only a few twists that don’t meaningfully change the core formula.  👍 The Good  Progressing through the early game is fun and familiar.  The new skill tree system is a nice addition.  The Roman theme has a lot of promise and the presentation is solid.  👎 The Problems 1. Feels like an Anno 1800 DLC, not a new game  The mechanics, pacing, and gameplay loop are almost identical to 1800. The resource chains, settlement building, naval focus, and progression all feel reused. If you’ve played 1800, you’ve basically played this.  2. Combat is almost nonexistent  In 25 hours I didn’t get invaded a single time outside the tutorial. For a game set in the Roman Empire, this is baffling.  No meaningful:  *Land battles *Legions *Caravans *Raids *Border conflicts  Just naval combat  This era begged for proper Roman armies, road networks, supply lines, and land-based logistics. Ubisoft played it extremely safe instead.  3. The campaign is shockingly short and ends abruptly  This was my biggest disappointment.  Act 1 teaches you settlement building. Act 2 introduces military and expansion. Act 3… sends you back to the starting province and then… the campaign just ends.  No final mission. No conclusion. No cutscene. No indication that it’s over.  I legitimately thought the game had bugged and kept playing for another 10+ hours waiting for something to trigger. After Googling it, I discovered: That really is the end.  One of the worst campaign implementations I’ve seen in a mainline Anno.  4. Sandbox gets stale fast  The first few hours are fun, but once your economy stabilizes, the game slows to a crawl. With no campaign missions and no external pressure, you lose every sense of progression. The game no longer pushes you, giving you no reason to keep expanding.  ⭐ Final Thoughts  Anno 117 had massive potential, the Roman setting practically writes its own mechanics, but the game never commits to them. Instead, it’s a safer, smaller, and far less ambitious version of Anno 1800.  If you love the classic Anno loop and just want more of it, you may enjoy it for a while. But if you were hoping for a fresh take, deeper systems, or a meaningful campaign, this one will likely disappoint.  Score: 6/10 A beautiful but shallow entry that feels unfinished and underambitious, with a campaign that ends before it even begins.

The game is very similar to ANNO 1800 which I quite liked and yet improved:     - the economics part is way more gamified. The placements of the end-product buildings gets you different bonuses so you have more incentive to make  visually appealing cities.      - the AIs are far less annoying than before and like to trade even better.  They no longer grow like mold on the islands or overwhelm you with annoying quests which if you don't fulfill lead to war. They still pose a threat and you can fight them but for me they are are far more enjoyable now.      - the most important resource in the game now is the population and keeping it happy/healthy/obedient appears a little bit more challenging.  A tighter balance and money harder to accumulate makes the game even  more interesting in my opinion. And I have not yet developed a big army which will require massive resources.     - the land army is a really nice addition to diversify the whole experience and to strain even further the tight resources.     What I did not like:     - the menu navigation is a bit more complicated than necessary. Too many submenus and categories. I prefer the menu organization  of 1404.    P.S.  I play only the sandbox part so I don't have any idea of campaign story.     P.S. I can't wait to see what comes next after the Egypt expansion.

I really love this game, with all its flaws, however I can't recommend it in its current state.  Campaign: vague, visibly short and full of open-ended questions. While the story gripped me and I got really involved with the characters (Mainly Marcia's story), it's only about 2 or 3 hours before you're abruptly tossed into sandbox mode; it felt more like a tutorial mode rather than a campaign. It's painfully visible how gutted the campaign got, I felt the 1st act should've stretched into tier 3 in Latium, giving enough space for the story to develop, and explore the characters. 2nd act is non-existent if you go the diplomacy route. 3rd act is just sandbox mode. Overall the campaign mode just seems like a tutorial.  Gameplay: Overall improvement from anno 1800 imho. However, there are several QoL issues and missing tools that make the game unbearable at times. Mainly blueprint mode bugs where slot buildings can't be constructed (fixed), the lack of stamps, land and sea unit pathing being atrocious; land units only blob rush, while ships employ drive-by tactics exclusively. AI governors summoning denarii and resources out of thin air as well, in classic anno fashion.  Conclusion: lovely game, but looking at the cracks in the wall has gotten annoying by now. Rome wasn't built in a day I know, but bad foundations will never make an Empire last.

TLDR: After 152 hours, I still can't stop playing.   Active pause is by far the single most important shift for me. I like a chill experience, and active pause makes this edition of Anno BY FAR the best for me. Adding a tech tree? = Genius! Adding diagonal roads? = Genius! Graphics and the Roman Era? = Superb! Zoom on Cursor? = May be nerdy, but I love when games set zoom to the cursor's location! So glad that my bet on paying for the Gold Edition will clearly pay off ... can't wait for the additional content and inevitable improvements. This one is a winner!  For those of you holding off (which I would normally do for ANY game, frankly) purchasing this game to see if there are any technical or performance issues, I can say that in nearly 30 hours of play, I've only had one crash. The graphical options are very good, so you can dial in whatever suits you / your machine (I did have to turn off NVIDIA auto-optimization because that kept screwing up my settings in-game...and at first I thought this was a Ubisoft issue, which it wasn't).   I also tend to hold off on buying games upon release just to wait for a sale and to have a few patches already in place. That's always good advice, of course, but in this case I not only bought the game, but I also bought the deluxe version as a vote of confidence in Ubisoft that it will take this already very fun game and improve significantly on it in the months and years to come. Overall:  Yes, the campaign ends abruptly with no indication that you've completed it, and there are definitely signs that the campaign is incomplete in other ways, but I enjoyed playing the campaign TWICE to see the different story lines (mostly the same story, but there are some differences to be had). For kicks, I even cleared Juliana (your main island) of all the ruins, which took a great deal of time since it requires a lot of horses. I'm now ready to start a sandbox game.   In sum, the game is not yet a polished as Anno 1800, but Anno 1800 also took a long time to get to its current state. That said, I simply can't go back to Anno 1800. Anno 117 is just a joy to play even in its current state, and I look forward over the coming months to see what will doubtless be improvements across the board in terms of gameplay, graphic performance, content, and everything else.   If you'd rather wait until those things are in place (and to wait for a sale), I couldn't blame you. I'll just say that for the first time in a decade or more I ordered a game upon release AND paid for the DLC in advance. That's how confident I am in this title.

[h1]Review of Anno 117: Pax Romana[/h1]  [h2]Introduction[/h2] With [i]Anno 117: Pax Romana[/i], Ubisoft Mainz delivers a new milestone in the long-running city-building series. From the very first hours, the game impresses with its visual detail and innovative mechanics.  [h2]Strengths[/h2] [b]Graphics & Atmosphere:[/b] The level of detail is stunning, and the world feels vibrant and immersive. Even on normal high settings, however, the game already shows how demanding it is on hardware.  [b]New Features:[/b] The planning mode, copy-paste function, and diagonal building are excellent innovations. They streamline gameplay and open up new creative possibilities for city design.  [b]Singleplayer Stability:[/b] Performance in singleplayer is remarkably solid. Aside from occasional FPS drops — usually when changing perspective or camera direction — the experience is smooth and largely problem-free.  [b]Publisher’s Progress:[/b] It is clear that Ubisoft Mainz has learned from the early mistakes of [i]Anno 1800[/i], which at launch was far less stable. [i]Anno 117[/i] demonstrates a much stronger technical foundation, especially in singleplayer.  [h2]Weaknesses[/h2] [b]Multiplayer/Co-op:[/b] Unfortunately, the situation changes in multiplayer. Persistent desynchronization issues appear after only a few minutes. Reloading saves is possible, but only after all participants restart the game entirely. For players who look forward to enjoying the campaign together, these interruptions are frustrating. [i]Anno[/i] thrives on long, uninterrupted sessions, and frequent breaks undermine that core experience.  [h2]Conclusion[/h2] [i]Anno 117[/i] shines with impressive innovations, rich detail, and stable singleplayer performance. Ubisoft Mainz’s progress compared to [i]Anno 1800[/i] is evident, showing that lessons have been learned. Yet its multiplayer mode suffers from serious synchronization problems that disrupt the flow. For solo players, this is close to a perfect [i]Anno[/i] experience. For those who prefer co-op, patience is required until stability improves. Planning security — a hallmark of the series — is weakened by these issues, which is unfortunate given how central careful preparation is to [i]Anno’s[/i] appeal.

Edit: [strike]Multiplayer does work now.[/strike] It broke again, not sure if related to time in game or just a bricked save but either way, Ubisoft strikes again.  Sadly, I still can't recommend this in its current state, AI shenanigans aside. For starters, the UI is abysmal. I'm not sure what whoever designed it was thinking, but it feels like they started with the 1800 UI and decided to make it worse in every way, partly because lots of information has been hidden. For example if you're in a ceasefire with someone you don't see it when you look at their island anymore (and you can still attack it, which makes no sense). Another example is in the production menus, where you have to expand the graphs that were already visible by default in 1800. I've no clue why hiding graphs is necessary, given they expands into empty space.  The campaign is abysmal. The writing is terrible, the voice acting is poor, the mechanics are horrendous and only serve to highlight some awful features. As an example, there's combat to take an island, and it's purposefully designed so that your ships can't reach and you have no access to siege... So you are left watching your soldiers die to gates (which are for some reason the only armed part of walls) while they throw torches at it for 30 minutes. Exhilarating gameplay. There's also a part where you can choose one of two directions, but without knowing you can go so far down one path that you're locked out of the other, even though you can still waste your time doing the other path's quests. Lastly, it's ridiculously short at about 10 hours, if you play slowly.  There's also numerous bugs: being unable to upgrade blueprints, being unable to target buildings or units in combat, upgrading the wrong things due to the diagonal building changes, the drag upgrading often doesn't work correctly with aqueducts, walls and roads, being unable to configure ships if you can't afford the cost of what you previously selected, the formation hot key doesn't work (and also puts archers on the front lines, land combat is half-baked in general), and others I can't recall but at least they can be found on the bug report page.  Some other downsides in comparison to 1800: there's not much variety with the islands, workforce seems to fluctuate much more, ship combat somehow got worse (they don't behave as you'd expect and clip through each other all the time), the world map looks terrible, the voice lines for population aren't as memorable and are generally lower quality (though there's some good ones).  As for the upsides, the city building part of the game looks great. The diagonal building is a welcome change despite some of the current bugs and limitations. Romans, of course. Ship upgrades are neat - though why can't you configure them after they're built?  Overall, if you like Anno, you'll like this - especially if you like the Romans, and who doesn't - but, as seems to be the theme with AAA games, we're left wondering why the developers make bizarre decisions and regressions. The UI and UX are the worst parts, they're such a massive downgrade from 1800 it's maddening. Sadly, I doubt either will be fixed as they're not often changed post-release in games, but here's to hoping.

Easily the best Anno I’ve played, by a mile. I can’t stop. Send help. I treat the campaign like a guided tour of new mechanics so I’m fully armed for sandbox mode, where my true personality (a benevolent dictator with spreadsheets) can finally flourish.  Now, about some of these negative reviews… A surprising number read like they were written by people whose PCs are held together with hopes, prayers, and a cooling fan made of cardboard. “It doesn’t run on my 10-year-old laptop!” my friend, that machine was already begging for retirement during Anno 2070.  And the Ubisoft account complaints, you’d think someone asked them to sacrifice a goat under a full moon.  “I PAID MONEY FOR THIS!” Yes. That is typically how goods and services are exchanged. The alternative would involve bartering livestock, and Steam hasn’t implemented that feature yet.  Is the game perfect? No. Will it improve the way Anno 1800 did? Almost certainly. That team iterates like it’s a competitive sport.  Maybe it’s just me, but modern gamer discourse sometimes feels like watching a crowd of people throw tantrums because the brand-new doll they bought can’t perform opera at launch. Deep breaths. Hydrate. Manage expectations.  As for me, I’m excited to see where Ubisoft takes this one. If this is the foundation, the future’s looking very bright for Pax Romana.

I am unsure how the engine is unable to handle 10 static icons that slightly move up and down, but there it is. I am talking 50% speed loss of the simulation, including stutters every 5-6 seconds. It's crazy.  Also the city building has mostly become a chore. Aqueducts are horrendously implemented, making it basically impossible to have one run through a city, because it will constantly glitch or tell you "invalid crossing", which means a street shouldn't be there. Worked in planning mode, but once you build it, it breaks. Also streets can totally cross under aqueducts and there is no rhyme or reason to why it won't let you.   The whole AI art thing is an issue, but the actual technical issues are more important I think:  It runs poorly, even on Medium settings and I far outspec the requirements. Diagonal building is fine, but also incredibly glitchy, you can't even place buildings right next to each other when building diagonally near a road because it will always snap to an arbitraty distance (couldn't find any way to disable snapping).  Also, how do you plant more trees for sawmills and stuff? In Anno 1800 they just forrested up themselves, but I couldn't find anything here and it doesnt seem to be automatic either.  Not technical but personal: The ingame design is very unclear to me and hard to read, especially when a lot of foliage is involved. Just a personal observation.

It sucks that that I'm leaving a dislike for Anno 117. The campaign stops abruptly after your return to Latium. I was stupid to be worried about spoilers, because the game has no story to be spoiled. Also, the amount of population you get from buildings and goods seems rather low. I don't know if this is a deliberate design/balance choice.  The positives are the game is beautiful, land combat is (kinda) fun and the diagonal construction offers a lot of freedom in how you build your cities, but that's about it.

It's a competent Anno game with a large amount of chains to work with and some unique mechanics with mixed populations, area effects, and diagonal building. It's a step above Anno 1800 in concept. In practice, it feels like it could have used another month to add some critical features like stamp tool, rotating groups of buildings 45 degrees (you can only rotate them 90, unless you do it single building at a time), and some more polish on the story.    However, the reason I've marked this as would not recommend is the use of AI art. I prepurchased the game prior to knowing about the AI art and while I've enjoyed the game, I can not recommend the game in full faith with its inclusion. If Ubisoft were to replace the generated assets I would change this to a recommends in a heartbeat.

Where to begin. This game, I was so excited. We all were, but boy is it flawed. I'll start with the good because it does exist. There is good here.  The game is pretty, the boats sailing is pretty, the world has a glow about it. It is pleasing to look at, at its core, but there is more to it that I will get in to with the cons.  The ground troops are fun, it is a nice addition to an Anno game and you can't have a game based in Rome and not have the ability to make legionares.  I actually like the idea of the troops costing population and I like that it isn't cheap. That makes the necessity of balancing your population, something that 1800 brought in with great effect, even more important. It also makes having more than one large city important, unlike 1800 where you could have little partial cities and focus all your resources on one city, cough cough crown falls cough couch. I think it is a great addition to the core concept of tycoon resource management of the anno franchise.  I don't hate the tech tree, it is flawed but I don't hate it. I'll get into that later.    Now for the bad  This game is hollow. It is empty. It lacks the soul of 1800 and I can pinpoint what it is. It isn't the flawed tech tree which lacks direction, IE later research options don't really feel late game it just feels more of the same and certain core mechanics are locked away in the research tab such as upgrading your warehouses. It isnt the piss poor campaign with a cringy and eye rolling story line. The reason why this game just isn't exactly FUN, is because the city you make, the world you make, is static. The famous paved roman roads look like basic cobblestone and not the precisely engineered masterpieces they had in the big city, the houses lack charm and character. I know 117 isn't going to have advertisements scattered everywhere like 1800 did, but you know what Rome did have? Graffiti. You know what these buildings lack? Another thing, 1800's engineer and investor houses had bakeries and storefronts at the ground floor. This one doesn't. Rome had bakeries and storefronts on the ground floor of houses. I get that the game has the actual bakery building but aesthetically, I should see kitchens and little restaurants at the base of the last two classes houses. Not to mention the houses don't feel like they mesh with the roads well, where as walking around in 1800 you felt like you were walking around in a city. Another static thing about this game is the people. Where are they? Anno 1800 the cities were BUSY with people, even the farmer towns had plenty of people walking around. And the sound design? 1800 had magical sound design. The clip clop of horses, the conversations, the simple NOISE of being in the city. This game has people talking sure, but it doesn't sound like there are thousands of people walking around. You don't hear footsteps or jingling or horse hooves. You just hear a quick quip here or there. The city doesn't feel real, it feels static. Another thing that is also static is the crops. Why did 1800 have beautiful flowing fields that waved with the wind that you could just sit back and watch, but this game which has barley, wheat, hemp, and lavender, all fields that should make stunning ripples, has them standing still and motionless. Panning over your city to go deal with something doesn't take your breath away as you see scores of farmers toiling a waving field. You just see dots of plants. It doesn't feel alive, it doesn't feel like a civilization. It is a painting painted by a burnt out art student who lost the passion. Sure, fundamentally it is pretty to look at with a first glance but the more you look the more broken and hollow the painting is. And that is what this game is as a whole, a hollow, soulless painting. That isn't even mentioning the AI, which the game unfortunately uses.  My biggest concern with the game is I don't know if DLC can save it, and I shouldn't have to wait for DLC to save it. Sure they want to add a crown falls for 117, sure they want to add the circus maximus, but will the game have a sole when those DLCs arrive? I don't think so. I want the roads to be sharp and made with perfect stones but I want trash scattered across it. I want graffiti beyond some A and N followed by check marks. I want storefronts underneath the roman apartments. I want dogs and cats zipping down the walk ways. I want HORDES of people crammed in the city. I want the fields to wave at me as I sail by. I want the game to come alive. And currently, it is just a husk with a smattering of make up but with lifeless glass eyes. Anno 1800 captivates you right out of the gates. 117 just makes you want to play 1800 and forget it. As of writing this review I haven't refunded the game yet but it is on my mind. I don't see the point of continuing playing when the superior game is already installed.    TLDR: The game is superficially fun at the start but falls apart as a lifeless husk fairly quickly.

Just starting up a Continuous Game I've put a full 20 hours into my latest save file (second, after I figured out how most stuff worked on my first one) and have been having a lot of fun. Anno 117: Pax Romana delivers exactly what it promises: a focused city-builder that rewards planning, patience, and a taste for Roman precision. It avoids the bloat that drags down many modern strategy titles and instead leans into clean mechanics, readable systems, and a steady sense of progression. The pacing feels deliberate in a good way, you’re always working toward something meaningful rather than drowning in micromanagement.  The atmosphere hits the mark: convincing Roman architecture, grounded sound design, and a presentation style that avoids kitsch. The campaign structure strikes a solid balance between guided scenarios and open-ended building, with enough challenge to keep veterans engaged without shutting out newer players. Production chains are logical, infrastructure actually matters, and the game respects your time by making cause and effect clear.  Is it reinventing the genre? No. But it doesn’t try to. What it does, it does well: a disciplined, elegant city-builder with historical flair, technical polish, and a strong sense of place. It’s a confident return to form for anyone who misses the classic Roman-building experience, and a smart, satisfying entry for anyone who enjoys thoughtful strategy.  The core game and mechanics are solid, it's not Anno 1800, but what is ? I'm enjoying it so far, can't wait to see what the future brings.

Unfortunately, I cannot recommend Anno 117: Pax Romana at this point – unless you plan to play it strictly in single-player. The multiplayer is essentially unplayable. Constant bugs, desynchronizations, and crashes often corrupt save files, making ongoing games impossible to continue. Having to restart from scratch is simply not an option for me — especially not just to play alone.  The campaign is actually a lot of fun, but it suddenly ends out of nowhere. You keep waiting for the next main quest, only to realize the campaign is already over without you really noticing. For a full-price title, this makes the whole experience feel short and unfinished.  Other than that, Anno 117 mostly plays like a regular Anno game with only a handful of new features. You can research deities, build diagonally, and there are a few new building effects — but that’s basically it. These minor additions do not justify a €60 price tag, in my opinion.  I truly hope the developers fix the technical issues and maybe add meaningful content later on, because the game has potential. But in its current state, I simply cannot recommend it.

I'm not going to lie, this game is, overall, disappointing. I understand that Anno 1800 leaves big shoes to fill, and comparing this game at launch to 1800 at completion is perhaps unfair, but 1800 at launch was a complete game with a full campaign that took you through almost every stage of building level.   As others have mentioned, the campaign in this game is ridiculously short. Barely a tutorial. You will get to about level 3 in citizen buildings, just barely to the point of needing to settle secondary islands, and not even to the point of shipping supplies between worlds. The campaign will abruptly end with no goal or reason to keep moving forward. Even if you prefer sandbox mode, the campaign shows off the game mechanics, which are slightly changed from previous iterations. I know more content is likely to come in future (paid) updates, but it seems ridiculous to have to pay for every little bit of a game that is $60 at launch (or $90 if you want future content automatically).  The building menus are also a mess in this game. Having to switch between three different menus (decorations, general building, and then class specific buildings) is messy and time consuming, but perhaps just a small complaint compared to everything else.  Lastly, of course, is the AI art (and who knows what else) in the game. It probably should have been obvious, seeing the downward trajectory Ubisoft has willingly decided to take, but no less disappointing. The art in previous Anno games was beautiful, and now it sucks to scrutinize every piece of art looking for hands with 8 fingers. Regardless, I think the AI question speaks more to the overall level of effort put into this game. At every turn, in every way, this game feels like corners were cut. I appreciate that Ubisoft has let us know that the game matches the team's "vision," but the vision is bleak.  I've played hundreds of hours in each Anno game previous to this (even the ones that weren't popular) and it sucks to say that I'm probably just going to uninstall this and come back to it much later to see if they address any of these issues. Sorry to say this might be my last Anno game if this is the direction that Ubisoft has decided to take the franchise. There is promise here, if issues can be resolved. The game is gorgeous, the gameplay is solid, and the mechanics are very similar to Anno 1800. I also enjoy playing the Celts and their unique mechanics. Also love having weather in the game! But these things are not enough to overcome the fact that this game should have been, at most, $20 given the amount of content you get and the fact that Ubisoft cut its labor costs by using AI.
