For fans of city-builders, the year 117 CE represents the Roman Empire at its absolute height, and Anno 117 embraces this setting with confidence. The presentation is one of the game’s brightest strengths. The tiers have different complexities and distinct personalities, with the Patricians standing out as particularly demanding and even a bit mean, which adds rich flavor to the social hierarchy. The Mediterranean-inspired soundtrack brings the landscape to life and blends naturally into the experience. The addition of hybrid tiers in Albion feels smart and refreshing, and the UI is clean, readable, and well matched to the era. At its best, the game feels like it could become a new Caesar IV or CivCity: Rome, something fans have wanted for years.  Unfortunately, the illusion cracks the deeper you go. The storyline, while never the main attraction in an Anno game, is extremely rushed and contributes little to the overall experience. The use of AI-generated backgrounds without proper review is disappointing, especially at the game’s price point. Gameplay systems also show signs of uneven development. Diagonal building is awkward and fights against a pathing system that can’t decide between linear or diagonal logic. Clicking on objects often becomes a struggle, and setting up trade routes feels unnecessarily fiddly.  Military development is another weak link. Armies move slowly and require too many residents to produce high-quality units. Specialists and area-of-effect mechanics behave unpredictably, sometimes shifting with minor population changes even when all needs are fully met. Performance issues cap things off: late-game cities introduce lag, forcing players to drop settings like ray tracing and cap their FPS just to keep everything stable.  Overall, Anno 117 boasts a beautiful Roman setting and several promising ideas, but the experience feels rushed and lacks the polish expected of a major release. With time, updates, and DLC, it may still grow into the grand Roman Empire simulator many hoped for, but right now it falls short of its full potential.  Pros:  -Gorgeous Roman Empire setting at its historical peak -Expressive, demanding tiers with distinct personalities, both charming and captivating -Mediterranean-style soundtrack that enhances the atmosphere -Clean, era-appropriate UI -Clever hybrid tiers in Albion -Strong potential as a spiritual successor to Caesar IV/CivCity: Rome  Cons:  -Storyline is rushed and thin -Price does not reflect the level of polish -AI-generated backgrounds lack proper review -Clunky diagonal building and inconsistent pathing -Frustrating click detection, especially for trade routes -Slow, population-heavy military production -Specialists and AoE mechanics behave inconsistently -Noticeable end-game performance issues

I can’t find any official forum, so I decided to write here. If you have a link to it, please send it to me.  First, let me say that I love the game. The things below are small details that I would love to see improved. The game is great—easily the best in the series—and for me it’s a solid 9/10. These changes would make it a perfect 10/10. I’m listing the following points from most important to least important:  1 – Some buildings behave strangely when placed at an angle. For example, the sheep farm: when placed normally, you can fit 3 fields in a row, but at a 45° angle you can no longer do that. There are several similar interactions, and it would be great if this could be fixed.  2 – The city-size debuffs feel way too strong, especially for lower-tier populations who cannot be made happy with goods. I often find myself placing theaters and baths in the poorest districts just to try to keep them happy, and they still remain unhappy. It feels like the correct strategy is to ignore fire, happiness, and health mechanics entirely and just throw in city watch buildings when the unstoppable disasters come anyway. If I were a developer, I would reduce the impact of city-size debuffs on lower population tiers—for example, Libertus only getting 25% of the debuff, Plebeians getting 50%, etc. I think this would encourage players to interact with the system instead of ignoring it.  3 – Specialists are far less interesting than in Anno 1800. In Anno 1800, specialists gave bonus materials, changed recipe requirements, and were extremely fun to use. Now when I place an Officum, it just feels like a complicated bathhouse with small production buffs. I would love to see specialists that, for example, allow you to use copper instead of iron for weapons in Albion, or a specialist who lets you make dye from lavender and gives bonus soap every 10 cycles. That would be much more exciting than what we have now.  4 – There is a lot of tech, but it eventually runs out. In a 100,000-population run, I know that for most of the game I won’t interact with the tech system at all. It would be nice to have repeatable tech, similar to permits from Anno 1800. For example, military tech could include a repeatable Praetorian Guard discovery where we assist the emperor in research and he grants us +1 unit of his personal guard. For civic tech, maybe repeatable permits for a faster Phoenician ship. There are tons of ideas that could make the tech system more interactive.  5 – A personal cosmetic request: Could we get a gate variant that doesn’t shoot and doesn’t require workforce? I don’t want to make a “liberty city” just because I want to decorate my town. Also, could we get curved aqueducts? They would look so much nicer if they could curve like roads do at angles.

Straight up, this is a mixed review. There are some very enjoyable aspects to 117, but it is let down in many others.  I have been waiting a long time for a Roman city builder that is not some fixed isometric mobile style game. Grand Ages Rome in 2009 was the last proper entry in the genre. Given how much I enjoyed past Anno titles, especially 1800, I thought this would be an easy win. Instead it has left a bitter taste.  The good: The setting is fantastic, with strong variety between the Roman and Celtic worlds and their civilisation tech trees. The music is excellent and the graphics have been solid for me at maximum settings with no issues that others seem to report.  The bad: After playing the demo I suspected the voice acting would be jarring. Thick British accents for Latin characters wear thin fast. It does not ruin the experience, but it does break immersion when voices feel out of place. The lip sync during animations is also rough and rarely matches.  What really undermines the game is the campaign. Without spoiling anything, it simply stops in act 2. You do not need to be a scholar of drama to know act 2 is not where a story should end. There is no reveal or cliffhanger, it just stops. It feels like the ending was cut entirely.  Then there is the paywall for cosmetic items. They might be ornamental, but I still feel short changed because the so called Ubisoft rewards are not rewards at all. They require Ubicoins which are extremely overpriced, so I will not be touching them.  Finally, as with every Ubisoft title, you must have a Ubisoft Connect account running in the background with an always online requirement. Not great for anyone who wants a straightforward single player experience.  Overall there is a lot to like, but not at the current price. Right now this feels like a $30 to $40 dollar game, not $90. I'll keep playing for the sandbox, but with much of the content paywalled or cut from the game, this feels dirty.  6.5/10. MID.

I want to start by saying that I have played every single Anno game since they were first released. My favourite is Anno 2205 (for the setting, not the stripped down mechanics), Anno 1800 and Anno 2070. Sadly Anno 117 pales in comparison.   Firstly, the setting - why have yet another Roman themed city builder? Look at the city builder tag on Steam and see just how many are Roman themed. I can guarantee that future DLC will have an Egyptian region which is the next highest overdone theme. Why not Central Europe, China or Japan? The Mongols or Russia? Scandinavia? There are so many more interesting themes to choose from instead of Rome.  Secondly - the mechanics. Anno 117 punishes the player for building more residences than required by increasing the negative impacts on your happiness/health/fire safety each time you increase a city level. I had fire stations covering every residence, and production building and was still -690 fire safety. This problem is increased by forcing the player to build production buildings in their residential blocks with their own positive and negative modifiers. You cannot play the game without doing this, and I really hope that they change the mechanic in later releases.  Thirdly - the islands are too small. I don't know why we still have these tiny little islands - half of which are covered in marshes in Albion (yet another terrible mechanic). I don't know if I was spoiled by Crown Falls, but we really need some bigger islands. This problem is further compacted by the fact that there is no workforce sharing between islands, meaning each island now needs an entire infrastructure just to export a handful of resources to my main island.   Lastly - the good things. The UI is great. Research is great. The Hall of Accolades is a great idea. It really is a beautiful game to look at.   My recommendation (and what I will be doing myself) is to hold off until some more interesting content is released and balances have been made.   In the meantime, I will be playing Anno 1800.

Alright, so I’ll give credit where it’s due, this game does improve on some things. The QoS upgrades are actually nice, buildings show you their radius, buffs and debuffs are clear, and getting goods for your residents doesn’t feel like you’re trying to solve a maths exam. There’s no newspaper spam, no passive aggressive citizens complaining every five seconds. If they get upset, they riot, you plonk down a police station, and boom social order restored. Honestly? I respect the simplicity.    But then… trading routes.  In Anno 1800, you could see exactly how long your ship took to do a loop. “Oh, this route takes 10 minutes, I make 4 per minute, therefore I move 40 to break even.” Clean. Logical. Adult game design.    In this one?  Either they removed it, forgot to add it, or I’ve suffered spontaneous selective blindness. I’m sitting here trying to calculate cargo like it’s a government tax form.    Okay, whatever I can cope.  But you know what I cannot cope with?    MULTIPLAYER. HOLY. HELL.    My mate and I live in Australia. Same country. Same internet gremlins. Yet somehow we get 500ms ping, as if our packets are being mailed by carrier pigeon through several alternate dimensions. The desyncs aren’t even the funny kind, the game just dies, refuses to recover, and then we have to restart the game AND Ubisoft Connect like we’re power cycling a life support machine.    And speaking of Ubisoft Connect…  Look, I’m convinced this software exists just to remind people how good Steam is. It’s unreliable, clunky, and somehow manages to be both slow and fragile. It’s like a launcher held together with spit and expired warranties.    And then there’s the historical accuracy bit…    I’m not expecting a university level history lecture, but some of the character choices feel… confusing. When you’re going for a specific historical period and then drop in completely anachronistic generals, it kind of breaks the immersion. It doesn’t ruin the game, but it definitely makes you squint a little.    FINAL VERDICT    There’s a good game buried in here, the core mechanics are fun, some QoL changes are genuinely great, but the multiplayer is currently held together with duct tape, hope, and Ubisoft Connect which makes playing with friends kinda sh#t which was the whole point of why I got it and that it is Roman time period.

[h1]Anno 117: Pax Romana - Game of the Year[/h1]  20 hours in and this is currently my 2025 Game of the Year (with the caveat that I haven't played EU5 yet).  Let's start with the basics. Anno is a city building series based on creating cities across islands and producing goods in increasingly-complex supply chains. You provide these goods to your citizens to upgrade their housing to the next level, which then opens the door for bigger, more complex supply chains. Unlike many games of this type, the houses don't devolve if the goods run out. However, fewer citizens will live in those houses and become increasingly irate until you meet their needs again.   It's a city builder that require the player to spin a lot of plates. Beyond just the basics of the supply chain management, you have trade routes, competitors, pirates, quests, city beautification, fires, riots, research and other mechanics. There's never a dull moment here and it's not the type of city builder you can just run in the background and it'll do its thing (aka City Skylines). This requires active participation by the player.  From a single player perspective, this is the best Anno has ever been right out of the gate. Anno has always done decently with the eye candy but this is head-and-shoulders above even the gorgeous Anno 1800. The soundtrack is nice too. and that's also to be expected.    Comparing it with past games, 117 is more user-friendly without losing any complexity. It has a good tutorial and enough options to suit anyone (more on that later). Yet it hasn't lost any of its complexity. If anything, with the advent of research and a more involved military aspect, this may be the most complex Anno yet. But it doesn't feel overwhelming, probably because 1800 with all the expansions had more depth than 117 has on release. That's really to be expected as releasing something as dense as 1800+4 seasons of expansions isn't possible or even advisable. Yet I don't feel like I'm missing anything because 117 adds mechanics that 1800 didn't have.  The diagonal roads are nice but that's a pretty minor thing. What I'm finding is the balance of having to constantly remake my city to accommodate new challenges. Like running aqueducts through town - really a necessity if you're going to prevent a fire from engulfing your entire town - requires some foresight or tough re-organizational choices. Putting manufacturing buildings in town create both challenges in placement and some consideration. For instance, you may want to place a +1 gold intermediate manufacturing building in town to get the money bonus but the -2 fire may cause more headaches than it's worth. The tech tree is similar in nature - it adds choice to the game but isn't anything earth shattering.  Combat is a big thing in this new title, though much of my time with the game has rendered it unnecessary. This isn't ever going to be Anno: Total War and the combat is a bit clunky but there seems to be a fair amount of depth there with different infantry, archers, cavalry and even siege weapons all offering strengths and weaknesses. The combat in the Anno series hasn't really ever interested me much and I doubt it'll change here. There's enough going on just building the cities, never mind worrying about military campaigns.   But what I may end up enjoying the most are the options and the ability to tune the game to my liking - and then change it as I want. The campaign was fine but after a few hours I decided to play in Sandbox, which is my usual MO with Anno. I made the game quite easy and started to build up my island(s) slowly as I figured things out. But I can go back at any time and add in AI governors, pirates and increase the difficulty of things like events and hazards. I'm not ready to do so yet but that offers all sorts of flexibility now and in the future. When I feel like I'm ready, I can go back and add in AI opponents after cherry-picking the best islands or putting a Navy together that keeps my people safe.  I've read some complaints about the UI and I generally find them a bit overblown. It's taken me a bit to get used to the UI but it's not a major issue and is built to accommodate new types of buildings and populations with the expansions. I believe people will look back and realize that this UI was a good idea.  Stamps/Blueprints aren't a thing in the game yet and that's a bit of a bummer at times. I can actually understand why - after a while in Anno 1800, I could throw down blueprints and just build to those. It took some creativity out of the game. Now everything onscreen is something I created. But at some point I'd like to get those back because they did make balancing out production so much simpler.  New game, 20 hours in and zero crashes or other issues. Nice.  I see complaints about multiplayer and I can't comment on that. I see complaints about the campaign not having a satisfying ending. I didn't bother finishing it because that's not how I like to play Anno.  Both of those issues are probably legitimate and if you want to play multiplayer or you're a campaign-driven player then maybe hold off. But if you like city builders or the Anno series, this is a must buy. I paid full price - heck, bought the Gold Edition for the expansions - and I don't regret it at all. I'm having a blast. In a year where there's been some truly outstanding strategy / building games like Farthest Frontier, Town to City, The Wandering Village and Two Point Museum - all of which deserve your hard-earned money and (more importantly) your time - Anno 117 is IMO the best of the bunch.   Verdict: Must buy, full price.

I'm a LONG time fan of the Anno Series.  117 is a welcomed breath of fresh air to 1800. 1800 obviously had more time to cook since release - on top of that many excellent mods that came out proceeding. Yet 117 feels SO much smoother by providing QOL features that streamline building, demolishing, and planning.   On top of that, there are some new gameplay mechanics that are welcome - land warfare, modular shipbuilding, diagonal building (huge), etc. At the same time, returning mechanics comeback in new ways that really get the noggin' joggin' - Sluice gates remind me of Enbasa's irrigation, but provide new arable land; Aqueducts provide production bonus to farms and mines - much like Electricity and Tractors.  The foundations of a GREAT game have been laid. I'm excited to see where they go with further expansions.   *Regarding half-baked campaign - My thoughts on this are that some cuts had to be made. this is UBISOFT after all. I fully expected some cuts, but I'm glad to see they didn't skimp on actual gameplay.   One complaint I have is the graphical performance. I run Bazzite Linux using a 9070xt on an Ultrawide monitor - 3440x1440. I can mainly get high-frame rate by using FSR (unspecified) and turning off Raytracing. This is a bit of a disappointment, but it's a tradeoff I'm willing to make. Sadly the image quality often feels like it's smearing all over the place (because of upscaling). I hope to see FSR4 implemented in the future. Overall there is NO hitching in the performance, barring the rare crash.

Multiplayer performance makes gameplay impossible in later stages. Desyncing causes the game to crash for everyone and then rejoining the game is difficult. Essentially an alpha version of the game. Will revisit if they fix this performance.

How quickly you research depends on how many citizens are around your academic build so it really is up to you on how you build your city around them.  Worth the price, I see myself playing this game for quite sometime

Don't let the negative reviews stop you from getting the game! I never leave reviews and this is my first time leaving a review for a game on Steam but seeing all the negative reviews I felt I needed to comment. The vast majority of the negative reviews aren't even for the gameplay.   To address some of the negative reviews I've seen: - Multiplayer Problems: I've been playing it with my wife this entire time and we've had some de-sync problems and they are annoying but also easily resolved by exiting out and loading the game again or loading back one previous save like the Anno website says. I'm sure they'll get these issues fixed soon, the game just barely launched. - AI Art: Personally I've barely seen much of the art and what I've seen is mostly in the background. If they did use AI art that is unfortunate, but the game is incredibly detailed and I'm sure their artists had more than enough to work on. Hopefully they'll replace it with real art, but it doesn't affect the game and the love and detail they put into it is easy to see. - Campaign Ending: I've seen a lot of people complain that it ends abruptly. Why does that matter? I'm sure they are going to add on to the campaign as they continue adding content to the game over the next few years. I'd rather have it end weird now and be a better/longer campaign in the long run than have them end it now just to "have an ending".  It's sad so many people are leaving negative reviews for non-gameplay related things, or things that will likely be fixed soon after launch. I hope they update their reviews as Ubisoft makes changes so that they continue to support and expand the game like they did with Anno 1800.   For some of the positive reasons you should get the game: - Building Area Affects: The new area affects around buildings make planning out your island even more fun. In Anno 1800 I just built all the residences in one place and all the production in another. - Knowledge Tree: It's fun to unlock new things and bonuses as you play that is unrelated to new citizen tiers. It also lets you choose what you want to focus on (military, civic, and economic) based on the game and what's happening. - Religion: The different bonuses from Gods and their shrines is a nice touch to make the different islands specializations more impactful. - Land Combat: I think it speaks for itself. The game has the ship combat I enjoyed in 1800, but now I can actually build walls and towers around my island as well as marching soldiers around the island to look cool while on patrol. - Albion: I never enjoyed the Old World in 1800, but Albion is more dynamic and enjoyable in my opinion. Being able to start in that region is also a nice touch.  There are a million more tiny things I've enjoyed that I'm probably forgetting, but I'd definitely recommend getting the game. I'm excited to see what they add to the game over the next few years.

In the days of my leisurely repose, I took up Pax Romana, a game promising dominion, trade, and the steady march of Roman order across distant shores. I cannot verify that every mechanic is perfectly faithful to the scrolls of our historians, yet I found myself drawn in like a senator to a heated forum debate.    The strategy is deep, the empire is vast, and every decision feels as weighty as choosing whether to fund the next aqueduct or patch the roads leading to a distant province. Battles unfold with satisfying clarity, cities rise with discipline, and the legions march with a sense of purpose befitting men who believe their labor honors something greater than themselves.    There were moments when I felt the temptation to overreach, to seize land for glory alone, but the game rewards careful stewardship and wise planning, virtues even Scripture teaches us to uphold. Build with patience, rule with humility, and your empire will flourish.    If you delight in strategy, in the slow but sure expansion of a realm shaped by your choices, and in the solemn grandeur of Rome herself, then this title will satisfy like bread and olives after a long journey.    Verdict: Worthy of a place in your library, citizen. May your empire prosper, and may you rule with justice, temperance, and the fear of God in your heart.

Underneath it's very polished exterior, fans of the anno series will find a very similar spiritual successor to anno 1800 with a couple important caveats --   1) Pro - there is more emphasis on RTS combat, with land troops 2) Pro - there is now a fun technology tree to unlock and discover upgrades for every aspect of your city 3) Pro - there is a diety worship and benefit system that unlocks island specific perks 4) Pro - many production buildings now also give area of effect bonuses or penalties, which makes city building more thoughtful than slapping down grid-like rows of houses 5) Pro - Houses can be upgraded more flexibly based on an either/or system of supplying commodities, so you're not forced to maximize literally everything (although you can)  6) Pro - There is a more thoughtful feedback loop to balancing happiness, flammability, and impressiveness scores for your city, and triggering parades brings in free specialists.   7) Con - there is NO commuter pier in the game to date, which means your workforces are locked into each island, while historically accurate, for better or worse.   I've had a ton of fun with the endless mode primarily, if you've liked any of the Anno games so far, you're in for a treat.

[h3]Finally, an Anno that dares to be different![/h3]    I absolutely love Pax Romana—and yes, I’ll say it loud: even with the AI art and the currently broken multiplayer.  This game is all about that addictive city-building vibe, Roman atmosphere, and gorgeous little details.  If you want to stare at markets, taverns, and marching legions for hours—welcome home!    To everyone raging in the forums about minor graphics, art choices, or missing features:  You forgot how to have fun. I play games for the fun, and Pax delivers.    Sure, it’s not perfect (when was Anno ever perfect on release?). But this feeling, this city, these late nights…  For me, it’s the best Anno in years. If you’re just here to nitpick, you’re missing the real experience.    TL;DR: Highly recommended for anyone who wants to get lost in city-building again. Haters can keep complaining—I’ll keep playing.

From the era when Anno 1503 first crossed my path, I have walked the long road of empire-building like a loyal servant of the Senate. Now, at last, the gods smile upon me and grant the chance to raise my banners in the age of Rome itself. Yes, the people grumble in the streets about the arcane workings of artificial intelligences and the ever-hungry tax collectors of House Ubisoft—but such things are mere distractions. The game still grants a governor endless hours to shape provinces, command legions, and adorn his city with ornaments fit for the divine.  It bears the likeness of Anno 1800, that much I cannot deny, and its art of war is as unwieldy as a fresh recruit’s shield. Yet ground troops now march with greater purpose, adding a measure of depth to the legions’ maneuvers. But truth be told, I care little for the politics of battle.  For my heart burns only for the rise of a new Rome—its marble, its markets, its majesty.  And so, by Jupiter and by my Emperor, I shall build Rome anew. 🏛️🔥

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐  Anno 117: Pax Romana is a remarkable blend of strategy and historical immersion that captivates players from the moment they enter its beautifully rendered world. The attention to detail in the game's graphics recreates the grandeur of the Roman Empire, making each city-building experience not only a task but a visual delight.  The gameplay mechanics are well-balanced, offering a satisfying mix of resource management and strategic planning. Players will appreciate the depth of the economic systems, which demand thoughtful decisions about trade, agriculture and urban development.   What sets Pax Romana apart is its commitment to historical authenticity. As you build your empire, you can't help but feel a connection to the past, making every victory and setback feel significant.  The community around Anno 117 is supportive, with players eager to share tips and strategies. This enhances the overall experience, making it easy to dive into and learn from fellow enthusiasts. Additionally, the game features multiple modes and challenges, which greatly increase its replay value.  Anno 117: Pax Romana stands out as a triumph in the city-building genre, delivering a polished and engaging experience. Whether you are a longtime fan of the series or a newcomer, this game promises dozens of hours of strategic fun and the thrill of empire-building. Ubisoft Mainz has crafted a title that not only respects the franchise's roots but also confidently pushes it into exciting new territories. Highly recommended for anyone looking to immerse themselves in the grandeur of the Roman Empire!

TLDR - If you like previous ANNO games you should also like this.     So first up, I'm on a I9 9900k, 3070 and 32gb of ram, 4tb M2 SSD. The game stays between 50and 60 fps at pretty much all times while over land and goes up to 90+ over water, and this is even on big islands with large populations, it holds up really well.    That being said there is some weird stuttering at times later in the game, but the frame rate isn't dropping, so its engine stuttering. Its not game breaking, just annoying. (note, this is all at 3x speed). The worst I saw was when I saw sending an armada of around 45 ships (which if you have played anno before, you know is absurd to begin with lmao) to destroy this island of raiders that I was tired of existing, it may have been an auto save thing causing it, not entirely sure, but randomly I'd have some heavy stuttering for 3-5 seconds and then it would be fine.    As for actual gameplay, its anno, if you've played one before you know basically what you're getting. It hasn't changed much from previous games. Military stuff seems better than other recent games though with more options for defence and troops.    If you've never played an anno game, I suggest watching some videos about it, but basically you're building towns/cities on islands that have specific types of resources on each one (gold and grape fertility on one, iron and hops on another for example) so you have to trade between your islands to get all the materials you need for the higher tier populations. That's the most basic rundown I can give you lol.    The UI is a bit strange, and sometimes I forget where stuff is even after 30 hours of play, but its not terrible and I can usually still find it in a few seconds. I would say its adequate, nothing spectacular and nothing terrible I can't deal with.    Currently (November 16th, 2025) there is an issue with interaction, where sometimes it seems mouse clicks just are not registered at all, this ranged from a mild annoyance while building to rage inducing when fighting a battle. Sometimes its also hyper sensitive to mouse movement, where I try to move a ship and my screen moves a fraction of an inch instead, in the heat of battle this can be really frustrating. So just be aware of that going in. Hopefully its fixed soon.    Outside of that, the graphics are nice, the gameplay is proper Anno, and Roman era is the best era, everyone knows that. This is the first Ubisoft game I've bought on release since probably 2017 or so, and I don't regret it.    I'd say as is, this is a solid 8/10 anno game even with the issues. If it gets the same support 1800 did this will easily become a 10/10 anno game in time.

Coming from playing all ANNOs i find the menus and buildings for this one kinda messy and confusing, islands are way too small and graphics are poorly optimized, reaching 40fps on my high end rig.  Dont buy this.

Honest review from a Anno Fan from the first hour of this series:     This game is absolutely astonishing. The graphic, volumetric clouds, lights, everything in this game looks eyecandy. The gameplay is not revolutionary, but the new gameplay mechanics are new and give a fresh wind for the series. Everything is bigger, so the isles are aswell.     But there are some things I would like to adress for people who might not sure about the purchase:     - Optimization is bad. Very bad. Beside that I am playing on ultra settings (1080P only), my fresh bought 5070 TI needs full usage of her power to let me play with not so stable 60 fps with only 5k population. Did not played further so far.     - QoL from Anno 1800 missing: You can´t choose the production tab out of the production buildings. It might seem not so bad, but it is. It makes the whole chain production management tedious and it doesn´t need to be. We know how it works better. This was only one thing, but there are many examples like this. Won´t adress any of them, but some aspects of the gameplay and UI are worse than in the previous title    - Quests: DANG, I JUST WANNA BUILD. They are very tedious, anyone wants to strictly annoy you.    So, do I recommend the game? Hell yes. It´s a nice and enjoyable city builder, but if you want to enjoy the beautiful graphic, you should have a high end gaming rig. But to be fair, the game looks with lower settings nice aswell.     I would say, buy it if you really want to enjoy the best city builder I played in the last years. But you won´t miss much if you want to wait until it gets optimized, because the game needs to cook atleast a couple of months to be stable.

Right, so I've been playing all the Anno games before this one...  It's good, it's pretty, the soundtrack is great and it does a bunch of new things, which is refreshing. And it's def—Anno, designed to keep you bound to your screen for hours.   BUT:  It's raw, (very) buggy, and lacks many conveniences compared to 1800. Let's think of it as teething issues that will be resolved in upcoming updates. I really, really hope so...  - Sometimes, for no reason at all, villagers don't count towards the population or miraculously consume a key resource at huge rates. This can cause your entire game to spiral out of control.  - Ships pop up out of nowhere, as if the game forgot about them and was like, 'Oh, ups, I guess here's a ship for you. ' Most ships can't even follow your trade ships across regions so the escort ships just pile up on the edge of the map!! - The pirates, while indeed dangerous and annoying to deal with, don't do much else. No missions, no trade...  - Horrible click registration. Either nothing happens when clicking on units or buildings, or it registers a left for a right click and so on... - Often, for weird reasons, you can't choose between straight and diagonal placement. Restart the game, and voila! Maybe, sometimes. - Can't build roads underneath diagonal aqueducts, resulting in diagonal aqueducts literally cutting your city and services in two... - The sound of the game often suddenly starts breaking down, absolutely unplayably so. Only solution: restart the game.  - The BLOODY ICONS!! You can't select goods if your mouse hovers over the label. So frustrating!! - It's a pain to switch between quests and keep an eye on what items are where, etc. This was def. Done better in previous games. - It's a pain to pick stuff up from the ocean, the animation and icons are waaay too finicky and small. - Sometimes, out of nowhere, the game crashes. WTF.  -A lot of quests are impossible or near impossible to do as you just can't find the items in your city.  - Pirates sometimes just freeze on the spot and jitter around. - A lot of core game mechanics (boni & mali of buildings, specialists, etc.) are cryptic, poorly visible and badly or not explained in the tutorial. - The GUI is.....okay. It takes a while to get used to, but it's definitely not optimized. Still a long way to go until this thing is anywhere close to 'smooth' - There's so much more minor and major technical, gameplay and usability stuff.....What I don't get: You put this game in front of a tester panel for a couple of weeks, and ALL of these things would have been picked up. So why not? So we have to pay full price for a title to do the testing for them? Not cool....  So, yeah, it's a good game, and I am super keen to see where future debugging, patching, and DLCs will take it. However, until these basic bugs, technical issues, and design flaws have been addressed, this game receives a negative review from me. My recommendation would be: If you're a fan, buy it! If you're intrigued by Anno, consider getting 1800 and a few select DLCs, and you'll have an excellent entry into the series while this game receives the TLC it deserves.

Coming from a long time series fan whose favorite game is 2205, I think I'm going to let this one cook for a bit before coming back.   The UI seems to exclude (or hide) a lot of information I'm used to having, and it's not clear if that is because it is irrelevant due to gameplay changes or because ithe UI is poorly designed. Either way I'm having a difficult time seeing when my actions have positive impact and when I've made a mistake until the game starts shouting at me, and that's... the entire game.  The English VO is usually rough in Anno, but here it is actually atrocious. The characters lack any distinguishing personality, like a community theater production of Julius Caesar, and the direction is all over the place. Roman workers in Italia with Cockney accents. All of the spoken Latin is mispronounced and delivered like it's being read off cue cards.  I started playing the game with the French VO. I don't speak French. I'd pay big money for Latin VO, but yes, I'm a weirdo.  I'm not excited by the return of this combat model -- pre-modern Anno combat suuucks -- but I haven't seen it in heavy action yet. Who knows, maybe they get it right this time. Eighth time's a charm.   Overall, the content is fine, it's clearly an Anno game, more in line with the early games in concept and intent, with some (but not all) modern quality-of-life improvements. It runs fairly cleanly for a AAA title in 2025, on high settings with a mid-tier card -- no complaints. I haven't encountered any obvious AI slop, but I'm sure it's in there.   It's an Ubisoft game in its first week; you know what you're in for. If you think building and supplying Roman colonies in a fictionalized 2nd century sounds like a good time, you're going to like this.
Multiplayer still unstable and crashing every 1-2 hours, but reconnecting works most of the time. Other than that, just another great Anno game.

There are so many Details Loving it :)   Bugs & Performance:  i didnt experience big performance issues - but the GPU load is very high on medium to high (not very high settings) on a 4070 Super + i7 12th gen. i have some optical glitches, where Focusing just don't work and fiddle around with camera&mouse to highlight a building.  there is a list of old unfixed bugs like you have to demolish and rebuild a building from planning mode.

Aside from the various Multiplayer connection issues - which will be fixed in the future i assume - the game suffers from a few things:  lack of depth - it obviously lacks the dlcs that expanded 1800 so much, but end game production chains are just a bit lacking. Specialists are mostly pure x area gets y minor buff things, no swapping out resources or extra production. Nothing really exciting there, especially compared to the variety in 1800.  The military feels a bit all over the place, ships path clunky and weirdly stop when issuing new commands. Land combat feels like mostly smashing two blobs into each other and the bigger one wins - unless there are ships in range, since those absolutely shred land troops in seconds.  The research tree feels a bit tacked on, some interesting things are in there, but also a lot of stuff you would expect to just get unlocked for free is in there as well - upgraded warehouses, better roads, ship repair cranes. With the time research takes this sometimes feels like you are being punished for not immediately knowing what to research in which order, making you wait for hours sometimes for somewhat crucial upgrades.  Quest & campaign story are... a thing, frankly the campaign isn't that compelling but fine, act 3 missing really hurts it though it just ends at what feels like the halfway point. Quests are somewhat bugged occasionally.  Id recommend the game aside from those things, as its still fun and i expect the tech issues to get fixed once the next update hits. Especially as the new mechanic of production buildings giving buffs/debuffs plays well and is interesting to use, so the overall core Anno game play is quite good.  HOWEVER: using AI generated slop as "art" in game by a studio of this size and resources is completely inexcusable.  Edit after the 1.3 update: Multiplayer issues are mostly fixed, depth is still lacking and the "replacement" of the Ai slop loading screen art was just someone cropping it and blurring out the worst errors. Might as well have stuck to the original slop if this is what the actual "creative vision" is. Absolutely no mention of the missing act 3.   At this pace maybe worth buying in two years at 90% off.

Overall, the gameplay is great, as expected from an Anno game. But I don’t want to give a positive review because of some major points, and maybe Ubisoft thinks twice about some decisions in the future when creating new games:  Cons: - AI usage: How are artists feeling when their work is being combined with AI art in a product? AI should only be used for design directions and not in the final product. Especially in such a big company. I’m an indie game dev, and even I create all art-related things myself! - The dialogues, quests, and campaign feel bland and not interesting – the campaign ends as if this is an Early Access game. - The character portraits feel really lifeless and emotionless and lack a lot of detail and soul. Anno 1800 did a far better job in that, much more detailed. There is also not a huge choice, and they are not really interesting. - Wokeness: Usually I am not jumping on the woke train, but the rival NPCs – what is the meaning of having all except one not being a Roman governor? Greek, Hispania, Chinese, Celtic, Mesopotamia, Egypt? Where are traditional Romans? I hoped to play a Roman-based game….  Gameplay: - Islands are too small, or the official buildings too big. They need a lot of space, and then there’s not much room for houses anymore. - It was more fun to “buy” the islands from others in 1800. Here you either need to wait a long time until you have 100 points with the Emperor, but the way to get there is not the funniest. In 1800 it felt really satisfying to buy the islands of the AI. - War & military: The constant attacking raiders – this was not so extreme in 1800 as well. This was supposed to be a very in-depth review by an Annoholic with 20+ years of experience with the franchise, but it turns out that Steam has a character limit for reviews. I guess that the format really isn't suited for a 2600 word essay.   I’ll give you the intro and outro and maybe I’ll upload a detailed review on Youtube.   Anno 117 makes a great first impression. The new mechanics are meaningful and feel like a genuine step forward for the franchise. The graphics and overall presentation are amazing and the game as a whole feels like the next logical step for the franchise.   Why the negative score then? Is it because of the extensive use of AI? Or maybe because of Ubisoft connect? No. While both of these are serious issues, by far the biggest problem with Anno 117 is that it launched in an unfinished state. With its terribly rushed campaign, braindead computer players, dozens of UI bugs and severe balancing problems, some parts of the game feel like a finished product, while others feel like they haven’t left alpha.   ...  Anno 117 has the bones of a truly [i]great[/i] game, but is unfinished and in my opinion needs at least 6 more months of work to fix its core mechanics. I very much doubt that the developer will delay working on the DLCs in order to fix the main game. Anno 117 is playable and most of the time it’s even enjoyable, but built up frustration from the multitude of bugs and lack of balancing left me disappointed.   I grew up with this franchise and it breaks my heart to think that this may well turn into another case where corporate greed has killed a beloved franchise. I really hope that the developers get their act together and it doesn’t come to that.

My Take on Anno 117 — Why All the Confusion? It’s Fantastic ------------------------------------------------------------ I honestly don’t understand some of the current reviews. I’m having a great time with Anno 117. Sure, there are tiny issues here and there — most of which I didn’t even notice until other people pointed them out — and let’s be real: those small things will be patched soon anyway.  About the “AI slop” complaints… really? -------------------------------------- I’ve seen some people calling the AI “slop” and using that as proof that the developers didn’t put care into the game — and honestly, I find that completely unjust. If anything, Anno 117 feels overflowing with care and craftsmanship. The sheer amount of detail, the animations, the atmosphere, the little stories happening in every corner of your city… none of that looks rushed or neglected. And even if there would be some hardly noticeable AI help in some illustration, who cares? It feels like nitpicking rather than a fair reflection of the game.  A World Overflowing With Detail ------------------------------- Calling this game lacking in detail feels wildly unfair. There’s a guy walking around in the audience selling authentic looking snacks in the amphitheater while a play is happening — how many city builders do that? The whole city feels alive in the same way Anno 1800 did, but with even more personality.  If there's a scale of detail where City Skylines 2 sits at the “empty parks, graphical glitches, zero charm” end, then Anno 117 is at the complete opposite. People are everywhere, doing things, animating the world. It’s like a living picture book you can zoom into. It sparks imagination, atmosphere, and inspiration — and it genuinely makes you want to decorate and improve your city just to see it flourish.  One of my favorite touches: how an uprising slowly builds — first a few angry people, then more, until it becomes a real, noisy crowd. It’s so cool and detailed.  Add to that the warm sunlight, the landscapes, and the overall world design, and honestly? I think it looks even more beautiful than Anno 1800.  The UI — Not Perfect, but Nothing Major --------------------------------------- I’m not the biggest fan of the UI style — it feels a bit too clean and modern for ancient Rome. But then again, what UI would be completely authentic without being annoying to use?  Functionally, it works fine. The only odd thing I noticed is that I can drag resources from the warehouse screen to a ship, but not directly from the standard view. No dealbreaker though.  Gameplay Improvements That Actually Feel Great ---------------------------------------------- The new features compared to Anno 1800 feel genuinely refreshing: - The research tree is great. - Not having to supply every island with the same goods again and again makes the game feel far less mechanical. - The overall rhythm is relaxing, atmospheric, and faithful to classic Anno.  Honestly, this might be the best Anno gameplay has ever been. I’ve only played a few hours so far, but everything points in a really good direction.  The Only Thing I Miss: The 1800 Setting --------------------------------------- I’ll admit it: Anno 1800’s setting is still unbeatable for me. It’s just the most exciting and interesting time period Anno has ever explored. It just fits the game the most for so many reasons. I can’t help wishing that Anno 117’s level of beauty and detail could someday be brought into that era.  But that’s personal taste — not a fault of Anno 117.  Final Verdict: An Easy Recommendation ------------------------------------- Anno 117 is a clear recommendation. If you’ve enjoyed any Anno game in the past, this is absolutely a must-play. For beginners — give it a try and see how it feels. For everyone else: it’s a wonderfully crafted city builder full of atmosphere, charm, detail, and classic Anno soul.

Edit: After reaching the endgame, I must say I am dissapointed by more than just the use of AI. The game feels very lacking in terms of (impactful) decisions and the economy itself is too easy to manage in my opinion. Furthermore, a part of the core gameplay is just not fun: settling islands. Settling islands costs money again (instead of influence like in 1800) and that means settling an island to aquire new ressources (especially in the early game) goes like this: you decide on an island. You wait until you have the required money (which can be A LOT because of the scaling with size and number of islands), which can take 10 or sometimes even 20 minutes. Keep in mind YOU CANNOT REALLY DO ANYTHING while waiting because most things cost money and you're trying to save that. And then you settle the island. I don't really get the decision making behind exchanging influence for money. WIth influence, you were at least able to proactiely do something by increasing population.  At this point I cannot reccomend the game. It feels like a step backwards. Which is a shame, because e.g. the use of verticality in this game is really good and a fun new aspect.

Less obvious reason, and for me this is why I cannot recommend the game in this state, is missing storage tab under the statistics menu. This may seem like an oversight or minor nuisance but it is not. The storage tab contained times of when different ships arrived. Since this is not there now, there is no way to control more advanced economics with multiple islands because you don't know how much time it takes for each ship to finish a round-trip: https://www.reddit.com/r/anno/comments/1oycmzj/what_happened_to_statistics_menu/. Pax Romana feels like a fresh breath woven into the long chronicle of the Anno series. The game greets you with a gentle, deliberate antiquity, where everything — from the tone of the marble to the shadow cast by an aqueduct — breathes not historical accuracy, but atmosphere. Here, architecture is not a backdrop but a text written on a diagonal. The city sheds its grid-like rigidity and becomes a living organism — bending like a river, unfolding like a sunbeam across the forum.  There is no rush in Pax Romana. This is a game for those who know how to savor the movement of time — like a wine that should never be swallowed in one gulp. It reveals itself slowly, organically, layer after layer, as if whispering to the player: “Don’t hurry. Watch your city breathe.” And in this lies its greatest strength. Those who try to speedrun Anno miss the invisible magic — that moment when the streets begin to live on their own, and you find yourself standing on a hill, quietly witnessing your world becoming complete.  The innovations here aren’t box-ticking exercises. Diagonal layouts, building clusters, environmental accents — these stem not from “now we can” but from “now it is beautiful.” Pax Romana hasn’t merely upgraded its city-building model; it has made its cities more natural, almost tangible. At times it feels as though you aren’t constructing an island, but unrolling a papyrus drawn by a Roman architect who has suddenly been handed modern tools.  Yes, the game isn’t perfect. And yes, like Anno 1800, it will mature over years — growing new content, cultivating young ideas, polishing its mechanics to a jeweler’s precision. But this is exactly the rhythm of its life. It is a platform-game, a soil-game, from which something truly grand will grow. And that is why it deserves a generous advance of trust: not because it is already the pinnacle, but because it clearly carries the promise of future greatness. Ubisoft Mainz has long shown that they know how to turn such promises into reality.  And perhaps most importantly — Pax Romana brings us back to the essence of the series. To a world meant not to be completed, but to be lived in. To cities meant not for ruthless optimization, but for admiration. To a slow beauty the modern industry too often forgets.  Pax Romana is an ancient prayer to rhythm and contemplation. And if you give it time, it will unfold like a city under the Roman sun — unhurried, majestic, carrying the quiet promise of a long and luminous life.

I cannot believe I waited years for this game. Anno is such a simple formula that I honestly thought even Ubisoft could not possibly mess it up. Anno 1800 is almost a decade old and is still a hundred times better than whatever this is. Here is the breakdown.    [h2] PROS [/h2]    • The graphics are mildly decent. Though even on the highest settings there is nothing truly impressive or memorable.  • The sound is mild and non intrusive.    [h2] CONS [/h2]    • This game launched as a final version, not early access, yet it is full of bugs. Carts float in the sky, troops refuse to encamp, you cannot assign units to buildings, and entire chunks of your population vanish with no housing changes. The only fix is to build an isolated block of homes or delete and rebuild everything so the game remembers your citizens exist. There are many more issues but this gives the picture.    • I have no idea why the story wants me to relate to the emperor’s daughter or wife. I do not. It feels like the developers pushed an alternative history angle just to check boxes. There has never been a female Roman emperor and forcing this new ideal makes the entire setting feel ungrounded and historically hollow.    • Troops handle like they were programmed by someone who has never played a strategy game. They ignore stances, ignore commands, and sometimes simply wander off. I set a group to patrol and when I needed them to stop and fight they kept strolling past the enemy like nothing happened.    • You cannot meaningfully interact with your citizens. This is supposed to be a city builder, yet I cannot click on a single person to see what they are doing or feeling. It is lazy development. Citizens are boring and repetitive. Entertainment centers show the same people doing the same animations forever. Musicians never change, riders loop in circles in the hippodrome, fighters repeat the same motion in the colosseum, craftsmen buildings show the same stale animation.    • The dialogue is dull and the characters feel like they were written by a committee trying to impress a modern social media crowd. Irresponsible greedy chauvinist man emperor. Righteous rebellious daughter. Passive nurturing but fragile mother who becomes a powerful leader only once the big bad man exits the story. New emperor who exists only to like war. Rebel Celt woman leader fighting against the awful Romans to protect her people. Random racial characters shoehorned in. It all feels forced and unnatural. I am very open minded but this was so artificially inserted that even I could not stomach it.    [h2] CONCLUSION [/h2]    They were handed a masterpiece blueprint on a silver platter. All they had to do was take the proven Anno 1800 formula and give it Roman architecture. Instead they created something unfinished, uninspired, and dramatically lower quality than a game released six years earlier. It is almost impressive how they managed to regress this far while having better tools, more money, and a decade of player feedback.    This game is not a step backward. It is a collapse. It looks worse, plays worse, performs worse, and feels like nobody involved understood what made Anno great in the first place. The whole product feels hollow, rushed, politically sanitized, and creatively empty.    They did not need innovation. They did not need reinvention. They could have used the Anno 1800 framework, let AI design buildings, and focused on polishing mechanics. Instead they delivered something that resembles a knockoff of their own series.    If this is the direction of modern city builders, something is seriously wrong. This is not evolution. This is decay.

Edit since nobody reads the comments:  I played on Ubisoft Connect during both betas and launch. Steam doesn't let you write a review until you've played a certain amount of time, so I left it running till the refund window and then it let me send my review. All my playtime is on Ubisoft Connect. For people attacking my Anno 1800 playtime as well, I have 1786 hours on Ubisoft Connect and own all DLC. That's why I have 36 minutes of playtime on Steam but 88/112 achievements.   Original review: They never finished the campaign. That's not a joke, the campaign just ends during act 2. Thought my game was bugged, looked it up, turns out that's it! They never finished the campaign before pushing it out. The other acts got scrapped. Sucks, because it was one of the better Anno campaigns until it just ends mid-sentence out of nowhere.   Lot of AI art in the game. Game overall feels half finished. Lots of little things adding up, like the AI was supposedly placeholder images. Pirates have flintlock pistols on their flags. (It's an asset from Anno 1800, they're over a thousand years early to the party in the year 117, haha.) Lots of small issues like that aren't that big of a deal but add up to making the game feel rushed or half-finished, like no field workers or industry workers spawning, but there's also major gameplay issues like MP currently not working. (Lots of desyncs and other issues making MP a huge pain to actually play. Supposedly a patch is coming in the following weeks to work on this.)   It's missing a lot of features/quality of life from the past games that 1800 had on launch. The diagonal roads and ground combat returning are nice, but not enough to carry the entire game. With Ubisoft apparently going under, give it a year or so to see if the Anno team manages to finish the game after whatever Ubisoft goes through, and what kind of fixes/DLC they push out. Remember, Anno 1800 wasn't great on launch, either. 5 years of DLC made it into my favorite entry next to 1404.

Anno 117: Pax Romana is a fresh and brilliant addition to the Anno series, bringing a truly polished and entertaining city-building experience back into the limelight. The jump deep into the Roman Empire works beautifully with this franchise; every settlement is alive with detailed architecture, busy trading, and the omnipresent hum of imperial ambition. Attention to historical detail is stunning, and the world is even more alive, from the sun-drenched Mediterranean coasts to the more rugged frontier provinces.  The gameplay hits a sweet spot between depth and accessibility. It is satisfying to build up production chains, the logistics feel smooth and intuitive, and the new regional mechanics encourage careful expansion without overwhelming the player. Managing Roman citizens, fending off political pressures, and balancing the needs of a growing province all combine for a gameplay loop that's incredibly rewarding.  Most of all, though, Anno 117 captures the joy that defines the series: the thrill of watching a small outpost flourish into a thriving Roman city. Whether you're a longtime fan or completely new to Anno, this is a rich, beautifully designed strategy game that’s easy to recommend.

I will start off by saying I literally do not care about AI slop artwork in the game. I wouldn't mind even more AI slop if it meant they addressed other things.  My biggest problem is the multiplayer that breaks after 10 hours or so and then just stops working. However, I just think the game overall suffers from a lack of polish and like every other AAA title these days, could have used another 6-12 months in development before release.  I am trying to be fair and not just complain about all of the ways this isn't Anno 1800 as I remember the base game before the 4 years of expansion packs that fundamentally reshaped the game. That being said, what's the point of all of that iterating if you just scrap it all and forget what works and doesn't work and you try and reinvent the wheel drastically. There was a lot that could've been reused and tweaked so that it still feels like a new game and not a reskin, but I think there was a bit too much done here just to be different.  The good: - Campaign is unusually good for an Anno game....while it lasts. It's just kind of ends abruptly. but hopefully you didn't download the game for a campaign. - The game is visually stunning. The lighting alone makes it very hard to go back. - Land combat is back and just a ton of fun.  - It's the Roman empire. Come on.  The meh: - Albion is a bad copy-paste job with some minor differences. Unlike the New World, it doesn't feel different enough, just repetitive.  - The tech tree mechanics are really unnecessary and I hope they are patched out. This isn't Civilization and putting improved roads behind a tech tree is just asinine. - The new area of effect system really takes away the charm of the game in my mind and turns it into the worst parts of Civ 6 where you spend more time worrying about adjacency than the actual production flow.  - The economy just really isn't balanced. The new needs system is okay, but doesn't feel polished at all.  The bad: - The UI is a mess. Almost every screen is a massive step backwards from 1800. It's difficult to find buildings because of how they're organized. You can't pin resources. Trade routes are hard to see. It makes it harder to do anything. - See the top rant. Too much was changed just to be different.

At full price, I can't recommend, and the reasons are thus:  1) It feels much more scaled down than 1800 or 2205 (or whatever year the future one was).  Populations, cities, armies, fleets all seem much smaller. Maybe it's a factor of the time period the game is set in; you don't get cities as massive or sprawling in 117 as you would in 1800. The islands also seem smaller, maybe because of the addition of diagonal building, (might be out to lunch, but 1800 houses were 3x3, but 117 are 4x4, and a single road can lock up 2 squares). I've also seen something here that I've never seen in another Anno game, that being identical islands in the same region. Same bays, same mountain and marsh layout, with just the resources being somewhat different.  2) Military. Maybe as a result of the smaller populations and incomes, it feels like militaries and fleets are harder to build and maintain. In the campaign at least I found myself with three military ships and barely had the population to build a single army stack even after developing the majority of my home island and starting to import goods from satellite islands to free up pop. Could just be me though.  3) Graphics. While you won't have a sprawling metropolis like 1800, the lighting at sunrise and sunset make the islands and cities you build look stellar, but the rest of the visuals seem like a slight downgrade. Takes over five minutes for my saved campaign to load in, and longer still for the details to finally pop in properly after that.  4: Misc. If a certain port owner is part of a quest that you don't want to trigger just yet, don't take contracts from them; trying to hand them in is impossible without triggering the quest you're trying to avoid.  Icons of a character talking will pop in, disappear, then a few seconds later the banner of an actual conversation with them will appear centre-screen. It's just a bit jarring. Everything regarding AI at this point. It's been covered to death. Pay artists, don't endorse environmental ruin or the theft of artists intellectual property. Just scummy.  If you like Anno, this game will be an okay time, but at the moment, I can't really recommend it for the price tag alone. All of the other stuff I can look past or tough out, but everything taken together for a near $100 game just comes off as criminal. When I feel the urge to play a game like this, my mind and heart are automatically going to 1800 again.

Anno 117: Pax Romana stands out as one of the most refined and confident entries the franchise has ever produced.  Some people online point out that a few promotional pictures look AI-generated. That isn’t a flaw — it’s simply part of modern marketing workflows. The important part is that the game itself is made by skilled developers, and their craftsmanship is obvious in every system, animation, and design choice. The world is stunning, richly detailed, and alive in a way that feels unmistakably hand-crafted.    The gameplay sets a new benchmark for the series. Every mechanic feels responsive, thoughtful, and deeply satisfying. Naval gameplay in particular is exceptional: the ships are beautifully designed, the movement is smooth, and the sense of exploration and logistics has never felt better.    Fans of Anno 1800 will immediately recognize its strongest elements here — but elevated. Anno 117 takes everything that worked in its predecessor and enhances it with smarter systems, cleaner interfaces, and a generous collection of quality-of-life improvements that streamline play without sacrificing depth. Production chains, citizen behavior, city layout, and progression all feel more polished and more intuitive.    This isn’t just another Anno title — it’s a bold upgrade to the formula. With greater technical polish, sharper design, and a powerful sense of vision, Anno 117: Pax Romana confidently proves how far the series has come and how high it can reach.

This game is the epitome of wasted potential that turned what should've been a resurgence of the Anno series into another nail in the coffin of a destitute Ubisoft.  The good:  * It's set in ancient Rome. The color palette, the graphical fidelity and art style (also see; the bad regarding some of the graphical fidelity), the return to land-based combat...it's a 'new' historical setting for a game that's been bereft with good, but sometimes just off-the-mark, historical/future placing.  * It's Anno - the technical aspects of the gameplay are here. Build a supply chain, build trade routes..profit. * The new hex/diagonal building system gives you more fidelity over your building/aesthetic placement and makes your towns look more organic than in prevous titles  The bad: * The writing is absolutely awful. Just spectacularly bad. The main character is infantilized like you wouldn't believe - every two seconds they're making some ridiculously stupid face/sound combination, like a toddler would with a parent when asking for some sort of emotional approval, or just stupidly fumbling around with words. And they're doing this to...the Emperor of Rome, while being appointed the governor of an island. And this isn't even the most idiotic or frustrating part of the writing. * The DEI involvement in the writing - the campaign story revolves around the Emperor of Rome making his wife his successor instead of adopting a son (like..say..maybe you, the character?) to be the new Emperor. This, of course, makes 'yt men' in Rome mad, and they overthrow her. It's just so poorly written and blatantly stupid. You have a female blacksmith - in ancient Rome. You have black centurion - in ancient Rome. It's dumb. It takes you out of the game. Granted - this isn't supposed to be a fictional historical title, nor is it supposed to be a particularly engaging one - but holy hell does the obviously shill writing make you want to vomit.  * The AI art they use for the loading screens and backdrops is stunningly bad. The previous titles all had such amazing artwork, which you can certainly achieve or get near with AI, and this game chooses the most half-cheeked garbage for its backdrops.  * The graphical fidelity, when zoomed out, loses its focus, and the game looks like a blurred mess. It's sometimes difficult to tell where your building placement is going and where it's effects are going to be.  * The switch from the road/distance based buildings affects back to the circular-style effect is a step backwards in game design and functionality. It's just..bad.   If you really, truly enjoy the Anno series or city builders in general, this might be a game you could pick up on sale. Otherwise..it's a strong avoid for anyone else, and even those in that vein, there are better out or even the previous Anno 1800 as a better overall pick than this.

Honestly the most frustrating Anno title I have ever played.   So far I played every game of the series and did find them OK. I did read a lot about "streamlined for a broader audience" , but its quite the opposite In older games you got kind of a motivation to expand, here it is rather frustration.    This one is like you take bad parts and make them worse, while put even the worst things you can come up with for game design.   Anno was always about build a city, with deep and complex gameplay. here you have a lot of design choices that makes the game kind of artificially to frustrating.   -1- Way slower production of goods and money   -2- You cant trade in earl/mid game with NPCs. It was like the best part in early game to kick start your production by buy all the basic resources from your NPCs competitors, but now due very high demand of diplomacy points you cant trade and I have already level 3 citizens.  -3- Religion and Science points for research? You have a very, very long tree there, this means start new maps is very exhausting and repetitive.  -4- To properly unlock all Religion perks you need many, many citizens next to temple, here is the twist, you can have only 1 god per island, considering only the starting island is big, you need to conquer your neighbours to properly benefit from it.     -5- City Maintenance is very annoying, you can cover up the map with enough fire watchers,medic houses and city security. So you frequently have to repair the city, which simply slows you down.     And that the main Issue I have, each new mechanic added to the game is there to simply slows you down. Who did think that can be a good game design?  Again, slow you down is only challenge for your patience.  After mid game, it feels like you have a grind game with no goal.  I really wish they could redesign the game in something feasible and fun.

Recensio Anno 117 ab Homine Romano-Celtico (version sans ♥♥♥)  Per Teutates et per Iovem… Anno 117 est ludus qui me fēcit dubitāre utrum sit melius imperia aedificāre an cervisiam bibere et diem finīre.  Initium facio in viculo humili, quasi pagus meus in Arvernia — porci, frumentum, odor fumī… omnia bona. Sed mox Romani adsunt: leges, tributa, et illa vox “viae rectae faciendae sunt.” Ita, bene, faciam vias rectas. Deinde ludus iubet: “Thermae quoque necessariae sunt.” THERMAE. Vix habeo stagnum plenum ranarum, sed faciam.  Graphicae? Tam pulchrae ut etiam druides nostri suspirent et dicant: “Haec magia haud mala est…”  Cives mei? Docti a Romanis queruntur sine fine: “Aquam volumus!” “Panem volumus!” “Togas pulchriores desideramus!” Amici… si tantum luxum petunt, veniant in silvas nostras et discant vivere sine tunicā.  Res oeconomica? Ars obscura pura. Uno momento mercatura Britannis ago, altero momento minus pecuniae habeo quam post tributa Caesaris triennia. Nihil intellego… sed ludus subridet atque sibilat: “Itera.” Et ego iterum ago. Stultus sum? Fortasse ita est.  Bellum? Hic me bene gero. Romani dicunt: “Exercitus disciplinatus exstruendus est.” Respondeo: “Nonne satis est manus mea Celtarum ebriorum?” Ludus negat. Ita… legionem creo. Et vincit. Sed Celtæ mei tristes sunt. Nemo laetus est. Vita dura est.  Conclusio: Anno 117 te facit sentire semis Romanum, semis Celticum, tota mente captum. Dum ludo, voces deorum audire videor: “Unus turnus amplius.” Et pareo.  X ex X — sicut cervisia optima: bibes, rides, et cras iterum facies.

The game introduces a mix of fresh ideas and familiar elements and at first this creates the impression that it could grow into a deep city builder, even though basic features like the stamp feature or trade statistics are missing. The positive impression fades once these systems are put into use.   [list]     [*] [b]Needs[/b]: Goods cannot replace each other, so you must still produce every earlier resource even if the point system suggests to focus on higher tiers.       [*] [b]Trade[/b]: At your harbour you can buy everything at a lower price than local production, which removes the purpose of building most supply chains. A proper statistical trade overview is also missing.      [*] [b]Workforce[/b]: Workforce requirements feel unbalanced because a single ship or unit can drain an entire city, which makes buying ships or goods far more sensible than producing them.       [*] [b]Religion[/b]: The gods offer unreliable effects that shift with faith levels, so they cannot support long term planning.      [*] [b]Campaign[/b]: The campaign ends without completing its own story      [*] [b]Multiplayer[/b]: Multiplayer currently doesn't work due to a "Trajan" or "Romus" bug. (whatever that means)      [*] [b]AI[/b]: The Art Style relies on AI assets that feel oddly lifeless      [*] [b]Personalisation[/b]: To personalise your character you can only chose from the portraits of inhabitants so far.   [/list]  Many systems look more like framework for future expansions. Can´t currently recommend but I hope it will get better with time.

I like the optional needs but the way it's implemented leaves the entire needs system lackluster, chains are straightforward, rarely even requiring an intermediate good. I am so sick of AAA gaming and I'm sick of Ubisoft.  I've been excited for Anno 117 ever since its announcement back in June 2024. Sadly, 117 is marred by so much AAA tomfoolery that it ultimately leaves a very sour and unpleasant taste in my mouth:   1. Ubisoft forced-parity. I am SO sick of Ubisoft trying so desperately to shoehorn their crappy bloatware onto my system. This game requires you to use the Ubisoft launcher. More fluff added to this already 100+ gigabyte game that wishes to merely sit in the background of your computer even whilst idle. They do this simply to collect data from their customers, mind you. There is no financial incentive beyond that very clear objective. It shouldn't be allowed, and it shouldn't be sold on steam if a third party launcher is required for it to function at all in the first place.    2. Technical performance should NEVER be an issue for games with this much commercial backing behind it - The fact that AAA companies have all but made it their MO to release half-broken trash-heaps on launch with the promise of fixing things later on is in of itself a fair reason to refund. 4090 - I had shadow flickering issues, LOD issues, and a UI bug that ONLY disappeared when I reenabled the crappy Ubisoft in-game overlay function (potentially trying to force overlay on customers) - these bugs existed with latest Nvidia drivers and turning off the Raytracing setting fixed a majority of them - but now that setting is broken, obviously...  a terrible first impression for a $60 game released by a AAA company.    3. AI usage. They sneaked their AI-usage label on us last minute prior to launch because they knew it'd cause a stir. Unfortunately, there are various assets in this game utilizing generative AI. You might be in the "who cares" camp, and as much as I understand that sentiment, the AI content is unfortunately used for all of the things you really hoped it wouldn't be used for - like the usually incredible key-art and concept art that shows up in the background of every loading screen. Anno 1800 had incredibly detailed, hand-crafted concept art, and even if merely aesthetic, the art has seen a clear major downgrade - but .... the ethical qualm does bother me too. If they're so comfortable taking creative shortcuts like this, then there's no reason why they won't try to shortchange the customer even further. It is yet another ripple in the deeply cynical state of AAA development. It is why the game feels oddly non-enthused by its own existence.   Those are my biggest gripes. The game isn't deeply terrible, but I do think it's deeply compromised by Ubisoft and the AAA woes of our modern market. It makes the game feel openly hostile towards the customer - and even then, Anno 1800 is still very pretty and much richer an experience.

I've played a whole lot of Anno over the years. I have over 600 hours in 1800 on UbiConnect and over 1000 in 2070 on UbiConnect + whatever I have here in 1404/2070. I have 1404 as a standalone game and I estimate around 1000+ hours in that as well. I fully expect I'll put a whole bunch of hours into this one.     I loaded up a continuous game and set the difficulty to max out of the gate because I kind of know what to expect out of an Anno game, and this one doesn't disappoint. All the features work as expected and my usual way of doing things work. Almost 5 hours and I was near the end of tier III population.    Diagonal roads and building placement is nice. Certain production buildings now give bonuses to residences if they're placed near them unlike the older games where you always built your residences far away from any production. You can still do that of course. But getting extra happiness or gold or health or whatever from productions in a town is nice.     The graphics are great. The sound is great. This game runs incredibly well on my 3070Ti/6900HS Zephyrus G15 laptop almost maxed out (no raytracing/DLSS) on 1440p with 60+ fps. This is the first AAA full-price game I've bought in 2025.     I've seen some reviews here mention they're upset with AI use in the development of this game... and I admit, I'm a part of the crowd that loathes anything AI generated and sloppy and I would make a negative review because of that as well. Even so, this game is definitely NOT AI slop like what we've been seeing come out lately. For me, it does get a pass because I can clearly see that the devs did put a lot of human work into the game and they wanted to make a quality, classic Anno game that functions as expected. I would like to see the devs going forward though to not use AI, even if it makes the development cycle longer because I value hard work made by other people.     The Anno series has been around since the 90's... and they all have been lovely hand crafted games. They should keep that ideal and run with it - hand crafted, human made-  as going forward, AI slop will become more prevalent and more and more people will get on the train to not buy anything with AI involvement. I'll be keeping an eye on this one going forward to see if AI is used more or less over time with DLC.     I will say though as a final note, the camera zoom in and out is incredibly buggy for me.
