I spent quite a lot of time playing Anno 1800, and I started it when all the DLCs had already been released. With Anno 117, I began playing at launch, and honestly, at first it feels cool and interesting, but the longer you play, the more problems you start noticing.  First, the production chains are very simple. Compared to what the skyscraper residents demanded in the previous game, the local nobility here is absolutely unpicky in their needs.  Second, there are no upgrades for public-service buildings. You can’t visually upgrade something like the Fire Station or the well (you can only improve them through leveling and specialists). It’s disappointing, because you can end up with a rustic village well standing next to a marble temple.  Third, optimization hidden behind research. I honestly don’t understand why anyone thought it was a good idea to lock the number of warehouses - which increase the city’s overall storage capacity - behind research.  Fourth, the RANDOMNESS. It’s great when randomization is controlled, like it was with the university in 1800. You would develop your scholar settlement, research items, and then pick the item you wanted. In this game, you just start a Legendary Specialist research project and receive a completely random, often unbalanced specialist. But that’s only half the problem. In Anno 1800, you could increase the amount of scholar points to speed up getting legendary specialists. Here, it’s completely broken: you do research and its cost increases by 25%, while increasing your science output by another 25% in the late game is practically impossible. As a result, you spend more and more hours just to get a single random specialist who might end up being useless. The same issue applies to the Emperor - your reputation with him affects trade with other players, but you cannot influence that reputation positively in any way. Only random tasks from him can change it.  In the end, compared to Anno 1800, the game feels much less responsive to player control. In many places you’re simply dependent on randomness and time spent on research. There’s also not much content -hopefully the DLC will fix at least that last issue.

Performance wise, I run this around 70fps on a 5060ti(16gb) at 1440p ultrawide(3440x1440) on a mix between medium and high settings. Without DLSS. Just turn off the ray tracing (no clue what that would add to a game like this tbh). It looks good, you can zoom in a see pretty good details on building etc. I've had no other technical issues, guess I was lucky as I've seen people in forums complain about crashes and other issues.  About the AI art, I assume it's mainly the loading screens and background in some dialogs. Other than that I wouldn't guess anything else is made by AI.  So to summarise, the underlying game is good imho, hence the thumbs up. But if you care or want to take a stand against some of the drawbacks, is up to you.

Anno 117: Pax Romana is exactly what I hoped for: a deep, elegant city-builder wrapped in the best Roman atmosphere I’ve seen in the genre. If you enjoy watching a tiny frontier settlement evolve into a bustling provincial capital, this game delivers that dopamine loop perfectly.    What I loved    ✔ Beautiful, living cities – Every district feels handcrafted. Markets buzz, legions march, farms expand realistically, and the day/night cycles make your cities look stunning.  ✔ Fresh Anno formula – Managing multiple provinces introduces a smart strategic layer. You’re not just building a city, you’re managing an empire’s supply routes, diplomacy, and local politics.  ✔ Narrative choices – Being a Roman governor actually matters. The decisions you make shape your provinces, unlock bonuses, and add a role-playing element previous Anno games didn’t have.  ✔ Addictive flow – One minute you’re balancing grain production, the next you’re expanding trade, building roads, or dealing with local factions. Hours disappear instantly.    What needs improvement    ✘ Performance hiccups – Even on strong hardware, large cities can stutter, and late-game performance dips are noticeable.  ✘ Some bugs at launch – A few quest tracking issues, UI quirks, and rare crashes in co-op. Nothing game-breaking, but noticeable.  ✘ Campaign could be stronger – It’s enjoyable but sometimes feels like a long tutorial. Free play is where the game truly shines.    Verdict    If you love city-building, logistics puzzles, or historical strategy games, Anno 117 is absolutely worth it — even in its launch state. It has the classic Anno “just one more upgrade…” addiction, wrapped in a rich Roman theme that makes expanding your provinces incredibly satisfying.    It needs a couple of patches, but the foundation is excellent.  If Ubisoft polishes it, this could become one of the best Anno entries ever.

So schlecht, ich hab Anno 1800 wieder angefangen...  Werde im Laufe aber die zahlreichen Kritikpunkte aufführen...  - Das System ist für "Very High" Settings ausgelegt. Dennoch sehen einige Assets stetig unscharf aus. - Die Ladezeiten von der SSD sind ungewohnt lang. Selbst andere Titel laden schneller. - Die Ladezeiten im Spiel - keine Ahnung aber ich darf hier grundsätzlich die gesamte Ingame Gallerie 1-1,5x ansehen... - Wer sagt, dass bei Übersetzung und Dialogen keine KI verwendet worden sein soll.... es wird kontinuierliche zwischen Lateinischen und Deutschen Begriffen gewechselt, Deutsche Begriffe sind ungewöhnlich übersetzt. - Die Ingame Grafik Art ist... boar. Allein die Tatsache, dass wohl jeder Römer purpur trägt ist...  - Die römischen Adler sind in einer Art dargestellt, dass man das arme Huhn lieber grillen will... - Schiffe insbesondere in Ingame Cinematics fahren lieber durch Wellen durch, als auf Ihnen zu gleiten....  - Nach 4h habe ich den ersten Teil der Kampagne fertig... -- UI wird nach einige Zeit "unresponsive" sprich die Wege und Gebäude werden nicht mehr errichtet.. man muss dann irgendwas anders anklicke und zurück -- einige Lagerhäuser holen trotz Upgrades die Waren nicht ab -- einige Sachen sind absolut unbrauchbar im zweiten Teil. Es macht nahezu keinen Sinn Militär zu bauen.. An sich endet der erste Teil mit den Ziegeleien. Man könnte den Teil wohl auch rushen, aber je mehr Gold man sammelt, umso mehr hat man im zweiten Teil an "Puffer" ...  - vorerst letztes Update: Hab heute mal meine bessre Hälfte dran gelassen, die keinerlei Anno 1800 Erfahrung hat: -- Das Tutorial ist aus ihrer Sicht unterirdisch und eben kein Tutorial. -- Gebäude drehen muss man sich raussuchen. -- die Infos zur Produktionskette sind im Baumenü (Dauer) besser dargestellt als in den ganzen Übersichten. -- Ohne die Suche nach alten Baupatterns wäre sie wohl nicht sonderlich weit gekommen, da man extrem viele Einwohnerstufe 1-2 braucht (1-2k) um auf +-0 zu kommen.  -- Die Anschlusspunkte sind bedürftig. Köhler und Holzfäller pflanzen Bäume erst nach Forschung, was die Optimierung schwer macht...  - Nach Sichtung einiger Reports zu Bugs und bekannten Fehlern habe ich auf Anraten mal die Treiber gesamt neu installiert. Leistung ist jetzt schlechter als vorher, wieso auch immer. Hab nun nochmal die Einstellungen massiv runtergesetzt, mal sehen obs was hilft...  - Nach nun mehr weiteren 50h: Es wird nicht besser. Die Städte fühlen sich leblos und lieblos an. Die Cutscenes sind.. unterdurchschnittlich. Selbst für Erstellung des Colliseums gibts nicht spezielles.  - Die NPC Gegner sind katastrophal. Interaktion untereinander ist nicht vorhanden. Dafür wird die fehlende Klasse durch Masse kompensiert (Unmengen an Befestigungen und Schiffen, durch die man sich mühsam durchquält für... nix.)  Wer römische Siedlungspolitik liebt, der greift zu Rome:Total War. Wer Anno mag, ist getrost mit Anno 1800 gut bedient.

Summary of the review: RECOMMENDED Pros; beautiful atmospheric world, satisfying city builder, charming aesthetic and anazing historic theme (not accurate but plausible), has good mod support. Hopefully will have a long life cycle like Anno 1800 has had. Cons: UbisoftConnect, Denuvo tempering, alongside enormous Gb requirement and graphically demanding. Campaign shallow as can get, entirely skippable, writing worse than on Anno 1800's campaign, and even smaller. Nothing to the likes of Anno 1404 on that department. More as follows; I was looking forward towards this entry the whole while this game has been promoted, now that it is out, I am glad to say the wait was rewarded! Very nice atmosphere, wonderful Roman theme, which I welcome wholeheatedly. Now, the performance could use some optimization, the UbisoftConnect extra layer alongside Denuvo are frustrating, a great game held kind of hostage by some bad and shortsighted business practices, and for the Anno veterans, or city builder seasoned around here I say: skip the campaign, it adds nothing really, go straight to the sandbox, where you can explore the beautiful new world without the caveats of the already to be expected poor writing and shallow storytelling.  It does not entirely replace Anno 1800, neither would I expect that, given the two cover different ages in history. Given the game will receive more content, more updates, and it already has some nice mods, I think it is specially rewarding joining the badwagon early. Even with the cons I mentioned, it is worth it. SUGGESTION: the game could use a built-in music player, which I think was something missing in Anno 1800. Probably will not be added but well...

I was really looking forward to this game. I played all the previous Anno titles and enjoyed them all, but I was completely disappointed in this one. The only thing I have to say that is good is, I like the Roman setting.   I play a lot of city builder/settlement type games and this one comes up as the most basic. Is doesn't require any strategy or thought even.  -Citizens need trees...plop down a wood cutter for unlimited trees. -Citizens need food...plop down a fishery and a field to make porage for unlimited food. There are no animations for these buildings. The boats just sit there and the field is always full grown without anyone working it. -Citizens need a market....plop one down and forget about it, because it has no use other than 'the people need it'. Goods are not sold there and people do not go there. -Citizens need a tavern...I thought to myself: I will just build it and worry about making hops and beer for it later, but you don't need anything for the tavern. No hops, no beer...nothing. Another building like the market that doesn't do anything. -Citizens need clothes...........this is where I gave up. There is absolutely no challenge to this one and I found it incredibly boring. Also, everything is built on a grid...there is no creativity needed for building when all the buildings face the same way and all the roads are locked in a certain direction.   Maybe my expectations were too high, maybe the other games I play are just infinitely more complex than this one. I certainly wasn't expecting a game that a 5 year old could master in about an hour. If you enjoy mobile games you might like this iteration.

Tl;dr The game itself is good, but there is a serious bug and security issue with Ubisoft Connect affecting some purchases, and Ubisoft is refusing to acknowledge it or take action.  I pre-ordered Anno 117 after playing the demo. I've played a bunch of Ubisoft games through Steam, including the demo, and had them connect to my Ubisoft account without issue. I downloaded and played Anno 117 for a few hours - a few bugs here and there, but otherwise a great experience.   It was only when I attempted to make use of Ubisoft Connect's rewards system that I noticed that the Ubisoft account I was playing on wasn't mine. It wasn't an account I had used on this device, ever - and I now have full access to it. Have, as in I can still access it and make changes just by booting up Anno 117. I am never, and was never prompted for a password to access this account.  I sought help from Ubisoft Support, and during 10 whole hours of back-and-forth emailing, they repeatedly closed my support tickets and made zero effort to identify, rectify, or take any action or responsibility at all on the matter. This has been the same experience for the many other people experiencing this issue. All of these issues and more have been brought up repeatedly to Ubisoft both on their support site and Steam Discussions, but their answer remains the same - "nothing we can do, be more careful next time(?)" The only way this issue can be fixed, according to Ubisoft, is for Steam to issue a refund - which they will only do if you have less than two hours of gameplay, so you have to catch the bug EARLY.  I am leaving this review after days of attempted correspondence with Ubisoft and their utter ignorance on the matter. If you suspect you are affected by it, and have less than two hours of gameplay, I can only suggest refunding the game. Ubisoft literally cannot afford to be treating their paying customers this way and I can only express disappointment in their handling of the matter.

I am sorely disappointed.  I played Anno ever sice 1404, and I especially loved Anno 1800. I was so sure of this series by this point that I didn't even want to see many videos of it, not to spoil the pleasure for myself.    ---GOOD:  -Core GAMEPLAY: it's an Anno game, I like it: production chains and logistics, neat.  -DIAGONAL option: nice addition, even though ortogonal grids are way more efficient; still good for variety's sake  -WORKSHOP adjaciency bonuses: you can't have them all, and you get to chose if you'd rather compact them with their chains and use specialists, or get a bonus integrating them in the city. It's a bit more chaotic than the perfect blueprints of the past, but it's a choice, and it's up to you.  -MORE POP DEPENDANCY: warehouses, towers, gates, ships and troops require population to work; it never felt as tight to me, I enjoy the challenge.  -LAND COMBAT:  it could make up for the lack of diversity in the ship combat, but to be honest, I got bored way before trying anything particularly cool with it.    ---MEH  -RELIGION: cool mechanics, nice options, even though I'd like a little bit more agency to it.  -TECH TREE: nice that you can gain boosts to early game options, and it gives progression, albeit in an UNSATISFYING kind of way. It feels not well integrated with the rest of the game.    ---BAD:  - The STORY is incomplete at best, and even what we got felt uninspired and bland. I don't care if it's not historically accurate (up to a certain degree), but what we got is really boring.  - The SHIPS: I knew better that to expect Anno 1800 Military Mod level of variety and complexity, but there isn't even an Albion ship design... Moreover, arrows , scorpions and onagers are fine, but what about ramming and boarding?! After all Rome invented the corvus because they wanted boarding to be a core in their tactics. The setting allows for little already and still, we get even less. It's just ship, bigger ship, and chunky bigger ship... wow.  -The QUESTS: fetch barrels, bring stuff and destroy way bigger fleets than you can manage in the early game; all without the faintest trace of story or progression. They got from the usual mildly boring to, frankly, insultingly stupid.  -IT IS BORING: I feel no wish to do anything other than to discover what the next chain is going to provide, but there are NO EXPEDITIONS, NO MUSEUMS, NO OIL POWER PLANTS (acqueducts are kind of clunky and provide no increase in population), NO SHIP ITEMS (captains are a bit lackluster)...   Why would I even need patricians and nobles I can just unlock quinqueremes and legions with equites? I really feel no wish to play and I'm 16h in, trying desperately to enjoy it.    I'm sad because I'd like to refound the game; I know I already played it, but truthfully you don't pay 90€ to play 20h of an Anno game.. You'd expect to play hundreds of hours and it took me 12h just to realise how SHALLOW it is.    I still hope for expansions and mods to make up for this, but I'm really let down

More FPS (Forces FG) on Nvidea cards.    1. Open the Nvidea app on your PC.  2. Go to the graphics tab.  3. Add the Anno117.exe to the programs list in programsettings. (C:\Program Files (x86)\Steam\steamapps\common\Anno 117 - Pax Romana\Bin\Win64)  4. Click on Anno117.exe in programsettings  5. Turn on "Smooth Motion"    Enjoy around 20-50 more more fps.

The game can be quite fun to play. Unlike with other Anno titles,  this instalment encourages organic city building which means I can build a hodge podge of a city and not have crippling anxiety that I'm building "off-grid". However, it is very much "baby's first Anno' - it is on the same level of simplicity as Anno 2070 - with no real struggles to keep your supply lines stable. In Anno 1800, I was constantly under pressure to manage my supply networks - and if I didn't, the game went south pretty quickly.   On a sad note, there is clear use of AI to do the background artwork. It's lazy, it's *ugly*, it's cheap and it's a mark on what would otherwise be an okay game. Also, the campaign just ends abruptly; there's no resolution or payoff - it just stops. It's like somebody asked an AI to write a story about Roman times but exclude any real sense of ramifications or stakes.  The response from the developers has been along the lines of "We've poured our hearts and souls into this game..", but the vast majority of people know that isn't true. Anno 1800 was truly this team's magnum opus - in trying to come up with the next instalment, they've produced a lazy, cash grab - which is, of course, Ubisoft's *true* purpose.   I would suggest that this game is only purchased when it is on a deep discount. 5/10.

A brilliant return for the series and possibly the best Anno yet  This game is a fantastic addition to the series, and the Roman setting is perfect for this style of city builder. The level of depth is impressive, especially with the different citizen classes, their needs, resources, supply chains, and the way it all fits together without feeling overwhelming, yet it can also be incredibly detailed. You can make mistakes without being completely punished, and the overall flow of building a city on an island—or multiple cities across many islands—feels natural and rewarding. There are plenty of handy tools to help you along the way. This is probably the easiest game for new players to finally get into Anno thanks to the guided pacing of the campaign, but it still ramps up enough to give long-time fans a proper challenge.  The big new highlight is the in-depth land combat. Having armies that can grow very large if your economy supports it, with multiple unit types, proper sieges, and ground battles adds a lot. It is a great expansion to the formula and gives almost a lite Total War style of land battles combined with the usual large-scale sea combat. This is the first time in many years that land battles have been fully fleshed out in an Anno game, and it fits the era perfectly. And unlike older Anno titles that featured ground combat, it is actually good.  The campaign itself is enjoyable, with a classic Roman storyline full of intrigue, shady deals, and political betrayal. It is somewhat predictable for anyone familiar with Roman history and culture, but still fun and a good way to ease you into the game.  There are a few rough edges. The pacing is very slow at times because certain production chains and population tiers take a long time to get moving, which might test players who prefer a faster tempo, but it is incredibly satisfying when it all clicks together. It has a borderline Factorio feel once you have a lot of different resources, production chains, trade routes, and islands with specialised buildings all supporting your growing empire.  The worst part is the voice acting and scripts. It is serviceable at best and often does not match the tone of the scene or the dialogue. Some of the writing is quite cringe, but to Ubisoft’s credit they do not shy away from portraying Roman culture honestly rather than sanding it down for modern audiences or attempting to redeem their more questionable practices, such as the use of slaves.  Performance has been solid for me so far, and the game looks excellent, with lively cities across multiple islands and great atmosphere and visuals across all provinces. It looks and feels fantastic.  One thing to note: the campaign is either unfinished or has been paced very strangely. It just ends suddenly. It’s clear there is much more to the story, but it’s either not finished, bugged and not triggering, or has been removed for some reason. The campaign isn’t anything major and the free play is where it’s really at. The campaign is only really good at easing you in but I can see how this would annoy some people and it really isn't acceptable even if the campaign isn't of any interest to you.  Overall, the strengths far outweigh the flaws. The city building is deep, immersive, and satisfying, the provinces feel alive, and the mix of governance, economy, diplomacy, and warfare is incredibly engaging. This is easily one of the strongest entries the series has ever had and absolutely worth playing for any fan of Anno or detailed city builders.

Beautiful game, but it unfortunately feels too arcade-like. The setting is realistic, and the towns and provinces feel authentic, yet the way buildings instantly appear with spawned citizens breaks immersion. Nothing is truly built—the pacing is so fast that it undermines the sense of progression.  I prefer city-builders where roads are paved section by section, resources are gathered and delivered to construction sites, and workers hammer away until structures gradually take shape. That process creates a far deeper sense of involvement.  While Manor Lords may progress more slowly, it feels far more immersive. It’s a shame, because with visuals this strong and a more believable populace, this game could have been one of the greatest city-builders of all time.

Anno 117 is a great addition to the Anno series, from someone who put a few hundred hours into Anno 1800. It has made some great improvements on its predecessor. The diagonal roads really make for more natural city layouts and the addition of buffs/debuffs like extra income or fire hazard from production buildings encourage you to build certain production buildings next to your residential areas. Together these two features make your city feel way more varied, random, and natural more akin to a real life city when compared to Anno 1800. The additon of military troops is also nice. Not to mention the game has great visuals and all the buildings look really well done. I also love the aqueducts as this game's version of Anno 1800's railways - they feel really right to the setting and, again, add variance to the look of your cities. Whilst the research tree felt daunting at first, I have come to really appreciate its addition the longer I play.  Some issues I have with this game include the awkward pathing of military units and the balancing of happiness, fire safety, and health decreases from city status level; it feels quite unforgiving atm. I also don't really care for modular ships, it's just not something that interests me. It's also really weird when you unlock Albion, there's no expedition quest like in Anno 1800, you just simply unlock it. The lack of expeditions in general definitely is a negative when compared to 1800 as I think these added extra things to do that gave you nice bonuses. Island variance can also feel a bit limited but the islands in the game as of now are really well made and look great.  All in all, I think its a great game thats only going to get better with time.

Never played any of the Anno games before, and after 35 hours in I'm really enjoying 117. Lost track of time and stayed up until 4:00 a.m. trying to make my city profitable and get to the next screen. Rage quit the first campaign as Marcia because I kept going bankrupt and all my peons kept deserting my city, and then I got mauled by fires and plagues, so . . . yeah, that sucked.     After finally actually watching some YouTube guides for newbs and learning some non-intuitive things (e.g., how to get your army into your ship), my new playthru as Marcus is chugging along quite nicely. Just fended off the first Celtic attack in Albion and now I'm off to find the lost legion.    Performance-wise, 117 is playing fine for me in 4K, Ultra everything, with DLSS enabled on the highest setting, but I've also got a gaming PC with a 4090 and 24Gb of Vram, plus another 40Gb reserved in the page file. And I'm overclocked to 5.3 GHz. No stutters, no random CTDs, nothing to complain about. The game has only crashed once, when I was trying to move my stupid archers. Don't really dig the "hold Alt and drag the troops" movement control, it's super clunky, Would be much easier if we could just select the archers and right click to move them just like moving a ship.     There also appears to be a bug with troop movement -- the screen freezes frequently (i.e, more than 50% of the time) when dragging the troops in formation, can't pan out or in, and can't move anywhere on the map. Saving, quitting and reloading fixes the issue, and usually the armies work fine after the reload, so not a deal breaker. At this point I'm so used to CTDs during combat ("Thanks, Skyrim!") it doesn't even faze me.    Visually very pretty, everything looks fantastic in 4K, but if you're playing on 2K for performance reasons, guess what? It also looks spectacular then as well.     All in all, two thumbs up.

After reaching the 4th population in Rome and testing out Albion im sold on the vision.  The way buildings give you bonuses and you have to decide which ones are worth implementing into your city layout each playthrough is a great way to shake up the usual Anno formula. On normal difficulty the game isnt very hard but you make your own difficulty through setting goals anyway. So on normal difficulty you will be able to build pretty cities since you are not forced to min max the layouts.   I think I will be in the minority but I love the cross progression and unlocking legendary characters aswell as starting ressources for your future playthrougs. This gives you an incentive to go achievement hunting but every bonus through that tree is optional.   What im mixed on is the large tech tree that scales extremely hard into the late game. Later technologies just take hours in the mid game and a lot of them are basic features you had from the start in other Annos. I think the tech tree will become well integrated into the game through future dlcs. More regions means longer playthrough time and with that more time to get through all the important tech tree nodes.   Progression does not feel very linear in the mid game. As I already mentioned tech nodes in the middle of the tree take very long. In tier 3 the buildings just get way too big and you are forced to have a very suboptimal layout or you save up the concrete to upgrade all your roads. And concrete roads are way too epensive so this took me a long time. In the meantime I played through the first population of Albion which was a nice change of pace. I love the way the swamp is integrated into the game but its also very hard since on your first playthrough since you dont know how big the area needs to be while choosing an island. The game mechanics make it optional to interact between the regions so I dont get why the balancing is so out of whack with the concrete production and road upgrades which take forever. Maybe on my next playthrough it will be better.  Overall what you get here is really worth the money. Graphically I think the buildings and the nature are really well made with the performance being servicable. As I explained parts of the progression are to my liking and others arent so decide with your own taste. At this point multiplayer is unplayable everybody is getting desyncs (Me and my friend were able to play for 1 hour until the desync broke the safe file). I think its a minor issue that will probably be fixed in a few weeks.  The basis of what is here will be greatly expanded and polished with updates and DLC in the coming years and im happy to be on this ride.

First things first. The game is beautiful, fun, engaging and good all around. The scenario is *chef's kiss. The gameplay itself doesn't differ much from 1800, which is a good thing. The management part, the trading part, finding and using specialists (items), planning your cities and industry paths is as fun as always in the series, but nothing brand new tbh (besides the military). The Roman architecture is beautiful to look at, watching the animations and all the details in your growing cities in different regions is stunning.  So why the negative review?  I am pretty disappointed in the usage of AI here. Knowing the Anno series for as long as it exists, knowing the small little details everywhere is what made the Anno series always special, this is actually just sad.  Seeing the loading screen "art" and hearing the voice lines immediately felt off. Now it’s confirmed and all we got is the most common excuse of "this was a placeholder" *insert South Park we're sorry meme*  Considering the huge success they had with Anno 1800 and the cash they generated, plus them getting funded by the German government with a good amount of money, seeing them use AI on "art" for ingame notifications or loading screens and voice lines to cut costs and not hire artists is inexcusable and unacceptable, in my opinion at least.  Yes, AI is here to stay. Yes, of course, use your AI tools in development to make your life easier coding or whatever. Or find a new, creative, useful way to implement AI in your game. But when AI replaces pure creative work out of laziness and cutting costs, then gets tagged on the Steam Store with an AI warning, following a statement that AI's "final product reflects our team’s craft and creative vision", I really question the new creative vision and path the devs are going for. Btw. the warning tag came a few days before release, for transparency reasons of course.  I now paid for it and will play it, but if the devs don't fix this issue the negative review stays and I won't do what I did for Anno 1800: Be a good boy and buy every DLC and cosmetic pack for the love of the game plus buy the gold edition of the next game of the series on release day.  If the devs don't love their game enough to pay artists, why should I?

I've been playing Anno since Anno 1602, when it first released.    There was skepticism on my end when it came to the new way that buildings interact with each other, but after playing the game for well over 28 hours in the first 2 days alone, I can safely say that this is an improvement over Anno 1800 when it launched.     I am very much enjoying the endless mode and the campaign was interesting enough to introduce the new features, giving the player some choices on how to tackle situations.     While I did struggle a bit with the UI and controls at first, I saw that there's a legacy mode for the controls at least, which I instantly switched to.  I am VERY MUCH enjoying the new challenge. Apropos mod support, they added a better API so things will be less hacky, which is always a great thing.     Overall my score at launch would be 89/100.   There are some bugs where I'm wondering how they got through Q&A, but hey, they are relatively minor annoyances. I can recommend this game to fans of the series and newcomers alike.

Oh boy, this is going to hurt. :(  First, I'd like to say that I play Anno for the chill vibes. I don’t play multiplayer or the campaign, only endless mode with no AI characters besides pirates and traders. I’ve played Anno since 1503 and have over 900 hours in Anno 1800.  Pax Romana brings some new systems to the series, some good, some bad but the underlying feeling after putting in the initial hours is: “I’m bored.”  The Good:  Visual Craftsmanship Greatness Anno continues to be beautiful. The attention to detail in the maps and buildings is insane. The new day/night cycle is very atmospheric, and you’ll take amazing screenshots in the early morning as the sun rises and lights your city beautifully.  The Religion Mechanic A good addition to the series. It lets you hyper-specialize your islands to better output resources for your capital. The only downside is that if you decide to change your patron god in the capital, you can easily send your capital into a death spiral.  Albion Albion is fun. The swap mechanic is nice and adds new challenges when planning expansion. Being able to unlock the secondary citizen type after reaching the Elder tier is great, it lets you play around with different city designs on other islands.  The Bad:  Army Combat This is utterly unnecessary and unasked for. If I want to play an RTS, I’ll play an RTS, not Anno. It feels like a waste of resources that could have been used to deepen other systems. The whole experience is shallow, and even the game admits this in its tutorial pop-up describing it as a simple rock-paper-scissors system.  Tech Tree At first glance it looks amazing, but similar to army combat, it’s ultimately shallow. Some technologies are just legacy mechanics gated by research; others are simple building buffs you get with zero difficulty beyond clicking the button. It’s basically “meet a ridiculous minimal requirement and queue up a bunch of research.” Beyond the early game, I never had to strategize or prioritize.  UI A lot of redundancies and non-intuitive design choices. - Why do I have to open a tab inside the port interface just to select the Upgrade button? - Why are all trade routes listed on a single page regardless of the region I’m in? - Why does selecting a mission delivery indicator take me to a lighthouse in a different region? - Why is island renaming in the HUD instead of in the port menu like previous games? - Why do notifications for ships being attacked or destroyed arrive late?  It really feels like most UI changes were made just for the sake of change and I hate it.  Economy Integration With Housing & Late-Game Building Size Many buildings are way too big, making city expansion unnecessarily complicated. This becomes very noticeable when unlocking the Equites and Patricians. You’ll spend more tiles on the Library, Bath, Forum, Theatre, and Temple than on the Patrician residences themselves early on. The idea of moving certain production buildings into the city is nice but allowing every citizen tier to receive every production-tier buff turns your city into a cramped mess.  Interactions With Story, Missions, and Events Most choices are no-brainers: pick whichever buff sounds nice. There’s rarely any need to read or consider consequences. None of the trade characters are memorable—the Emperor is an ass, Diana won’t shut up, and Valeria is as bland as English cuisine.  Conclusion:  It’s a fun game, and I’ll keep playing it for a while, but I don’t think it surpasses Anno 1800 in the depth of its core systems. Most of the new systems are shallow and don’t require meaningful thought, planning, or effort to maintain their benefits. What kills it for me is how similar it feels to Anno 1800 like I’m playing the same game with dumbed-down systems. As I said at the start, after a couple of hours I just felt bored. The idea of taking the Anno series to the greatest empire in history should bring excitement and the feeling of a different era, not just a reskin. If you haven’t played Anno 1800, you’ll probably love it. But for my playstyle and my experience with the series, it’s a disappointment.

[h1]Best city-building and management game[/h1] Anno 117 takes everything fans love about the series and elevates it to a whole new level. The blend of stunning visuals, deep economic systems, and immersive world-building makes every hour spent in the Roman frontier feel rewarding.  [h3]Resource Management:[/h3] The game’s production chains feel both intuitive and satisfyingly complex. Balancing raw materials, workforce needs, and city expansion is addictive, and the Roman-era twist gives every resource new character.  [h3]Diplomacy:[/h3] Diplomacy finally feels alive. Neighboring factions react meaningfully to your choices—alliances, trade agreements, rivalries—they all shape the strategic landscape and give your empire personality.  [h3]Economy:[/h3] The economic system is one of the strongest in the series. The flow of goods feels dynamic, cities genuinely feel interconnected, and optimizing trade routes is both relaxing and strategic.  [h3]Island Shares Trading:[/h3] Buying shares of another player’s or AI’s island is a brilliant mechanic. Earning a portion of their island’s income based on your share percentage adds a new financial layer—and fully acquiring an island by obtaining all shares feels incredibly satisfying.  [h3]Land & Naval Battles:[/h3] Combat is streamlined yet impactful. Naval battles are gorgeous and tactical, while land battles add a completely new dimension to Anno gameplay, making military strategy more important and enjoyable than ever.  [h3]Campaign:[/h3] If you really missed the campaigns of Electronic Arts games, then you will love this game since Anno is not just city building and management game, the campaign were beautifully crafted and well written.  [h3]Cons as of November 15, 2025[/h3] There are day one bugs like the newly added diagonal roads are a little wonky sometimes, and the planning mode also glitchy and not so smooth most of the time. However, these issues are not a deal breaker and they are not really annoying for me because they can be fixed easily and I am sure this will be fixed on a later patch.  Overall, Anno 117 is a rich, beautifully crafted strategy experience that rewards both thoughtful planning and creative city design. Whether you're expanding your Roman provinces or competing economically with rivals, every moment feels engaging.  Highly recommended for city-builder lovers and strategy fans alike!  [h2]Performance[/h2] I know this is one of the most negative reviews about this game. Currently I didn't experience any performance issues playing the game in 1440p, I disabled Raytraicing and everything are on high and I still have a fix 60fps and I am good with that.

To put it short: 117 has the foundation for a fantastic Anno game and already provides many hours of entertainment as it is right now. Sadly it is plagued by a few, mostly minor problems that seem to be mostly a fruit of Ubi's well-known greed.   The Gameplay is what you expect from an Anno game. Fun, intuitive with some nice advancements from the previous games, even though no real "grand" revolutionary innovations. The end-game is a bit lacking, but the same was true for 1800. I'm sure they'll fix that up with the DLCs, though it would it have been too much to ask to add a few more innovations in the base (60€) game?  Some of the balancing feels off, I feel. For example the work force for military units is incredibly high. One of the cheapest units uses about the same workforce as the entire liberti infrastructure of a medium-sized settlement. The same can be said for ships if you add rowers to boost its speed against the wind.   The graphics are gorgeous, but rather ressource intensive - though it's not *too*bad as long as you're satisfied with not putting everything on the highest settings.  The soundtrack is good, but a bit short. It isn't quite as epic as the gorgeous 1404 soundtrack and maybe not quite as good as 1800 aswell but still good.   The campaign is rather short, but that was never the hallmark of the Anno games.   It's mostly the little things that are frustrating:   there just doesn't seem quite as much care and detail put into the game as in the prequels. One example: in 1800 every single kind of ware had a short and quick sentence (often a pun or quick joke) as a description added to it. Not so in 117  Lots of the (cosmetic) content is locked behind twitch drops or the Ubi store and that just doesn't feel fair for a full-price game.   Ubisoft Connect and Denuvo are mandatory.  One of the worst offenders: THE USE OF AI ART IN LOADING SCREENS (and not even declaring the game has AI content). Ubi, Seriously?! Is that really necessary?! The Anno games always had such gorgeous, wallpaper-worthy art in the loading screens and now you replace it with AI slop? Do you really think the few bucks you save that way are worth it?! Do you really think cheaping out on stuff like that will make the players more favourable and save your stocks that dropped by like 85% the last three years? Come on, get a grip, this is just so frustrating! This could have been a fantastic game if it weren't for you trying to penny-pinch your customers!   So in the end I'll still give it a recommendation: It's still a good Anno game that will provide countless hours of fun if you like the type of games. It's clear that the devs put a lot of heart and attention into most of the aspects and some of the flaws will certainly be ironed out by the DLC.  It's just so frustrating that they cheaped out into so many of the details whhich just wasn't necessary... So I wouldn't fault anyone who will be waiting until the game is on sale either.

When you launch Anno 1800, you're instantly thrown into the atmosphere of wonder. You get immersed into romance of exploration and scientific progress through elaborate artwork. "Pick me! Will explore distant lands!" enthusiastically speaks to us the fan-favorite Bente Jorgensen. From the get-go, the game presents itself as a handcrafted fairy tale. I don't consider 1800 a perfect game at all -  it got quite a few flaws for my taste, that said I need to give credit where it's due, it's obviously a labor of love.  Shockingly, 117 is nothing like that. You pick the rival governors in complete silence. Things like loading screens and god artworks are AI-made, because apparently instead of artists Ubisoft paid DEI consultants to add the much-needed female emperors and black centurions to the game. Actually, no, they were not needed at all, and it's cringe to see this kind of stuff this days when a certain orange guy won the election and a certain blonde has great jeans and does not feel sorry about it. Those AI images in particular are so bad even the (normally an echo chamber) subreddit is pissed about it. And look, I frequented the forums for months leading up to release, and Ubisoft knew very well about all of these things. They had more than enough time to do something about it. And yet, they chose not to.   The above is not some anti-woke crusade, but rather aimed to help set the right expectations. This is more of an Urfin Jus' last wooden soldier than it is a GOTY masterpiece. In the dev diaries, Ubi Mainz basically say it bluntly how they picked Rome because that would sell well, and not because of any intent to deliver a game that would try to stay true to the setting. Romans here are just a skin. Indeed, Ancient Greece would be a much better pick for the setting - island-based city-states, gods, naval combat , even female blacksmiths that Ubisoft wanted to include so much would raise no eyebrows. It seems to obvious in hindsight, that it's kinda shocking they didn't go for that. Then again, Ubi Mainz explain that in their dev diaries as well - Roman Empire is a better fit for the game they can milk for years with DLC, which they'll obviously do after 1800.  Alright, let's talk about the actual game, shall we? If you ignore the campaign, the visuals and the setting and focus on pure gameplay, it's surprisingly... fine. It knows very well what it wants to be, and it wants to be a city-builder. There is much more emphasis on urban planning, you make most of your profit by ensuring your buildings are in proximity of some other buildings (a-la Civilization 6), so if you're the kind of player who likes creating/theorizing perfect layouts, you're gonna feel right at home.  A bit point of marketing (whether deliberately or otherwise) was the "Total War" thing. Indeed, you can now recruit land units and go capture the villa of your AI rival. It's a simple system, nowhere near Total War, but it does the job. What surprised me is the actual villa is very durable, which is likely balanced around siege engines or late game. Another novelty is the emperor who gets kinda pissed when you attack other governors, so you can end up with a giant imperial armada taking other your island in retaliation, which is exactly how I failed my first run. Best part is I wasn't even sad about it, and there's the Hall of Fame (like Grand Gallery from 1800) that lets buy bonus goodies for future playthroughs based on the achievements you unlocked. I haven't really tested it, but my impression of the AI is it's just for show once again - on the other hand, you can now defeat it much earlier if you want to, as doesn't defend its port with OP cannons several tech tiers above what it would reasonably have access to. When you defeat the governor you can recruit him/her as a specialist to work in your city. And then, you can even invite a new governor to replace the defeated one - that way you can have all governor bonuses on the same save. You can also engage in diplomacy is them - but that system is weird, you don't really have an opinion with any rival, but rather a set of modifiers that seemingly mostly rely on your progression. Like "-100 your economy sucks".  Another feature that has become staple by now is different biomes - Albion with its marshlands is somewhat different from Latium in how it plays, although the gameplay is still pretty formulaic. For example, if in Latium you can build bakeries next to residences for +2 income -2 fire safety, you can do the same in Albion but with breweries. So while the bioms could be more distinct, I respect the system for trying to be more modular. You now have different options to satisfy the same need, even between biomes - like you can bring in wine from Latium for Aldermen in Albion. I imagine this system will help address my criticism of 1800 where each DLC massively overhauled the existing systems, instead of expanding on them.  So in the end, I'm a bit torn. I'd say you'd like 117 if you can enjoy it as "a game for mathematicians" (as one of the forums regulars aptly called it); if you don't care about the Roman setting or historical authenticity; if you don't mind buying a game that for now will be inferior to the one you likely already have in your library; if you're not a Total War fan and elaborate combat simulation is not important to you; if you don't mind paying full price for  an AI slop; if... well, this is starting to become too many "if", so... Get it on sale in a year or something?

Unfortunately, I don’t think Anno117will keep a high review score as more time passes. The first few hours and initial supply chains are fun and satisfying, but the game drops off hard past that once you realize it’s all window dressing around a bare-bones, AI-generated shell of its predecessor.  I’m a longtime Anno fan since 1404, i have a minor in Roman History (just for fun, not for marketability), and I even made my own custom Risk board game based in the Roman Empire in the year 117, coincidentally enough. So you can imagine that i was SOOO excited for Anno 117. However, it feels altogether stripped bare of the features, complexity, and secondary systems that made Anno 1800 such a masterpiece. There are no Museums or zoos for collecting rare items and beautifying your cities, no Expeditions for fun little story beats (some people didn’t care for these but I LOVED THEM and was terribly disappointed they left them out of 117), no Electricity (obviously) or comparable system that makes you rethink your layout, a severely hamstringed Specialists system, a clunky and unintuitive console-friendly UI, and an infuriating combination of DRM and launchers — though i give that last one a pass since 1800 has the same problem. In exchange for those losses you get ground military units and diagonal roads that are barely usable.  It feels to me like 117 was designed to have as pretty of a facade as possible, but relegated the core systems and endgame to the background while entirely abandoned the art and story and humanity that made 1800 so endearing and “comfy”.  For example, the AI loading screens and art. In 1800, loading screens showed gorgeous concept art of slice-of-life snaps within the world. These were a joy to see between screens because they showed the dedication and multi-discipline effort that went into making the game. In 1800 you could really feel how these drawings, the elegant UI, the solid underlying gameplay, the orchestral soundtrack, and the well-written short-story-esque Expeditions came together as a fusion of all these forms of artistry to make a product that truly felt like a piece of art in and of itself.  In 117 these drawings are replaced by AI-generated hodgepodges of vaguely Roman stuff only tangentially relevant to the gameplay and setting. There are no expeditions, and the story writing that is there in the campaign reads like it was written by an early version of ChatGPT. Importantly, the Steam page disclaimer about the existence of this AI was added only days before its release, which is seriously scummy imo.  Ultimately I’m a little bit heartbroken, as silly as that sounds. I had utmost faith after 1800 and it’s DLC in this team, even preordering the gold edition and taking off the day to play — something I NEVER do (for good reason, clearly). But after 1hr50m of game time i decided to refund Anno 117; I had been more than happy to support the team that brought me so many hours of joy in Anno 1800, but it’s clear to me now that those artists and writers have been replaced by auto-generated AI slop. And I’ve no interest in paying $60-$90 for AI.

TLDR: Game is one of the worst modern Annos, with some baffling decisions. This review used to say it's good, but the longer I play the more baffling the nonsense becomes, hence the downgrade.  This used to say: "Downvoted, because of Ubisoft." After playing more, that is still true, but the way they broke the campaign and the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ region switching nonsense being back but worse now than it has ever been, makes this a bad review on its own merits.  Also there are more and more signs this got kicked out of the door too early, I would suggest avoiding buying any 1 year dlc packs, Ubisoft won't last that long if they have to pull a "Hail Mary" like this.  The Good: - Excellent gameplay, with meaningful changes made to the formula. - Runs batter than 1800 (for me at least)  The Meh: - Due to the new diagonal building options you can make better use of your islands, everything looks way more natural and pretty, BUT it is oh so fiddly to use sometimes, resulting in sizeable plazas or road that you can't really do much with. - Trees now only grow on certain grid squares as the Romans by 117 have apparently forgotten how to use a shovel. You need to unlock this tech by going to Albion on a mystical quest to figure out how to tie a plate to a stick.  The Bad: - Ubisoft Launcher takes longer to load than the game. - ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ mobile game meta progression locking off gameplay that you paid for, but only once in your purchases lifetime. Who the hell thought this was a good idea? - The trees now being bound to a tile is probably the worst idea since the chocolate spear. Why? Because it makes it impossible to predict whether you have placed a group or tree consuming buildings correctly. This requires so much mindless micro that it actually actively puts me off the game after I played it for 14 hours. Please just get rid of this it adds nothing and is REALLY annoying. At first I thought the tech tree was a neat idea, in hindsight after playing more it just adds absolutely pointless busy work, it adds nothing to the game.  Outlook: - I hope Ubisoft Mainz survives the inevitable demise of Ubisoft. - For the love of whatever you like please get rid of the need for:     - Regions     - Trees on tiles     - Tech tree     - Meta progression They all add nothing to the game and are just wasting the players time. - Can we please have the rest of the campaign that we paid for? - People who paid the pre order and have a season pass do not get the first DLC -> time for Ubisoft to close doors, seems they are getting very desperate.

Pro: - You get the familiar Anno 1800 gameplay with a few small but nice additions. - At least you can now build at a 45-degree angle. - It's  quite pretty  Con: - The multiplayer is still broken, and a patch hasn’t even been announced. - The campaign ends rather lovelessly in a void. - The AI-generated content doesn’t really give off a AAAA feeling. - The UI/UX is… interesting, to say the least (production buildings for construction materials hidden outside the construction menu; tutorials and notifications always arriving a bit too late, etc.). - Research feels like a sad version of the Path of Exile skill tree. - Performance is mediocre at best. - Pretty much everything the AI does is just unproductive window dressing. - There are land battles again. My first one went like this: the Albion pirate completely ignored me for 6 hours, then suddenly decided to “attack” an island. The small warship I had parked in front of the harbor wiped out both of her land units without any meaningful resistance (or at least attempt to flee), completely stopping the attack. Even at sea, Anno 1800 did much better. - Considering the escalating building sizes, the islands feel rather compact.  Just stick to Anno 1800 if you're not a hardcore fan of the scenario.
Still maintaining its status as the only good Ubisoft IP left.

With a heavy heart this is a negative review at this state. Not because it's a bad game, but because it fails to deliver what 1800 has done so well, not even 1/5th of it.   Currently, the newly implemented designs around production, housing and meta resource gathering do not invoke any type of fun gameplay. What was supposed to create different types of organic growth, has forced this efficient stacking of buildings to which every game you play is played in the exact same way.   Islands, of which there is sadly only a few different variations and not nearly enough content, look the same no matter what you do, because there is no reason to make any decisions differently. Often same "layout" of an island will always have the same fertility, so it's basically just copy paste to create your resource export and that's about it.  The campaign and overall narrative is really not at any level of being complete, as it both abruptly ends in act 2 and creates no real layer of decision making or role play with the Emperor during the game. It's just a really ineffective stat that will not change your decision making at all.  Quests, that were in 1800 used to provide a whole layer of gameplay and exposure to different systems in the game, are here just 2 different types of fetch or destroy quests that you do on a cooldown to earn more money. This is a mobile game loop and really feels like a cheap solution to the income problem rather than a joyful system we get to explore in 1800.  This is not nearly as big of a debacle as Civ 7 was, and there is a clear road forward if Ubisoft decides to invest the necessary time, but in this state sadly this is just not a fun experience.

It’s deeply frustrating that so many of the Quality of Life (QoL) updates that made Anno 1800 great are completely missing here. Basic mechanics, like comprehensive trade information, should be foundational features in the base game. It feels like the improvements developed on the same engine were deliberately excluded from this new title. This practice of gradually drip-feeding core updates and necessary QoL features—seems designed solely to prolong the product's lifecycle and cash cow the player base. It’s a cynical move that treats players with selective memory, assuming we'll forget the superior state of the previous installment.  Beyond questionable business practices, the inclusion of clearly sub par AI assets is a major disappointment. While I don't object to the use of AI in general, the low quality of these specific assets is blatant and unacceptable. The fact that such obviously poor visuals made it past quality control and into a premium priced, live game is genuinely atrocious.  While the new inclusions and content look promising, the overall package reflects a sad business strategy and a significant drop in the high quality established and maintained by Anno 1800. Purely on the QoL front, aside from the brand new content, this feels like a regressive step.  Because of these concerns, I've decided to refund the game for now. I'll hang around and wait for a significant sale, hoping that necessary fixes and QoL features are patched in by then.

Requiring the Ubisoft launcher causes major issues for a lot of people. Some, including me, are entirely unable to even launch the game. If you’re a fan of Anno, you won’t be disappointed. This is essentially 1800 with fresh mechanics—some so good that you’ll wish they had been in 1800 all along. And personally, the Roman era setting is far more appealing to me.  If you’re new to the series, this is still a solid entry point—but I’d argue Anno 1800 remains the best place to start.  Performance-wise, expect some rough patches. The game can get noticeably choppy during busy moments, like when a battle happens while multiple AI/NPC messages pop up. This happens even on strong hardware and with settings like ray tracing turned off.  Visually, the world is absolutely stunning. However, there’s a strange “blurriness” at times—not a technical issue, just a general visual clutter that can make things feel less clear than in 1800. I’m still figuring out the best way to describe it.  Now, the dialogues and character animations… oof. The designs themselves are great—full of personality—but the animated versions suffer. The textures look odd, and the eyes can be downright unsettling. On top of that, the voice lines and lip sync are consistently off. Sync doesn’t need to be perfect, but this awkward delay makes it feel like a performance bug rather than a stylistic choice.  Still, with the hope that better optimization and polish are on the way, this is a strong YES from me. Anno 177 is a very good game, and I’m having a great time with it. It’s just hard not to compare it to 1800—and in that comparison, it feels noticeably less refined.

What I have seen so far, exactly the sequel anyone who played Anno would hope for!   I have >500h in Anno 1800 in Ubisoft Connect and played other Anno games in the past. This is taking all what we are familiar with and adding new interesting improvements and additions.   We can build diagonally now, and even carefully plan our whole city in pause mode. Finally omg, thank you!! Now we can actually focus and enjoy planning our mega-complex cities without being interrupted by pirates, fires, or diseases every two seconds. And no, the answer wasn’t simply to play without pirates or with low accidents. I want to play on the hardest mode most of the time, while still being able to relax and unwind when carefully planning my city. They also added research trees, religion and other interesting features now.  Performance has been flawless on my PC, the game runs very well. Graphic looks nice! If you’re on an NVIDIA card, updating your drivers might help as recent versions include Anno-related optimizations.  The blueprint mode feels smooth and it looks pretty, although I notice a small bug that does not let you upgrade your building if previously build in blueprint mode on a mountain or river slot. I had to destroy the blueprint and build it normally. Not a big deal, but hopefully this will be fixed soon.   Yeah, I agree we do not need the Ubisoft launcher, some cosmetic rewards should not be behind twitch drops or pay fees, and some of the conversations in the campaign feels a bit lame. But honestly I am looking for playing sandbox at high difficulty levels immediately after finishing the campaign and enjoy the game regardless of cosmetic features.   Not a fan of land combat units, but let’s see if I’ll be proved wrong. I’m looking forward to more complex and additional content in the future, like more veteran AIs and new regions to manage.

I wanted to be able to play a game like Caesar 3, with better graphics, and the option to play peaceful; >>PAX<< Romana.  So far I've played one custom game this way, and honestly it's great. It looks *fantastic*, and is highly customizable, so playing peaceful is an option. Compared to Caesar 3, some of the simulations are a bit simplistic, but I don't mind too much, especially since the QoL features makes it a breeze to play mostly.  I'm missing C3's trading with other NPC-owned provinces, but overall it works. It's a bit annoying that you DO have to play both provinces (celtic and roman) in the same game, if you want access to *all* goods, instead of being able to trade for the raw materials and make it. You wont be able to build just one city either. To get all the raw resources out of a province, you will have to settle several islands and set up trade routes between those, to access all the items. And you trade in absolute numbers, so no simple "sharing" of resources between islands. So the old idea of having just one massive map to build a massive sprawling city on, doesn't seem possible. You can have a fairly large city, but nothing like C3, so be prepared to manage several outposts seperate from your major city (or make several major cities). Not crazy about the forced island gameplay.  I wanted a relaxing Roman city builder that evoked the feeling of playing C3, but with gorgeous visuals and some QoL. I absolutely got that, and so far I'm really enjoying myself.   (PS: German and French audio, but no Italian? Really? Come on)
A worthy chapter in the Anno series and a solid foundation for further expansion in future DLCs. I can see myself sinking another few hundred hours into the franchise.

I really would love to recommend this game but right now i cant (this will hopefully change!). I run a i9 14900k, 64 GB RAM and i am also one of this id... people that bought a 4090. While my PC can handle the game, its absolutely absurd how much utilization this game brings to my GPU, even when i deactivated Raytracing and so on. With all settings at max, the FPS often fall below 60 FPS and WQHD resolution without upscaling.  This in general isnt an issue, but the high utilization of the GPU (especially in dialog-cutscenes) causes the GPU fans to start getting aggressive and loud which makes playing not a joy. I encountered a handful of games in recent years that achieved this behavior, but looked much much better in return.  I had to massivly lower the graphics quality and the FPS-limit to reduce the utilization. Sorry dear Devs, but the game should not need this amount of graphics power... i really hope their will be some more optimization, then i will also change my review.

I'll be honest, I wasn't planning on buying this game immediately. It was on my radar, but not a day one purchase. Then I saw the reviews. A bunch of them screaming about one thing, AI slop. Which made me buy the game immediately! I will change this review later to reflect to actual quality of the game. Right now it's a small protest against the flood of negative review that focus solely on the use of AI generated content.  When  AI tools threaten the livelihood of millions of junior developers it’s all fair game. But the moment a tool appears that might impact the specific livelihood of visual artists, it's suddenly a moral catastrophe and an existential threat to all "human art.". This selective outrage is hypocritical and I’m pretty sure many of these divas use other generative AI tools themselves. I have a serious problem with this categorical dismissal of an entire class of tools. For small developers, solo creators, and indie teams on a tiny budget, AI tools can be an absolute lifesaver. They can bridge the gap between a vision and a functional product. Should a dev be shamed out of business because they used AI to help generate textures, icons, or concept art they could never afford to commission?  To be clear, AI content isn't a magic bullet. It's best used mindfully, as part of a broader creative context, and ideally with total upfront transparency from the developer. It seems that wasn't the case here and Ubisoft isn't a small dev, which is a totally fair criticism of the developer's communication.

AI art in a game of this caliber is not acceptable. $90 preorders without a prior disclosure that the Roman Empire would be reduced to slop - after the striking visuals of the previous title - is not acceptable. And locking assets behind some weird currency linked to my Ubisoft account, are you kidding me?    Just pay some damn artists and let me play the game without having to access a shop! Nobody wants this!

Honestly Anno Pax 117 is the first game in YEARS where you can instantly tell the devs aren’t living in the technological stone age. The AI tools they used? Yeah, that’s why everything feels so smooth and actually functions instead of collapsing like half the “artisan handcrafted” disasters that barely load on day one. It’s hilarious watching people clutch their pearls about AI when this game literally shows how it SHOULD be used: let the machine do the boring repetitive junk so the humans can actually focus on making good gameplay. Wild concept, I know. The systems here react faster, the logic is cleaner, and the whole experience feels like it was made by a team that lives in 2025 instead of pretending it’s still 2004. But go ahead, keep pretending AI ruins games while I’m over here playing a city builder that runs better, updates faster, and has more going on than studios who take five years to release a glorified content patch. This is progress whether people like it or not, and Pax 117 embraces it instead of crying about it on forums. If that offends your worldview, that’s between you and whatever decade you’re stuck in. For everyone else, this game absolutely slaps BECAUSE it uses AI, not in spite of it. 10/10, future’s here, cope accordingly.

[h1] IT'S GOOD - WITH FLAWS [/h1]  Coming from hundreds of hours of Anno 1800, my top 3 games of all time, I was seriously looking forward to this Anno set in the Roman era. I'm 6 hours in and I've been enjoying it. So far, I definitely give it a thumbs up. However, there are many, many things that just haven't quite hit that Anno "itch" for me and there are numerous things the devs attempted to improve that have been lackluster.   If you've never played an Anno game - this is a production/resource management city-builder in which the main objective is building various cities on islands to fulfill your ever-growing city needs with production buildings to meet those needs. You will have to keep an eye on consumption rates and trade/transport goods with AI, traders, and/or your own islands (as they have different resource types) to meet the needs of your growing city. As your city grows, you upgrade your houses, in which higher-tier citizens will move in and require more advanced needs that will require new production - rinse and repeat.   So far, I have enjoyed the game. However, I previously mentioned it hasn't scratched that Anno "itch" for me yet. I can't yet tell if it's game-limiting or era-limiting. In Anno 1800, each time you went up a tier, you really [i] felt [/i] the progression. Since it's in the 1800's you are going from simple farmers, to simple iron/coal workers, into advanced engine/steam production, into managing electricity, oil, and fuel, and all the way up to massive skyscrapers and farms using tractors and fertilizer. From tiny wooden sailboats to iron steam ships, the progression was just perfect. I realize much of this was DLC - but still, the progression was obvious and logical. It was (and still is) awesome!   So far, this game, each new tier you increase, there's just very little feeling of progression. My farmers turn into plebs. My plebs just want better food. My plebs turn into Romans, and they just want even better food and a few extra buildings. Okay...I guess. Many production buildings are just reskinned from Anno 1800. Again, I'm not sure what else could be added here, but the progression just feels lackluster. Going from a farmer needing "X" food, to the next tier citizen needing "Y" food, to the next tier citizen needing "Z" food with a few extras, just isn't doing it for me -- at least not yet. Obviously, I'm dumbing it down here a bit, as obviously there's more needs they want - but there's just no big advancement. Just more needy citizens. I'm only on tier 3 citizen, but there's only 4 in the game right now. So I'm not expecting much more, but I could be wrong.   And the new features? The curved roads and ability to build at angles now. The devs really touted this as an awesome new Anno feature so you no longer have to build boring grid-shaped cities. I was excited about this feature, but this is all negated with the new area-of-effect production buildings that really pushes you to build...grid-shaped cities. For, you can't really have an efficient range if you're building curved and angled roads and cities - as there will be massive gaps. This entire system isn't fleshed out and I found my self rarely using it because the game really forces you to have area of effects on your residences. But when you have so many buildings to place that have area of effects and so many are differently shaped buildings, open corner spaces can't be filled, decoration is lacking, then any attempt to deviate from grid-shaped cities leaves undesirable inefficiencies in your city. I'm glad it's there, but it just seems counter-intuitive with the new area-of-effect buildings that promote production buildings in your cities (as well as others that effect residences). It's like the two systems are fighting each other. As one promotes open and sparse curved spaces and the other promotes extremely condensed square cities.   The UI, Music, and atmosphere is okay. While the UI is a bit confusing at first, you get used to it and it's not too bad to use. However, it's rather generic. There's really not a strong Roman feel to it. The music, being okay, it too doesn't really have a solid Roman sound to it. I'm nit-picking here a bit, as all of these are not bad. But I was expecting just a bit more.   Lastly, the production/consumption rates have been dumbed-down. Man, this was so disappointing. This IS A SUPPLY/DEMAND GAME! I WANT STATS! I WANT INFO! I WANT AN ANALYSIS! I was really hoping they would focus more on this part since this a supply-chain production management game. This was lacking a bit in Anno 1800 and it somehow feels even worse in this game.   Why is this one bad? For example, if you need A to produce B, but your storage gets full of B, production B will stop - since it can't store it. When you look at your production graph, it's now very deceiving. As it will appear you're making A, but there's no demand for it (because production shut down of B since your storage is full) and it will also give the appearance that your citizen demand for B is higher than your supply. This is because your citizens are still using/consuming B, but B stopped producing because the supply is full.   So to recap, B isn't producing because the storage is full, but your citizens demand it, so your graph will appear you're production is too low for demand - even though it's not, it's just producing faster than citizens are consuming it. In addition, A is still making, so the graph of that product will show you're producing it, but there's no demand for it, because B isn't making it because the supply is full. But no where in the graph does it tell you any this! It would be incredibly helpful to have all of this info on the same screen, so you can see your production is higher than your demand, but your production stopped because the supply is full. Just simply adding how much you have of the product on this page would help a lot. But either way, I'd love to see more info on the screen then the dumbed-down simply supply/demand bar of each product.   Overall, though, I do recommend it. I've only encountered 1 bug, and that was placing a blueprint for an aquaduct when I didn't have the supplies. 15-20 minutes later, I go to click build as I now have the supplies but it is now permanently stuck in a "blueprint" state and cannot be removed, deleted, or built. I can build aquaducts elsewhere on my island, but that one is now permanently broken and stuck in a blueprint state.   Overall after 6 hours so far... Not a fan of the roman setting. Thankfully, it's SUPER innacurrate. All of the Romans have a British accent, because nothing says Latin, like fish and chips. The women are oppressed?O_o  So this is Rome post inclusion of the catholic religion? Where are the churches then? "Slavery bad but ok if criminals..." nah honey, Rome wasn't a paragon of virtue. As a matter of fact, by today's standard, they'd be considered to be monsters but that's what makes them cool.(in an edgy sort of way)    This game is basically like a British woke fantasy of Rome.(in terms of setting)     Now for the game proper: It's good. Probably because they didn't care about the setting.    Things that are not good:  You can't go full native. Despite what they said, you can't go full Celtic. You will still need to use some 100% Roman buildings in Albion. Also, you get less gods, less space, less population in Albion. You can start there yes but it's like handicapping yourself as population is used for army and ships now. So you get less of those if you focus on Albion.  The UI is sluggish and buggy. Not a big deal but probably why a lot of reviews said that the game is unoptimised.  It's not that the game is slow, it's that the UI makes it feel that way.  Even if your computer doesn't get even a little warm, it'll feel delayed at times because of it.  Upgrades are locked behind research instead of population caps.  Rendering, depending on the level of zoom, can be off.(I'm looking at you clay baker...)  Where do I deactivate the invasive tutorials?  What's good:  Anno is back. Multiple saves per game. Land armies are back. The British accent makes it like "My little goofy empire", which I find fun. The ships are like F1 cars on the water but the seas are pretty large, so it balances out. Naval battles seems better balanced. Production effect on surrounding buildings matters to some extent, which "can" be enjoyable to puzzle out. Trade routes are more dynamic than before. You can fortify your city. Population management is more advanced than 1800. Production seems more balanced out, A few production chains use the same base buildings, so it's more fun to try to make them work together. Gods and timed research are a nice additions, in terms of mini-games. The day and night cycle are better handled.

Simple review here:  Loved ANNO 1800? It has gotten everything you saw in ANNO 1800; all the complexity is there and more. Trade route setups, placements are still there and they have gotten even more complex.  But here's the thing, here your style of governance actually matters.  What do I mean?  You can always max every upgrade with time. But you want to reach higher knowledge tier. You actually invest in school systems. You make more of them. Your low-level citizens don't demand that you build an school for them. You can do it nevertheless! And it pays off! You get to the upgrades faster.    The campaign is much more engaging. Even though it is linear, the story is much nicer than 1800, and you enjoy the story. Especially, because you now have this realistic military side that uses population and workforce instead of influence. So it is actually much harder to spam a zillion ships!    I am still discovering and it is absolutely amazing. Can't wait to spend thousands of hours into this!    On the graphics side:  I have RTX 4070, and am playing on 1920x1080 180Hz. Enabled anti-aliasing and it feels good. I am playing on medium quality right now, and game is really smooth; no lags.

Preliminary review: So far so good. I would say this is on par with Anno 1800 upon its release. It has potential to be as fully fleshed out as 1800 plus DLCs, or even a bit better. The major mechanic changes seem to be the addition of land military, a research tree, and religion. Campaign mode is a good way to get introduced to the game world and characters, I recommend it even for Anno vets.   As with all Anno, the game boils down to a goods production and building placement puzzle all about balancing economic growth and pop needs to increase income. Map enemies, intra-island transportation, and island culture add good complexity.   My main critique would be the research tree. It's massive and each research item is relatively minor and incremental, I think it might distract from the fun of the game. Jury is still out on that. On the brightside, research helps you focus and choose which playstyle to embrace. One issue with Anno 1800 after all the DLC is that the game became so massive with so many plates in the air that I rarely continued into late game.   My second critique is that this is just an evolution of Anno 1800, so don't expect anything revolutionary. If you liked Anno 1800 and you dig the classical period theme, you'll absolutely love this game.   Performance Notes: I'm running the game through GeForce Now 4k on Mac and it's mostly smooth sailing. Visually stunning (maybe 20% better than 1800). There are some issues with brightness (had to adjust GFN settings to compensate for dim screen). There are some small buggy issues like UI pop ups overlapping and dolphins flying through ships, but otherwise everything works as it should.
