great game.  diagonal streets and the new areas of effect truly elevate it into a true city builder. i always had a bit of an issue calling anno 1800/previous iterations actual city builders because… you just put down stuff on a grid to fulfill needs. but 1) having diagonal building, enabling much more organic city growth and more importantly, the areas of effect, especially on public buildings is great. sure, the bath house is only really needed by the 3rd tier, BUT it gives such massively powerful bonuses that u want to ensure it covers as many residents as possible to get the bonuses. having settlements of only liberti still kinda want a grammaticus because it gives extra research speed, eventho they dont actually need it - which feels much more realistic and organic than “school needed for tiers 2-3 then remove”.  the atmosphere is great, the music is fantastic, the graphics are gorgeous, it runs as well as it expect it to be (obviously its very demanding - it looks fantastic, but it doesnt per se run bad) the never ending loop of expanding is fun and engaging. the military system is pretty great. it has variety, options, offers different strategies and some depth but knows to not be completely overcomplicated and overbearing as it is just a  “side” mechanic. also the customizable ships are very cool. albion is great, the swamp mechanic is interesting the different paths for the population are distinctly fun and unique. the religion & tech tree mechanics are great, i also love the 8 attributes. (eventho i personally recommend setting the hazard protection thingy to above 0, its quite rewarding to not get incidents when you manage properly and 1000 is too high).  for weaknesses: the campaign/story stuff isnt that great, the side quests are as bad as they always have been in anno. i feel like there isn’t that much island variety and the distribution of fertilites could use some rebalancing. its certainly not game breaking and totally playable, but i do notice some resources being very under-, others overrepresented. no major bugs/issues for me, a weird pop up or translation error here and there but thats totally fine. i’m a bit bummed out by the fact that there is no 5th population tier im latium. overall there is still more content than base game anno 1800, considering albion basically has 5 tiers now and that, with the new optional demands system there is more to do if you want to fully support every population tier. still tho i kinda expected 5 tiers in latium and there is no one truly overly difficult production chain at the end. [eventho i expect more tiers will come with DLC in the future].  incredible game, absolutely recommended. excited for egypt!

Since most of the desyncs in multiplayer have been fixed, I'm happy to recommend this game. There are still occasional desyncs, but they can usually be worked around or reloaded now, and they are much less of an issue than they were at launch.  Anno has been a co-op staple for me for a long time, and this one feels like a big step forward from Anno 1800 in a lot of ways. There is already a ton of content and replayability, and I'm excited to see the DLCs roll out and watch the game grow into an all time great.  It is an amazing game to chill with the boys (or solo) and build big cities. You can even go into first person with CTRL+SHIFT+R (press Escape to leave first person) and walk around what everyone has built. You can also co-op as the same faction, which me and my friends love, because it lets us settle and build our own islands while still having shared islands that feel like group projects.  Building cities feels more complicated than in 1800, but in a good way. Having to work production buildings into your housing blocks pushes you toward more creative layouts, even if things still end up mostly rectangular.  This is the first Anno title with diagonal roads, diagonal buildings, and diagonal decor, which makes city building way more fun than before. It keeps naval combat from Anno 1800 and adds land combat on top of it. The land combat feels a little rough around the edges and I'm still learning it, but having both land and sea combat in an Anno game is something I have always wanted. Honestly, I'm just thankful the devs managed to pull it off.

Pax Romana, are you kidding me?  I just wasted 10 hours of my life on this game. Ten. Hours. The game kept telling me it “auto-saved recently”, so I thought everything was fine.  Then, when I finally tried to manually save, the game didn’t even open the save menu – it just kept throwing me into the load screen. Over and over again. No warning, no error, nothing.  I quit the game thinking at least the auto-save did its job. But guess what?  There was no save file. Not a single one. Everything wiped. I have to start all over again because the game can’t handle the most basic feature every title should have by default: a working save system.  And the best part? I paid almost 90 euros for this. Ninety. Euros. For a game that can’t even guarantee your progress stays saved.  How is this acceptable in 2025? This completely killed any motivation to continue. Honestly, fix your game.

The game is good and I am really enjoying it. This is my first Anno game and it's been great.  I just finished Act II, so I am back in Latium, though of course still have access to Albion. Here are the points I think are worth mentioning from my personal experience: - The gameplay itself is fun and I really enjoy the detail you can go into when organising and decorating the city. - It's beautiful, I can't stop taking screenshots (press F1). - It has first person which is cool (CTRL SHIFT R). - The story is pretty interesting and I'm keen to see where it goes. I like that it's there but more in the background so I can focus on my cities. Can't wait for Aegyptus! - The island setup is cool and I like that you rely on ships a lot. A single ship is very important for trade/combat. - I do hope future provinces will be more land based compared to the now island setup. - I saw people complaining about the loading screens? I think they're appealing, AI or not. Why would you thumbs down a game just because you don't like the loading screen art? - Seems the multiplayer is currently broken (I don't know, I don't have friends). - Having so many cities that run at the same time that you have to manage can be a bit overwhelming, but the point is to get them self sufficient so that you can focus on new cities or upgrading/decorating a city. - I have not had any performance issues. - I have not had any issues with regards to Ubisoft Connect. I was already signed in when I first launched the game and it has been seamless so far. - Soundtrack is amazing. - The final battle in Albion in Act II was a little underwhelming and felt easy. Though I might have overcompensated with troops. EZPZ. - Speaking of troops it feels like a simplified version of Total War (like Rome 2), which I enjoy. - It's first and foremost a city builder with all the other elements attached. Okay i think that's enough. In summary I have not had much (if any) negative experiences and I highly recommend the game. Ubisoft fix your multiplayer! FYI: 1080ti | Ryzen 7 5700X | 32GB RAM | SSD

[strike] Disclaimer, I am still relatively early in this title, and am a fan of the series. My expirience is exclusively in the Campaign mode. [/strike] I'm updating this now that I'm through the campaign.  [h1]Overall[/h1]  This game has been a delight thus far. Gorgeous graphics, well written and interesting plot, a few nice UI improvements over previous titles, solid gameplay - all marred by a few mildly annoying bugs and UI issues.  I am having an absolute blast.  [h1]Gameplay & UI[/h1]  Overall it plays very similar to previous titles, but with a few nice touches:  - The ability to build in diagonals, allowing buildings to have eight possible orientations instead of four is a nice touch and can help to make more aesthetic and natural looking towns, although residential grids are still king. One drawback is that building on the diagonal is less space-efficient, but it is still nice to have the option. Also, allowing diagonals can be toggled to bring back the classic 4-direction expiriance. - Each need category can be fulfilled by two items, and there is always a local choice for fulfilling a need, making expansion strategies much more flexible. Or you can fulfill both and stack the bonuses (which is almost allways worth doing). - Major QOL improvement - you can now set stock and trade thresholds for all goods at in island at once, saving so much clicking and copy-pasting over previous titles. I've been making this suggestion since Anno 2070 and am delighted to see it finally here. - Some UI elements are not obvious - in previous Anno games, you could see construction good amounts in a display at the top. Now that display is at the bottom of the screen, and rather than just displaying amounts, those are now the buttons to push to build those materials supply chains. It took me a while to figure that out. - Chapters 1 and 2 of the story are great, however you can tell there was supposed to be a chapter three that just kind of gets missed. Solid start, feels unfinished.   [h1]Combat & Ships[/h1]  The ability to customize ships is cool, but the UI for doing so and the UI for choosing what ship to build could use a little more clarification.  Ship combat is same as it always has been in Anno games, decent but nothing special. The land combat is an interesting twist, although the AI needs some improvement. Troops generally mob up and charge at each other, and aren't very smart about their routes.  Where these two things meet can get awkward, the number of times I have accidentally thrown a legion overboard instead of disembarking them on an island... easily fixed by sailing back to pick up the floating men, but still. Also, you can often cheese land armies by parking ships near the shore and disembarking then reembarking the same legion to bait enemies into hails of ship fire.  Static defenses can be quite powerful, and balance there needs a bit of tuning.   [h1]Graphics and Sound[/h1]  A notable improvement over the previously solid Anno 1800 in the graphics department, waves, islands, ships, and buildings all look amazing. Additionally, the game adds weather effects and lots of little details that just help.  Voice acting is solid, although the accent choices are in some cases... rather odd. Why does this Roman guy have a cockney accent? IDK, it still somehow works, but is just a bit odd. Music is beautiful, if rather somber and slower paced than previous titles in the series. Possibly a little too restful.  I know several people are saying they are expiriencing slowdowns and graphics stuttering related to the mpaa setting, but I personally have not observed this. No performance issues here yet either, although that may change as my trade fleet expands.  [h1]Ratings[/h1]  Overall: 7/10 - Still solid, but some bugs, UI, and AI issues and an unfinished campaign storyline are holding it back from 10/10.  Gameplay (island and construction): 10/10 - no notes. Gameplay (combat and trade): 8/10 - solid, AI needs some improvement yet, especially on land. Story & Campaign: 8/10 - Keeping this spoiler free, but I really enjoyed what of the story was there.... until it abruptly ended. It just feels unfinished when you get to that point. UI: 7/10 - Notable improvements over previous titles, but some new issues as well. Still overall better than it was, with plenty of room to grow. Sound: 9/10 - Enjoyable and atmospheric. Bugs: 5/10 - There have been a few - a few optional random quests that are unbeatable because a required resource isn't mapped correctly, had one quest to destroy a ship in Latium and bring back the cargo spawn the cargo in Albion, and as I only had one ship at the time, I failed the quest because the transit times are so long. Some UI elements deform as numbers change. Nothing major, but a lot of little things really drag this number down.  (My original rating was 9/10, but that was before the issues in the endgame started to pop up)

Its exactly what I wanted out of the next Anno game. Here's the things I love so far.  -They made some great choices at making your city look aesthetically beautiful much easier like providing floor tiles that can fill in gaps of any size and walls that can give a clear deliniation of where a city starts and ends.   -The new needs system is so much better, you now have multiple choices to get to a next tier rather than not having access to a single resource blocking your progression completely. So for example your base level peasants need access to either a market or a tavern to fill that need they don't have to have both, but getting both is still valuable since filling that need provides more income and happiness.   - Population upgrades aren't strictly linear either, you can take the celtic population down their own pop tier path or down a unique celtic-roman one, or do a mix of both.   - The research tree is pretty massive and provides a ton of unique affects and buildings leading to a pretty length progression system in each game.   As far as my complaints, it's really more so just that this game just came out and I wish there was even more. I did enjoy the scenarios of Anno 1800 and those aren't present here, Also obviously I'd love for more regions but again the game did just come out.

After 25 hours in Anno 117, I’m completely hooked and more excited than ever to keep going. The game feels incredibly polished, the atmosphere is rich and immersive, and that classic Anno vibe is absolutely alive. Watching settlements grow, fine-tuning production chains, and exploring the Roman world has been consistently satisfying.  Performance continues to impress. I’m running the game on a Samsung Odyssey (32:9 ultrawide) with a GeForce 3070, and it runs smoothly on high settings without any noticeable drops. Playing Anno across such a wide field of view feels amazing and really brings the world to life.  The only feature I’m still missing is the ability to save building layouts, like in Anno 1800. I'm recommending it based on the single player experience.      The multiplayer still needs some TLC though.  They tout that it's ready for 16 player multiplayer, but the game experience is not really built around to have active gameplay for all four players under a single governor at the start of the game.  When the core loop is city building, it's hard to have enough resources or gameplay for all four players kicking things off.  It usually leaves people out of really playing and experiencing everything the game has to offer.    The UX for getting people in and out of the game leaves a lot to be desired as well.  It SHOULD be drop in/drop out to provide maximum flexibility... but when people hot join, everyone gets kicked back to a load screen as that player joins in.  And if you disconnect/desync, the quickest way to get everyone back in is to reload the save at the player management menu, and single invite each person-> wait for them to load in->invite the next one, etc.  It's very janky.    There's also numerous desync and disconnection bugs that plague longer play sessions, which makes re-inviting people extra painful that it has to happen often.

Ignore the handful of initial complainers. Let the SteamDB stats speak for themselves. The vast majority of players right now are too engrossed with the game to take time to review.  Optimization is fine. Story in the campaign is not exactly Shakespeare but no one comes to Anno for the storytelling. Really it serves more as an extended tutorial than anything. The heart of the game is free play mode and logistics.   The UI may be a bit different for existing players but after time with the demo and now the full release I can say I do actually like it.  Visuals are gorgeous and very well inspired, though readability could be ever so slightly tweaked. Tough to do while still staying true to the standardized Roman architectural style, though, so understandable.   The new additions of diagonal building, production buildings influencing houses in their area, and even the new military system and defensive structures all make city building feel more organic and less chunky.   While there are only 4 tiers of Roman citizens(with 3 tiers each of Celtic and Romano-Celtic citizens) one would think there’s less difference but I would argue it trades a small amount of height for a far larger amount of width, in terms of how your cities grow. Most players form previous titles would drop off before hitting the most advanced tiers anyway so honestly I think it’s for the best. More difference is appreciated. They’ve already promised Egyptian populations to come late next year so we are on track to have the most diverse set of populations to care for yet! May even see higher tiers added later as DLC for those who want those even higher levels of complexity in production chains.   Military is fine. Naval combat adding mod-able ships adds depth and trade-offs for different modifications. The reintroduction of ground combat adds more strategic depth and as in other ANNO games, war is less of an end goal as it is a means of achieving economic supremacy, so I’m not expecting it to be like more dedicated RTS titles.   Overall I think it is safe to call this Anno’s finest offering yet and promises to become my new addiction.

As an Anno fan, playing since I was a child with 1602, I think Anno 117 is a jumbled mix of good and bad. I am going to put a negative review even though I do like things about it because I think as a first time player I would have a horrible experience compared to buying Anno 1800 which is the new benchmark since 1404.  The GOOD: - The new diagonal system for roads makes the towns feel natural even if the roads cause some frustration now because of missing triangle buildings or decorations causing voids. I think this can be mitigated in time with learning the new system. - The same old mechanics are revamped and while not as complex as Anno 1800, they are still satisfying. - The new mixed goods to level up/perfect a citizen tier is a step in the right direction. - The graphics as always are phenomenal - Land combat is a big plus, I really liked this feature and it was missed in Anno 1800  The BAD: - The new diagonal road system combined with the fact that now you feel pressed to place production buildings in cities makes the entire thing fall apart. By tier 2 you have Governers Palace (equivalent of the town hall in Anno 1800), bakery, tunic producer, hat producer, market place, tavern, school/university/library, temple. That is a lot of buildings needed in the city if you want to maximize efficiency. If the goal was to make us choose then the method implemented is not at all good. It feels like optimization is not key anymore and instead the game fights your optimization. The new diagonal roads makes it seem even more impossible as the amount of bonus/buff buildings force you to a grid for efficiency. - There are still bugs that are as stupid as building a blueprint on a river slot when you don't have the resources and then getting the resources for it makes it so you need to delete the blueprint and replace the building. Graphical glitches when deleting blueprints. Weird decision that when you change options in the graphics menu, it auto applies and makes you wait as it applies but maybe you wanted to change 2-3 options well wait for each one to auto apply the moment you choose a different option. Response banners don't always pop up so you have no idea what the respective person says on some actions you take, just the portrait and X button appear. - The UI was changed since the beta, but it's not that much better. Enter campaign, first mission on the island is to setup the lumber production. Good luck, it doesn't tell you how and the UI is not evident as the production chain for selecting the buildings is hidden behind pressing on the lumber resource icon (not evident since the previous Annos didn't have it like this). The duplicated tools for destroy, move, copy is still present. Once on the hotbar on the right and second in the construction building menu. The UI still doesn't allow for quick look at the fertilities on an island, you need to hover over the top of the map and then have it have a popup off to the right. The icons are too similar especially for different fish type which look too close. Just overall the UI is a step back and fights you, Anno 1800 may not have had the best UI but it didn't feel like it fought you when you wanted to quick look at some info. - The performance is subpar to bad, it's playable but it definitely needs optimization passes. On a 7800X3D with a RTX 4090 and 32 GB of RAM, installed on a Samsung Pro NVMe SSD (not sure which model exactly) on ultra (recommended by the game) at 1440p I get the amazing FPS of 35 with a starter town. Not much going on. With DLSS on I do get more FPS, I capped mine at 60 but it's constant. However DLSS is a crutch and should not be the norm. I remember reading about Anno 1800 trying to get it to run on Intel Integrated Graphics. What happened to those guys? Did you fire them for profits? - Lumberjack huts don't replant trees? Coal burners don't replant tree? If I am not mistaken these are research options but they feel like a dumb decision to cut out basic functionality to expand the research tree. And they are locked on the Albion path. So you're telling me Romans didn't replant trees or at least have it happen naturally???? - Mixing radius buffs with road distance for certain buildings is horrible. Circles just in general are a pet peave of mine, I loved Anno 1800 grid based area for lumberjack huts and other buildings. Why perfect circles if the game is still on a grid? Also the radius being a circle on buff/bonus buildings is a step back, Anno 1800 got rid of "influence" radius for buildings and it is one of the best decisions and now they have gone back. - Popups? It seems someone from a previous mobile game came in and made the decision to have popups for unlocking things. It's fake accomplishment dopamine. Luckily you can select to never show this again.  I'm sure there are more problems I can't remember right now. This is why I am leaving a negative review. I think it can improve, and I hope it does. I'm invested in this franchise's success and I will be keeping the game. However I think if you had to choose between this and Anno 1800, go get 1800 on sale with the DLCs. It's more refined, it's performant, it doesn't have a mess of a UI. Hope it improves and I don't regret keeping this game in the future.

If you’re a fan of city-builders, strategy, or just great PC gaming, I strongly recommend Anno 117: Pax Romana. I’ve been playing the Anno series since Anno 1602 launched back in 1998, and this entry absolutely stands tall with the best of them. The blend of Roman-era aesthetics, deep economic simulation, satisfying expansion mechanics, and that classic Anno pacing makes it a phenomenal addition to the franchise. As someone who put 200+ hours into Anno 1800 and bought all the DLC, I had high expectations for Anno 117 — and I’m happy to say it delivers in all the right ways.  The setting of the Roman Empire is perfect for the Anno gameplay loop. Every city feels alive, every trade route feels meaningful, and every decision has weight. Instead of just spamming production buildings, you actually think about supply chains, logistics, terrain, and long-term planning. It scratches that deep strategy itch while still giving you that relaxing Anno charm.  Pros:  Gorgeous visuals and amazing atmosphere  Deep, satisfying logistics and city-building  The Roman theme fits Anno far better than I expected  Great early gameplay flow and satisfying progression  Large, detailed maps that reward planning  Strong sense of growth as your settlements evolve into bustling provinces  Runs well after adjusting a few graphics settings  If you loved Anno 1800, this is absolutely worth picking up. It has that same “just one more building” feeling that keeps you up way too late, combined with a fresh new world and mechanics that feel both familiar and exciting.  It’s clear the foundation is strong, and with future updates and DLC, this is shaping up to be another long-lasting, endlessly replayable Anno entry.  If you’re a city-builder fan or an Anno veteran, I definitely recommend it. Looking forward to sinking hundreds of hours into this one.

Has your brain turned into a nest of maggots ? go to the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ hell UB dog , maybe youre not dog , youre just paramecium !  ===  First, let me point out two flaws I’ve noticed:  The first flaw: Road design issues. Although roads in Anno 117 can now curve more smoothly and have sloped sections, this feature should have been introduced in Anno 2205 or Anno 1800. Instead, it’s only available in 117. Furthermore, it doesn’t allow you to generate an entire smooth curved road at once; you still need to adjust and build it in segments, which becomes especially noticeable when constructing roads around buildings. Lastly, the addition of curved roads leads to awkward interaction between building rotations, snapping, and button layouts. The controls feel clunkier and less comfortable, making it less convenient than before. If you're unsure about placement, it’s safer to just stick with straight roads.  The second flaw: Texture and refresh issues. This old problem started in 2205, carried over into 1800, and persists in 117. Even when the game is installed on an SSD, the lag between camera movements and the slow texture and material refresh is still present. I suspect Ubisoft implemented some kind of LOD caching system, but this issue worsens as the number of objects increases. There are times when an entire building’s textures and materials don’t refresh at all and remain stuck in a blurry, low-LOD state.  The third issue: This is neither a flaw nor an advantage: performance. A quick note: if you want to experience 2K high or ultra settings smoothly, you'll need at least a 4070 or higher. To run 4K with max settings and DLSS at 40-60 fps or above, you’ll need a 4090 or 5090 GPU. The overall graphics are great, but compared to Anno 1800, the building materials and details haven’t changed much. If performance is still a concern, you’ll likely need to disable ray tracing. Honestly, I think 117 isn’t particularly well-optimized because you still rely heavily on DLSS and powerful hardware to boost performance. So, take caution and carefully consider whether you should buy it at launch.  ===  Now, let’s discuss the game’s features and strengths:  Anno 117, set in ancient Rome, continues the series' tradition of rich humanistic atmosphere and detailed social dynamics. The city-building aspect, with diverse and meticulously designed buildings, effectively recreates the vibe of a Roman city. The game’s lighting, terrain, and natural environment further emphasize the beautiful Mediterranean flair, adding to the immersion. As a sequel, 117 is a bit more complicated and intricate than 1800. In 1800, you only needed to meet population thresholds to unlock new buildings, and production bonuses were handled by guilds. In 117, however, you need to build academies to acquire knowledge. Only with knowledge can you research different technologies to unlock more buildings. By constructing temples to worship different gods, you gain faith to boost production output for specific buildings. These two elements—integrating Roman-era social beliefs and the flow of knowledge—add depth to the game’s strategy layer.  Players can also build governor’s offices and administrative buildings to assign experts who unlock special buildings and technological improvements. These experts can also affect production buildings and residential areas, providing a variety of positive buffs. However, these actions will make the game take longer, as the game becomes more complex than 1800. The layout design, based on the influence range of different buildings, also adds to the difficulty of planning your city. It’s likely that new players won’t find it as intuitive, and you’ll often find yourself thinking about what to do next or waiting for research to finish. After all, players can’t multitask like AI. Early on, 117 requires more simultaneous planning compared to 1800, and to speed up faith and research, you need to increase your population, which in turn requires more resources. This cycle continues, creating a more complex and time-consuming loop.  The game also brings back army unit training, and naval ships have received more detailed attention. Barracks and shipyards require part of your population and resources to maintain troops and fleets, adding to the operational complexity. Your society progresses gradually—you can’t rush through it. In my view, Anno 117 continues to expand and add complexity to the supply chain system, rather than simplifying things. This increase in complexity enhances gameplay depth and immersion. However, for players who want to quickly enjoy the game’s flow, atmosphere, and city-building aspects, it may be an obstacle. Compared to 1800, 117 offers fewer resources and supply chains because of its different historical setting, but that doesn’t make it simpler.  After playing the game, I think new players need to consider not only system requirements but also whether the game’s mechanics suit their playstyle. Depending on your available free time, you should weigh whether it’s worth purchasing. Personally, I don’t think it’s too expensive. If you just want to enjoy city-building and don’t want to worry about all the complex systems, you might want to wait a bit until mods come out. If your priority isn’t city-building but rather experiencing the economic simulation and management, and you’re not interested in the campaign, you can start with sandbox mode. I also recommend new players disable AI opponents and pirates, and just take things slow at first. Once you’re comfortable, you can turn everything back on.

If you are coming from Anno 1800, this game will feel different. Yes, it has Anno vibes, and encaptures the spirit that is Anno well enough, but its different.  First of all, an aspect that feels different to previous installations of Anno is the importance of building co-dependence and influence areas. Using them wisely will lead to a prosperous city, not using them at all will still work but make your life more difficult. Secondly, coming from Anno 1503 being my first ever Anno, the tech tree is a welcome addition making its comeback, but again - its something different compared to the most well-received Anno 1800. The integration of health, safety, happiness and other values being tied to influenced buildings makes the game difficult to balance at first, but once you got the drill, you get into some kind of flow building your organic cities - and that they are, because how well they designed this system certainly makes you appreciate how cities organically grow. No industry section in the corner of your island, but rather buildings neatly tied into your city borders.  Technically speaking the game runs smoothly, no encounters of crash within the short time of playing at the point of this review, no random FPS drops, stable performance, comparable to Anno 1800, but take it with a grain of salt given the specs of my PC.  Definitely recommend this game, gives you a fresh perspective of what Anno can be while still adhering to the spirit that has always been Anno.  Edit: And honestly, negative reviews about Ubisoft Connect are pretty much worthless. This necessity could've not only been foreseen as every steam-release of Ubisoft required Ubisoft Connect, but also with just a single thought about crossplay you could've known that this was coming. After all Steam has not yet conquered the console market.

[h1]The Anno Everyone Imagined[/h1] [hr][/hr] [b]Anno 117[/b] is exactly the kind of game many fans have wanted for years: an Anno set in the ancient world, starting with small settlements and growing into an empire. It feels like a natural evolution of the series, and for me already one of the most exciting Anno games in a long time. [hr][/hr] [b]🏛️ The Setting Feels Like a Perfect Fit for Anno[/b] Building up from simple beginnings to massive Roman cities feels incredibly satisfying and gives the whole game a strong identity.  [b]🎨 Visuals and Atmosphere are Fantastic[/b] Beautiful, detailed, and full of atmosphere in a way that really sells the Roman Empire setting.  [b]⚙️ It Builds Well on Anno 1800[/b] A lot of the quality-of-life features and improvements carry over, so it feels modern and comfortable right away.  [b]📖 The Campaign is Too Short[/b] Some parts are very well staged, but it ends too early and leaves the late game without much structure.  [b]🏗️ The Late Game Needs a Stronger Goal[/b] Something like building the Colosseum or competing for the emperor's favor would have added much more weight.  [b]🧩 It Could Use More Long-Term Depth[/b] Especially in the endgame, where something like Anno 1800's museum, zoo, or item systems is missing.  [b]🌍 The Potential is Enormous[/b] More provinces, more monuments, more depth, and a lot of room for future content and mods. [hr][/hr] [b]A fantastic foundation with room to grow. One of the most exciting Anno games in years. 👍[/b]

As you can see from some reviews some people will just cry that a game is a copy of a game that came before it. Yes Anno is still Anno...obviously. Somehow this surprises people. It's also about a 20 second wait for the Ubi launcher the first time you launch the game so these complaints not recommending the game are a bit nonsensical to me. Heres a list of good changes imo. There's a surprising amount  -This is a step up graphically. Anno 1800 looked great, this just adds a bit more weather and atmosphere. The building models and maps are more detailed but things like character models are a significant upgrade.   -Performance has mostly been a locked 60 with high/very high, raytraced shadows, and DLSS on balanced/quality. I run a 4080 with a R7 9700x and 32gb DDR5 @ stock speeds. I have a 35 inch 3440x1440p monitor so I was very happy with this. When I turned raytracing off and unlocked the frame rate I was getting 100fps in my small town. There was way too much screen tearing without it locked though so I went back.   -They introduce the game mechanics quite a bit better this time around. New players will have an easier time with this. Small things like game tips popping up in a silent visible notification on the top left of the screen instead of most games where tutorial tips are literally forced on you and often pause the game. This allows some learning at your own pace if you're new.  -This has a much more fleshed out combat and military system which was needed. Actual ground combat was a welcome surprise. I'm excited to be able to play a military RTS, city builder, and logistics simulator all in one game. There's actual varied mechanics to the individual ships and units which is cool to see.   - You can now build more unique cities. The ability to build curved roads and rotate buildings at a 45 degree angle adds quite a bit to the variety surprisingly.   - I didn't think I would care for yet another historical  setting but thay manage to make their take on the roman era interesting. I do hope the next title is something completely different though. I'm salavating at the idea of a 1950's Soviet/US era or something that's a fresh take for the series.   -This launched with more standalone items compared to 1800 if I'm remembering correctly. Decently cool statues and ornaments.   -This developer is great with UI and this game improved on the already good UI in the previous game  -Added a religion mechanic with varied interesting options  -The story is halfway intriguing so far, which was not expected. I saw some negative reviews that called it "woke" which is just hysterical/sad to me.   -They've kept some quality of life stuff introduced as dlc in the previous game.    -Added a research progression tree with three main varied subcategories that will allow rushing certain builds/playstyles which gives you a much more varied playthrough if you want to start fresh. This tree impressed me because it determines pretty impactful changes that can alter a playthrough.  -They added a brush tool that allows you to brush over buildings to change the look of them which adds to the city building element even more.   Overall, I think this is in an excellent spot. It adds meaningful and welcome changes to the formula without removing core elements that make Anno the series it is. They're doing a lot with this game and I'm looking forward to seeing what they can do with it in the coming years.

Anno 117 differs significantly from previous titles and in a good way.  Anno games can be classified as city builders with management core and some mild strategy aspect. The core of the game is about producing different resources for a given population tier, then once all goods are supplied upgrading said population buildings and producing more complex goods, which require manpower from previous tier forcing you to expand the city from the lowest tier every now and then. As you progress furhter, the number of steps to be repeated grows. On top of that, you face several competitiors who also develop their islands and depending on your playstyle will trade or fight with you. But all in all it is still mainly a citybuilder and managment game.   Anno 117 differs from previous titles in the sense that the game puts emphsis on building production buildings in specific places. Some provide more population or greate tax, but they may also cause fire hazard or make people unhappy. The effects stack making it highly preferable to spred production buildingd across town and to clump the more harmful ones. Thus creating more realistic cities with less effort.  It is also worth noting that the game went back to its roots and brought back land combat. Protecting your cities no longer means having a strong fleet and costal fortifications, but also being able to defend against raids from any side. This both creates complexity, and allows you t build armies, walls, forts and more, even if only for the sake of beautybuilding.  Another interesting change is that now a player can start in one of two regions as opposed to being locked to the same start. Furthemrore in Albion a player can choose from different population tiers, each offerings different path and theme. These systems are simple, but create unique experiences and will for sure be used in the future more.  As a Rome themed game, it seems to be doing a fine job providing a theme that was very common two decades ago. There are certain hiccups like the not so Italian accent for the cast, but overal the theme is represented rather well.  Performance wise the game has been improved since the demo, but it is for sure slightly above medium rigs as of the release of the game.   While it is my personal preference that Anno 1800 is a better game, Anno 117 offers a very solid, very enjoyable gameplay loop at the day of the release. If you are interested in Anno games I would advice you to pick one with the theme or mechanics you prefer as both offer enjoyable experience though slightly different. As time goes by and Anno 117 gets more DLCs, the line may start to blurr and season 1 looks promising with two new regions, one with volcano, one in Egypt. Combined with land armies and waried population tiers, I believe future for this game might be worth attention both now and especially in the future.  But it is also worth mentioning AI was used in this game. For instance loading screns bare marks of AI generation and it harms the quality of the game. It is unknown which if any other parts of the game have gen AI content, which makes some people hesitant about the overal quality of the game. Keep this in mind when making a choice.

Et Tu, Ubisoft? Another Woke Wreck in the Rubble of Rome. Avoid This Buggy, Bare Bones Blunder  [b]Oh, Anno 117: Pax Romana, where do I even start?[/b] You promised me the glory of the Roman Empire, a sprawling city builder where I could channel my inner Trajan and forge an eternal legacy of aqueducts, legions, and olive oil empires. Instead, what did I get? A half baked toga party crashed by modern politics, technical tantrums, and enough empty promises to make Caligula blush. I have sunk 20 hours into this mess, mostly watching my GPU sweat like a gladiator in the Colosseum, and I am here to warn you: skip this one unless you enjoy paying premium denarii for a game that is shallower than the Tiber after a drought. Ubisoft, you have done it again, turning what could have been a triumph into a tragic comedy of errors. Let us unpack this imperial disaster, shall we? Picture this: a game that struts in with all the pomp of a triumphal parade, only to trip over its own sandals and face plant into mediocrity. It is not just disappointing. It is a betrayal of history, hardware, and honest gameplay.  [b]The Woke Woes: History Hijacked for Hashtags[/b] Gods above, the wokeness is everywhere, like graffiti on the Forum walls screaming "Diversity or Die!" They shoehorn in female blacksmiths hammering away like it is no big deal, but newsflash: in 117 AD, women were not swinging 10kg mallets in forges hotter than Vulcan's forge. They were running textile shops or influencing from the shadows, not pretending to be Xena the Warrior Princess in a historically male gig. And do not get me started on the "diverse" North Africans portrayed as sub Saharan elites. Actual history says they were Mediterranean Berbers and Punics, olive skinned folks akin to Italians, not some anachronistic nod to modern quotas. No Black emperors? Septimius Severus was Punic Italian, not Wakandan royalty. No direct Chinese contacts? Yet here is Ubisoft tossing in Asian envoys like it is a Silk Road swap meet. Female rulers calling the shots? Ha! In Trajan's era, women had zilch official power. No legions, no votes, just sneaky family politicking. This is not artistic license. It is ideological fanfic, courtesy of Ubisoft's "Project Rise" DEI mandates and those sweet German/EU subsidies that demand you cram in equity checkboxes or lose funding. It is like they are saying, "Render unto Caesar what is Caesar's, but only if Caesar identifies as non binary and leads a pride parade through the Senate." Witty? More like woke washing history to appease BlackRock's ESG scores, insulting both ancient Romans and us players who want immersion, not a lecture. Tyrants rewrite history, Ubisoft. Congratulations on joining the club. And let us not forget the slave mechanics, oversimplified to the point of parody, as if Ubisoft feared offending the gods of sensitivity by making anything too gritty or real. It all feels like a corporate checklist, not a love letter to antiquity. If history is a banquet, this game serves up fast food versions of the classics, supersized with agenda and skimpy on substance.  [b]Performance Pitfalls: A Chariot Race to the Crash Site[/b] But hey, maybe the gameplay saves it? Nope, it is a buggy chariot race straight off a cliff. Performance is atrocious. My RTX 4080 was cooking like it was auditioning for a Pompeii reenactment, even after turning off ray tracing, which defaults to max, because why not fry your rig? Crashes galore: open the build menu? Boom, error code exile. Waves in the water? They reveal ugly terrain glitches like the gods forgot to render the sea properly. Frame drops on a 4090/i9 setup? Loading times longer than a Senate filibuster? Freezes, not responding, and alt F4 becoming my most used hotkey? Ubisoft's support pages are full of "report your bug" pleas, but fixes are scarcer than honest politicians in Rome. And this is launch day stuff. The early access demo was trashed for the same reasons. It is like they optimized for a wax tablet, not a modern PC. Ubisoft, your engine has more holes than Swiss cheese in a legionary's rations. I have seen better stability in a house of cards during an earthquake. Add in the AMD GPU woes from the beta, where menus crash harder than the Roman economy under inflation, and you have a recipe for rage quits. Why ship something this unpolished? It screams rushed development, probably to hit those quarterly targets while the stock tanks. Players deserve better than this digital dumpster fire.  [b]Content Catastrophe: Shallow as a Dried Up Aqueduct[/b] Then there is the lack of content, which hits like a barbarian sack of Rome. Compared to Anno 1800's depth? This is a kiddie pool. No Docklands style hubs, no collection buildings, devs straight up said "no plans," and cross regional trade is so optional it makes the game feel like easy mode for toddlers. Regions are not equal. Some are fleshed out, others barren as the Sahara. The campaign is a snooze: build, expand, repeat, with diplomacy and warfare tacked on like afterthoughts. It is gorgeous, sure. Diagonal roads make towns feel natural, islands alive. But it is all surface shine hiding shallow mechanics. You will blitz through tiers, then realize it is DLC bait city. Needs polish? More like a full expansion pack to not feel like a step backward. Replayability? Only if you enjoy restarting from scratch because the demo's design actively deters you. It is like Ubisoft shipped half a game, expecting us to pay for the rest later. Where is the meaty logistics, the intricate supply chains that made predecessors addictive? Here, it is streamlined to boredom, with naval systems that promise epic seas but deliver puddle skirmishes. And the narrative? Thin as papyrus, barely scratching the surface of Roman intrigue. If Anno 1800 was a feast, this is scraps from the table, leaving you hungry for more. But more costs extra, naturally.  [b]Ubisoft's Undoing: An Empire in Freefall[/b] And let us not forget the bigger picture: Ubisoft's epic fail parade. Their stock has plummeted 85% since 2021, nearing all time lows after flops like Assassin's Creed Shadows, delayed amid "woke" backlash. Feudal Japan with DEI twists? Yikes. Cancelled games? Oh boy: that post Civil War Assassin's Creed "Project Scarlet" axed because it might "offend the KKK." You cannot make this up. Corporate cowardice at its finest. Beyond Good & Evil 2? Vaporware. XDefiant? Shuttered with 143 layoffs. Skull & Bones? Delayed to death, launched DOA. High budgets, low sales, and a "wild ride mostly downhill" per analysts. Yves Guillemot calls Shadows' controversy a "wake up call," but here we are with Anno 117 repeating the sins. Bowing to politics, ignoring players, and churning out unoptimized messes. Market shares collapsed, bookings slashed from $2.4B to $2.2B for 2025. Ubisoft is dying, folks, and this game is just another nail in the coffin. They are like Nero fiddling while their empire burns, except Nero at least had better PR. How many more franchises will they butcher before shareholders pull the plug? It is a tragedy worthy of Sophocles, but with microtransactions instead of catharsis.  [i]In the end, Anno 117 is not Pax Romana. It is Pax Ridicula, a ridiculous peace where history bends the knee to agendas, tech fails spectacularly, and content starves like plebs in a famine.[/i] If you want Rome done right, replay Anno 1800 or boot up Civilization. Ubisoft, vade retro. Go back and fix your mess. 2/10, and that is generous for the pretty pixels. Save your sesterces, legionaries. This one is not worth the march.  also, a small note, mods actively ban anyone one the forums discussing this topic but the far left gets to say and do whatever they want without punishment.

Anno 117: Pax Romana feels like a missed opportunity—visually polished but creatively stagnant, offering little beyond a Roman skin atop Anno 1800’s familiar mechanics.  Despite its promising premise—governing provinces during the height of the Roman Empire—Anno 117: Pax Romana rarely ventures beyond the path laid by its predecessor. The logistics and multi-island trade routes remain intact, but they’re recycled wholesale from Anno 1800, with only superficial tweaks to justify the new setting.  - Core gameplay loop is nearly identical to Anno 1800: build housing, satisfy citizen needs, unlock new tiers, and expand trade networks.  - Combat and diplomacy systems remain shallow, with minimal strategic depth or tension. Land warfare returns, but it’s more of a checkbox feature than a meaningful evolution. - Cultural divergence between Roman and Celtic provinces is underutilized. While you can choose to enforce Roman customs or preserve Celtic traditions, the impact is mostly cosmetic. - The game introduces a Discovery Tree for tech progression, but its structure feels arbitrary. - Citizen progression is more flexible, allowing multiple goods to satisfy needs.  - The game is undeniably gorgeous, with detailed architecture, lush environments, and charming animations. But beneath the surface, it’s all too familiar. - UI improvements and quality-of-life features make it more accessible, especially for newcomers. Yet for veterans, this polish doesn’t compensate for the lack of innovation.  Verdict If you loved Anno 1800 and simply want more of the same with a Roman twist, Pax Romana delivers. But if you were hoping for a bold reinvention or deeper historical immersion, you’ll find it dull, derivative, and disappointingly safe. It’s not a bad game—it’s just the same game, again.

Same Anno we all admire and love; finally in a setting that's fun.  Graphics are amazing, city building aspects are refreshing; day one... this [i]feels[/i] good to play. Combat is pretty straightforward, tutorial is almost not needed; supply chains, population class management, and deity buffs are all integrated very well for classical antiquity flavor.  Very impressed with the launch status - game is stable and beautiful.  10 hours in and this has already reserved a seat in the top 5 grouping of all time city builders for me. The artistic approach is phenomenal; the attention to detail with each building is engrossing and the ability to rotate buildings after they have already been placed and change their look with the aesthetic tool is very immersive. Not to mention the ability to move again if you made a mistake with the placement.  Watching the aqueducts rip through a sprawling city littered with a myriad of different farms,  coastal buildings and housing districts makes this game not feel like you're just placing buildings just to place them - this feels alive.  Invading another governor sends the population scattering and fleeing - the custom farm tool to hug extra space and not simply be a square building scattered around your city.  This experience feels organic, which is lacking from this genre; somebody paid attention to the little details and minutiae of city life and injected it into this title and the labor of love shows.  And then you get to the Celts.  I almost wept at the graphics of a stormy marshland, the sun being reflected off the indentation of wagon wheels in the mud, and the sway of the reeds with the wind. And then came the joy of then using said marshland for farming, grazing and raw resources. I have been spamming my f12 button wishing I could have this as a screensaver.  I haven't felt this excited to play into a city builder more than a couple sessions since Manor Lords and Banished. And this gives both of those a run for their money - and it's day one.  Briefly looking at the roadmap, it looks to include Pompeii style volcanic activity, or farming from the aftermath, games and festivals with iconic buildings, and the Egyptian delta... All of which looks to be actual exciting additions and not slop just for the sake of updating the game.       This game was worth the wait.

Performance has improved slightly after the Day 1 patch. Yet, I still wouldn't recommend this game to seasoned players. If I could, I would give this game a mixed review. The game itself is excellent, but it's plagued by minor bugs (e.g., being unable to build blueprinted buildings on top of resources such as minerals). There's a disconnect between the size of your city and the immense buildings you can make when you haven't even progressed past the second tier of citizens. Lastly, the Roman setting, albeit interesting, is more abstract than the setting of Anno 1800. To me, the reason for this is that I can't "realistically" imagine how a Roman city would have looked, but I can quickly Google "Brussels" and get inspiration.  On the topic of theme, the connection between theme and mechanics was stronger in previous installments than in this one, so it seems more like a reskin of 1800 than a game built on the concept of creating a Roman city.

Make sure to update nVidia drivers to latest, it’s important   5070ti 7800x3d, dlss balanced, ray tracing off, high settings, 4K - I’m getting 70-100 fps, even 120 when zooming in   Got problem on start, but now when got good graphics settings game is great   Graphic isn’t the best I’ve seen (Manor Lords created by one man got much much better graphics, much better units, close look is brilliant etc. shame on Anno devs in this part) but it’s still nice. If manor lords is 9/10 then Anno is 7/10. It’s nice.  Gameplay is great, military units move and behave quite naturally. I like it very much. Campaign got interesting story and is great to start game. Generally it’s very good strategic game but need performance improvements for sure.  Also I don’t fully like black centurion - but, ugh, I just know it’s not historical. I just have to ignore it, stupid propaganda.

GOTY  Downsides: - The UI is a lot worse than in 1800. They could have kept the same exact UI and it would have been 2x better than the one they have currently. - A lot of mechanics that made Anno 1800 great and replayable have been removed (collectible items, optimizing your production with items and collections)  Still a decent game but nowhere near even Anno 1800 release version.
